Star Wars the Old Republic

GURPS 4E
Conversion

A consolidated GURPS 4e companion for the Old Republic era — every rule of combat, gear, machine, and world in one volume. Twelve documents, one reference.
Combat · Ranged · Damage · The Living Force · Vehicles · Equipment · Droids · Skills · Force Training · Trade · The Galaxy · Factions

How to Use This Compendium

A map for the reader opening this cold — what the book is, what you need, and where to start. Naming note: catalogue armament lines say “blaster cannon” — at ship scale that is the same weapon family as the laser-cannon entries on the anti-vehicle table (light/medium/heavy map 1:1). The character sheet maps the names automatically.

What This Is SW

This is a companion to GURPS 4th Edition, not a standalone game. It assumes you have the GURPS Basic Set for the core mechanics — attributes, the 3d6 success roll, character points, advantages and skills. What this volume adds is everything the setting needs that the core rules don’t cover: a tuned combat layer, a complete Force-tradition and training system, a full equipment and materials economy, vehicles and starships, droids, and the galaxy itself. It is built for the Old Republic era but the systems are deliberately era-portable — the mechanics work for any point in the Old Republic or beyond; only the named worlds and factions in Document X are era-specific, and they are easily swapped.

What You Need to Play

  • The GURPS Basic Set (Characters & Campaigns) for the underlying system.
  • This compendium, for everything setting-specific.
  • Three six-sided dice, pencils, and the companion character sheet (referenced throughout for its training, fuel, and shield trackers).
  • A GM and one or more players. Nothing here assumes a particular party size or composition.

How the Ten Documents Fit Together

The volume runs rules first, setting last — mechanics you reference constantly up front, the world that uses them at the back:

DocumentCoversYou’ll Reach For It When…
I · Core CombatThe turn, maneuvers, defenses, grappling, martial artsAnyone swings, shoots, or dodges.
II · Ranged CombatGuns, accuracy, suppression, cover, reflecting boltsThe shooting starts.
III · Damage, Wounds & RecoveryDR, wound types, criticals, armour divisors, healingSomeone gets hit — the heart of the lethality model.
IV · The Living ForceAlignment, the traditions, powers, lightsaber Forms, progressionA Force-user does anything Force-related.
V · Force TrainingStudy-hours, regimens, mentors, trials & the climb through the tiersA Force-user wants to grow.
VI · Vehicles & StarshipsScale, dogfights, fuel, the ship catalogue, boardingThe scene leaves the ground.
VII · Equipment CompendiumWeapons, armour, shields, gear, materials, and the trade economyAnyone buys, sells, or carries anything.
VIII · DroidsDroid types, construction, behaviour, the droid catalogueA droid is built, bought, or fought.
IX · SkillsThe skill list, the point ledger, crafting and the Maker’s chainsYou need a skill, or you’re building by points.
X · The GalaxyThe era’s history, regions, worlds, factions, reputation & lexiconYou need the setting the rest of the book lives in.

Where to Start

If you’re a player: read the Galaxy overview (Doc X, Part I) for the world you’re in, skim Core Combat and Damage so you know how a fight feels, and — if you play a Force-user — read The Living Force tradition that fits your character. Everything else you can look up at the table.

If you’re the GM: read I–III properly (the combat engine), skim VII and VI for what the economy and ships can do, and read X for the setting and the reputation system. The quick-reference cheatsheets at the end of several documents (the combat cheatsheet, the Crew Operations sheet) are built to live at your elbow during play.

Everyone: the navigation panel (the ☰ Navigate button, top-left) opens every document and section; the Contents page below does the same. Cross-references throughout are live links — tap any “see Doc IV” to jump straight there.

Reading Conventions

A few marks recur throughout. The SW tag flags a Star Wars-specific rule layered over generic GURPS. The HOUSE tag flags a house rule original to this volume. Damage is written in GURPS dice (3d, 8d+8); an armour divisor in parentheses ((2), (10)) means the attack ignores that fraction of DR, and no parenthesis means full DR. Legality runs — / Lic / Res / Mil / Illegal / Rare (the LC column). Prices are baselines — the Variation System bends them by place and situation. For dense notation, the Jargon Index (Doc VI) decodes stat-block shorthand and the Galactic Lexicon (Doc X) decodes setting terms.

Balance & Errata Log

Living record of rules corrections — what changed, and why. The sheet and this volume move together.

2026-06-19 — Forms balance, Saber/Lightning Lock & the Force-defence pass.

  • Saber forms — mobility and ferocity now bend defences, not pad damage. Ataru drops the old flat “+N damage if moved” for a Momentum Strike rider: move 2+ hexes and your strike is +to-hit while the target defends at −X (+1/−1 rising to +3/−3 by Battle Master) — speed they cannot read, which tells against the high Parry/Dodge where flat damage was moot. Juyo trades Fury Stack’s chip damage for Overwhelming Tempo (each defended attack cracks the foe’s guard — cumulative −1 to their defences, cap −2 / −3 / −4). Vaapad gains Channel the Current (bank +to-hit per incoming attack, cap +2 / +3 / +4) and its reactive techniques now also penalise a dark-sider’s defences. The sheet’s Saber Forms text moves with the book.
  • New: Saber Lock & Lightning Lock (House). A blade-bind in Doc I §1.8½ — a Quick Contest of the wielder’s Force attribute (not the saber’s up-to-+20 skill), triggered on purpose or by an accidental 1d-on-a-6 clash between saber-survivable blades, with margin-scaled outcomes. A lit lightsaber now always parries Force Lightning, and the parry starts a sustained Lightning Lock at the heavy-energy Tutaminis rate (1 FP per 4 damage held). Force Lightning, Electric Judgment, Tempered Blade and the Bolt-Deflection table cross-reference it; Tempered Blade adds +1 to the contest — resolving the old “does a saber catch lightning?” contradiction.
  • Force-power defence — consistency & propagation. The attacker-parity “or Force skill if higher” resist is now confined to Force-on-Force / Force-connection powers (kept on Drain Force; added to Sever Feeding, Rend the Thread, Eat the Name; stripped from the mental/mind-reading powers Mind Shard, Fracture, Surface Read, Deep Read, Mindbreak Veil and the shock power Force Jolt). Mind Block is named as a universal mental defence on every fear/mind power. Pain/agony resists with HT (Hex of Agony moved Will→HT; High Pain Threshold still halves). Every statted power’s Defence line is now carried as a concise def field into the sheet’s force-power rows and the GM console’s NPC data.
  • Weapons & armour. The experimental greatsword binds to Two-Handed Sword (it was defaulting to Broadsword); the Crushgaunt is AD 3 across the sheet, GM console and this table; the thrown vibro-pike keeps AD 4 as a dedicated armour-piercing munition; and five faction armours (Republic marine, Kolto-weave, Hutt enforcer, Gamorrean harness, Echani lattice) gained per-location DR so the hit-location system reads them.

2026-06-18 — Ship weapons: mounts, warhead flight & balance.

  • Mounts have sizes, and hulls have a hardpoint budget. Every ship weapon now sits in a Small / Medium / Large / Capital mount; a larger mount holds any smaller weapon, never the reverse. Each class carries a fixed count of each size (see Mounts & Hardpoints) — a fighter can’t bolt on a turbolaser, and a frigate’s gun count is now a real ceiling rather than a vibe. The sheet enforces and totals it per ship.
  • Guided warheads have travel time. Beams hit the turn they fire; missiles arrive 0–2 turns later and torpedoes 1–3, by range. In the air they can be shot down by point-defence and dodged again — the trench-run shot is now a gamble, not a guaranteed hit. The sheet prints the impact turn; the GM console flies the warhead on its turn clock and resolves it on arrival.
  • Sheet weapon stats reconciled to this table. A stat-drift in the sheet had the heavy laser at RoF 3 (canon RoF 1), the laser cannon at RoF 3 (RoF 2), the burst laser at RoF 13 (RoF 8) and most cannons at Rcl 2 (Rcl 1, which is what lets a quad turret saturate). The sheet now reads its numbers from the catalogue here — the burst laser in particular drops from a 13-shot firehose to its intended 8.

2026-06-17 — Ship damage rework (Hull HP pool).

  • Hull is a hit-point pool again — and the Damage Threshold (DT) stat is retired. The 11 Jun “no HP pool / compare-penetration-to-DT” model let a single post-shield hit destroy a random component outright (a 6d×3 cannon = 53 through ≥ 2×DT → system dead, and the location roll could read reactor), so a fighter went from shielded to gutted in one bolt. Resolution is now shields → hull DR − AP → a Hull HP pool. A plain hit just whittles the pool; systems break only at integrity thresholds (½ and ¼) or under a called shot. Hull HP is set by hull class (≈ Hull DR × 5–7), so no per-ship bookkeeping and no edits to the 65 catalogue cards.
  • Crippled vs destroyed. Hull HP 0 = Crippled (adrift, shields/drive/guns dead, hull intact and salvageable — feeds the prize economy); past −½ max = Destroyed. A single hit exceeding the whole pool still vaporises (a turbolaser on a fighter). Ion never vaporises and never touches hull — ×2 vs shields, into systems.
  • Ranged stat block completed. Ship weapons now carry RoF and Rcl alongside Acc; range stays the band table (Knife ±0 · Close −4 · Medium −6 · Long −8 · Extreme −12, Acc cancels). RoF 5+ feeds the to-hit bonus; Rcl drives extra hits from a wide-arc turret.
  • Module slots by class. Each hull has a slot budget (fighter 1–2 … capital 12+); structural mods and active systems each cost a slot — size now caps how much kit a hull can carry.

2026-06-11 — Ship combat pass.

  • Range bands replace metre-counting. Gunnery now uses five flat band modifiers (Knife-fight ±0 · Close −4 · Medium −6 · Long −8 · Extreme −12). The old “km × 1,000 on the Speed/Range Table” reading produced −16s and worse at ordinary dogfight ranges — mathematically unrollable, and never how it was played.
  • Every ship weapon now carries an Acc rating (Aim one turn → +Acc on the next shot), and the anti-vehicle table header now correctly reads AP — it was still labelled “AD” from the retired armour-divisor model.
  • Ion weapons keyed to Ion Gunner; Gunner defaults at −2 (and vice versa). The sheet picks the right skill automatically.
  • Launcher ammunition is explicit. Warhead counts live on each ship card; a “missile rack” is a 4-shot launcher. No more infinite torpedoes.
  • Hull damage restated in DT, not HP. There is no hit-point pool at ship scale: a crippled hull now degrades the vessel’s DT by 25% (50% breached), so a battered ship breaks up under fire — same attrition, component-native math. (Superseded 2026-06-17 — hull is an HP pool again; DT retired.) Repair ladder restated to match: jury-rig ◐→ working in combat · Field Patch ✕→◐ out of combat · hangar restores ● fully.
  • Shields rebanded ×5 — the missing half of the AP conversion. When ship damage went 1:1 (and hull DR was cut ÷5), shield pools were never rescaled — every screen sat at ⅕ strength: a fighter’s 60 SP died to a single 6d×3 hit and the old “1–5 SP per turn” recharge meant nothing. All shield values are now ×5 across the catalogue, the sheet and the GM Console Bridge (Aurek 60→300 · Hammerhead 200→1,000 · Centurion 420→2,100); recharge is 10% of maximum per calm turn, halved on damaged emitters. Ion weapons get their niche back — ×2 vs shields now strips screens in half the passes instead of tying lasers at one. The sheet’s own ship list still carried pre-conversion hull values (Aurek DR 40 vs canon 8) — it has been regenerated from this volume’s full 65-ship catalogue, and saved ships conform themselves on next open.
  • Force & stances conform pass. The sheet and compendium catalogues are now verified against each other, both directions: the sheet gains the two minor-tradition capstones it was missing (The Sky Made Answer, Baran Do T5 · Heart of the Multitude, Zeltron T5 — every minor tradition now runs T1→T5 like the great books); the compendium gains the Force Deflection power entry (Common Force T2 — it was referenced by the Bolt Deflection technique but never statted) and the two Iktotchi battle stances that existed only on the sheet; and the sheet gains Echani Flowing Mirror, which existed only here. Both catalogues now hold the same 44 stances. Point costs audited: all 205 powers sit exactly on the 5/10/20/35/60 ladder — no outliers.

Player’s Primer · A

Creating a Character

Six steps from blank sheet to playable hero — with a worked example

Overview

What this is. The fast on-ramp to a playable hero — the meaning behind every field on the sheet and the order to fill them in.

How to use it. Read the six steps once, then build alongside the live sheet, which does the point maths for you. The worked example at the end shows a full 150-point character end to end.

Start Here

Your First
Character

What the numbers mean · how points are spent · the order of operations

Everything else in this book describes the galaxy your hero lives in and the rules they live by. This chapter is where you make the hero. You do not need to memorise anything — the live character sheet does the arithmetic. What this chapter teaches is what the choices mean, and the order to make them in, so the sheet’s fields stop looking like a cockpit you have never flown.

How Points Work

The Budget. You build a character by spending Character Points. The campaign default is 150 points — a capable professional at the start of their story. Your GM may set a different number; everything below scales to whatever total you are given.

Where Points Go. Four things cost or refund points: attributes (the priciest), advantages (perks you buy), disadvantages (flaws that refund points), and skills (cheap — and where most of a character’s competence actually lives).

The Disadvantage Limit. Flaws give points back, but you cannot bury yourself in them: the campaign caps how much you may claim from disadvantages (commonly −75). Spend the refund on attributes, advantages, or skills.

The Six Steps

Work in this order — each step feeds the next, and the sheet is laid out to follow it top to bottom.

StepDo ThisOn the Sheet
1. Concept & SpeciesDecide who they are in a sentence, and pick a species. Human is the baseline; other species adjust attributes and add traits.Character tab
2. AttributesSet ST, DX, IQ, HT. All start at 10 (average).Attributes block
3. SecondaryHP, Will, Per, FP, Basic Speed & Move — mostly derived; tune if you like.Secondary block
4. TraitsBuy advantages; take disadvantages for points back.Traits tab
5. SkillsBuy what your hero can do. This is most of the build.Skills tab
6. Finishing TouchesStarting gear & credits by wealth, background, and a final balance check.Equipment tab · budget bar

Attributes & What They Cost

The four basic attributes start at 10 and define everything downstream. Raising one is expensive; lowering one below 10 refunds points.

AttributeGovernsCost per +1
ST — StrengthDamage, lifting, HP10 points
DX — DexterityMost combat & physical skills, Basic Speed20 points
IQ — IntelligenceMental skills, Will, Perception20 points
HT — HealthStamina, recovery, FP, Basic Speed10 points

Rule of thumb. DX and IQ are the powerhouses — each raises a whole family of skills — which is why they cost double. A single point of DX can be worth more than ten points of skills.

Secondary Characteristics

Derived for free. HP starts equal to ST, Will and Per equal to IQ, FP equal to HT. Basic Speed = (DX + HT) ÷ 4; Basic Move = Basic Speed rounded down.

Tuning them. Buy these up or down on their own — HP at 2 points each, Will and Per at 5, FP at 3, Basic Speed at 5 per +0.25. Cheaper than raising the whole attribute when you need only the one thing.

Advantages & Disadvantages

Advantages are paid-for edges — Combat Reflexes, High Pain Threshold, Fit, Force Sensitivity (the gateway to Document V), wealth, rank, allies. Each has a flat or per-level cost.

Disadvantages are the flaws that make a hero a person — a Code of Honor, a Sense of Duty, a Vow, an Enemy, a phobia. They refund points and hand the GM hooks for your story.

Pick for character, not maths. The best disadvantages are ones you want to roleplay. A flaw you never feel is point-padding; a flaw that bites is a story.

Codes of Honour & Cultural Obligations

“A blaster tells you what a being can do. A code tells you what they will not do — and that is the only thing you can ever really trust.”
— attributed to a Jedi Sentinel of the Restoration, teaching at Dantooine

Most disadvantages describe a flaw a character has. A code describes a line a character holds — a thing they will not do even when doing it would save their life, win the fight, or fill their hand with credits. That is why a code refunds points: it takes options off the table, and in a galaxy this dangerous, options are everything.

But a cultural code does something a personal quirk never can. It says who you are, and whose you are. Break a Vow and you have broken your word. Break the Resol’nare and you are no longer a Mandalorian — not in your own eyes, not in your people’s, not (they will tell you) in the eyes of whatever waits past death. The codes in this section are the ones that carry that second weight: the faiths, the creeds, and the cultural debts that make a being part of a people, and that can cast them out.

How a code lives, and how it breaks

Read this first. A code is not a checklist the GM audits each scene. It is a pressure — most felt in the moment when keeping it costs something. Run them this way:

  • The code bites at the hard choice, not the easy one. A Jedi who spares a beaten foe loses nothing; a Jedi who must spare the foe who just killed their padawan is paying the code. That scene is the point of taking it.
  • A breach is a story beat, not a fine. When a character breaks their code, don’t dock points — spend it. Reputations shift, kin turn cold, a master’s holocron goes silent. The bookkeeping is the least interesting consequence.
  • Codes erode; they rarely shatter. A Jedi does not fall in one swing. A Mandalorian does not become dar’manda the first time they bend a tenet — they become it the day they stop trying to come back. Let the slide be gradual, and let the character feel each step.
  • The way back is conduct, not apology. Cultures with exile (Mandalorians, Wookiees, the Echani, the Sith) almost never accept words as restitution. You re-earn your place by living the code again, in front of the people who watched you break it.

Code of Honor (Jedi) — −10 to −25 (by level)

There is no emotion, there is peace.
There is no ignorance, there is knowledge.
There is no passion, there is serenity.
There is no chaos, there is harmony.
There is no death, there is the Force.
— The Jedi Code

The Jedi Code is not a set of rules so much as a way of standing in the Force: from stillness, never from storm. The disadvantage binds the conduct that stillness demands.

What it holds you to. Act from serenity, never from anger, fear, or hatred — the three doors the dark side waits behind. Guard against attachment, the love that becomes possession and the grief that becomes wrath; a Jedi may love the galaxy, but may not need any single part of it so badly that losing it would break them. Defend the innocent and serve the Force and the Republic before yourself. Treat the blade as the last answer, never the first — a Jedi who can talk, should. Answer the Council, even when the Council is wrong. And never, for any prize or any grief, reach for the dark.

What it costs you. The price of serenity is that you may have to watch someone you love walk into danger and not let attachment move your hand. The price of restraint is letting a guilty being live because killing them would be vengeance, not justice. A Jedi who kills in anger, abandons the helpless to save their own skin, or draws on the dark “just this once” has broken faith — and in the Force, that breach is felt before it is confessed.

Mechanics — the depth of the vow. The Jedi Code is a leveled disadvantage: the deeper the renunciation, the more it binds and the more it returns. Each level folds in a heavier restraint — the “Pacifism” end of the path is built in, not bolted on.

LevelPtsWhat it adds (cumulative)
Knight−10Serenity over passion; never the dark side; restraint; serve the Force and the Republic before self; answer the Council.
Guardian−15+ defend all living things and never abandon the helpless, even at cost (the weight of Sense of Duty, all living things).
Consular−20+ the blade is truly the last resort — kill only at utmost need, and feel it afterwards (Reluctant Killer).
Master−25+ renounce attachment and possession, bound by a Vow — the ascetic ideal, and the rarest path.

Most player Jedi take the Knight or Guardian level; Master is the austere ideal few hold. The Grandmaster requirement below is met at Guardian (−15) or higher. Falling from any level is the slow slide toward the dark described above — not a refund.

Code of Honor (Sith) — −5

Peace is a lie, there is only passion.
Through passion, I gain strength.
Through strength, I gain power.
Through power, I gain victory.
Through victory, my chains are broken.
The Force shall free me.
— The Sith Code

Where the Jedi Code is a discipline of restraint, the Sith Code is a licence — but a licence with teeth, and the teeth are the disadvantage. It does not forbid cruelty; it forbids weakness.

What it holds you to. Strength is the only law and the only right — what you can take and hold is yours. Never show weakness, to an enemy, an ally, or an apprentice; mercy that reads as softness invites the knife. Repay every slight — an insult left unanswered is a crack others will widen. Accept that an apprentice’s duty is to grow strong enough to one day supplant the master, and a master’s duty is to be ready when they try; this is not betrayal, it is the order of things. Allies are tools, kept while useful. Betrayal is doctrine — but the doctrine cuts both ways, so never be the one caught unready for it.

Why it is only −5. The Sith Code almost never forces a Sith to act against their own interest — that is the whole point of it. It costs them only the things a predator can’t afford: the mercy that looks like weakness, the insult swallowed, the back left unguarded out of trust. A code that rarely demands sacrifice is a cheap code, mechanically — hence −5.

Mechanics & stacking. −5 (the “low” tier). It stacks with the mandatory dark-side Corruption disadvantages a Sith accrues as they climb (Bloodlust, Megalomania, Intolerance, Paranoia — see Doc IV’s corruption ladder). Those carry the real weight on the sheet; the Code is the conduct around them. A Sith with the Code but none of the corruption is a fledgling; the galaxy will correct that.

Code of Honor (Resol’nare) — −10 to −25 (by level)

Ba’jur bal beskar’gam, ara’nov, aliit, Mando’a bal Mand’alor — an vencuyan mhi.
(“Education and armour, self-defence, our tribe, our language and our leader — all help us survive.”) — the Resol’nare, recited in Mando’a

The Resol’nare — the Six Actions — is the spine of Mandalorian identity. And the crucial thing, the thing that makes a Mandalorian unlike any other people in the galaxy: it is not blood, and it is not birth. It is conduct. A Human, a Twi’lek, a Togruta, a Zabrak — anyone who lives the Six Actions is Mandalorian, fully and without asterisk. And anyone born to the clans who abandons them is not, no matter whose child they are. Aliit ori’shya tal’din — “family is more than blood.” The mask makes the Mando.

The Resol’nare — the Six Actions
  1. Ba’jur — Education. Raise your children as Mandalorians. Teach them the Actions, the tongue, the way of the armour. A people is only ever one untaught generation from extinction.
  2. Beskar’gam — The Armour. Wear it. Beskar’gam means “iron skin”; the armour is not a uniform but a second self, handed down, repaired, never abandoned. To take it off and hide is to hide what you are.
  3. Ara’nov — Self-Defence. Defend yourself and your own. A Mandalorian does not start every fight, but never fails to finish one; helplessness is not a virtue.
  4. Aliit — The Clan. Contribute to the welfare of the tribe. The aliit comes before the self; your strength is the clan’s, and the clan’s strength is yours. Vode an — brothers all.
  5. Mando’a — The Language. Speak the mother tongue. A people that loses its words loses its memory; to keep Mando’a alive is to keep every Mandalorian who ever lived alive in it.
  6. Mand’alor — The Leader. When the Mand’alor — the “sole ruler” — calls, you answer. Not blindly, but bound: the clans are a confederation of fierce, proud houses, and the one thing that makes them a people rather than a brawl is that, when the horn sounds, they come.

Dar’manda — the worst fate the culture knows. To break the Resol’nare — to refuse the Actions, to abandon the clan, to put down the armour and live as something else — is to become dar’manda: not Mandalorian. The word does not merely mean “exile.” In Mandalorian belief a dar’manda has no heritage, no people, and no soul — no place in the manda, the collective oversoul where the remembered dead endure. It is a spiritual erasure, reckoned worse than death, because death with the Actions kept is not an ending. (Their remembrance for the fallen: Ni su’cuyi, gar kyr’adyc, ni partayli, gar darasuum — “I’m still alive, but you are dead; I remember you, so you are eternal.” The dar’manda gets no such remembrance.)

The way back. Dar’manda is not always permanent — but the road back is never words. It is conduct: to take up the armour again, live the Six Actions in front of the people who watched you abandon them, and be claimed anew by a clan willing to vouch for you. Many never make it. Some never try, and that — the day they stop trying — is the day the state becomes a sentence.

In play. The Resol’nare bites at the ordinary, pragmatic temptations: hide the armour to slip through a checkpoint unnoticed; ignore the Mand’alor’s call because this contract pays better; leave a clan-sib behind because carrying them is slow. None of these alone makes a character dar’manda — but each is a step toward it, and a Mandalorian feels every one.

Mechanics — how absolutely it is held. Like the Jedi Code, the Resol’nare is leveled — from a soldier’s pragmatism to a zealot’s absolutism.

LevelPtsWhat it binds
Creed−10The Six Actions, lived with a soldier’s pragmatism — the ordinary Mandalorian.
Strict−15The dogmatic reading (Death Watch zealots, old-blood traditionalists): the Actions as absolute law — any breach, even a sensible one, is dar’manda, and the Mando will fight or die rather than be seen to compromise.
No Quarter−20+ the warrior’s hunger made creed: grant no quarter in a worthy fight (the Combat Honor below, taken into the code itself).
Zealot−25+ Fanaticism for the clans and the Mand’alor — the creed held above survival itself.

Code of Honor (Mandalorian, strict) in older sheets and the GM console is simply the Strict (−15) level by another name.

Naming note. Code of Honor (Mandalorian) and Code of Honor (Resol’nare) are the same trait under two names — older sheets and the GM console say “Mandalorian”; the canonical name is “Resol’nare.”

Combat Honor (Mandalorian) — −10

Mandalorians are not merely bound to a creed — they hunger for the fight. Battle-hardened and blood-hungry, a Mandalorian who has drawn does not stop at advantage; they finish it. This trait can ride alongside the Resol’nare or stand on its own in a merc with the old fire still in the blood.

What it holds you to. Once a worthy fight is joined you see it through to the kill — no breaking off while a foe still stands, no prisoners, no survivors. The one mercy the creed allows is conversion: a foe who throws down their weapon and takes up the Mandalorian way — who would become aliit, kin — may be spared and claimed, for the clans grow by conduct, not blood. Everyone else, the sands keep.

Mechanics. −10 — the Mandalorian reading of Bloodlust: a self-control roll to disengage or hold back, surrender accepted only on the terms above, and never a feigned retreat you do not mean. It stacks freely with the Resol’nare; a strict, battle-hungry Mando carrying both is a −25 character before any other flaw — exactly the weight such a warrior should bear.

Cultural Obligations of the Peoples

Most peoples in the galaxy carry an obligation that comes with the blood — or, for the Mandalorians, instead of it. Many of these already live on the species templates as ordinary GURPS disadvantages; what follows names the creed behind the trait and gives the GM one place to find it.

Mandalorian — the Resol’nare. See above. The only people in the galaxy whose membership is a choice and a discipline rather than an accident of birth, and the keenest for the fight. Code of Honor (Resol’nare) [−10 to −25 by level]; most warriors also carry Combat Honor [−10].

Wookiee — the Life-Debt (wyaaa). When a Wookiee’s life is saved, the debt incurred is not a favour to be repaid and forgotten — it is a bond for life, and the Wookiee joins the honour family of the one who saved them, protecting them as fiercely as their own kin. It is freely given and absolutely binding; a Wookiee who walks away from a life-debt is shamed before their people. This is distinct from the racial Berserk — a life-debt is loyalty, not fury. Sense of Duty (life-debt holder) [−5]; or Vow (major) [−10] when the bond is formally sworn before kin.

Trandoshan — the Scorekeeper’s faith. Trandoshans worship the Scorekeeper, the goddess who tallies each hunter’s worthy kills as Jagannath points and weighs them at death. The hunt is holy; trophies are scripture; and the most blessed prey of all is the Wookiee — which is why Trandoshan and Wookiee have hunted each other to the knife for generations. A Trandoshan believes their power to regrow severed limbs is the Scorekeeper’s favour, granted to the brave and withheld from the coward. This faith is the reason behind their racial Bloodlust, and gives it direction: not mindless slaughter, but the relentless pursuit of a worthy kill. Bloodlust [−10] (already racial), read as the Scorekeeper’s hunt; optional Fanaticism (the Scorekeeper) [−15] for a devout one.

Echani — combat is conversation. To the silver-haired Echani, a fight is not violence but speech — the most honest language there is. You learn who someone truly is by crossing blades with them; to fight an Echani is to be introduced, and to spar is to converse, court, or grieve. An Echani will not refuse an honest challenge, settles disputes blade-to-blade rather than with lies, and holds that to deceive with the body — to fight falsely — is the deepest dishonour. Code of Honor (Echani) [−5]; optional Truthfulness [−5] (the body cannot lie). Pairs naturally with their racial Combat Reflexes and Empathy.

Cathar — the blood owed for the Burning. The Cathar were a proud feline people until Mandalore the Ultimate’s crusade burned their world and hunted them nearly to extinction. The survivors are few, scattered, and they remember. Many carry a slow, banked vendetta against Mandalorians — not hot-blooded, but patient and certain. Intolerance (Mandalorians) [−5]; or Vow / Sense of Duty (avenge Cathar) for one who has made it their life. Pairs with their racial Bad Temper.

Selkath — Manaan’s neutrality. The amphibious Selkath sit atop the galaxy’s only source of kolto, and they have made neutrality an absolute, sacred law: the kolto flows to all sides or to none, and Ahto City’s courts are famously incorruptible — they will sell to Republic and Sith in the same hour, and revoke from either for the smallest breach of the peace. A Selkath raised in that tradition takes no side in others’ wars and holds the kolto truce above any cause. Vow / Sense of Duty (the kolto peace) [−5]; pairs with their racial Pacifism (Reluctant Killer).

Iktotchi — the Arbiter’s impartiality. The twin-horned, precognitive Iktotchi of Iktotch were installed under the Csilla Accords as the galaxy’s neutral arbiters — austere, incorruptible judges of disputes between the great powers. Their gift of foresight is the basis of the role: an Iktotchi who can feel the shape of a lie, or the death down one road, is meant to rule without favour. The obligation is to that impartiality, and to their own clan. Vow (impartial judgement) [−10] for a sworn arbiter; Sense of Duty (own clan) [−5] (already racial). Cross-reference their Precognition / Empathy advantages — the code is built on them.

Dathomiri (Nightsister) — the sisterhood’s law. Dathomir is matriarchal, and the Nightsisters rule it through clan, blood-ritual, and the spirits of the world they commune with. A Nightsister’s first loyalty is to the sisterhood and the clan-mother; the Nightbrothers serve the sisters, not the reverse; and a witch’s sworn word, backed by the spirits, is not lightly broken. Sense of Duty (clan/sisterhood) [−5] + Social Stigma (feared witch) [−5] (both already racial). For a sworn ritual-oath, add Vow (minor/major).

Hutt — avarice, and the sanctity of the deal. The Hutts organise themselves into kajidic — cartel-clans — and live by two truths that sit oddly together: everything is negotiable, and a struck deal is sacred. A Hutt will cheat you blind in the bargaining and then honour the bargain to the letter, because a Hutt whose word is worthless has no business at all. Face, debt, and status are everything. Greed [−15] + Gluttony [−5] (already racial); optional Sense of Duty (the kajidic) [−5].

Sith species / Pureblood / Massassi — the old empire’s pride. The red-skinned Sith and their warrior-bred Massassi were the people whose name the dark-side conquerors took. What survives is caste-loyalty and the pride of an empire that ruled the galaxy once — bred to serve, certain of their own superiority, contemptuous of lesser blood. Bloodlust [−10] / Social Stigma (feared) [−5] (already racial); optional Intolerance (lesser species) [−5] for a Pureblood.

Chiss (Ascendancy) — the cold discipline. The blue-skinned Chiss of the Unknown Regions serve the Ascendancy and its ruling houses with a famously cold meritocracy. Their defining stricture: the Chiss do not strike first — they may pre-empt a proven threat, but will not be seen as the aggressor, and a Chiss who starts an unprovoked war disgraces their house. Loyalty runs to the family and the Ascendancy above any outside cause. Code of Honor (never the aggressor) [−5]; Sense of Duty (ruling house) [−5] for a house-sworn Chiss. Pairs with their racial discipline (Tactical genius, Eidetic Memory).

Republic soldier / Sith trooper — the uniform. Not a racial creed but a faction one, common to PCs and NPCs alike: the Duty a sworn soldier owes. The Republic trooper’s is freely given and lawful; the Sith trooper’s is bound as much by fear as by oath. Duty (Republic / the Empire), frequency to taste [−5 to −15]; Soldier’s Code of Honor [−10] for a line-trooper who lives it.

Bothan — the way of the spynet. The furred Bothans are the galaxy’s spies and brokers, and the Bothan way treats information as both currency and honour: debts of intelligence are remembered, clan reputation in the spynet is everything, and a Bothan who burns a source or sells out their clan’s network is finished in the only economy that matters to them. Sense of Duty (clan spynet) [−5]; optional Secret / Reputation (within the spynet).

People / FactionThe creedTaken mechanically as
MandalorianThe Resol’nare — the Six Actions; identity by conduct; the hunger for a worthy fightCode of Honor (Resol’nare) [−10 to −25] + Combat Honor [−10]
WookieeThe Life-Debt (wyaaa) — a bond for lifeSense of Duty (life-debt) [−5] / Vow (major) [−10]
TrandoshanThe Scorekeeper’s hunt — Jagannath pointsBloodlust [−10] (racial); Fanaticism [−15] (devout)
EchaniCombat is conversation; the body cannot lieCode of Honor (Echani) [−5]; Truthfulness [−5]
CatharThe blood owed for the BurningIntolerance (Mandalorians) [−5]; or Vow
SelkathManaan’s neutrality — the kolto peaceVow / Sense of Duty (kolto peace) [−5]
IktotchiThe Arbiter’s impartialityVow (impartial judgement) [−10]; SoD (clan) [−5]
Dathomiri (Nightsister)The sisterhood’s law; the spirits’ wordSoD (sisterhood) [−5] + Social Stigma [−5]
HuttAvarice, and the sanctity of the struck dealGreed [−15] + Gluttony [−5] (racial)
Sith / Pureblood / MassassiThe old empire’s caste-prideBloodlust [−10] / Social Stigma [−5] (racial)
Chiss (Ascendancy)The cold discipline — never the aggressorCode of Honor (never the aggressor) [−5]
Republic / Sith soldierThe uniform — sworn DutyDuty [−5 to −15]; Soldier’s Code of Honor [−10]
BothanThe way of the spynet — information is honourSense of Duty (clan spynet) [−5]

Defaults, not straitjackets

Every entry above is a default, not a sentence. A Mandalorian raised off-world who never took the armour; a Trandoshan who renounced the hunt and is hunted for it; a Selkath gun-runner who sold the neutrality for credits; a Sith Pureblood who finds the empire’s pride hollow — all of these exist, and all of them are more interesting for breaking type. Drop the trait, swap it, or invert it — but do it deliberately, and let the character carry the weight of being the exception. The point of a code is never the points. It is the moment it costs you something.

Skills

Skills are where competence lives, and they are cheap next to attributes. Each is governed by an attribute and has a difficulty — Easy, Average, or Hard — that sets how many points buy a given level.

PointsEasyAverageHard
1attributeattr −1attr −2
2attr +1attributeattr −1
4attr +2attr +1attribute
+4 eachevery further +1 to the skill costs 4 more points

Read it this way. A high attribute lifts every skill under it; a few points in a skill lift just that one. Most builds raise the controlling attribute a little, then buy the handful of skills they lean on up a notch. The full list and what each skill does is Document IX (Skills).

Worked Example — Tarn Vance, Republic Trooper

Here is a 150-point character built end to end, so the steps stop being abstract. Tarn is a Republic Army line trooper — tough, disciplined, deadly with a rifle, bound by a soldier’s code.

What We BoughtDetailPts
— Attributes (90) —
ST 11+1 × 1010
DX 12+2 × 2040
IQ 11+1 × 2020
HT 12+2 × 1020
— Secondary (14) —
HP 13+2 HP × 24
Will 12 · Per 12+1 each × 510
— Advantages (25) —
Combat Reflexesfaster defences; never freeze15
High Pain Thresholdshrugs off shock penalties10
— Disadvantages (−25) —
Code of Honor (Soldier’s)obey lawful orders; never abandon a comrade−10
Duty (Republic Army, 12−)called up often−10
Sense of Duty (Squad)will not leave them behind−5
— Skills (46) —
Guns (Beam Weapons) 16DX+4, Easy12
Soldier 13IQ+2, Average8
Tactics 12IQ+1, Hard8
Brawling 13 · Knife 12Easy3
Stealth 12 · Throwing 12Average4
First Aid 11 · Savoir-Faire (Mil) 11Easy2
Intimidation 12 · Survival 11 · Climbing 11assorted9
TOTAL150

The result. Tarn rolls against 16 to hit with his blaster carbine — he connects most of the time, even under fire, and Combat Reflexes keep him from being caught flat-footed. No genius and no Jedi, but in a firefight he is exactly who you want beside you. Drop these numbers into the sheet, add a Republic carbine and a combat jumpsuit from Document VII, and he is ready to play.

Player’s Primer · B

The Core Game

How every roll resolves — the one engine under all the rules

Overview

What this is. The single dice engine under every rule in the book — roll 3d6, try to roll under your target.

How to use it. Learn it here once; every later document is just a special case of it. Keep the critical and modifier tables handy for your first few sessions.

The Engine

Three Dice,
One Rule

Success rolls · criticals · margins · contests · the flow of play

Every test in this game — swinging a saber, slicing a door, talking past a guard, holding your nerve — runs on the same small engine. Learn it once here and the rest of the book is just special cases.

The 3d6 Engine

To attempt something uncertain, roll three six-sided dice and try to roll equal to or under your effective skill or attribute. Low is good. That number — your skill, plus or minus any modifiers — is your target.

Why 3d6. Three dice cluster around 10–11, so a target of 10 succeeds about half the time, a 14 most of the time, and 16+ is nearly automatic. Skill above 16 is not wasted — it eats the penalties from range, darkness, wounds, and haste.

Critical Success & Failure

RollResult
3 or 4Critical success — the best plausible outcome
5Critical success if your target is 15+
6Critical success if your target is 16+
≤ targetOrdinary success
> targetFailure
17Critical failure (ordinary failure if your target is 16+)
18, or 10+ over targetAlways a critical failure

Margins & Contests

Margin of success is how far under target you rolled — it measures how well you did, and many rules (damage, repairs, first aid) pay out on it.

Contests. When two characters oppose each other — arm-wrestling, a chase, a battle of wills — each rolls against their own skill and the larger margin wins. A tie favours the defender or the status quo.

Reaction rolls. When the outcome is someone’s attitude rather than a skill, the GM rolls 3d6 on a reaction table, shifted by your behaviour and any relevant advantages.

Modifiers & Difficulty

The GM sets modifiers for the situation; they are simply added to or subtracted from your target before you roll — the single most important lever in play.

SituationTypical modifier
Trivially easy · ample time · good tools+1 to +4
Routine task, normal conditions0
Difficult — bad footing, poor light, rushed−1 to −4
Severe — long range, near-dark, badly wounded−5 to −10

The Flow of Play

Out of combat, play is conversation: the GM describes, you act, and dice appear only when an action is both uncertain and consequential. Most of the game is talking, not rolling.

In combat, time snaps to one-second turns in a strict order of actions — the whole system is Document I (Combat); ranged fire is Document II; what damage does is Document III.

Between adventures, downtime lets you heal, train new skills, and spend earned points — Force growth is Document V (Force Training); everything else uses the study-hour rules on the sheet.

Document I

Core Combat & Martial Arts

Turn structure · Maneuvers · Attacks · Defenses · Reactions

Overview

What this covers. Second-by-second melee and martial arts — the turn, maneuvers, attacks, the three active defenses, posture, grappling, and the legal/forbidden combinations.

How to use it. Skim the Turn and Maneuvers chapters before your first fight; treat the rest as reference you reach for mid-combat. Ranged fire is its own document next door.

Part I

The Turn

Initiative · Basic Speed · The Step

A turn is one second of real time. Each combatant gets one Maneuver (see Part II), defends incoming attacks with one Active Defense per attack (subject to the multiple-defense penalty), and — if Force-sensitive — may use one Special Reaction. Free Actions and Quick Actions sit on top of the maneuver subject to the rules in Part V.

Element What You Get Limit / Cost Notes
ManeuverOne action slot1 / turnChoose from Part II.
Active DefenseDodge, Parry, or Block1 / attackMulti-defense penalty after the first.
StepMove 1 hexFree in most maneuversExcluded from Do Nothing, Change Posture.
Special ReactionForce Push / Pull1 / turn · Force-only · Full FPHouse Offensive permitted in AoA; defensive forbidden.
Quick ActionPush/Pull, Fast-Draw, device activation1 / turnHouse Offensive + Fast-Draw permitted in AoA.
Free ActionSpeak, change facing, drop itemUnlimitedNo roll, no cost.
InitiativeOrder each roundBasic Speed (HT + DX) ÷ 4Tie: higher DX; further tie: 1d6.
SurpriseSide acting first1 roundSurprised: no maneuver, no active defense (DR only).

Initiative & Basic Speed

Basic Speed. (HT + DX) ÷ 4. Fractions are retained — Basic Speed 5.25 acts before 5.00. Cost: 5 points per +0.25. Combat order each round: highest Basic Speed first. Tie: higher DX. Further tie: 1d6.

Surprise. If one side is surprised, the other acts first for one round regardless of Basic Speed. Surprised combatants may take no Maneuver other than Do Nothing and gain no active defenses (DR still applies). Combat Reflexes prevents the ”frozen” effect of surprise.

The Step

Step. One hex of movement (1 m = 1 hex) in any direction. Free in most maneuvers: Attack, Defensive Attack, Aim, Evaluate, Feint, Concentrate, Ready, Wait, and All-Out Defense. Not available with Do Nothing or Change Posture; replaced by full Move with Move, Move and Attack, and All-Out Attack (optional).

Stepping into a hex covered by an enemy Wait may trigger that Wait. Stepping into the enemy’s own hex invokes the close-combat rules.

Advantages Affecting the Turn

Several advantages from GURPS Characters directly affect turn structure. They are not redefined here — refer to the source — but their interactions matter:

Combat Reflexes. +1 to all active defenses; +1 to Fast-Draw; +2 to Fright Checks; +6 to IQ rolls to wake up or recover from mental stun; +1 to your side’s initiative (+2 if you’re the leader); you never ”freeze” in surprise. Critical to nearly every combatant in this campaign.

Enhanced Time Sense. Includes Combat Reflexes; acts before all combatants without ETS regardless of Basic Speed. Cannot stack with Combat Reflexes.

Extra Attack. +1 attack per turn within Attack, AoA, Committed, Defensive Attack, or M&A. Most normal humans cap at level 1.

Compartmentalised Mind. +1 mental maneuver per turn per level. Allows two Concentrate powers in one turn, or one Concentrate + one physical maneuver. Does not grant extra physical attacks.

Part II

Maneuvers

The 21 actions you may choose from each turn

Choose exactly one maneuver per turn. Attack modifiers (Rapid Strike, Deceptive, Targeted, Beat, Feint as substitute attack) apply within your chosen maneuver and are detailed in Part III. Maneuvers govern movement allowance, attack opportunities, defensive capacity, and what kinds of Quick Actions and Reactions may join them in the same second.

Maneuver Source Move Attack(s) Defense Notes
MoveBasicFull—NormalReposition, close, reach cover.
Change PostureBasic——NormalStand, kneel, crouch, prone, sit.
AimBasicSteppreps nextNormal+Acc to next attack; +1/turn extra, cap Acc/2.
EvaluateMASteppreps nextNormal+1/+2/+3 (1–3 turns). Counters foe feints / Deceptive.
FeintBasicStepfeint onlyNormalQC of melee skills. Margin = defense penalty next attack.
ReadyBasicStep—NormalDraw, reload, clear jam, change grip.
ConcentrateBasicStep—NormalFor Force powers tagged Concentrate. Will roll on hit.
WaitBasic—conditionalNormalDeclare trigger; act before trigger completes.
Do NothingBasic——NormalRecover from Stun (HT/IQ). Or pass.
AttackBasicStep1NormalStandard. One attack with any ready weapon.
Defensive AttackMAStep1 (−2 dmg / −1/die)+1 one defenseCautious. May parry with attacking weapon.
Committed Atk (Det)MAStep or 21 (+2 hit)−2 all, no retreatMelee. 2nd step → −2 hit. No defense w/ atk limb.
Committed Atk (Strong)MAStep or 21 (+1 dmg / +½ die)−2 all, no retreatMelee thr/sw — not lightsabers.
Move and AttackBasicFull1Normal — no retreatMelee cap 9 or DX+2. Ranged −2 or Bulk.
AoA (Det)BasicStep/full1 (+4 hit)NONECannot defend. Offensive SR/QA + Fast-Draw OK.
AoA (Strong)BasicStep/full1 (+2 dmg / +1 die)NONEThr/sw — not lightsabers.
AoA (Double)BasicStep/full2NONESame or different targets.
AoA (Feint)BasicStep/full1 (free Feint first)NONEFree Feint fires before the attack.
AoA (Long)MAStep/full1 (reach +1)NONESw at −2 dmg / −1 die.
AoD (Incr)BasicStep—+2 one defensePick Dodge, Parry, or Block. Balanced: +1 to two (MA).
AoD (Double)BasicStep—Two per attackTwo rolls per attack — different types or two parries.

How Maneuvers Work

The summary table above lists all twenty-one at a glance; the rules below give each one in detail. A few maneuvers forbid certain attack modifiers:

  • Move and Attack forbids Rapid Strike.
  • Ranged attacks forbid Rapid Strike, Deceptive, Beat, Ruse, Feint, Combination — use ROF (Doc II) for multiple shots.
  • All-Out Attack (Strong) forbids modifiers needing defensive capacity (Defensive Feint redirection, Riposte).

Movement & Stance

Move. Full Move along any path. No attack. Active defenses normal. To attack while moving, use Move and Attack.

Change Posture. Switch between standing, crouching, kneeling, sitting, supine, or prone. No step, no attack. Active defenses defend as your starting posture. From prone to standing is one Change Posture; Acrobatic Stand may compress this (see Part VII).

Preparation

Aim. Add your weapon’s Acc to your next ranged attack. Aim a second turn for an extra +1; a third (or more) for +2 — that’s the cap (you cannot keep accumulating beyond +2 over Acc). Step permitted; you may not turn more than one hex face. Broken by any other maneuver, by stun, by changing target, or by any successful attack landing on you (Will roll to retain your Aim). Aiming at multiple targets requires Enhanced Tracking.

Evaluate. Study one foe. +1 to hit on your next attack against them; +2 with two turns; +3 with three or more (cap). Step permitted. Broken by stun, another maneuver, or attacking before claiming the bonus. (Martial Arts) Counters foe Deceptive / feint penalties on YOUR defense up to net 0, until you attack and claim the bonus.

Feint. A Quick Contest (QC — both sides roll, higher margin wins) of your best Melee, Unarmed, or Cloak skill vs. the target’s best of the same (or their DX). Your winning margin becomes a penalty to ONE active defense vs. your next attack on that target, next turn. Step permitted. Weapon must be ready. Resisting feints uses your BEST weapon skill — even an unfamiliar weapon in hand. Multiple feints in the same maneuver: only the most recent applies. Feinting never unreadies a weapon. Defensive Feint: margin penalises their next ATTACK on you instead.

Ready. Draw a weapon, reload, clear a jam, activate a device, change grip (Regular / Defensive / Reversed — MA), change polearm reach, or shift between one- and two-handed grip. A weapon stays ready until lost or put away. Putting weapons away normally takes two Readies. Fast-Draw success is a Quick Action (Part V).

SWConcentrate. Sustained mental effort. Required for Force powers tagged Concentrate. Step permitted; no attacks. You cannot run two Concentrate maneuvers in the same turn without Compartmentalised Mind. If any successful attack lands on you (including ones you parry or block), you must make a Will roll — failure breaks your concentration and you lose any FP already spent on the power. Instant Force powers fire from your normal action slot — they do not require Concentrate.

Wait. Declare a specific trigger. When met during another’s turn, your Wait fires and you act before the triggering action completes. The trigger must be specific — entering a hex, drawing a weapon, using a named power, a signal. You may not move when declaring Wait, but when your Wait fires you may use Attack, AoA, Feint, or Ready (with their normal step allowance). If two combatants’ Waits trigger at once, resolve with a Quick Contest of the relevant skill, modified by Combat Reflexes, Basic Speed, and whether either Wait involves movement.

Do Nothing. Take no action. Defenses normal. Required while physically stunned (HT to recover) or mentally stunned (IQ). Default if your Wait’s trigger isn’t met.

Attack Maneuvers

Attack. One attack with any ready weapon. Step permitted. Defenses normal. Extra Attack adds attacks. Rapid Strike trades one attack for two at −6 (−3 TbaM/WM) — melee only. Dual-Weapon Attack trades one attack for two (one per hand) at −4 each.

MADefensive Attack. A cautious melee probe. Damage is reduced by 2, or by 1 per die, whichever is worse. In return, pick ONE defensive benefit, declared before you roll: (a) +1 to one of your active defenses this turn — if your weapon is balanced, this may even be the same weapon you attacked with; (b) if your weapon is unbalanced (“U”), you get +1 Parry with a DIFFERENT weapon, OR may parry with the same weapon at no bonus (an exception to the U-rule); (c) if you kicked, +2 vs. Leg Grapples and +2 DX to avoid falling. Step permitted. If you used Defensive Attack to grapple, your target gets +1 to defend against the grab.

MACommitted Attack — Determined. Single melee attack at +2 to hit. Step, or two steps (taking a second step costs −2 to hit, so the net hit modifier becomes 0 with two steps). You cannot defend with the limb that attacked — no parry with the attacking hand, no block if you attacked with a shield, no dodge if you kicked. All your OTHER defenses are at −2 this turn. Cannot retreat.

MACommitted Attack — Strong. Single melee attack at normal skill, +1 damage. ST-based thrust/swing only — not lightsabers. For very high-ST attackers, the GM may scale the bonus to +1 damage per two full dice of basic damage if that’s better. Same movement and defense rules as the Determined variant.

Move and Attack. Take your full Move and make one attack at any point during or at the end of the movement. Melee skill caps at 9 (or DX+2, whichever is higher). Ranged attacks suffer −2, or the weapon’s Bulk penalty if worse. You may NOT retreat this turn. You may NOT use Rapid Strike.

All-Out Attack

All AoA variants: NO active defenses until next turn. Offensive Special Reaction, offensive Quick Actions, and Fast-Draw remain permitted (see Part V). Step or full Move is allowed; attack happens during or at the end of movement.

Determined. Single attack at +4 to hit.

Strong. Single attack at normal skill, +2 damage or +1 per die. ST-based thrust/swing only — not lightsabers.

Double. Two attacks vs. same or different targets. Does NOT require Extra Attack. With Extra Attack 1 → 3 attacks. May substitute one attack with Rapid Strike (→ 3 attacks), Dual-Weapon Attack, or Feint.

Feint. Free Feint, then one attack — margin applies to defense on the strike. Same target. Melee only.

MALong. Reach +1 for this attack. No hit penalty. Thrust: full damage. Swing: −2 dmg / −1 per die.

All-Out Defense

Increased. +2 to one chosen defense (Dodge, Parry, or Block) for the turn. Step permitted. No attacks. Other defenses normal. Balanced (MA): +1 to TWO different defense types instead.

Double. Two defense rolls against EACH attack — either succeeding stops the hit. Two different defense types, OR two parries with different weapons. Not two Dodges. Second defense subject to the multi-defense penalty. Step permitted. No attacks.

Part III

Attack Types
& Modifiers

Rapid Strike · Deceptive · Targeted · Beat · Ruse · Feint · Wild Swing

Attack modifiers apply within your chosen maneuver — they are not separate maneuvers. Multiple modifiers stack unless they contradict each other or the maneuver’s constraints (e.g., Telegraphic + Deceptive contradict each other; AoA(Strong) forbids modifiers that need defensive capacity).

Modifier Range Cost / Roll Effect Stacks With
Rapid StrikeMelee−6 each (−3 TbaM/WM)Two attacksDeceptive, Targeted, Committed, AoA(D)
Deceptive AttackMelee−2 hit per −1 defenseForces defense penaltyRapid Strike, Targeted, Committed
BeatMeleeQC ST-skill vs. DX/ST-skillPenalty to one named defense—
RuseMeleeQC IQ-skill vs. Per-skillPenalty to next defense—
TelegraphicMelee / Ranged+4 hit / target +2 defForce the hitRapid Strike, Targeted, Committed
Targeted AttackMelee / RangedSkill penalty by locationHit specific locationRapid Strike, Deceptive, Telegraphic
FeintMeleeQC DX-skillPenalty to next defense—
Defensive FeintMeleeas FeintPenalty to next ATTACK on you—
Dual-Weapon AttackMelee−4 eachTwo attacks (one per hand)AoA(D), Targeted
Wild SwingMelee / RangedSkill capped at 9Target obscured / unseenTelegraphic, Targeted
CombinationMeleeTrained skill, variesBought-off Rapid StrikeManeuver modifiers only

Trading Hits for Hits

Rapid Strike. Trade your one attack for two with the same weapon (or two unarmed strikes), each at −6 to skill — or −3 with Trained by a Master or Weapon Master. The two attacks may target different foes; each is defended separately. Melee only — for blasters and other ranged weapons, use Rate of Fire (Doc II) instead. Forbidden during Move and Attack. Combines with Deceptive Attack (applied per hit), Targeted Attack (per hit), and Committed Attack. With Extra Attack, Rapid Strike replaces ONE of your attacks; the rest are at normal skill. (MA) Cinematic option: three or more attacks at −6 each (−3 with TbaM/WM); three attacks at −12 each (−6 TbaM). GM may set a skill-12 minimum for cinematic Rapid Strikes.

Deceptive Attack. Accept a penalty to your attack roll to force a penalty on your foe’s defense. For every −2 you take on your attack, the target defends at −1. There’s no fixed cap — go as deep as your skill allows. Melee only — blasters cannot be deceptive. Stacks with Rapid Strike (per hit), Targeted Attack (penalties sum), and Committed Attack (penalties sum). Contradicts Telegraphic. TbaM’s defense-halving does NOT reduce Deceptive’s penalty. Evaluate cancels Deceptive on YOUR defense, up to a net of 0.

Dual-Weapon Attack. Trade your attack for two — one with each weapon (or fist) — at −4 each. Off-hand suffers additional −4 without Ambidexterity or Off-Hand Weapon Training. Targets same or different; defender at −1 vs. the second attack (buy off with Dual-Weapon Defense). Melee standard; cinematic variants for Pistol and Bow exist (Doc II). Cannot combine with Rapid Strike same maneuver. May substitute in AoA(Double) → 3 attacks. Buy off the −4 with the DWA technique (Hard, default skill −4).

Setting Up the Hit

MABeat. Physically batter your foe’s guard aside. Requires the Feint maneuver. Quick Contest: your weapon skill (using ST) vs. their weapon skill (using ST or DX, their choice). You target ONE specific defense — a particular parry, a particular block, an unarmed guard, or the Dodge of a grappled foe. If you win, that defense is penalised by your margin against ALL attackers until the end of your next turn. You qualify to Beat only if you blocked/parried their attack last turn, OR they blocked/parried yours, OR they’re grappled. No damage. Melee only.

MARuse. Verbal misdirection, a sudden gesture, a thrown trinket. Requires the Feint maneuver. Quick Contest: your weapon skill (using IQ) vs. target’s Per-based weapon skill (if they’re wary), DX-based weapon skill (to reset their guard), or Tactics. Your winning margin penalises one defense against your next attack. Resolved like Feint otherwise; eligible for Defensive Feint use. Melee only.

Feint. Quick Contest of best Melee, Unarmed, or Cloak skill vs. target’s best of the same or DX. Margin → penalty to ONE active defense vs. your next attack on that target, next turn. Resisting uses BEST weapon skill — even an unfamiliar one in hand. During Rapid Strike: feint at the same −6 (−3 TbaM). Multiple feints same maneuver: only the most recent applies. Never unreadies your weapon.

Telegraphic Attack. +4 to hit; target gets +2 to active defense. Both melee and ranged. Rarely worth taking at high skill — the +2 to defense usually outweighs the +4 to hit. Useful versus compromised defenses (stun, retreat penalty) when hitting is critical. Stacks with Rapid Strike, Targeted, Committed; contradicts Deceptive.

Targeted Attacks

Targeted Attack. Aim for a specific hit location. Skill penalty depends on location. On hit, damage and wound effects apply to that location (full table in Doc III). Repeating the same Targeted Attack twice on a foe → +1 to their defense on third and later attempts (predictability).

LocationPenKey Wound Effect
Skull−7×4 wound mult. vs. pen. DR
Eye−9Skull DR doesn’t protect
Face−5Knockdown / stun on major
Neck−5×2 cut (sever); ×1.5 cr (snap/strangle)
Vitals−3×3 imp / pi (any) / tib-burn
Groin−3Major on cut/pi; −4 acts 2d t.
Torso0Default location
Arm / Leg−2Cripples at HP/2
Hand / Foot−4Cripples at HP/3; drops wpn
Weapon−5 / −4 lgDisarm or break

Visibility & Improvisation

Wild Swing. Attack when you cannot clearly see the target — backward, behind cover without sight, against invisible foes, in pitch dark. Skill capped at 9 regardless of normal level. All other modifiers apply afterwards (so Telegraphic Wild Swing at −7 hit visibility = skill 9, then +4 = 13; target defends +2). Distinct from Move and Attack — Wild Swing is about visibility, M&A about movement.

MACombinations. Learned multi-attack technique — a named sequence (punch-elbow-knee, etc.). Mechanically a Rapid Strike with bought-off penalty per technique level. One Combination max per maneuver. Cannot also use Rapid Strike or DWA same maneuver. Compatible with AoA, Attack, Committed, Defensive Attack; NOT Move and Attack. Maneuver modifiers apply to all strikes — AoA(Strong) gives +2 to every strike. Bought as Hard techniques.

Part IV

Active Defenses

Dodge · Parry · Block · Multi-defense · Retreat · Riposte · Facing

Each incoming attack permits one active defense — Dodge, Parry, or Block — with the multiple-defense penalty applying to each defense after the first. The active defense slot is independent of Special Reaction (Part V), which may also fire once per turn.

Defense / Modifier Formula or Roll When / Notes
DodgeBasic Speed + 3, drop fractions; −encAlways available (subject to facing).
ParrySkill ÷ 2 + 3, drop fractions; weapon modRequires ready weapon.
BlockBlock skill ÷ 2 + 3, drop fractionsRequires ready shield, buckler, or cloak.
Multi-Def (RAW)−4 cumulative per def after firstBasic Set.
Multi-Def (TbaM/WM) HOUSE−2 cumulative per def after first — ONLY for defenses with the trained weapon/skillHalving applies to that defense type only; all OTHER defenses use the RAW −4 cumulative.
Retreat+3 Dodge / fencing / Boxing / Judo / Karate parry; +1 other parry / BlockOnce / turn. Melee. Costs your step.
Acrobatic DodgeAcrobatics roll: +2 / −2 to DodgeOnce / turn. Crit fail: fall.
Dive / Sideslip / Slip−1 / −1 / −2 + retreat bonusRetreat options (MA). Dive ends prone.
Feverish Defense1 FP → +2 to one defenseOnce / turn. Not with Committed / AoA.
Sacrificial DodgeDodge −2; take hit for allyEnds prone.
Riposte [armed or unarmed]Trade 1–4 from Parry scoreOn success → counterattack vs. penalised defense.
Counterattack (technique) [armed or unarmed]Tech roll; foe −2 to defenseAfter any successful Dodge, Parry, or Block.
Stop Hit (Wait variant, MA) [armed or unarmed]Both attack; lower margin defends at −1/−3Wait fires into their attack.
Stop Thrust [thrusting weapon, or punch/kick]Wait + thrust into charging foe+1 damage per 2 m of their Move, max +3.
Aggressive Parry (MA) [armed or unarmed]Parry −2 → strike attacking limb. Unarmed: thrust crushing to the limb. Armed: your weapon’s thrust damage (sabers cut straight through — ideal).Crit fail: self-injury (you hit your own parrying limb/weapon awkwardly).
Hand-Clap Parry (MA) [UNARMED only]Karate −2; bare hands catch a small weapon (knife/fist) — not polearms or sabersCinematic. You must have a free hand; catches & can grapple the weapon.
Defensive Grip (MA) [WEAPON only]+1 Parry frontal; −2 attack; −1 reachReady to change grip. A two-handed grip on the weapon — meaningless unarmed.
Special ReactionForce Push/Pull, full FPHouse Force-only, 1/turn, in addition to active def.
Side facing−2 to all defensesAttacks from side arc.
Rear facingIf unaware: NO defense at all. If aware: −4 to defend360° Vision: treat as side, −2 instead of −4.

The Three Defenses

Dodge. Move out of the way. Always usable, subject to facing. Always your fallback when you have no weapon or shield ready. Base: Basic Speed + 3, drop fractions. Modifiers: −encumbrance level, +1 Combat Reflexes, +Enhanced Dodge, +DB shield. Cannot dodge attacks you do not perceive (rear arc unless aware).

Parry. Deflect with a ready weapon. Base: weapon skill ÷ 2 + 3, drop fractions. Modifiers: +1 Combat Reflexes, +Enhanced Parry, weapon mod, retreat bonus (+1 typical; +3 for fencing / Boxing / Judo / Karate). Some weapons have +1/−1 parry mods; some are unbalanced (“U”) — cannot parry the turn after attacking with that weapon. Parry with off-hand at −4 unless you have Ambidexterity. Lightsaber Parry: Lightsaber skill ÷ 2 + 3, no weapon parry mod. Lightsaber Forms may grant additional bonuses (Doc IV).

Block. Interpose a ready shield, buckler, or cloak. Base: Block skill (Shield, Cloak) ÷ 2 + 3, drop fractions. Modifiers: +1 Combat Reflexes, +Enhanced Block, +DB shield. Cloak Parry (MA): a cloak skill user may parry with the cloak in addition to blocking. Cloaks: Parry 0, Block 0–1.

Multiple-Defense Penalty

Standard (RAW). First defense in a turn: no penalty. Second: −4. Third: −8. Fourth: −12. Cumulative. A hard ceiling on how many attacks even a skilled fighter can fend off in one second.

HouseMATrained by a Master / Weapon Master. Multi-defense penalty is halved only for defenses using the weapon or skill the advantage covers. Weapon Master (Lightsaber): halved on Lightsaber Parries — second −2, third −4, fourth −6. Trained by a Master: halved on parries, blocks, and dodges with their trained martial-arts skills (Karate, Judo, etc.). Defenses outside the advantage use the full −4 cumulative penalty. House rule modifying Martial Arts p. 122, which RAW halves all defenses. Still critical against rapid blaster volleys when the specialisation matches the threat (Doc II).

Movement-Based Defense

Retreat. Step away from a melee attacker as you defend. Bonus on that single defense: +3 to Dodge, or to Parry with Boxing, Judo, Karate, or any fencing skill. +1 to any other Parry, or to Block. Applies only against that attacker. Once per turn. Costs your step (can’t already have stepped). Forbidden during Committed Attack, Move and Attack, or AoA. Movement bonuses do not multiply; retreat is one hex.

MADive. Standing/kneeling → prone, under a melee attack. −1 + retreat bonus. End your turn prone (future attackers: +3 to hit). Useful when cornered with allies nearby.

MASideslip. Standing only. Step sideways — same distance from attacker. −1 + retreat bonus. Net 0 most defenses; net +2 with Dodge or fencing/Boxing/Judo/Karate Parry. Use when you cannot cede ground but want geometry advantage.

MASlip. Standing only. Step CLOSER to attacker (potentially into close combat). −2 + retreat bonus. Net +1 best, −1 worst. Additional −1 vs. Stop Thrust. Use when distance is more dangerous than closing.

Acrobatic Dodge. Before rolling Dodge, declare Acrobatic Dodge and roll Acrobatics. Success: +2 to Dodge. Failure: −2 to Dodge. Critical failure: fall. Once per turn. Stacks with retreat and All-Out Defense (Increased Dodge). Don’t attempt without good odds — the −2 on failure outweighs the +2 on success unless you’re solid.

Extra Effort

MAFeverish Defense. Spend 1 FP before rolling an active defense → +2 to that roll. Once per turn (defensive Extra Effort). Incompatible with Committed Attack and AoA. Stacks with Defensive Attack’s +1, AoD (Increased) +2, retreat bonus, Defensive Grip +1. Critical failure: 1 HP injury.

Sacrificial Dodge. When an adjacent ally is attacked, dive in front of them. Standing, crouching, or kneeling — not prone. Dodge at −2. Success: attack misses entirely; you end prone. Failure: you take the hit instead; end prone. Cannot perform if you used AoA, Committed Attack, or Move and Attack this turn. Counts as a defense vs. this attack — multi-defense penalty applies after.

Counter-Strike Defenses

MARiposte. Before rolling a parry, declare Riposte and accept −1 to −4 from your Parry score (cannot reduce parry below 8 before Enhanced Parry and Combat Reflexes). On successful parry: your first attack next turn on this foe, with the parrying weapon, forces them to defend at the chosen penalty. Parried hand/weapon → penalises Parry with that hand. Parried shield → penalises Block. Parried unarmed (kick, bite, slam) → penalises Dodge. Half (drop fractions) applies to OTHER defenses they try against the Riposte. Failed parry: hit as normal — no special drawback. Cannot combine with Deceptive Attack on the Riposte — Riposte IS a Deceptive Attack. Stacks with prior Feint.

MACounterattack (Technique). After a successful Dodge, Parry, or Block, your next attack on the foe (this turn or first attack next turn) is a Counterattack. Hard technique, default skill −5, max = skill. Per combat skill. Penalises target’s active defenses by −2. No defensive risk like Riposte — but smaller bonus. Cannot combine with Riposte same turn.

MAStop Hit. Wait variant. Declare you intend to attack a specific foe. When they attack you, both roll to hit. If only one hits: struck fighter defends at −1, or −3 if parrying with the attacking weapon. If both hit: lower margin defends with the penalty. Tie: both penalties apply. Can use unbalanced weapon (Stop Hit and parry are one move). Cannot parry AFTER a Stop Hit — Wait spent.

Stop Thrust. Wait variant against a charging foe (Move and Attack, AoA + Move). Trigger when they enter your weapon’s reach. Strike with a thrusting weapon. +1 damage per 2 hexes of their Move (max +3 from velocity). Consumes your attack — you cannot parry their attack with the same weapon. Knockdown interrupts their attack entirely.

MAAggressive Parry. Parry that strikes the attacking limb or weapon for damage. Aggressive Parry technique at −2 from Parry score (Karate or weapon skill). Success: parry succeeds AND inflicts thrust damage to attacking limb. Crit fail: self-injury (hand/weapon HP). Useful for crippling rather than just deflecting.

MAHand-Clap Parry. Catch an incoming attack between palms in one motion. Karate −2. Success: parries AND grapples the weapon (or fist). Small weapons only — knives, fists, short blades. Not polearms or lightsabers. Highly cinematic.

MADefensive Grip. Hold weapon closer to body, optimised for parry. +1 Parry vs. frontal attacks. −2 to attack with that weapon. Reach reduced by 1 (Reach 1,2 → C,1). Switching into / out of grip takes a Ready maneuver.

Facing & Flanking

Front Arc. Three hexes — directly in front and the two front-side. Defenses normal.

Side Arc. Two hexes perpendicular to facing. Defender at −2 to all active defenses. Attacker gets no bonus to hit beyond your defense penalty.

Rear Arc. The hex directly behind and the rear-sides. Unaware of a rear attack, you get NO active defense — no Dodge, Parry, or Block; it lands if it beats your defense number, leaving only DR. To defend, first become aware — a Per-based roll (Hearing, Tactics), or Combat Reflexes / Danger Sense — then defend at −4. 360° Vision: no rear arc; treat rear attacks as side (−2). Peripheral Vision: widens your front arc but keeps the rear arc — you can still be ambushed from directly behind. Juyo’s 360° technique (Doc IV) removes facing penalties on YOUR attacks only; foes still get rear bonuses against you.

Changing Facing. Free action during your turn when combined with movement (step or full Move). Cannot change facing out of turn except as part of a Wait’s action.

Part V

Turn Economy

Free Actions · Quick Actions · Special Reaction

Your turn comprises one Maneuver plus an envelope of Free Actions, Quick Actions, and (if Force-sensitive) one Special Reaction. The maneuver dictates what you can attempt; the rest determines what else fits in the same second of action.

Action Type Cost Limit Permitted In Source
ManeuverYour action slot1 / turnAlwaysBasic Set
Active DefenseDefense slot1 / attackAll except AoABasic Set
Free Action—UnlimitedAny maneuver including AoABasic Set
Quick Action (defensive)Varies (FP, roll)1 / turnNot AoAHouse rule
Quick Action (offensive)Varies1 / turnAll including AoAHouse rule
Special Reaction (def.)Full FP1 / turn · Force-onlyNot AoAHouse rule
Special Reaction (off.)Full FP1 / turn · Force-onlyAll including AoAHouse rule
Fast-DrawSkill roll1 / turn typicalAll including AoA — crit fail drops the weaponBasic Set

Hierarchy of Action

Each turn, in order: (1) Maneuver — your action slot. (2) Active Defenses — one per attack with multi-def penalty. (3) Free Actions — unlimited, no cost. (4) Quick Actions — one per turn (or more with specific powers). (5) Special Reaction — Force-sensitives only, one per turn.

All-Out Attack suspends defensive uses of items 4 and 5, and suspends item 2 entirely. Offensive Quick Actions, offensive Special Reaction, and Fast-Draw remain permitted during AoA. All-Out Defense permits all five fully.

Free Actions

Definition. Cost nothing; unlimited per turn. May not involve attack, defense, or a skill roll except as listed: speak (~5 seconds of speech); change facing as part of a movement-including maneuver; drop a held item; open a hand; observe (passive Perception when GM calls); maintain tracking of a target you can already see.

Quick Actions

HouseQuick Action. Done alongside your maneuver. Most have a cost (FP, skill roll, or per-turn limit). Limit: one per turn unless a specific power grants more. During AoA: only OFFENSIVE Quick Actions and Fast-Draw permitted; defensive QAs forbidden.

HouseForce Push / Pull as Quick Action. Full FP cost — no Quick Action discount. Range and effect as the standard power. Two modes: DEFENSIVE (redirect a threat, push an ally aside) and OFFENSIVE (shove or pull a foe). Allowed alongside Attack, Defensive Attack, or Concentrate (different power). During AoA: offensive use only. Counts toward 1 QA / turn.

Fast-Draw. Fast-Draw (skill) roll. Success: weapon drawn as a Quick Action — no Ready needed. Failure: weapon not yet ready; maneuver proceeds, try again next turn. Critical failure: drop the weapon. Combat Reflexes: +1 to Fast-Draw. Permitted during AoA — useful to draw the weapon used in the AoA itself.

HouseOther Quick Actions. Single-touch device activation (commlink, sensor visor, jetpack). Reach for a small item on your person (one-hand grab). Drop held weapon and activate a new one same turn. GM call. If a skill roll or measurable time is required, it’s a Ready — not a QA.

What Cannot Be a Quick Action. Concentrate Force powers (Concentrate IS a maneuver). Any attack roll. Reloading, grip/reach/weapon-switching adjustments — all Readies. Drawing a heavy or two-handed weapon (Fast-Draw unavailable). Two simultaneous Force powers — needs Compartmentalised Mind. Defensive Quick Actions during AoA.

Special Reaction

HouseSpecial Reaction. Force-sensitives only. Once per turn, use Force Push or Pull as a triggered Reaction — full FP, on top of your active defense (it does NOT consume the defense slot). Cannot stack with itself. DEFENSIVE mode: redirect or deflect a threat (bolt, grenade, lunge), or shove self/ally clear — forbidden during AoA. OFFENSIVE mode: shove or pull a foe in response to their attack — set up a strike, knock them off-line, pull them into reach — permitted during AoA. Forbidden while unconscious, stunned, or unable to perceive; usable for self or an adjacent ally. Vaapad’s Absorbed Fury (Doc IV) shares this Reaction slot — pick one per turn.

One Reaction per turn. A Reaction is a single triggered action taken out of turn, once per turn — full stop. It costs neither your maneuver nor your active-defense slot. Each turn you may take one of: a Special Reaction (Force Push/Pull, above — Vaapad’s Absorbed Fury shares it); a Force counter (Force Rebuke, Force Mirror, Inversion, Greater Rebuke, or Force Anchor — Doc IV); or a form Reaction such as Guardian Throw. One, not one of each. (Severance Mark’s bonus Reaction against a marked working is the sole exception.) Your active defenses are not Reactions: Dodge, Parry, Block, and Tutaminis each answer every attack you are aware of, one per attack, subject only to the multiple-defense penalty. Tutaminis catches a bolt in place of a parry — it is an active defense, and never spends your Reaction.

Examples

  • Defensive — blaster bolt strikes you. Spend Push FP; QC of Push skill vs. attacker’s skill. Win = bolt off-line.
  • Defensive — grenade lands at your feet. Force Pull a heavy object onto it, or Push it back.
  • Defensive — ally about to be impaled by a lunge. Force Push the ally one hex; QC vs. attacker’s skill.
  • Offensive (AoA-legal) — mid-AoA, Push a foe into your saber arc — shove them off-balance for −2 to their defense.
  • Offensive (AoA-legal) — mid-AoA, Pull a foe into close combat so your reach applies.
Part VI

Posture

How body position affects attack, defense, and movement
Posture Attack Defense Hit Against Move
Standing00NormalFull Move
Crouching−20−2 to hit (from outside C)× 2/3
Kneeling−2−2−2 to hit× 1/3
Sitting−2−2−2 to hit0 (M&A 1 hex only)
Crawling−4−3+2 close / −2 ranged1 hex / turn
Prone / Supine−4−3+3 close / −2 ranged—

Combinations & Stacking — What Fits With What

One turn = one maneuver, plus a free active defence against each attack (a Reaction), plus your Free Actions, plus — by house rule — one Quick Action and (Force-users) one Special Reaction. The tables below show which attack modifiers may be layered inside an attack, and what each maneuver permits. Anything not listed as compatible is not allowed together.

Attack-modifier compatibility

ModifierStacks withCannot combine / notes
Deceptive AttackRapid Strike · Targeted (location) · Committed · All-Out Attack · Dual-WeaponNot Telegraphic (opposite intent). Melee only. Each −2 to hit buys −1 to the foe’s defence.
Telegraphic AttackRapid Strike · Targeted · CommittedNot Deceptive. Melee only. +4 to hit, foe +2 to defend.
Rapid StrikeDeceptive · Telegraphic · Targeted · Committed · AoA(Determined)Melee only. −6 each attack (−3 for the trained weapon, TbaM/WM).
Targeted Attack (hit location)Almost everything — Deceptive, Telegraphic, Rapid Strike, Committed, AoA, ranged AimRanged & melee. Repeating the same location: foe +1 to defend on the 3rd+ try.
Feint / Beat / RuseSets up your next attack (any modifiers)Melee only. Feint is its own maneuver; Beat/Ruse happen within it. Result is a penalty to one of the foe’s defences.
Dual-Weapon AttackDeceptive · Targeted per weaponTwo ready weapons; off-hand at −4 without Ambidexterity. Not with Rapid Strike on the same hand.
EvaluateAdds to your next attack (+1/+2/+3)Own maneuver. Cancels a foe’s Deceptive against your defence until you attack.

What each maneuver allows

ManeuverAttack options it permitsYour defences this turn
AttackAny one attack + any compatible modifiers aboveNormal (free active defence per attack)
Committed AttackDetermined (+2 hit) or Strong (+1 dmg); plus Deceptive, Rapid Strike, TargetedLimited: attacking limb cannot defend; other defences −2; no retreat. No Feverish Defence.
All-Out AttackDetermined (+4 hit) / Double (two attacks) / Strong (+2 dmg) / Long (+1 reach); plus Deceptive, Rapid Strike, TargetedNone. Offensive Quick Action, offensive Special Reaction, and Fast-Draw still legal.
Move and AttackOne attack, skill capped at 9 (or DX+2); Telegraphic is fine, Deceptive/Rapid Strike pointless under the capDodge or Block only — no Parry.
Defensive AttackOne attack at −2 dmg (−1/die); pick one defensive perk+1 to one defence (or the other listed perks)
All-Out DefenseNo attack+2 to one defence, or +1 to two types, or two defences vs the same attack (Double). Permits Quick Actions & Special Reaction fully.
