Range penaltySpeed/Range TableThe greater the distance, the harder the shot. 2 hexes = 0, 10 m = −4, 100 m = −10, 1000 m = −16.
Posture modYou & targetCrouching target: −2 to hit. Prone target: +3 close range / −2 ranged. Your own posture also affects your skill (see Doc I Part VI).
CoverBattlefieldPartial cover adds penalty to hit (low wall −2, firing slit −4, peek around corner −4 or worse). Full cover blocks the line of sight entirely.
SituationalGM callDarkness, smoke, wind, target movement, your own movement (Move and Attack −2 or Bulk), shock penalties, stun.

The Ranged To-Hit Calculation

Net Roll. Effective skill = base skill + Acc (if Aimed) + SM (target) − range penalty − cover penalty − situational. Roll 3d ≤ effective skill to hit. The hit/miss math is identical to melee; what differs is which modifiers apply.

Example. A Sith trooper with Beam Weapons (Pistol) 12 fires at a Jedi (SM 0) at 20 m from a kneeling position. Range: −6 (Spd/Rng for 20 m). No Aim. Net to-hit = 12 + 0 (SM) − 6 (range) − 2 (kneeling shooter) = 4. Needs a natural 4 or less. With one turn of Aim (Acc +2 for a standard blaster pistol): net = 6. With two turns: net = 7. With three turns: net = 8 — that’s the Aim cap reached.

Repeating Targeted Attacks. If you take the same Targeted Attack against the same foe twice, your third and later attempts at that same location face +1 to the foe’s defense (or, for ranged, you’re predictable enough that they’re already moving). Switch locations to avoid the predictability penalty.

What Ranged Forbids

Ranged attacks are a different animal from melee. Several attack modifiers are not available with a ranged weapon:

  • Rapid Strike — to get multiple shots, use the weapon’s Rate of Fire (Part IV).
  • Deceptive Attack — you can’t fake out a target with a blaster bolt. Use Aim, position, or surprise instead.
  • Beat — physical battering of guard. Melee technique only.
  • Ruse — verbal/gesture misdirection. Melee only.
  • Feint — Melee only. Cannot feint with a ranged weapon.
  • Combination MA — trained multi-strike technique. Replaced by RoF.
  • Dual-Weapon Attack — cinematic variants exist for Pistol and Bow specifically (see Doc IV / equipment doc); the default rule is that one ranged weapon fires per turn.

What still works with ranged:

  • Targeted Attack — pay the to-hit penalty to hit a specific location.
  • Telegraphic Attack — +4 to hit, target gets +2 to defense. Rarely worth it at range, but useful against stunned or compromised foes.
  • Wild Swing — when you can’t see your target, skill capped at 9.
  • Move and Attack — at a penalty (Part III).
  • Aim — multi-turn, see Part II.
  • All-Out Attack (Determined) — +4 to hit. Foregoes defenses.
  • Committed Attack (Determined) — +2 to hit, defense penalties. Less common at range but legal.

What Counts as a Ranged Attack

Definition. Any attack that originates from your weapon at a distance greater than reach (1 hex for unarmed, longer for melee weapons). Includes thrown weapons, projectile launchers, beam weapons, energy weapons, blasters, slugthrowers, bows, crossbows, slings, blowguns, gas grenades (the grenade itself), Force lightning, Force-projected attacks, and the deflection-back of blaster bolts using a Lightsaber (Part VII).

Close-combat range with ranged weapons. Most ranged weapons can be used in close combat (within 1 hex of the target) but suffer a Bulk penalty applied as a negative to skill (Part III). A long rifle in close combat is awkward; a holdout pistol works fine.

Part II

Accuracy
& Aim

Weapon Acc · multi-turn Aim · bracing · scopes · keeping the bead

Accuracy is the difference between point-and-shoot and a deliberate, precision strike. Almost every ranged weapon has an Acc rating from 0 (improvised, thrown weapons) to 6+ (sniper rifles, lightsaber-pikes designed for thrown work). Acc is a bonus added to your skill only when you spend a turn (or more) Aiming first. Snap shots without Aim get no Acc bonus. This is one of the largest pieces of ranged combat math — Aim well, and a moderate-skill shooter outperforms a high-skill snap-shooter at range.

Aim Action Turn(s) Required Bonus Cumulative Notes
Snap shot (no Aim)0 turns+0—Weapon’s Acc does NOT apply. Pure skill.
One turn of Aim1 turn+AccAccWeapon’s full Acc bonus on next attack.
Two turns of Aim2 turns+1 moreAcc + 1Holding the bead, settling in.
Three or more turns3+ turns+1 moreAcc + 2 (cap)This is the cap; further Aiming doesn’t help.
Bracing (one-handed)Bonus to Aim+1 Acc—Weapon rested on something solid: knee, fence, branch.
Bracing (two-handed / kneeling / prone)Bonus to Aim+2 Acc—Maximum stability. Prone is the canonical sniper position.
Telescopic sightPer scope level+1 to +4 Acc—Long-range optic. The sight’s bonus is added to weapon Acc.
Reflex sight—+1 Acc—Snap-target optic. Useful at close to medium range.

The Aim Maneuver

Aim. Your maneuver for the turn. Point your weapon at a specific target and prepare to fire. Step permitted (one hex). You may not change facing more than one hex face. You attack on your next turn using the Aim bonus — or continue Aiming for further bonuses, up to the cap. The Aim bonus applies to your next attack ONLY; firing consumes it.

Multi-Turn Aim. First turn of Aim: gain your weapon’s full Acc. Second turn: +1 more (total Acc + 1). Third or later turn: +1 more (total Acc + 2 — the cap). You cannot keep accumulating bonuses by Aiming longer than three turns.

Bracing. Resting the weapon against something solid stabilises your hold. One-handed brace (against a wall, table, vehicle) gives +1 Acc. Two-handed brace, kneeling, or prone gives +2 Acc. The bracing bonus is added to the weapon’s listed Acc before you compute your Aim total. So a Beam Weapons (Rifle) Acc 4 weapon braced prone counts as Acc 6 — and three turns of Aim yields +8.

Targeting Optics. Scopes and reflex sights add to the weapon’s Acc when used. A 4× scope on an Acc 4 rifle effectively makes it Acc 6 for an aimed shot. Some optics only function when Aiming (telescopic) and some help at all times (reflex). Optics may have a range below which they don’t help (a 10× sniper scope is useless at close range).

Maintaining Aim

Losing Aim. You lose your accumulated Aim bonus if any of the following occur between Aim turns:

  • You take any other maneuver besides Aim (including Wait, Concentrate, Ready, Move).
  • You move more than one hex (the step permitted with Aim).
  • You change facing by more than one hex face.
  • You change target (re-Aim from scratch on the new target — start over at +Acc).
  • You become physically or mentally stunned.
  • You fall, are knocked prone, or change posture (other than Drop Prone, which the GM may allow as a free brace).
  • Any successful attack lands on you (including those you parried or dodged). Make a Will roll to retain Aim. Failure: Aim lost. Critical failure: weapon misaligned, drop the weapon or lose your next turn re-orienting.

Aim Through Cover. You may Aim through a firing slit, peephole, or partial-cover gap. Your shot suffers the cover penalty on firing, but you retain Aim normally as long as line of sight to the target persists.

Aiming at Multiple Targets

Enhanced Tracking (Advantage). You can maintain Aim on more than one target simultaneously — one per level of Enhanced Tracking past the first. Without this advantage, you must pick a single target each Aim turn. Useful for skirmishers preparing for unknown threats.

Targeting (Advantage). Some species or characters have a built-in targeting sense (innate, cybernetic, or Force-derived). Adds +1 to +3 to all ranged attacks regardless of Aim. SW Force-users may use the Force Sense advantage to provide a targeting bonus for blind shots or shots through obscuring smoke (Doc IV).

Acc vs Skill — Which Matters More?

At skill 12 with one turn of Aim, a Beam Pistol (Acc 2) shoots at 14. The same character snap-shooting at the same target: 12 vs the same range penalty.

The +2 from Aim is worth more than a +1 skill increase in nearly every campaign — Aim gets you the bonus turn after turn from a single advantage point spent on the WEAPON, not on years of training. Always Aim if you can spare the turn. A static-line gunfight between two competent shooters will be decided by who Aimed first, not who has the higher skill.

Sniper Math. A sniper with Beam Weapons (Rifle) 14, prone bracing (+2), Acc 4 weapon, 4× scope (+2), three turns of Aim: 14 + 4 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 24. Against a SM 0 target at 200 m (range −12): net 12. The same shooter against a SM +3 speeder at 200 m: net 15. Snipers play a different game from gunfighters; they own the long-range engagement entirely. SW In Star Wars settings this is what makes Mandalorian marksmen and Bothan snipers terrifying — they exploit Aim to the cap and engage from out of return-fire range.

Part III

Range, Speed
& Bulk

Speed/Range Table · 1/2D vs Max · Bulk · ST minimums · Move and Attack

Distance is the single biggest modifier in ranged combat. The Speed/Range Table gives a penalty based on metres between shooter and target — anything beyond two metres starts costing skill. Speed and SM use the same table read the other direction: a fast-moving or larger target is easier to track. Past the weapon’s 1/2D range, damage drops by half. Past its Max range, the weapon physically cannot reach.

Spd/Rng Size mod Measure (m) Spd/Rng Size mod Measure (m)
+0−50.3 m−6+620 m
+0−40.5 m−7+730 m
+0−30.6 m−8+850 m
−1−21 m−9+970 m
−1−11.5 m−10+10100 m
−1+02 m−11+11150 m
−2+13 m−12+12200 m
−3+25 m−13+13300 m
−3+37 m−14+14500 m
−4+410 m−15+15700 m
−5+515 m−16+161000 m

Reading the Table

Range Penalty. Find the distance to your target in the Measure column. Read across to Spd/Rng for the to-hit penalty. Less than 2 m: no penalty. 10 m: −4. 100 m: −10. 1000 m: −16. The penalty is subtracted from your skill before you roll.

Size Modifier. The target’s stat block gives its SM. Add SM to your skill (positive for large targets, negative for small). A speeder at SM +3 cancels three points of range penalty; a mouse droid at SM −3 adds three.

Speed. A fast-moving target reads as larger on the same table because you can plot their trajectory. A speeder moving 10 m per second adds an effective +4 to its ”size” for tracking purposes. The GM may apply this rule to vehicles, mounted riders, or charging beasts; humans on foot move slowly enough that speed-tracking rarely applies in person-scale combat.

1/2D and Max Range

Half Damage (1/2D). Every ranged weapon lists a 1/2D range. Up to and including that distance, damage rolls normally. Beyond 1/2D, the projectile has lost energy: damage is halved (round down). The to-hit roll is unaffected by 1/2D — only the damage drops.

Maximum Range (Max). The farthest the weapon can physically reach. Past Max, the shot misses regardless of skill — the bolt or bullet falls short. Max for blasters is typically 300-1000 m depending on weapon; thrown weapons cap much closer. Listing example: blaster carbine: 1/2D 500 m, Max 1500 m — at 600 m, the shot hits but does half damage. At 1600 m, it doesn’t reach.

Beyond 1/2D in Practice. Most blaster engagements settle inside 100 m because the damage drop-off matters more than the skill penalty. A blaster carbine doing 3d burning at 500 m becomes 1d+1 at 600 m — still lethal, but armour stops it cold. Snipers carry specialised weapons with much longer 1/2D ranges to fight at distance.

Bulk

Bulk. A weapon’s awkwardness in close-quarters work. Listed as a negative number — Bulk −1 (compact pistol), −4 (carbine), −6 (heavy rifle), −8 (heavy weapon). Bulk applies in three situations:

  • Move and Attack. When you make a Move and Attack with a ranged weapon, your skill suffers −2 OR the weapon’s Bulk penalty, whichever is worse. A Bulk −4 carbine fired during a sprint takes −4; a Bulk −1 pistol takes only −2 (because the pistol’s Bulk is less than the M&A −2).
  • Close Combat. Fired in close combat (within 1 hex of foe), the weapon takes its Bulk penalty to skill. A holdout pistol (Bulk −1) is barely affected; a longarm (Bulk −5) is a poor choice.
  • Concealment. A weapon’s Bulk determines how easy it is to hide. Bulk −1 weapons may be concealed easily. Bulk −2 requires a coat or holster. Bulk −3 or worse cannot be hidden under normal clothing.

Holdout Skill. The Holdout skill (IQ-based) opposes another character’s Search (Per). Holdout grants a bonus if the weapon is small enough (Bulk −2 or better) and the hider is well-prepared. Force-sensitives may use Force Sense as an alternative to Search vs concealed weapons (GM call).

Minimum ST

Weapon ST. Heavy weapons require minimum Strength to wield without penalty. Listed as ST 10 (typical rifle), ST 12 (heavy rifle), ST 14 (heavy weapon), etc. If your ST is below the requirement, suffer −1 to skill per point of difference, and the weapon’s RoF and Rcl may also degrade (GM call — usually each point below ST = +1 to Rcl).

Two-Handed Weapons. Any weapon listed with † or ”two-handed” requires both hands to fire. Using one hand: −1 to skill per point of ST below 1.5× the weapon’s ST minimum. So a ST 10 character firing a two-handed rifle (min ST 10) one-handed: 1.5 × 10 = 15; 15 − 10 = 5 below → −5 to skill. Difficult but not impossible for trained shooters. Trained by a Master or Weapon Master can reduce or remove this penalty as appropriate to the campaign.

Weapon ST by Mass. HOUSE A melee weapon’s minimum ST scales with its mass, not a flat tier. As the guideline the sheet applies: one-handed ≈ 6 + weight (kg); two-handed ≈ 5 + weight (two hands lend leverage), rounded to the nearest point, with +1–2 for brute or unbalanced heads (great-axes, mauls, war-glaives). Where this differs from an older flat figure in the tables, the mass-based value governs — a 9-kg bardiche is min ST 15†, not 11; an 8-kg force pike is 14†. Below the requirement, take the usual −1 to skill per missing point.

Blade Material & Armour Divisor. SW A melee weapon’s armour divisor follows its edge material. A vibro-edge shears armour at AD 2. Cortosis matches that AD 2 and additionally negates a lightsaber’s armour divisor, shorting the blade for 1d turns on a successful parry. Beskar — the hardest — cuts at AD 3 and likewise turns a saber aside on a parry; a weapon forged whole from it (beskar war-blade, war-maul) is near-indestructible. Phrik and songsteel hafts (electrostaff, force pike, war-glaive) resist a saber without carrying an edge divisor of their own.

Move and Attack with Ranged Weapons

M&A Ranged. Take your full Move; attack at any point in your move at −2 or your weapon’s Bulk, whichever is worse. No retreat this turn. No Rapid Strike (already forbidden with ranged). Aim is lost. Targeted Attack penalty applies on top of M&A penalty. Useful for repositioning while suppressing.

M&A Skill Cap. Per RAW, Move and Attack caps skill at 9 (or DX+2, whichever is higher) for melee — but ranged M&A does not use this skill cap; it uses the −2 or Bulk penalty instead. So a skill-15 shooter doing M&A keeps her skill 13 (−2) and gains the mobility, where her melee counterpart would be stuck at skill 9.

Mounted & Vehicular. Firing from a moving vehicle or mount applies M&A penalties to the shooter — see Doc VI for vehicle-specific modifiers (relative motion, bumpy terrain, gunner positions). For person-scale combat, treat as M&A.

Part IV

Rate of Fire,
Recoil & Bursts

RoF · Rcl · how many hits land · suppression · sweeping fire

Melee combat uses Rapid Strike, Dual-Weapon Attack, and Combinations to land multiple blows in a turn. Ranged combat uses Rate of Fire — the weapon’s mechanical ability to put bullets, bolts, or beams downrange in one second. RoF is the most important difference between ranged and melee mechanics. A skilled gunner with an automatic blaster can land four to six hits in the same turn a swordsman lands two. The cost is recoil, ammunition consumption, and reduced precision per shot.

Concept Definition Typical Values
Rate of Fire (RoF)Maximum shots the weapon can fire in one turn1 (single shot) · 2-3 (semi-auto) · 4-19 (burst) · 20+ (full auto / heavy)
Recoil (Rcl)Per-shot accuracy degradation in a burst2 (blaster pistols & rifles) · 3 (heavy blasters, repeating) · 4+ (cannons, heavy weapons); shotguns & single-shot launchers Rcl 1
Single shotFire one round/bolt. RoF and Rcl don’t matter.Default for any RoF 1 weapon.
BurstFire 2+ shots in one turn. Roll once. RoF and Rcl determine how many hits land.RoF 4+ weapons can split bursts (Part IV below).
Suppression FireFire a full burst into an area to deter movementAnyone in the area must Dodge or be hit (see below).

How Hits Are Resolved in a Burst

One Roll Per Burst. When you fire 2+ shots at the same target in one turn, you make one attack roll. The number of bullets/bolts that actually land depends on three things: your margin of success, the weapon’s Rcl, and the burst’s RoF. Crit rolls (3-4 or skill+5/6 for high skill) work normally — they fire all rounds in the burst at full effect.

Bullets That Hit. On a successful attack roll:

Hits = (Margin of Success ÷ Rcl) + 1

Rounded down, capped at the burst’s RoF. Margin is your effective skill minus the die roll. A skill-14 shooter rolling a 9 hits with margin 5; on a Rcl 2 weapon that’s (5÷2) + 1 = 3 hits. Each hit rolls damage separately. The defender makes ONE active defense; if it succeeds against the attack roll, all bullets in the burst miss.

RoF (shots fired)High RoF Hit Bonus
1–4 shots+0 to hit
5–8 shots+1 to hit
9–16 shots+2 to hit
17–49 shots+3 to hit
50–99 shots+4 to hit
100+ shots+5 to hit

High RoF Hit Bonus. Spraying a lot of fire into a target area is easier to land at all (one shot will hit), even if the per-shot accuracy is poor. Read the table from your actual shots fired this turn — a RoF 8 weapon firing only 4 shots gets +0; firing all 8 gets +1.

Recoil

Rcl Defined. Recoil is the penalty per shot after the first hit when firing a burst. Lower Rcl = more hits per burst. Blasters in this setting carry a real Rcl 2–3 — bolt blowback, venting, and barrel climb still throw off a sustained burst (heavy and repeating models kick harder still). They remain strong rapid-fire weapons, but a burst no longer lands a hit for every point of margin. Slugthrowers (where they exist in Star Wars settings — old Mandalorian and Tusken weapons) have Rcl 2 or 3.

Bracing & Rcl. Two-handed bracing, prone position, and bipods reduce a weapon’s Rcl by 1 (minimum 1). A Rcl 2 carbine fired prone counts as Rcl 1 for the burst. This is the gunner’s force-multiplier — a braced burst is roughly twice as effective as a moving one.

High Pain Threshold & Rcl. High Pain Threshold does not affect Rcl — recoil isn’t pain, it’s physics. But shock penalties from being shot DO apply to your skill on subsequent turns; HPT ignores those.

Worked Example: Blaster Carbine Burst

Sith trooper, Beam Weapons (Carbine) skill 14, fires a 6-round burst from a standard blaster carbine (RoF 8, Rcl 2) at a Jedi 10 hexes away. One turn of Aim was spent (Acc 4).

  • Effective skill: 14 + 4 (Aim) − 4 (range 10 hex) = 14.
  • High RoF hit bonus: firing 6 shots = +1.
  • Final skill: 14 + 1 = 15.
  • Roll 3d. Result: 9. Margin of success = 15 − 9 = 6.
  • Hits = (6 ÷ 2) + 1 = 4 bolts connect.
  • Jedi rolls Lightsaber Parry. Net 17. Roll 11. Margin 6, hold all four bolts (Part VII).

If the Jedi had failed his defense, all four bolts would have struck and rolled damage separately. Sith troopers do not fire RoF 1 in this setting — they fire bursts; the campaign treats them realistically as a result.

Splitting a Burst Between Targets

Multiple Targets. A weapon with RoF 4 or higher may split its burst between two or more targets. Declare the split before rolling. Each sub-burst is rolled as a separate attack with appropriate range penalty for that target. Each sub-burst uses its own RoF total to compute hits and the High RoF Hit Bonus.

Split Penalty. Each split sub-burst suffers an additional −2 to skill per target beyond the first (you’re tracking multiple targets and dividing your attention). A RoF 8 blaster splitting 4 shots each at two targets: each sub-burst takes a −2 penalty. Three-way split: each at −4. Four-way: each at −6.

Sweeping Fire. A specialised RoF 16+ weapon may sweep across a line of contiguous hexes. Each hex along the sweep takes 1-2 shots distributed across the burst; targets in each hex roll their own defense vs the share of the burst directed at them. Sweep mechanics are best for crowd control and area denial — see also Suppression Fire below.

Suppression Fire

Suppression. A burst of RoF 5+ may be directed into a hex, doorway, or short line instead of at a specific target. Anyone in or moving through the suppressed area must succeed at a Dodge roll vs the attacker’s effective skill, or be hit by the burst. If multiple people are in the area, each rolls Dodge separately. Suppression fire consumes the entire burst’s ammunition. Effective for forcing foes to abandon a position, deny a corridor, or prevent reinforcements from crossing open ground.

Suppression Defenses. Dodging suppression fire follows normal Dodge rules — Acrobatic Dodge, retreat, and AoD all apply. Diving prone as a defense gives the standard +2 vs missile fire and is the most common response to incoming suppression. The defender ends prone in an adjacent hex (or the same one if already in cover).

Heavy Weapons in Suppression. Tripod-mounted or vehicle-mounted heavy blasters with RoF 20+ can suppress an entire wide area, with each hex resolved independently. This is when fixed-position weapons earn their cost — they own a corridor until taken out.

Part V

Targeting
at Range

Targeted Attacks · Wild Swing · target movement · burst + targeted

Targeted attacks work the same at range as in melee — pay the to-hit penalty for the location, get the wound effect. Most of the table from Doc I applies unchanged. A few interactions are unique to ranged combat: how Targeted stacks with bursts, how Wild Swing handles obscured targets, and how target movement affects your shot.

Location To-Hit Penalty Notes (Ranged-Specific)
Skull / Brain−7×4 wound mult vs penetrating. Headshots are the signature sniper kill. Skull DR 2 still applies.
Face−5Crit Hit Table reads one row better. Common targeted shot — easier than skull, still lethal.
Eye−9Only imp / pi / tib-burn. Blaster bolts qualify. ×4 wound mult, no DR. Cripple at HP/10 (blinded).
Neck−5cr ×1.5, cut ×2. At range, cutting damage is rare — most ranged neck shots use pi or imp.
Vitals−3imp / pi / tib-burn only. ×3 wound mult. The default ”I want this one dead” ranged shot.
Heart−5Vitals + auto major wound. Death likely. Sniper specialty.
Groin−3cut/pi/imp = auto major wound. Stun, knockdown. Males: cr shock ×2.
Arm / Leg−2Cripple at HP/2. Damage cap at limb HP (excess lost). Cutting cripple = severance.
Hand / Foot−4Cripple at HP/3. Common when disarming a shooter rather than killing.
Spine−8Penetrating types only. Cripples both legs (lower) or both arms (upper).
Weapon−5 (small) / −4 (large)Disarm or break their weapon. Lightsaber hilts are surprisingly fragile — a successful weapon strike on a Force-user is a signature move.

Targeted Attacks & Bursts

Targeted Bursts. When firing a burst with Targeted Attack, the location penalty applies to the whole burst — you’re aiming the weapon at one spot. Compute margin of success as normal; each bullet/bolt that connects (Hits formula from Part IV) lands at the targeted location. So a Vitals-targeted burst from a RoF 4 blaster with margin 4 lands two bolts to the Vitals.

Splitting Burst & Targeted Attack. You may split a burst between locations on the same target (head + torso, etc.) — declare the split and each sub-burst takes its own location’s to-hit penalty plus the −2 per additional target/location split. Mostly cinematic; rarely the optimal play.

Targeted + High RoF Hit Bonus. The High RoF Hit Bonus (Part IV) applies on top of the Targeted Attack penalty. A RoF 8 burst at the Eye: −9 (Eye) + 1 (High RoF) − range etc. Snipers running heavy weapons can sometimes overcome the Eye/Heart penalties by sheer volume.

Wild Swing & Obscured Targets

Wild Swing (Ranged). When you cannot clearly see your target — through smoke, around a corner, in darkness, at an invisible foe — your skill caps at 9 regardless of training. Other modifiers (Aim, range, cover) apply afterwards. Aiming through smoke counts as Wild Swing unless you have a Force Sense, infrared, or similar non-visual sensing.

Blind Fire. Firing without any line of sight (over a wall without looking, around a corner) is Wild Swing with an additional −5 penalty. Bursts can still suppress under blind fire even if no individual shot is likely to land.

Force Sense for Ranged. SW A Force-sensitive may attempt a Force Sense roll to negate or reduce the Wild Swing cap — success means you can shoot at full skill at obscured/unseen targets. Smoke, fog, total darkness all allow Force Sense substitution. See Doc IV for the precise mechanic and FP cost. The Wild Swing skill cap remains if the Force Sense fails.

Target Movement

Moving Targets. A target moving at speed adds to the effective range penalty via the Speed/Range table. Roll on the table for the target’s speed in hexes per turn (1 hex/turn = 1 m/s), add that bonus to your skill (it’s a positive — speed makes them more trackable as a ”bigger” target via velocity). At person-scale, foot movement is usually only 1-2 hexes/turn so no meaningful bonus. Vehicles, mounts, and starships at high speed move enough for the rule to matter.

Predicting Movement. If your target is moving in a clear direction and you Aim, you may predict their location for the upcoming turn. Roll an IQ-based Tactics or Beam Weapons (whatever) at no penalty if the target is moving predictably; at −2 if their movement is erratic. Success: your Aim is preserved and you may fire on their predicted hex instead of their current one. Failure: Aim lost, no prediction.

Recoil & Stun Interactions

Shock Penalty & Ranged. If you took damage last turn, you suffer a shock penalty (−1 per HP lost, cap −4) on this turn’s actions. For ranged combat this applies to both the to-hit roll and the per-shot Aim calculation. A wounded sniper trying to Aim takes the shock penalty on top of any other modifiers.

Mental Stun & Ranged. If mentally stunned, you cannot Aim (you’re not focused). You can still fire snap shots at half effective skill if you choose; most combatants use Do Nothing to recover. Force-sensitives may use Combat Reflexes +6 IQ to recover at start of turn.

Part VI

Cover &
Concealment

DR by cover type · partial cover modifiers · pop-up shooting · line of sight

A tactical firefight is decided by who controls cover. Bullets and blaster bolts stop when they hit something dense enough; concealment hides you but doesn’t deflect anything. A bush conceals; a stone wall covers. The difference matters when a shot is fired anyway — concealment is a to-hit penalty for the shooter; cover is a DR value that absorbs damage before it touches you. Both can apply at the same time (you’re hidden in smoke behind a wall).

Cover Type DR To-Hit Penalty Notes
Wooden door / fence3—Standard interior door. Bolts pass through after first hit.
Thin tree2-3—Sapling or thin trunk. Limited protection.
Thick tree trunk15+—Mature hardwood. Full body cover.
Brick wall8-15—Standard masonry. Holds up to small arms; cracks under sustained fire.
Sandbag wall12—Field fortification. Absorbs blaster bolts well; degrades with damage.
Concrete wall24-100—Heavy construction. Stops most personal weapons. Heavy weapons defeat eventually.
Hardened steel plate70+—Vehicle armour, blast doors. Only heavy weapons or explosives penetrate.
Permacrete / durasteel SW50-200—Star Wars structural materials. Buildings, walls, bunkers.
Low wall (partial)cover behind−2 to hit targetTarget torso/head exposed. Shooter at full DR for legs.
Firing slit / windowcover around−4 to hit targetSmall aperture. Target shoots from cover at normal.
Around a corner—−4 to hit (peek)Shooter exposes only weapon and one eye; reduced firing accuracy.

Cover vs Concealment

Cover. Physical material between you and an attack. Provides DR against shots that hit it before reaching you. If the cover has more DR than the attack’s damage, you take nothing. Examples: walls, vehicles, large trees, sandbags, blast doors. Cover degrades — sustained fire eats away at a brick wall’s DR over time (GM call, typically −1 DR per major hit).

Concealment. Visual obstruction that does NOT block shots. Smoke, fog, darkness, foliage, dust. Provides a to-hit penalty to the shooter, typically −2 (partial) to −10 (total darkness). Concealment does not deflect bolts — if they hit, they hit. Stack concealment with cover for full tactical protection.

Mixed Cover and Concealment. Most real cover provides both. A line of bushes is concealment (−2 to hit) but also gives some leaf/branch DR (DR 1-2). The GM picks the dominant property; usually concealment dominates because bushes don’t stop bolts.

Partial Cover

Low Wall (Crouching Behind). Target’s lower body is fully shielded; torso and head are exposed. Shooter takes −2 to hit. Successful hits roll for hit location normally; legs (6-7, 13-14) are blocked by cover (treat as miss, or re-roll, GM choice). Most fortifications work like this.

Firing Slit or Embrasure. Target shoots from inside cover through a small opening. Shooter takes −4 to hit the defender; the defender’s own shots take no penalty (they have a clear arc). The classic bunker engagement: very hard to dislodge.

Behind a Corner (Pop-Up Shooting). Step out from cover, take your shot, step back. Shooter takes −4 to skill for the pop-up shot (less time to aim). Defenders shooting at you while you pop up may suffer a penalty too — they have to predict your timing. Some campaigns use a Quick Contest of Tactics or Beam Weapons skill to determine whether you pop up before they fire or after.

Through a Doorway. A standing fighter in a doorway is partly silhouetted, partly framed. Shooters get a small bonus (+1 to hit) if the defender is backlit; the defender gets a small penalty firing through (−1) because the doorway frame narrows their arc.

Line of Sight

Line of Sight (LoS). An unobstructed straight line from shooter to target. Required for any normal ranged attack. Cover, walls, obstacles, terrain all block LoS unless there’s an opening. Force-sensitives may use Force Sense to ”see” through obstacles for targeting purposes (Doc IV), at a cost.

Diagonal LoS in Hex/Square Maps. The GM judges whether a line passes through a partial blocker. As a guideline: if a straight line from shooter’s hex to target’s hex crosses the edge of a cover hex, the shooter sees the target at partial cover (−2). If it crosses through the cover entirely, no LoS.

Visibility & LoS. LoS requires both an unobstructed path AND ability to perceive. In darkness, smoke, or behind opaque cover you may have LoS in the geometric sense but no visual contact — treat as Wild Swing (skill cap 9) or roll a Force Sense.

Smoke, Fog, Darkness

Smoke and Fog. Partial smoke: −2 to hit. Heavy smoke (smoke grenade, thick fog): −5 to hit. Total whiteout: Wild Swing only (cap 9) unless using Force Sense or infrared/thermal optics.

Darkness. Twilight: −1 to hit. Moonlight: −3. Pitch black: −9 to hit, or Wild Swing. Night optics, infrared, or Force Sense can negate. Most blasters illuminate slightly when fired — your position may give away your location.

Tactical Use of Concealment. A skilled combatant uses concealment to MOVE, not to fight from. Moving through smoke or behind concealment lets you reposition before opening fire from a different angle. Concealment is generally consumed quickly — wind blows smoke away, vision adjusts to darkness, foes triangulate sound.

Cover Mechanics for Defenders

Cover DR vs Damage. When a shot would hit you behind cover, apply the cover’s DR first, then your armour’s DR, then the remainder hits HP. If the cover absorbs all damage, you take none and the cover degrades. If the cover absorbs partial damage, the remainder hits you normally.

Cover Degradation. Cover takes damage from any hits that exceed its DR. Most cover has its own HP; once depleted, the cover is destroyed and provides no further protection. Brick walls might have 30+ HP per m² section; sandbags 20; a wooden door 10. GM tracks degradation when it matters tactically.

Sacrificial Cover. A teammate or hostage standing between you and a shooter provides body-cover. Their body has its DR + HP between bolt and you. Used hostiles call this ”human shield”. The hostage gets shot first; bolts that overkill carry through.

Part VII

Defending
Against Ranged

Dodge · drop prone · Lightsaber bolt deflection · the iconic Jedi defense

For non-Force-users, defending against ranged attacks is mostly Dodge. Cover, drop-prone, and movement-based defenses do the rest. Parry doesn’t work against bullets or arrows in RAW — they travel too fast for most weapons to intercept reliably. The single major exception is the lightsaber, which by virtue of being a meter of plasma can interpose between bolt and body in a way nothing else can. This part covers ordinary ranged defense, then the Lightsaber bolt deflection mechanic in detail — the iconic Jedi defense, built here from scratch for the campaign.

Defense vs Ranged Notes
DodgeFull effectAlways your default. Subject to multi-defense penalty.
Parry (melee weapon)Forbidden RAWYou cannot parry a bullet, arrow, or thrown weapon with a sword. Too fast.
Lightsaber Parry SWAllowed — specialLightsabers deflect blaster bolts. See the dedicated mechanic below. Catching Force Lightning instead forms a Lightning Lock (Doc I §Saber Lock).
Block (shield)Full effectShield/cloak interposes. Block roll vs ranged hit.
Drop Prone (free)+2 vs missilesFree action as a defense. Ends prone. Common reaction to incoming fire.
Dive for Cover+3 if reaches coverMovement-based defense. Ends prone behind cover if achievable.
RetreatNOT allowed (RAW)Missiles fly faster than you can move. Movement defenses (Dive/Sideslip) still work.
Sideslip / Slip MA−1 / −2 + retreat bonusMA movement defenses. Step sideways or closer instead of retreating.
Acrobatic Dodge+2 / −2 DodgeAcrobatics roll modifies Dodge. Crit fail: fall.
All-Out Defense+2 / DoubleSame as melee. AoD(Increased) on Dodge is the standard ”duck and weave” option.
Force Push / Pull as SR House SWQC vs attacker skillSpecial Reaction (Doc I Part V). Deflect bolt or shove ally aside. Forbidden during AoA.

Ordinary Defenses vs Ranged

Dodge. Move out of the path. Basic Speed + 3, drop fractions; modifiers as Doc I. Subject to multi-defense penalty when defending against more than one attack in a turn. Dodge applies once per ranged attack — a burst counts as ONE attack against your Dodge (you defend the whole burst with one roll). Bursts vs Dodge can be brutal — if you miss your Dodge by even 1, the entire burst lands (all hits computed from Part IV).

No Retreat vs Missiles. Per RAW, you cannot use the Retreat bonus against ranged attacks — the missile is moving faster than your step. You CAN still use Acrobatic Dodge, AoD, drop prone, or dive for cover, all of which involve other movement.

Block vs Ranged. A shield can intercept arrows, bullets, and blaster bolts. Block skill ÷ 2 + 3, drop fractions, +1 Combat Reflexes, +DB. Block follows the same multi-defense penalty as Dodge. Successful block: the shield takes the damage (apply shield DR vs the attack; shields have HP and can be destroyed). Note that cloak Block works for bolts physically interposing, but flame and lasers may set the cloak on fire — GM call.

Drop Prone as Defense. Drop to prone as a Free Action used as your defense for one incoming attack. +2 to Dodge against that attack (missile fire is hardest to land on prone targets at range — but they’re easy to hit at close range, +3 close / −2 ranged per Doc I posture rules). Ends your turn prone; future ranged attackers shoot at −2; future melee attackers at +3.

Dive for Cover. Take an All-Out Defense (Dodge) variant: roll Acrobatics or Dodge, modified by terrain. Success means you reach the nearest cover in one motion, ending prone behind it. The defense applies to the attack triggering the dive. Combatants in firefights frequently use this — a +3 retreat bonus replacement when retreat itself is forbidden.

Movement-Based Defenses

Sideslip / Slip MA. From Doc I: step sideways (Sideslip, −1 to def) or closer (Slip, −2 to def) instead of retreating. Both replace the forbidden retreat bonus with a positional change. Useful when a sniper has pinned you — slip sideways to break their angle.

Acrobatic Dodge. From Doc I. Roll Acrobatics; success gives +2 to Dodge, failure −2, crit fail you fall. Stacks with Dive for Cover for a +5 Dodge on a single critical defensive moment.

Lightsaber Bolt Deflection REACTIVE— the iconic Jedi defense

A lightsaber’s plasma blade can intercept incoming blaster bolts — slow enough that a Force-sensitive combatant can perceive the bolt’s trajectory and bring the blade between bolt and body. The mechanic builds on Doc I’s Lightsaber Parry: Lightsaber skill ÷ 2 + 3, drop fractions, +1 Combat Reflexes, +Enhanced Parry, no weapon parry mod, lightsaber Form bonuses (Doc IV). Three modes apply when parrying ranged attacks:

SWDeflect (default). Lightsaber Parry at normal skill. On success, the bolt is deflected into a random hex (roll 1d for direction: 1 = forward past defender, 2-3 = either side, 4-6 = back along the bolt’s original path or into the ground). The bolt does not threaten anyone unless it lands on someone in the random hex (GM rolls for incidental hits). On critical success, the defender also reflects automatically (see below). On failure, the bolt hits normally. On critical failure, the bolt may hit a vital location on the defender (GM call) or strike an ally adjacent to the defender.

SWRedirect (chosen target hex). Lightsaber Parry at −2. On success, the bolt is deflected into a hex of the defender’s choice within the original shooter’s distance from the defender. The bolt does not roll a new attack against anything in the destination hex — it simply lands there. Useful for guiding bolts harmlessly into walls, into the ground beside enemies (as a warning), or to clear a friendly’s path.

SWReflect (back at attacker). Lightsaber Parry at −4. On success, the bolt is sent back at the original attacker — re-rolled as an attack against the original shooter, using the defender’s effective Lightsaber Parry skill for the new attack roll (not the original shooter’s). Apply range from defender to original shooter (which is the same as original-shooter-to-defender, so usually the original range penalty repeats). The original shooter rolls a normal defense — they were not expecting their own bolt. Crit successes apply normal Crit Hit Table rules to the new attack on the shooter.

Multi-Defense Penalty. Each Lightsaber bolt deflection counts as a separate active defense and accumulates the multi-defense penalty as normal — first defense free, second at −2 with TbaM/WM trained on Lightsaber (the general Part IV rule — most saber wielders have it) or −4. A burst of 6 bolts needs up to 6 separate defense rolls. The cumulative penalty is what makes large blaster volleys dangerous — even a master saberist runs out of defense at ~5-6 bolts per turn.

Reflect Against Original Shooter. The reflected bolt is a ranged attack from the defender; the shooter defends normally with any ready defense — Dodge, Block, or their own Lightsaber Parry. Most shooters are unprepared for their own bolt, which is why the maneuver lands so often — but a mounted blaster turret or another Jedi shooter defends with no special penalty.

Multiple Targets via Reflect. A skilled Jedi may use Redirect or Reflect to clear a room of Sith troopers — each defense reflects a bolt back at the original shooter, ideally. The cumulative multi-defense penalty quickly degrades effectiveness, so picking which bolts to reflect (vs simply deflect) is a tactical choice each turn. Reflect a particularly dangerous shot (heavy weapon, marksman); deflect the rest into random hexes.

Bolts That Cannot Be Deflected. Some attacks bypass Lightsaber Parry:

  • Stun bolts — by default these CAN be deflected as normal blaster bolts. GM may rule that stun energy passes through the plasma blade unchanged.
  • Area-of-effect attacks — grenades, area-stun pulses, large explosives. Lightsaber cannot block area attacks; use Dodge, dive for cover, or Force Push.
  • Multiple simultaneous strikes — if more bolts arrive than the defender can defend against (multi-def penalty exceeds skill), the remainder hit at full effect.
  • Vehicle-scale weapons — heavy laser cannons, repeating turbolasers, etc. Lightsaber Parry generally fails against weapons designed for vehicles (Doc VI).

Special Reaction Bolt Redirect. A Force-sensitive may use Force Push or Pull as a Special Reaction (Doc I Part V) to deflect a bolt without using their Lightsaber Parry slot. Quick Contest of Push/Pull skill vs the attacker’s skill; success means the bolt goes off-line. This is an alternative to Lightsaber Parry — pick one per incoming attack. Used most often by Force-sensitives without a lightsaber, or as a backup when multi-defense penalty has stacked too high to risk another Parry.

Working Example — A Mandalorian Ambush

A Jedi (Lightsaber skill 18, Combat Reflexes, Weapon Master) walks into a chamber. Three Mandalorians open fire simultaneously — RoF 4 bursts each from carbines (Rcl 2, Acc 4), 15 hexes range, no Aim.

Mandalorian A fires first: 4 bolts. Skill 14 − 5 (range 15 hex) + 1 (High RoF for 4 shots = +0; wait, 4 = +0) = 9. Rolls 9 — margin 0, 1 bolt connects.

Jedi defense: Lightsaber Parry at 12 (18 ÷ 2 + 3) + 1 CR = 13. First defense, no multi-def penalty. Chooses Reflect at −4 → 9. Rolls 8, margin 1. Bolt reflects back at Mandalorian A, who Dodges at 7. Mandalorian rolls 9 — bolt hits him with his own carbine damage.

Mandalorian B fires: 4 bolts. Skill 14 − 5 = 9. Rolls 7, margin 2, 2 bolts connect.

Jedi second defense: Lightsaber Parry 13 − 2 (multi-def, halved by WM) = 11. Chooses Deflect (no penalty mode). Rolls 9 — succeeds. Both bolts deflected into random hexes (one hits the wall, one strikes the floor near Mandalorian C).

Mandalorian C fires: 4 bolts. Skill 14 − 5 = 9. Rolls 6, margin 3, 2 bolts connect.

Jedi third defense: Lightsaber Parry 13 − 4 (multi-def cumulative, halved) = 9. Chooses Deflect. Rolls 11 — fails. Both bolts strike the Jedi at full damage.

The Jedi takes two carbine bolts to the torso. Even with master skill, four attackers firing bursts overwhelm the defense in one turn. The lesson: positioning to fight one or two foes at a time matters more than skill.

Part VIII

Reloading
& Weapon Operation

Ready maneuver · Fast-Draw (Ammo) · malfunctions · jam clearance

Mechanical weapons run out of ammunition; they jam, misfire, overheat. Blaster weapons in Star Wars settings are particularly robust but still need cell swaps and occasional maintenance. This part covers the basic maneuvers for keeping a weapon in service. Capacity and reload specifics for individual weapon types belong in the equipment document.

Action Maneuver Time Notes
Reload (standard)Ready1 turnSwap magazine, cell, or quiver. One Ready maneuver.
Reload (Fast-Draw success)Quick ActionQA, alongside maneuverFast-Draw (Ammo) skill roll. Success = reload as QA. Failure = need Ready next turn.
Draw weaponReady1 turnFrom holster, sling, hilt, etc. One Ready.
Draw (Fast-Draw success)Quick ActionQAFast-Draw (Pistol / Long Arm / etc.) roll. Success = ready weapon and may fire same turn.
Clear jamReady1-3 turnsTime depends on weapon and jam severity. Armoury skill helps.
Cool down (overheated)Ready or waitVariableBlasters can overheat after heavy sustained fire; may need a turn or more of inactivity.
Change settingReady1 turnStun ↔ kill, single ↔ burst, energy intensity, etc.
Activate deviceFree / Ready / QAVariesPower on, deploy bipod, extend stock. GM judges based on single-touch vs multi-step.

The Ready Maneuver in Ranged Combat

Ready Maneuver. From Doc I: draw a weapon, reload, clear a jam, activate a device, change grip, shift between one- and two-handed grip. Step permitted. Defenses normal. The fundamental ”do a thing with my equipment” maneuver. Reloading the typical blaster cell or magazine is one Ready maneuver. Some weapons require multiple Readies (drum magazines, belt-fed weapons, multi-shot grenade launchers reloading individual rounds).

Multiple Readies. Belt-fed weapons, complex reloads, or careful maintenance may take 2+ Ready maneuvers. The GM should declare upfront how many turns a reload takes for any non-standard weapon. Players can short-cut some of this with Armoury skill (well below 10 turns minimum to maintain a working weapon; high skill enables 1-turn reloads of complex weapons).

Fast-Draw

Fast-Draw (Weapon). Per-weapon-family skill that lets you draw the weapon as a Quick Action. Fast-Draw (Pistol), Fast-Draw (Long Arm), Fast-Draw (Hilt) for lightsabers, Fast-Draw (Knife), etc. Roll the skill at the start of your turn; success = the weapon is ready and you may attack with it the same turn. Failure = weapon not yet ready, use Ready next turn instead. Critical failure = drop the weapon. Combat Reflexes: +1 to all Fast-Draw rolls.

Fast-Draw (Ammo). Reload as a Quick Action. Particularly common for blaster cells (Star Wars settings) and clipped magazines (slugthrowers). Roll the skill at start of turn; success = reload completed as QA, you may also fire this turn with your normal maneuver. Failure = reload not done; use Ready maneuver next turn. Crit fail = drop the ammo.

Fast-Draw and AoA. Fast-Draw remains permitted during All-Out Attack (offensive uses only). A common technique: draw the weapon as a Fast-Draw QA, then immediately All-Out Attack with it for +4 to hit. Most professional gunners use this opening move.

Malfunctions and Jams

Malfunction Number (Malf). Some weapons list a malfunction number — the natural die roll on the attack that triggers a mechanical failure. Most modern blasters are Malf 17+ (very reliable); older or jury-rigged weapons might be Malf 14 or 15 (jam frequently). Roll natural Malf or higher on the attack die → the attack fails AND the weapon needs clearing.

Jam Clearance. A jammed weapon requires a Ready maneuver and an Armoury skill or weapon-specific skill roll to clear. Failure on the clearance roll → another Ready maneuver to try again. The jam consumes 1-3 turns of effective combat time, sometimes longer. Smart combatants carry a sidearm or drop to melee while their primary is offline.

Catastrophic Failure. Critical failure (3-4 on the attack die against a weapon with low Malf) may damage or destroy the weapon. The weapon takes its own listed catastrophic damage (typically the Crit Miss Table on Doc I cheatsheet). A blaster cell rupture can be ugly — heat damage to the wielder’s hand and possibly weapon destruction.

Maintenance and Reliability

Armoury Skill. The skill of weapon maintenance, jam clearance, modification, and repair. Per-weapon-family or per-weapon (Armoury [Beam Weapons], Armoury [Slugthrowers], etc.). Regular maintenance keeps your weapon at its listed Malf number; neglecting maintenance increases the Malf number (worsens reliability) at GM discretion.

Field Conditions. Dirty, wet, dusty, or hostile-environment conditions degrade weapon reliability. The GM may apply a temporary +1 to Malf in such conditions. Blasters generally fare better than slugthrowers in space dust, vacuum, and submersion; some weapons are sealed for extreme environments.

Cell Capacity and Reload Frequency. Blaster cell capacity varies by weapon size. Pistols typically hold 100 shots; carbines 50; rifles 30; heavy/repeating weapons 20 or less. Specific capacities and recharge mechanics will be detailed in the equipment document. For combat purposes, track shots and reload before the cell runs dry — running dry mid-burst is a particularly bad way to be caught.

Part IX

Combinations
Reference

What stacks · what ranged forbids · quick lookup

Legal ranged combinations and the comprehensive forbidden list. Each table focuses on one maneuver, modifier, defense, or action and shows what it can stack with at range. Subject to FP cost, skill caps, and per-turn timing. Combinations involving melee components are covered in Doc I; this is the ranged-specific lookup.

Aim Stacks

CombinationNetEffect
Aim alone+Acc on next attackStandard one turn of Aim. Cap at Acc + 2 with three turns total.
+ Bracing (one-handed)+1 AccRest weapon on solid object before Aiming.
+ Bracing (two-handed / prone)+2 AccMaximum stability. Stacks with multi-turn Aim.
+ Scope / optic+1 to +4 AccOptic level adds to effective Acc.
+ Enhanced TrackingAim on multiple targetsMaintain Aim on one extra target per Enhanced Tracking level.
+ Targeted Attack (next turn)Aim bonus + location penaltyAim then fire at specific location. Most common sniper play.
+ All-Out Attack (Determined) next turnAim + AoA +4Three turns Aim then AoA = +Acc + 2 + 4 = +Acc + 6. No defenses.
+ High RoF Hit Bonus+0 to +5 by RoF firedAim + spray = enormous effective skill on first shot.

Rate of Fire Stacks

CombinationNetEffect
RoF burst (full)One roll, hits = (margin ÷ Rcl) + 1Capped at RoF. Single attack roll.
+ High RoF Hit Bonus+0 to +5 by RoF5-8 = +1, 9-16 = +2, 17-49 = +3, 50-99 = +4, 100+ = +5.
+ Bracing (two-handed)Rcl reduced by 1 (min 1)Prone/kneeling brace dramatically increases burst effectiveness.
+ Aim (then burst)Acc bonus on burst hit rollAim applies once; bonus carries to all bullets in burst.
+ Targeted AttackLocation penalty on hit rollAll burst hits land at the targeted location.
+ Split burst (per target)Each sub-burst rolls separately−2 per target beyond first. Each gets own High RoF bonus.
+ Suppression FireFull burst into areaAnyone in area Dodges or hit. Consumes whole burst.
+ Move and Attack−2 or Bulk to hitMobile burst fire. Aim lost.

Targeted Attack Stacks (Ranged)

CombinationNetEffect
Targeted aloneLocation penaltyEye −9, Vitals −3, Heart −5, etc.
+ Aim (multi-turn)Aim bonus offsets location penaltyThree turns Aim, +Acc + 2, then Vitals −3 = net +Acc − 1 vs no Aim. Massive net improvement.
+ Burst (RoF 2+)All hits at locationBurst → location, all connecting bullets land at the target spot.
+ Telegraphic+4 to hit, +2 foe defenseTelegraphic stacks with Targeted. Used vs stunned/wounded foes.
+ All-Out Attack (Determined)+4 to hitAoA + Targeted = sniper’s signature move. No defenses.
+ Committed Attack (Det)+2 to hit, −2 defLess common at range. Works.
Repeating same Targeted AttackFoe +1 to defense on 3rd attemptPredictability penalty after twice in a row.
+ Force Sense SWNegate Wild Swing capTargeted attack at obscured location through smoke/walls.

Defense Stacks (vs Ranged)

CombinationNetEffect
Dodge aloneBase DodgeAlways available. Subject to multi-defense penalty.
+ Acrobatic Dodge+2 / −2 by Acrobatics rollOnce per turn. Crit fail = fall.
+ Sideslip / Slip MA−1 / −2 + retreat bonusMovement defense; replaces forbidden Retreat vs ranged.
+ Drop Prone (free)+2 vs missilesFree action defense. Ends prone.
+ Dive for Cover+3 to defense if cover reachedEffectively AoD(Increased Dodge) + dive. Ends prone in cover.
+ AoD (Increased Dodge)+2 to DodgeNo attacks. Standard ”duck and weave” play.
+ AoD (Double)Two defense rolls per attackOne Dodge + one different (Block, etc.) or two Lightsaber Parries (Doc I).
Lightsaber Parry (Deflect) SWNormal ParryBolt deflected to random hex on success.
Lightsaber Parry (Redirect) SWParry at −2Bolt deflected to chosen hex on success.
Lightsaber Parry (Reflect) SWParry at −4Bolt re-rolls vs original attacker’s defense.
+ Special Reaction (Force Push) House SWQC Push vs attackerDeflect bolt without consuming Parry slot. Forbidden during AoA.

Cover Stacks

CombinationNetEffect
Low Wall (partial)−2 to hit youLegs covered. Hit location rolls re-roll on covered area.
Firing Slit−4 to hit youDefender shoots out unimpeded.
Around a Corner (Pop-Up)−4 to your skillLess time to aim while exposing.
+ Concealment (smoke/fog)Additional −2 to −10Stacks with cover. Hides position even if not blocking shots.
+ Drop Prone+2 def + coverBehind low wall and prone = nearly impossible to hit at range.
+ Cover DR vs damageApply before armourCover DR absorbs first; degrades over time.

Forbidden — Cannot Use With Ranged

  • Rapid Strike — melee only. Use the weapon’s RoF for multiple shots.
  • Deceptive Attack — melee only. Cannot fake out a target with a bolt.
  • Beat — melee only. Physical guard-batter doesn’t work at range.
  • Ruse — melee only. Verbal misdirection doesn’t help your trigger pull.
  • Feint — melee only. No bluffing a bolt.
  • Combination MA — melee technique. RoF serves the same role.
  • Riposte — requires Parry; Parry vs ranged is only Lightsaber Parry, and Riposte doesn’t extend to bolt deflection mechanics.
  • Aggressive Parry / Hand-Clap Parry — strikes against the attacker’s limb. Doesn’t apply at range — the foe is too far.
  • Stop Hit / Stop Thrust — both melee defenses against charging foes.
  • Retreat bonus — RAW forbids retreat against missile attacks. Movement defenses (Dive, Sideslip, Slip) substitute.
  • AoA (Long) MA — reach +1 doesn’t apply to ranged.

Permitted with Ranged

  • Aim (multi-turn) — the core ranged buildup.
  • Targeted Attack — location penalty applies as melee. Eye, Vitals, Heart, etc.
  • Wild Swing — skill cap 9 when target obscured.
  • Telegraphic Attack — +4 to hit, target +2 def. Both melee and ranged.
  • Move and Attack — −2 or Bulk penalty. No retreat this turn.
  • All-Out Attack (Determined) — +4 to hit. No defenses.
  • All-Out Attack (Strong) — +2 dmg (only for thr/sw-based weapons; rare in ranged).
  • All-Out Attack (Double) — two attacks. Useful for firing the weapon’s RoF in two separate bursts at separate targets.
  • Committed Attack (Det) — +2 to hit, −2 other defenses.
  • Cinematic Dual-Weapon Attack (Pistol or Bow) — see equipment doc.
  • Force Sense for Ranged SW — substitute Force Sense for visual targeting through obscurement.
  • Special Reaction (Push/Pull) House SW — bolt deflection or ally protection. Forbidden during AoA.

Cross-Document References

For combinations involving melee modifiers, see Doc I Part IX (Combinations Reference). For hit location wound effects in detail, see Doc I cheatsheet’s Hit Location & Crippling table. For Force-related ranged interactions (Force Push/Pull, Force Sense, lightsaber Forms), see Doc IV. For weapon-specific data (RoF, Acc, Bulk, Rcl, capacity, malfunction by model), see the Equipment Compendium (Doc VII).

Part X

The Ranged
Playbook

Every option · every modifier · the combined Range/RoF/Rcl threshold tables

This is the comprehensive lookup. Every action a ranged combatant may attempt, every situational modifier that might apply, and the full threshold reference for Rate of Fire, Recoil, and Range — collected in single-glance tables. Use it during play to find a rule without rereading earlier parts. Star Wars-tagged entries are setting-specific; the rest is GURPS 4e RAW.

① Aim & Setup Actions

Action Maneuver / Cost Effect Notes
Snap ShotAttack maneuver+0 (no Acc)Pure skill. Weapon’s Acc does NOT apply. Default when you fire without prior Aim.
Aim (1 turn)Aim maneuver+Acc on next attackStep permitted (1 hex); facing change ≤ 1 hex face.
Aim (2 turns)Two Aim maneuvers+Acc + 1Holding the bead, settling the breath.
Aim (3+ turns — cap)Three+ Aim maneuvers+Acc + 2 (final cap)No further benefit beyond turn 3. Stop and shoot.
Brace (one-handed)Posture, before Aim+1 to weapon AccRest weapon against rail, wall, vehicle, knee.
Brace (two-handed / kneeling / prone)Posture, before Aim+2 to weapon AccStandard sniper position. Stacks with Aim turns.
Optic / scopeMounted on weapon+1 to +4 AccScope’s bonus added to weapon Acc before Aim math.
Re-Aim (new target)Aim maneuver+Acc freshStarts over from +Acc; lose any accumulated bonus.
Lead Target (predict)During AimAim on hex they’ll occupyIQ/Tactics/skill roll. Success = retain Aim on predicted hex. Failure = lose Aim.
Concentrate — Force Sense SWConcentrate maneuverNegates Wild Swing capForce-sensitives only. See Doc IV for FP cost.
Wait (weapon ready)Wait maneuverFire on declared triggerReflex shot when foe enters arc, draws weapon, crosses doorway.
Fast-Draw (Weapon)Quick ActionWeapon ready as QASkill roll. Permitted during AoA. Crit fail = drop weapon.
Fast-Draw (Ammo)Quick ActionReload as QASkill roll. Failure = use Ready maneuver next turn instead.
Change SettingReady maneuverSwitch modeStun ↔ kill, single ↔ burst, energy intensity. SW Blaster setting toggle.

② Single-Shot Attacks

Action Maneuver / Cost Effect Notes
Standard AttackAttack maneuver1 shot at full skillDefault. Step permitted. Active defenses normal.
Move and AttackM&A maneuverFull Move + 1 shot−2 OR weapon Bulk, whichever is worse. No retreat. Aim lost.
All-Out Attack (Determined)AoA maneuver+4 to hitNO active defenses. Step or Move. Offensive QA + Fast-Draw OK.
All-Out Attack (Double)AoA maneuver2 shots / burstsTwo targets or two bursts (same or different foes). NO defenses.
Committed Attack (Determined)Committed maneuver+2 to hitOther defenses −2; no retreat. Uncommon at range but legal.
Defensive AttackDefensive Attack maneuver−2 dmg or −1/die+1 to one defense. Rarely useful with ranged (you’re not parrying with the weapon).
Telegraphic AttackModifier on Attack+4 to hit, target +2 defUseful vs stunned, prone, or otherwise compromised foes.
Targeted AttackModifier on AttackLocation penalty appliesSkull −7, Face −5, Eye −9, Vitals −3, Heart −5, Arm/Leg −2, etc.
Wild SwingModifier on AttackSkill capped at 9Target obscured / unseen / behind you with no awareness aid.
Off-Hand ShotModifier on AttackSkill −4No penalty with Ambidexterity or Off-Hand Weapon Training.
One-Handed Long ArmModifier on Attack−1 per ST below 1.5×Two-handed weapon used one-handed. Penalty stacks with off-hand.

③ Burst & Rate-of-Fire Attacks

Action Maneuver / Cost Effect Notes
Standard Burst (full RoF)Attack maneuver1 roll; hits = (margin ÷ Rcl) + 1Capped at RoF. Defender makes ONE active defense vs the whole burst.
Aimed BurstAim + AttackAim bonus on burst rollAcc applied once to the burst roll; all hits land at the targeted location/area.
Targeted BurstAttack with TargetedAll hits land at locationLocation penalty applies to burst’s single roll. All connecting bolts hit the chosen spot.
Split Burst (2 targets)Attack maneuver−2 per sub-burstDeclare split before rolling. Each sub-burst rolls separately with own range/cover.
Split Burst (3 targets)Attack maneuver−4 per sub-burstRoF 6+ required to meaningfully split three ways. Each gets own High RoF bonus.
Split Burst (4+ targets)Attack maneuver−6+ per sub-burstHeavy-weapon territory. Each sub-burst needs at least 2 shots to bother.
Sweeping FireAttack maneuverSweep contiguous hexesRoF 16+ required. Each hex defended separately by its occupants.
Suppression FireAttack maneuverArea denialRoF 5+ into hex/doorway/line. Anyone in or crossing must Dodge or be hit. Consumes full burst.
Move and Attack BurstM&A maneuver−2 or Bulk worseFull Move + burst. Common with autoblasters during cover advance.
Bracing a BurstPosture before burstRcl reduced by 1 (min 1)Two-handed / kneeling / prone / bipod. Doubles effective hits at given margin.
High RoF Hit BonusApplies to burst rolls+0 to +5 by shot count1-4 shots +0 · 5-8 +1 · 9-16 +2 · 17-49 +3 · 50-99 +4 · 100+ +5.

④ Special & Unconventional Shots

Action Maneuver / Cost Effect Notes
Pop-Up Shot (around corner)Attack maneuver−4 to skillStep out from cover, fire, step back. Less time to aim. May trigger foe’s Wait.
Pop-Out (full reveal)Attack maneuver−2 to skillStep out, fire normally, end exposed. Foes target you at no cover penalty next turn.
Rear-Arc Fire (behind you)Attack with Wild SwingSkill cap 9Cannot see target. Unaware = no shot. With 360° Vision: side-arc penalty only (−2, no cap).
Over-Shoulder FireAttack maneuver−4 to skillSideways fire while running forward — you can SEE target but body is awkward. Half-twist while moving.
Reflexive Wait FireWait maneuverTrigger fires on eventDeclare trigger. Acts BEFORE the triggering action resolves. Combat Reflexes +1 if it matters.
Quick Draw + ShotFast-Draw QA + AttackDraw & fire same turnFast-Draw roll; success = weapon ready, then any attack maneuver. Standard gunslinger opening.
Quick Draw + AoAFast-Draw QA + AoA+4 to hit on first shotFast-Draw remains legal during AoA. Highest opening burst-of-fire damage.
Disarm ShotTargeted Attack (Weapon)−5 (small) / −4 (large)Strike the foe’s weapon. Damage rolled vs weapon HP; may break or knock free.
Warning ShotAttack maneuverAimed missIntentionally hit near (not on) target. May force Will/Fright check. GM may apply social/morale effects.
Indirect Fire (arcing)Attack with Throwing/launcher−2 minimum to hitLob projectile over cover (grenade, mortar). LoS to landing hex required, not target. Cannot use Aim’s Acc bonus without spotter.
Cover Fire (overlapping)Attack maneuverPer SuppressionCoordinated team suppression. Multiple shooters’ areas overlap; defender Dodges each separately.
Snap Fire from ProneAttack maneuver+2 brace from proneIf already prone with weapon ready: bracing bonus applies even on snap shot.
Drop-and-ShootChange Posture + AttackCannot in same turnStandard → prone takes Change Posture maneuver; no attack same turn. Use Acrobatic ground variants if available (Doc I MA).
Trick Shot (cinematic)Attack maneuver−4 to −8 dependingRicochet, around obstacles, through a swinging door. GM call. Cinematic-only.
Overcharge / Overload Shot SWAttack with risk+1 die damage; Malf +2Overcharge blaster cell for one shot. Greatly increases jam/destruction risk for next attack. GM call.

⑤ Force-User Ranged Options SW

Action Maneuver / Cost Effect Notes
Force Sense TargetingConcentrate or QANegates Wild Swing capAim at obscured/unseen target through smoke, walls, darkness. FP cost (Doc IV).
Force Push (Offensive)Force power, attack rollKnockback / disarmRanged via Will roll. May knock foe prone or disarm. See Doc IV.
Force Pull (Offensive)Force power, attack rollDrag foe / objectPull weapon from hand, foe into reach, etc.
Force LightningInnate Attack, attack rollBurning / fat damageSith / Dark Side power. Treated as Innate Attack (ranged) per Doc IV.
Bolt DeflectLightsaber ParryNormal skillDefault mode. Bolt to random hex on success. Doc II Part VII.
Bolt RedirectLightsaber Parry−2 to ParryBolt to chosen hex within attacker’s distance.
Bolt Reflect (back at shooter)Lightsaber Parry−4 to ParryNew attack vs original shooter using defender’s parry skill.
Special Reaction (Push / Pull) HOUSE1 / turn, full FPQC vs attacker skillDeflect bolt without consuming Parry slot. Forbidden during AoA. Doc I Part V.

⑥ Defenses vs Ranged Attacks

Defense Maneuver / Cost Effect Notes
DodgeStandard defenseBasic Speed + 3Always available. Subject to multi-defense penalty.
Drop Prone (as defense)Free action+2 vs missileEnd prone. Future ranged: −2 to hit you / future melee: +3 to hit you.
Dive for CoverAoD variant+3 if cover reachedEnd prone behind cover. Acrobatics or Dodge roll.
Block (shield / cloak)Standard defenseBlock ÷ 2 + 3Shield takes damage. Cloak may catch fire from energy.
Lightsaber Parry SWStandard defenseSkill ÷ 2 + 3Lightsaber only. Three modes (Deflect / Redirect / Reflect — see Part VII).
Acrobatic DodgeModifier on Dodge+2 / −2 by Acro roll1/turn. Crit fail = fall.
Sideslip / Slip MAMovement defense−1 / −2 + retreat bonusSubstitute for forbidden Retreat vs missiles.
AoD (Increased Dodge)AoD maneuver+2 DodgeNo attacks. Standard duck-and-weave.
AoD (Balanced) MAAoD maneuver+1 to two defensese.g. Dodge AND Lightsaber Parry each +1.
AoD (Double)AoD maneuverTwo rolls per attackEither succeeds = no hit. Second at multi-def penalty.
Feverish Defense MA1 FP, 1/turn+2 to one defenseStacks with everything except AoA / Committed.
Sacrificial DodgeDodge variant−2 Dodge, take hit for allyDive in front of an ally. End prone.
Special Reaction (Push / Pull) HOUSE SW1/turn, full FPQC vs attackerDeflect bolt or shove ally aside. Forbidden during AoA.
Cover Behind ObjectPositioningDR + to-hit penaltyCover absorbs damage before armour. See Part VI.

Ranged Modifiers Reference

Vision · distance · movement · environment · facing

Visibility & Obscurement

Condition To-Hit Penalty Notes & Workarounds
Full daylight / well-lit0No penalty. Default condition.
Overcast / shaded0No penalty. Reduced contrast at long range — GM may apply −1 past 200 m.
Twilight / dusk−1Civilian human vision marginal. Most professionals carry night optics.
Moonlight−3Mid-range only. Night Vision reduces penalty by level.
Starlight / dim−6Heavy night-optic territory.
Pitch black−9 OR Wild Swing (cap 9)Hearing/Force Sense/IR may substitute. Without them, can’t see target at all.
Light smoke / haze−2Thin smoke screen, dust, light fog.
Heavy smoke / fog−5Smoke grenade, thick mist. Hard to acquire target.
Total whiteoutWild Swing (cap 9)Snowstorm, ash cloud, dense smoke at point-blank.
Through partial foliage−2 (concealment)Bushes, branches. Concealment only; bolts pass through.
Force Sense (SW)Negates obscurementFP cost (Doc IV). Replaces visual targeting through any obscurement.
Infrared / thermal opticsNegates darknessDoesn’t help with smoke (heat-source signature still visible) or fog (sometimes).
Macrobinoculars / scopeNegates range −2 to −5Better targeting at long range. Useless inside scope’s minimum range.

Distance, Speed & Size

Factor Modifier Notes
Range to targetFrom Spd/Rng TableDoc II Part III. Read distance in metres → penalty.
Past 1/2D rangeDamage halvedTo-hit unaffected. Damage drops only.
Past Max rangeAuto-missProjectile cannot physically reach.
Target Size Mod (SM)+SM to skillHuman 0, droideka +1, speeder +3, AT-ST +5, mouse droid −3.
Target speed+speed-SM bonusFast target reads ”larger” via velocity. Speeder at 10 hex/turn (10 m/s): +4. Foot movement rarely matters.
Stationary target+0Standard. Most combatants aren’t truly stationary; the GM applies this only when explicitly so.
Target crouching−2 to hitHard to spot smaller silhouette.
Target prone+3 close / −2 rangedHuge target close, tiny target far.
Target running across LoS−2 to hitLateral movement adds difficulty separate from speed-SM.
Target moving toward / away+0 (small)Range changes mid-turn; recompute next turn.
Target on high ground+1 to hit youBetter view from above; default the elevated foe has the advantage.
You on high ground+1 to hit themReciprocal advantage when YOU have elevation.

Movement, Posture & Facing While Firing

Situation Modifier Notes
Move and Attack (forward)−2 or Bulk worseFull Move + 1 attack. Aim lost. No retreat. No Rapid Strike.
Move and Attack (sideways)−2 or Bulk worseSame penalty. Change facing as part of movement allowed (one face per hex).
Move and Attack (rear arc)Wild Swing (cap 9)Running forward, firing behind you. 360° Vision reduces to side arc (−2).
Move and Attack (over shoulder)−4 to skillTwist to fire while still moving forward. Awkward but possible.
Sprinting (M&A + extra Move)−2 or Bulk + extra penaltySome weapons cannot fire at sprint speeds. GM call.
Shooter crouching−2 to your skillFrom Doc I posture. Cancelled if Aiming & bracing.
Shooter kneeling−2 to your skillDoubles as brace if weapon rested → net change.
Shooter prone−4 to your skillBUT +2 brace if weapon supported → net effective −2.
Shooter mounted / vehicleM&A penaltyPlus relative velocity, terrain, gunner-vs-driver split (Doc VI).
Side facing−2 to defensesDefender penalty. Attacker no bonus to hit beyond defender penalty.
Rear arc (unaware)NO defense at allDefender unaware = bolt lands if it hits attacker’s defense number.
Rear arc (aware)−4 to defensesPer Doc I. 360° Vision: treat as side, −2 only.

Environment, Weather & Atypical Conditions

Condition Modifier Notes
Light wind0 (close) / −1 (long)Past 30 m, light wind starts affecting projectiles.
Strong wind−1 to −3 by rangeCommon in canyons, open plains, desert. Pi/imp affected more than burn.
Gale-force / storm−3 to −5Difficult shooting. Throwing weapons may be useless.
Heavy rain / snow−1 to −3Both vision (concealment) and projectile (deflection) effects.
UnderwaterMost ranged uselessSlugthrowers: lose 1 m of effective range per metre travelled, then auto-miss. Energy weapons may overheat or short out. Spearguns and harpoons designed for it.
Vacuum / space0Blasters fire normally. Slugthrowers need self-oxidizing rounds. Bolts travel forever (no air resistance).
High gravity−1 to −2Projectile arc affected. Energy weapons unaffected.
Low gravity+0 (range extends)1/2D and Max extend in low-G. Recoil more disruptive.
Sound suppressionReduces detectionSlugthrowers may use silencers. Blasters: cannot be silenced (plasma discharge is acoustic).
Sound of weapon fire~50-100 m outdoorsCombat decisions resolve in 1 turn; sound reaches well after impact. Mechanically rarely matters.
Tracer rounds−2 to StealthYour bursts light up your position. Easier to be found.
Holographic / illusionGM callForce Sense or Per roll may discern; otherwise treated as concealment.

Personal Condition Modifiers

Your State Modifier Notes
Shock penalty (just took HP damage)−1 per HP (cap −4)Applies next turn. Brain wound caps at −8. High Pain Threshold negates.
Physical stun−4 + HT to recoverRecover by spending Do Nothing maneuver and rolling HT.
Mental stun−4; cannot AimIQ each turn to recover. Combat Reflexes +6.
Reeling (HP ≤ ⅓)Move & Dodge halvedSkill unaffected, but you move and dodge poorly.
Crippled armCannot fire that weaponTwo-handed weapons unusable. One-handed possible with off-hand only.
Crippled handDrop held weaponCannot grip. Fast-Draw new weapon to other hand.
Blinded (one eye)−3 to rangedLose depth perception. Critical at long range.
Blinded (both)Wild Swing or Force SenseCap 9 without aid. SW Force Sense fully substitutes.
Off-balance (just crit-defended)−2 DX & defensesUntil Ready maneuver clears it.
Encumbrance−1 to −4 DodgeSkill unaffected. Reduces your defensive mobility only.
FP exhausted (FP ≤ ⅓)Move/Dodge halved; ST halved for damage; −2 skillPer Doc I HP/HT/Shock rules.

Target Concealment vs Cover (the distinction)

Type Effect Examples
ConcealmentTo-hit penalty onlySmoke, fog, foliage, darkness, hologram. Hides position; doesn’t stop bolts.
CoverProvides DR absorbing damageWalls, vehicles, large trees, sandbags, blast doors. Stops bolts before they reach you.
Mixed (typical)BothMost real cover combines both. Brick wall = total cover; bush = mostly concealment with marginal DR.
Partial cover−2 to hit (and DR for that body section)Low wall, sandbag berm. Legs blocked.
Firing slit−4 to hit defender; defender no penalty firing outBunkers, embrasures.
Around corner−4 to your pop-up shotOne-sided cover. Step back into safety after fire.

RoF · Rcl · Range

The threshold tables — where mechanics change

Many ranged combat values trigger different mechanics at specific thresholds. These tables collect every threshold in one place: when a weapon’s RoF unlocks suppression or sweeping fire, when its Recoil makes burst hits efficient or punishing, and what ranges put a target in each combat band. Use these alongside Doc II Parts II-IV for the math.

Rate of Fire (RoF) — Thresholds & Effects

RoF Hit Bonus Unlocks / Special Mechanics Typical Weapon Class
1+0Single shot. No burst, no High RoF bonus.Bolt-action rifle, sniper rifle, slug pistol, throwing knife. SW Blaster pistol on single fire.
2+0Small burst. 2 shots possible. Defender rolls one defense.Semi-auto pistol, double-barrel. SW Some sport blasters.
3+03-round burst. Tactical default for many weapons.Burst-fire pistol, hunting rifle.
4+0Threshold to split burst between targets (Part IV). 2 shots per target.Standard blaster carbine on burst mode.
5+1Suppression Fire unlocks. Threshold to deny corridors / areas.SMG, blaster carbine, light auto.
6–8+1Solid burst. 3-way split possible at −4 per sub-burst. Common tactical fire.Blaster carbine sustained fire, assault rifle, E-11 variants. SW
9–12+2Heavy burst. 4-way split possible. Suppression fully effective.Heavy blaster rifle, support weapon.
13–16+2Threshold to Sweeping Fire across contiguous hexes.Light repeating blaster, squad automatic.
17–24+3Sustained suppression on multiple hexes. Crewed weapon territory.Heavy repeating blaster, tripod-mounted.
25–49+3Area-denial weapon. Single shooter holds a corridor.Heavy repeater, vehicle pintle mount.
50–99+4Vehicle-scale weapon. Can suppress whole intersection.Speeder-mounted blaster, AT-PT turret.
100++5Walker / starship anti-personnel mount. Owns the battlefield.AT-AT chin gun, Z-95 nose guns.

Recoil (Rcl) — Thresholds & Effects

Rcl Hits Formula at Margin m Tactical Implication Examples
1(m ÷ 1) + 1 = m + 1 hitsEvery margin point = one more hit. Lasers and blasters dominate burst combat at Rcl 1.SW Blasters (all), laser rifles, ion weapons.
2(m ÷ 2) + 1 hitsMargin 2 = 2 hits; margin 4 = 3 hits. Standard burst weapon.Blaster carbine, light slugthrower, energy carbine.
3(m ÷ 3) + 1 hitsMargin 6 = 3 hits. Needs decent margin to multi-hit.Heavy rifle, slug machine pistol, AR.
4(m ÷ 4) + 1 hitsMargin 8 = 3 hits. Difficult to land multiples — single-shot territory unless braced.Heavy slugthrower, vehicle-scale autocannon.
5+(m ÷ 5+) + 1 hitsEffectively single-shot unless braced. Heavy weapon needs tripod or mount.Anti-vehicle weapon, fixed-position cannon.

Bracing & Rcl. Two-handed bracing, kneeling/prone position with weapon supported, or a bipod reduces a weapon’s Rcl by 1 (minimum Rcl 1). A Rcl 2 carbine fired prone becomes Rcl 1 — effectively doubling the hits at any given margin. This is why braced bursts are so dangerous: a margin-4 prone burst from a Rcl 2 weapon lands 5 hits (Rcl 1) vs only 3 hits unbraced.

Range — Combat Bands & Thresholds

Range Penalty Combat Band Mechanics & Considerations
0–2 hex0Point blank / close combatClose-combat penalties apply. Bulk penalty to skill. Most defenses available.
3–5 hex−2 to −3Brawling distanceIndoor / cramped fight. Pistols ideal. Long arms penalised by Bulk if used close.
7–10 hex−3 to −4Room-to-roomIndoor combat default. Burst fire devastating. Cover is everything.
15–20 hex−5 to −6Hallway / open roomSniper unlikely; carbines and rifles equal here. Most blaster engagements settle here.
30–50 m−7 to −8Outdoor close engagementAim becomes essential. Pistol effectiveness drops sharply past 30 m.
70–100 m−9 to −10Medium engagementRifle territory. Multi-turn Aim recommended. Many handguns approach 1/2D range here.
150–200 m−11 to −12Long engagementCarbine 1/2D often passed — damage halved. Sniper territory begins.
300–500 m−13 to −14Sniper engagementScope + brace + Aim cap required to hit. Most weapons past 1/2D.
700–1000 m−15 to −16Extreme long rangeSpecialised sniper / heavy weapon only. Most blasters at Max range here.
Beyond MaxAuto-missOut of reachProjectile cannot physically arrive. Choose a weapon with longer reach.

1/2D and Max Range — Damage Thresholds

Threshold Effect Notes
Inside 1/2DDamage rolls normallyFull effect. Weapon’s listed damage applies.
Past 1/2DDamage halved (round down)To-hit unaffected. The bolt/bullet reaches but with reduced energy.
Inside MaxShot can hitApply standard range penalty.
Past MaxAuto-missCannot reach. Choose another weapon or move closer.

Worked Example. Blaster carbine: 3d burning, 1/2D 500 m, Max 1500 m. At 200 m: 3d burn (inside 1/2D). At 600 m: 3d burn ÷ 2 = ~5 damage average (past 1/2D, halved). At 1600 m: auto-miss (past Max). Most blaster fights settle inside 100 m because damage halving past 1/2D matters more than the −2 to skill from extra distance.

Combined Reference — Quick Threshold Lookup

Value RoF Effect Rcl Effect Range Band
1Single shot onlyEvery margin = +1 hit0-2 hex (no penalty)
2Small burst(m÷2)+1 hits3 hex (−2)
33-round burst(m÷3)+1 hits5 hex (−3)
4Split-burst threshold(m÷4)+1 hits — single-shot likely10 hex (−4)
5Suppression Fire unlocks  +1 hit bonus(m÷5)+1 hits — braced only15 hex (−5)
6–8Tactical burst, 3-way split, +1 hit bonus—20-50 m (−6 to −8)
9–12Heavy burst, 4-way split, +2 hit bonus—70-200 m (−9 to −12)
13–16Sweeping Fire unlocks, +2 hit bonus—500-700 m (−14 to −15)
17–49Area denial weapon, +3 hit bonus——
50–99Vehicle weapon, +4 hit bonus——
100+Starship / walker mount, +5 hit bonus——
Document III

Damage, Wounds & Recovery

Damage types · hit locations · crippling · criticals · recovery

Overview

What this covers. What happens after a hit lands — damage types, penetration vs armour, hit locations, wounds, shock, crippling, criticals, and healing.

How to use it. This is where the armour-divisor and split-DR system pays off; read the Damage Types chapter first, then use the rest as a lookup.

Part I

Damage Types

The nine types and what each does · armour divisors · the Star Wars set

Damage in GURPS is classified by type, which determines its wound multiplier, how it interacts with Damage Resistance (armour), what locations it can hit, and what special effects it triggers. A blaster bolt and a sword thrust may both roll 2d damage, but the bolt is tight-beam burning and the sword is impaling — they wound very differently. Nine damage types cover effectively all weapons in the Star Wars campaign.

Type Abbr Wound Mult Typical Sources Key Effects
Crushingcr×1Clubs, fists, slams, falls, mauls, electrostaffs (cinematic), thrown rocks, blunt forceCauses knockback (1 hex per damage − HP/2). Strangle / neck-snap at ×2 to Neck.
Cuttingcut×1.5Swords, axes, vibroblades on swing, vibro-daggers on swing, claws, broken glassSevers limbs at the crippling threshold (special: cutting cripple = severance). ×2 to Neck (sever).
Impalingimp×2Spears, arrows, bolts, sword thrusts, vibroblade thrusts, vibro-dagger thrusts, vibrospears, lightsabers SWHits Eye, Vitals, Heart, Spine, NeckV/A, Joints. ×3 to Vitals/Heart. Limited at limbs (×1 cap).
Piercing (small)pi-×0.5Holdout slugthrowers, small needles, civilian sidearms, dart gunsHalf wound multiplier. Hits Vitals normally (rare ×3). Loses to most armour.
Piercing (standard)pi×1Standard slugthrowers, slugpistols, hunting rifles, projectile weaponsNormal wound mult. Hits Vitals at ×3. Subject to penetration math vs DR.
Piercing (large)pi+×1.5Heavy slugthrowers, rifle rounds, anti-personnel rounds×1.5 wound mult. Hits Vitals at ×3. Limb damage capped at ×1.
Piercing (huge)pi++×2Anti-vehicle rounds, sniper anti-materiel rifles, autocannon shells×2 wound mult. Limb damage capped at ×1.
Burningburn×1Fire, flamethrowers, lava, plasma, sun exposure, electrical arcs, ion (special)Sets things alight. Damages gear. Eye protection useless against bright burn.
Tight-Beam Burningtib-burn×1Lasers, blaster bolts SW, ion weapons (vs droids), focused energyPenetrates DR (some weapons have AD 2). Hits Eye / Vitals / Heart (qualifies like imp/pi). Sets fires.
Corrosioncor×1Acid, certain alien venoms, droid acid jets, chemical burnsDamages and degrades armour (−1 DR per 5 damage). Eye blindness on Face hits.
Toxictox×1Poisons, radiation, certain alien venoms, biological agentsBypasses DR partially. HT roll to resist. Often delayed onset.
FatiguefatFP onlyStun bolts SW, exhaustion, sleep gas, electroshock prods, certain Force powersDamages FP rather than HP. Knock unconscious at FP ≤ −1× FP max. No permanent injury.

Crushing & Cutting
