Crushing. The default unarmed and blunt-weapon damage type. Wound multiplier ×1. Crushing damage does not impale or sever — it bruises, breaks bones, ruptures organs by impact. Knockback applies (Doc I cheatsheet HP/HT section): foe pushed back 1 hex per full (damage − HP/2). Special location effects: Skull ×4 like all penetrating, Neck ×1.5 (snap/strangle), Vitals are only damaged by crushing through the rib cage if the damage is high enough to count as a major wound. Crushing damage from a male groin hit gives ×2 shock penalty (cap −8) per the cheatsheet. Cannot hit Eye, NeckV/A, Vitals, Heart, or Joints (those locations require penetration types).

Cutting. Wound multiplier ×1.5. Severs at the cripple threshold — cutting damage that meets or exceeds a location’s crippling threshold doesn’t just cripple, it severs. An arm crippled at HP/2 by cutting damage is amputated cleanly, no longer attached. Neck cut: ×2 (sever the head). Cutting cannot hit Eye, Vitals, or Heart — too small a target for a swung blade. Cutting hits NeckV/A at ×1.5 (bleed-out follows).

Impaling & Piercing

Impaling. Wound multiplier ×2. The signature wound type for thrust weapons and arrows. Penetrates the body deeply: hits Eye (×4), Vitals (×3), Heart (×3 + auto major wound), Spine (cripple both legs/arms), NeckV/A (×1.5 + bleeding). Critical at long impalement: a spear thrust to the Vitals is the most efficient kill in the game. Limb wound multiplier is capped at ×1 (excess imp damage to a limb is lost — it doesn’t transfer to torso). Impaling cripples but doesn’t sever (unlike cutting).

Piercing (Four Tiers). Modern projectile weapons use one of four piercing tiers depending on caliber. pi- (small) is ×0.5 — holdout slugs, dart needles. pi (standard) is ×1 — slugpistols, hunting rifles. pi+ (large) is ×1.5 — heavy rifles. pi++ (huge) is ×2 — autocannon, anti-materiel. All four can hit Eye, Vitals, Heart, Spine, NeckV/A, and Joints (penetration types). Limb wound multiplier is capped at ×1 for pi+ and pi++ (rifle rounds don’t multiply damage on extremities). Vitals multiplier scales: pi/pi+/pi++/imp all reach ×3 to Vitals.

Burning & Tight-Beam Burning

Burning. Wound multiplier ×1. Fire, flame projection, plasma, electrical arc, lava, certain Force powers. Sets adjacent materials alight on a sufficient burst (GM call; typically 2+ damage onto flammable cloth/fur). Damages equipment alongside the wielder. Cannot hit Eye, Vitals, Heart unless the burn is very specific (laser eye damage handled as tib-burn instead). Burn damage from a Face hit can blind (HT roll vs FP penalty).

Tight-Beam Burning. SW Wound multiplier ×1. The signature damage type of laser weapons and blaster bolts. Differs from regular burning in that the beam is a focused pulse — small enough to qualify as a penetration type for hit-location purposes. Tib-burn hits Eye (×4), Vitals (×3), Heart (×3 + auto major wound), Spine, NeckV/A, Joints — the same locations available to impaling. Standard blaster bolts have Armour Divisor 1 against worn armour — armour applies its full DR, so a suit rated to stop a bolt does so. Heavy and military-grade blasters carry AD 2 as their armour-defeating premium, and the heaviest weapons more. (Beam-reflective and reflec coatings are already priced into each armour’s rated DR.) Sets fires on hit; cauterises wound channels (reduces follow-on bleeding, GM call).

Corrosion & Toxic

Corrosion. Wound multiplier ×1. Acid and chemical burns. Special: every 5 damage that penetrates DR reduces the target’s armour DR by 1 permanently at that location. On Face: HT roll or blinded that side. Continuing damage from acid pooled on the target: 1 HP per turn for 1d turns (or until cleaned off, GM call).

Toxic. Wound multiplier ×1. Poison, radiation, biological agents. Bypasses physical DR partially (sealed armour stops it; cloth doesn’t). HT roll to resist (the toxin’s listed resistance number). On failure, takes listed damage, possibly delayed onset. Multiple-stage poisons: each stage is its own HT roll separated by the listed time interval. Antidote skill rolls or specific items can negate stages.

Fatigue (Stun) Damage SW

Fatigue / fat. Damages FP rather than HP. The signature damage type of stun bolts, electroshock weapons, sleep gas, certain Force powers, and exhaustion effects. HT roll to resist (often Stun Bolt has a fixed resistance, e.g. HT-3). Damage is applied to FP; when FP reaches 0 the target is at risk of unconsciousness (see Part V); when FP reaches −1×FP max the target is unconscious. Fatigue damage does not cause permanent injury — wakeup happens after 15 minutes of rest, with 1 FP recovered per 10 minutes after that. Stun weapons are the legal, non-lethal default in most polities.

Stun Bolts vs Armour. SW Stun bolts penetrate cloth and most light armour normally but are stopped by hardened sealed armour. The GM may rule that a target in sealed armour rolls HT to resist with the armour’s DR added to the resistance (effectively immunity to most stun bolts).

Star Wars Damage-Type Notes

SWLightsaber Damage. Lightsabers deal imp + burn. Treat as impaling for hit-location purposes (qualifies for Eye, Vitals, Heart). The ”burn” portion means the wound is cauterised — no bleeding HP loss, no NeckV/A bleed-out HT rolls, but the cripple/sever is permanent. Lightsabers have Armour Divisor ∞ against most non-cortosis armour (cuts through nearly all DR). See Doc IV for blade-on-blade Lightsaber interactions.

SWBlaster Bolts. Standard blaster bolts: tib-burn, Armour Divisor 1 — worn armour applies its full DR, so most armour turns or survives an average standard bolt. Heavy blaster variants: tib-burn AD 2; the heaviest support weapons AD 3. The intent: a soldier in real armour shrugs off the first sidearm shot, while heavy weapons still punch through. Hardened combat armour (DR 12+) ignores small-arms blaster fire entirely.

SWIon Weapons. Ion blasters / carbines / cannons do ion damage — treated as tib-burn against droids with AD 5 (devastating to mechanical targets), and fat damage (or 0) against organic targets. Ion is the standard anti-droid armament. Some droids have ion-shielded systems that resist with HT.

SWSonic Disruptors. Sonic weapons deal a unique damage type that bypasses most DR and reduces the target’s damage threshold. Treat as tib-burn for hit-location purposes; reduces effective HP/2 (Major Wound threshold) by 5. Often outlawed. Cannot fire on full-auto.

SWDisruptor Rifles. Disruptor weapons (distinct from sonic disruptors) destroy matter at the molecular level. Treat as imp damage with Armour Divisor 10 and ×4 wound multiplier to organic targets. Often illegal. Rare. Inflicts horrifying wounds; the GM may rule that disruptor damage destroys hit locations outright at much lower thresholds.

SWCarbonite Weapons. Carbonite-projecting weapons fire a blast of solidifying liquid carbonite. Treat as 1d crushing damage (impact of the carbonite glob) plus a freezing effect: target makes HT roll or is immobilised for 1d turns (cannot move from current hex, may still act with limbs not encased). Full carbonite freezing is a planned process requiring specialised equipment, not a combat action.

Part II

Penetration
& Armour

DR · armour divisors · blunt trauma · layered armour · armour categories

Between rolled damage and applied injury sits armour. The defender’s Damage Resistance (DR) is subtracted from each hit’s damage. What’s left — the penetrating damage — multiplies by the damage type’s wound multiplier to determine actual HP loss. The math has a few wrinkles: armour divisors increase penetration against specific armour types, blunt trauma transfers some force through armour that stopped a bullet, and layered armour stacks DR with diminishing returns. This part covers the rules; specific armour items belong in the equipment document.

Armour Category Typical DR Notes
No armour (clothing, robes)0Cosmetic only. Subject to full damage.
Padded / soft armour1-2Flexible. Transmits blunt trauma. Useless against most energy weapons.
Light armour3-6Civilian security, light patrol. Mostly rigid, some flexible sections.
Medium armour7-10Standard military issue. Defeats sidearms; vulnerable to heavy weapons and bursts.
Heavy armour11-16Shock troopers, special forces. Rigid plate; no blunt trauma transmission.
Specialised / elite armour17-24High-end combat plate. Defeats most personal weapons.
Powered armour20-30+Servo-assisted. Provides ST bonus and life support; heavy.
Vehicle / starship armour40+Defeats nearly all personal weapons. Anti-vehicle weapons required.

The Penetration Calculation

Standard Formula. Penetrating damage = damage rolled − DR. If DR ≥ damage rolled, no penetration: injury is 0 (subject to blunt trauma below). If penetration is positive, multiply by the damage type’s wound mult to get final HP injury. Example: a tib-burn bolt rolls 8 damage, target wears medium armour DR 6 at the hit location. Penetration = 8 − 6 = 2. Tib-burn ×1 = 2 HP injury.

Armour Divisor. Some weapons defeat armour better than others. Armour Divisor (AD) is a multiplier on the weapon’s penetration: effective DR = target DR ÷ AD. A heavy blaster with AD 2 facing DR 6 armour treats it as DR 3 (halving the armour); a standard blaster at AD 1 faces the full DR 6. AD 5 cuts DR to a fifth. Lightsabers use high divisors — swing AD 5, thrust AD 10 — enough to ignore ordinary armour without being literally infinite, so saber-resistant metals can still bite. AD doesn’t change the rolled damage — only the armour value it must overcome.

Worked Example. A bolt rolls 8 damage. The target wears medium armour (DR 6) at the hit location. Weapon has AD 2. Effective DR = 6 ÷ 2 = 3. Penetration = 8 − 3 = 5. Tib-burn ×1 = 5 HP injury. If the bolt had been targeted to Vitals: ×3 multiplier = 15 HP injury — almost certainly major-wound territory.

Blunt Trauma

Blunt Trauma. When flexible armour (mesh, cloth, leather, lighter scale) stops a hit, some force still transfers through. Every 5 points of damage the armour stops causes 1 HP of crushing blunt trauma damage (round down). The blunt trauma damage hits the target’s HP directly, without DR — the armour stopped the bolt but bruised the wearer. Rigid armour (steel plate, heavy ceramic, dense metal alloys) does not transmit blunt trauma — its rigidity disperses the impact. RAW: blunt trauma only applies to flexible armour (DR with no rigid backing).

Blunt Trauma Worked Example. A blaster bolt rolling 12 damage hits a target wearing flexible vest DR 8. Pen = 12 − 8 = 4, tib-burn ×1 = 4 HP. Blunt trauma not normally applied to energy weapons (the burn doesn’t transmit as impact). A slug rifle bullet (pi+) rolling 12 damage hits same vest: 12 − 8 = 4 penetration, ×1.5 = 6 HP injury. Plus 8/5 = 1 HP of blunt trauma from the stopped portion = 7 total HP. Blunt trauma matters most against high-DR-but-flexible armour like ceramic mesh undersuits.

Knockback — Melee & Kinetic Shove

Knockback. Crushing attacks — and to a lesser degree cutting and large kinetic hits — don’t just wound, they shove. For every full multiple of (the target’s ST − 2) in a single blow’s basic damage, the target is knocked back one metre. So a ST 10 target (ST−2 = 8) struck for 18 points is pushed 2 metres; 36 points pushes 4. Crushing applies in full; cutting knocks back at half; impaling and piercing do not knock back (they punch through rather than shove). Knockback is measured from the basic damage before the wounding multiplier, and the push happens whether or not the blow penetrates DR — a heavy hit on good armour still rocks you.

Falling Down. A target knocked back one metre stays standing; knocked back two or more metres, they must roll against the best of DX, Acrobatics, or Judo or fall prone (−1 per metre beyond the first to that roll). A character knocked into a wall or hard object takes the “stopped” distance as extra crushing damage (1 HP per metre not travelled, GM call). Knockback is the cinematic answer to “why did the Wookiee just punt that Gamorrean across the cantina.”

In play. The companion sheet’s Log an Incoming Hit tool computes knockback automatically for crushing and cutting hits and reminds you of the DX/Acrobatics/Judo roll, alongside any blunt trauma through flexible armour.

Heavy Impact & Concussion SW

HouseHeavy Impact (cannon-class). The largest weapons — assault and blaster cannons, plasma lances, repeating heavy blasters, rockets and missiles — do not merely burn; they land like a runaway speeder. A weapon tagged Heavy Impact imparts knockback as if it were crushing (use its basic damage, before any wounding or ×N multiplier — see Knockback above), even though it does burn or explosive damage, and even when DR stops the penetrating damage entirely. A cannon bolt that splashes off heavy plate still throws its target off their feet.

HouseConcussion Through Plate. Heavy Impact also drives a shockwave through rigid armour — the one case where rigid plate transmits blunt trauma. When rigid armour (or beskar) stops a Heavy-Impact hit, the wearer still takes 1 HP of crushing blunt trauma per full 10 points the armour stopped (round down): the plate holds, the ribs do not. Ordinary weapons still bounce off rigid plate with no blunt trauma; only Heavy-Impact and crushing attacks concuss through it. Against beskar — immune to the bolt itself — this and raw knockback are often the only things a heavy weapon can do, and they are enough to matter.

Worked Example — Cannon vs Plate. A blaster cannon (6d×3, basic 6d ≈ 21) hits a trooper in heavy battle armour (DR 22) who has ST 11. The bolt: 6d×3 ≈ 63, −22 DR = 41 × burn — lethal if it lands clean. But suppose the trooper instead wears DR 70-equivalent plate and the bolt is stopped: he still suffers knockback of 21 ÷ (ST−2 = 9) ≈ 2 metres (roll vs DX/Acro/Judo or go prone) and 21÷10 = 2 HP concussion through the plate. Nobody walks through cannon fire untouched.

Layered Armour

Layering. A character can wear multiple armour pieces stacked. Two pieces of armour with DR 6 and DR 4 give combined DR 10. There’s no inherent stacking limit but practical limits — encumbrance and movement penalties — apply. The GM may rule that stacked rigid armour interferes with each layer’s effectiveness (cumbersome, hot, restrictive). Most combat armour is modular: rigid plate over a flexible body suit, with optional ablative layers.

Armour by Hit Location. Most armour suites provide DR per location separately. Torso plate often has higher DR than limb covers; helmets vary widely; vital extremities (hands, eyes) are frequently uncovered. When a shot connects, check the DR of the specific hit location, not a single body DR. Targeted Eye shots ignore most armour entirely — eyes are typically uncovered or shielded by transparent material with minimal DR.

Per-Location DR Reference (energy/kinetic). Armour-divisor is uniform across the suit; DR varies by where the plate is thickest. Torso and head take the full rating; arms and legs run a little lower; hands and feet lower still; a dash means that location is uncovered by that suit and uses only innate or layered DR. These are the same values the character sheet applies per hit location.
ArmourHeadTorsoArmsHandsLegsFeet
Heavy clothing / robes—1/11/1—1/1—
Blast vest—8/6————
Combat jumpsuit—6/55/4—5/4—
Mesh armour—28/622/5—22/5—
Reflec armour—40/032/0—32/0—
Light battle armour—12/1210/10—10/10—
Battle armour18/1818/1816/1613/1316/1613/13
Republic trooper armour20/1820/1818/1614/1318/1614/13
Sith trooper armour20/1820/1818/1614/1318/1614/13
Heavy battle armour25/2525/2525/2521/2125/2521/21
Powered battle armour30/3030/3030/3026/2630/3026/26
Neo-Crusader armour (base) — DR 40 · AD 2.5 · ½ vs cutting · ENC Heavy40/4040/4040/4040/4040/4040/40
Refined beskar plate (vitals & skull) — DR 50 · DD ÷10 vs all50/5050/50————
Jedi combat robes—4/34/3—4/3—
Czerka security armour—14/1412/12—12/12—

Edge Cases & Special Penetration

Chinks in Armour. A Targeted Attack at −10 to skill can find a ”chink” — a joint, seam, eye slit, or weak point in armour. On success, the attack treats the armour as having DR 0 (or DR 1 for very rugged armour). High-skill characters use chinks to defeat otherwise impenetrable armour. Lightsaber wielders rarely need to bother — swing AD 5 / thrust AD 10 already negates most armour, though a chink is still the only personal-weapon route through beskar.

Sealed vs Unsealed Armour. Sealed armour (full life-support, sealed helmets) prevents toxic/corrosive damage from reaching the wearer through gas or splash. Unsealed leaves gaps. Most military-grade armour is sealed (vacuum-rated). Sealed armour DR is full; unsealed reduces it by 2-4 against corrosive splash or gas. Most direct-fire weapons ignore this distinction.

Energy vs Kinetic Distinction. Some armours are tuned for one damage class. SW Reflective / mirror-coated armour: halves the AD of blaster fire. Beam-resistant nano-weave: halves laser AD. Cortosis-weave: negates the lightsaber’s armour divisor and may short out the saber for 1d turns. Specific named armours and their interactions belong in the equipment document.

Force-Touched Armour. SW Some armour and certain robes are imbued with Force resistance — DR vs Force-power damage (Force Lightning, Force Push impact, etc.). This is a Doc IV mechanic; the armour’s DR is rated separately for physical and Force damage.

Damage to Armour

Armour Wear. Armour absorbs damage; over time it accumulates wear. The GM tracks armour HP per piece — rigid plate has higher HP than flexible layers. Hits exceeding the armour’s DR by more than 5 may damage the armour (1 DR temporarily lost until repaired). Critical hits damage armour as a default effect. Corrosion permanently reduces DR (1 DR per 5 corrosive damage absorbed).

Repair. Armoury skill at the appropriate specialisation (Body Armour). Repair time depends on damage severity and armour complexity: simple armour 1-4 hours, complex armour 8-24 hours. Specific parts and cost belong in the equipment document.

Part III

Hit Locations

The full table · sub-locations · quadrupeds & droids · the rolling logic

A ranged or melee attack that hits a non-Targeted location rolls 3d to determine where it landed. The same 3d distribution puts most hits on the torso (rolls 9-10) where the largest mass is, and progressively less common hits to limbs, head, and extremities. Targeted Attacks bypass this roll entirely — pay the to-hit penalty and aim. The compressed Hit Location & Crippling table on the Combat Cheatsheet is the quick reference; this part has the full text, sub-locations, and non-humanoid variations.

3d Roll Location To-Hit Penalty Damage Types Allowed Notes
3–4Skull / Brain−7All damage typesBuilt-in DR 2. ×4 wound mult vs penetrating damage. Major wound HT at −10. Death likely.
5Face−5All damage typesCrit Hit Table reads one row better. Cr → knockdown HT at −5. Cor → blinding.
—Eye (target only)−9imp / pi / tib-burn onlyBypasses skull DR. ×4 wound mult. Cripple at HP/10. Both eyes = blind.
6–7Right Leg−2All; imp/pi+/pi++ ×1 capCripple at HP/2. Cutting cripple = severance. Move drops to 1, prone DX to avoid.
8Right Arm−2All; imp/pi+/pi++ ×1 capCripple at HP/2. Drop everything held. No attacks with that limb.
9–10Torso0All — standard multsDefault impact. Targeted attacks within Torso: Vitals at −3, Heart at −5.
—Vitals (target only)−3imp / pi / tib-burn only×3 wound mult. Penetrating dmg = major wound. Cannot be crippled but can kill outright.
—Heart (target only)−5imp / pi / tib-burn onlyTreat as Vitals + auto major wound on any penetration. Death very likely.
11Groin−3All damage typesCut / pi / imp = auto major wound. Stun. Males: cr ×2 shock (cap −8).
—Spine (target only)−8cr / cut / imp / pi / tib-burnCripple both legs (lower) or both arms (upper). Severance = paralysis below.
12Left Arm−2(As Right Arm)Cripple at HP/2.
13–14Left Leg−2(As Right Leg)Cripple at HP/2.
15Hand−4All; imp/pi+/pi++ ×1 capCripple at HP/3. Drop everything held; cannot grasp.
—Neck Vein/Artery (target only)−8cut / imp / pi / tib-burn×1.5 damage. HT each turn vs bleeding or lose 1 HP. Lightsabers cauterise.
16Foot−4All; imp/pi+/pi++ ×1 capCripple at HP/3. Move halved; no footwork defenses.
—Arm / Leg Joint (target only)−7cr / cut / imp / pi / tib-burnCripple at HP/4 (half normal limb). Joint locks.
17–18Neck−5All; cr ×1.5 (snap), cut ×2 (sever)Severance ends combat. Major wound → HT or stun.

The Rolling Logic

When to Roll. Roll 3d for hit location whenever an attack succeeds without a Targeted Attack modifier. The 3d bell curve concentrates hits on the torso (rolls 9-10 are most common, ~25% of hits). Targeted Attacks skip this roll — the attacker chose the location and paid the penalty.

Reading the Table. Roll on the table for the random location. If the location requires a damage type the attack doesn’t deliver (e.g. crushing on the Eye row), apply the attack to the parent location instead (the Eye becomes Face; the Vitals becomes Torso). The damage-type restriction column tells you what’s valid.

Sub-Locations. Some locations are sub-locations — Eye (within Face), Vitals/Heart (within Torso), Spine (often via Groin or Torso depending on angle), NeckV/A (within Neck), Joints (within Arm/Leg). Sub-locations are target-only: you cannot randomly roll into them. Choose to Targeted-Attack the sub-location to access it.

Adjusting for Posture & Angle

Posture Adjustment. When the target is in an unusual posture, the GM may shift the hit-location roll. Prone target hit by a ranged attack: legs and feet are more exposed than torso; the GM may apply a +2 to the roll (shifting toward leg/foot hits) or rule that the attacker chose. Sitting/crouching targets shift away from torso slightly. Kneeling targets are tightly compacted — close to standard.

From Above / Below. Attacking from above shifts hits toward Skull/Face/Torso (roll 3d, treat torso rolls as Skull on 9 result, etc. — GM call). Attacking from below shifts toward Groin/Legs. Heavy stairs combat and aerial bombardment use these adjustments.

From Side / Behind. Side hits work normally on the 3d table — the target’s lateral mass is what’s exposed. Hits from behind on an unaware target skip active defense entirely (Doc I); the location roll is still the standard 3d. Rear-hit Vitals shots come from the back through the spine.

Non-Humanoid Hit Locations

Quadrupeds. Animals with four legs use a modified 3d table: 3-4 Skull, 5 Face, 6-7 Right Foreleg, 8 Right Hind Leg, 9-10 Torso, 11 Underbelly, 12 Left Hind Leg, 13-14 Left Foreleg, 15 Neck/Tail, 16 Spine, 17-18 Heart/Vitals (target only). Crippling thresholds same as humanoid limb (HP/2). Underbelly is often more vulnerable — many quadrupeds have weaker armour there.

Multi-Limbed Species. SW Multi-armed sapient species: roll on the standard table, then if Arm comes up roll 1d to determine which of the additional arms. Cripple thresholds apply per limb. Multi-armed species may act with multiple weapons in parallel (see Doc IV).

Flying Creatures. Add Wing locations to the 3d roll table — typically replacing Arm slots. A winged combatant uses wings for both movement and (in some species) grappling. Crippling a wing grounds the creature; falling damage may then apply (Part VIII).

Droid Hit Locations SW

Droids use a modified hit location table reflecting mechanical anatomy. The default table below applies to bipedal humanoid-form droids (war droids, security droids, battle droids, protocol droids, assassin droids); GM-adjusted versions for wheeled (astromech), tracked, hovering (probe), or quadruped chassis described in the notes below.

3d Roll Location To-Hit Penalty Damage Types Allowed Notes
3–4Sensor Head−7All damage typesBuilt-in housing DR (typically 2). ×4 wound mult on penetration. Major fault = control disruption. Shutdown / death likely on penetration.
5Faceplate / Photoreceptors−5All damage typesCrit Hit Table reads one row better. Corrosion can blind sensors. Major wound → HT (system stability) at −5 or system fault.
—Primary Optics (target only)−9imp / pi / tib-burn onlyCripple at HP/10. ×4 wound mult, no DR. Sensor-blind that side. Both optics destroyed = full sensor blindness.
6–7Right Manipulator (Arm)−2All; imp/pi+/pi++ ×1 capCripple HP/2. Drop everything held. No attack / grasp / manipulate. Two-handed weapons unusable.
8Right Weapon Mount−2All; pi+/pi++ ×1 capCripple HP/2. Cannot fire that integrated weapon system. Equivalent to crippled arm for combat purposes.
9–10Chassis (Torso)0All — standard multsDefault impact. Targeted attacks within Chassis: Power Core at −3.
—Power Core (target only)−3imp / pi / tib-burn only×3 wound mult. Penetration = critical system fault (system stability HT or shutdown). Cannot be ”crippled” — can outright shutdown / destroy droid.
11Locomotion Drive−4All; pi+/pi++ ×1 capCripple HP/3. Movement halved or stopped entirely. For wheeled droids: drive wheels seize. For legged: stride locks.
—Servo / Joint (target only)−7cr / cut / imp / pi / tib-burnCripple HP/4. Joint locks. Effects per parent limb (manipulator or locomotion).
12Left Weapon Mount−2(as Right)Cripple HP/2.
13–14Left Manipulator (Arm)−2(as Right)Cripple HP/2.
15Manipulator Tool / Grip−4All; pi+/pi++ ×1 capCripple HP/3. Drop everything held; cannot grasp. Equivalent to crippled hand.
—Power Conduit (target only)−8cut / imp / pi / tib-burn×1.5 damage. HT (system stability) each turn or bleed 1 HP system power. Repair: Mechanics skill, not Physician.
16Servo Foot / Tread−4All; pi+/pi++ ×1 capCripple HP/3. Move halved. Balance penalty (−2 to stability rolls). For tracked droids: tread section severed.
—Comm / Sensor Array (target only)−8cr / cut / imp / pi / tib-burnCripple HP/4. Loss of long-range communication and coordination with other droids. Critical for networked droid units.
17–18Control Bus / Neck Joint−5All; cr ×1.5 (lock), cut ×2 (sever)Severance = total shutdown (head and torso separated). Major fault → HT or shutdown.

Droid Chassis Variations

Wheeled / Astromech Droids. Replace Locomotion Drive (roll 11) with Wheel/Tread Drive. No ”Servo Foot” (roll 16) — instead, second drive section or repulsor unit. Treadwell-class droids: 4-wheel chassis means each wheel can be targeted separately (roll 1d to determine which when crippling triggers).

Hovering / Repulsor Droids. Probe droids, certain medical droids: no legs. Replace Locomotion Drive and Servo Foot with two separate Repulsorlift Units. Crippling a repulsorlift unit halves Move; crippling both grounds the droid (it falls, taking falling damage by mass).

Quadruped War Droids. Add two extra leg locations to the table. Each leg crippled at HP/2 (more legs to lose, more functional with partial losses). Crippling two legs grounds the droid; crippling three immobilises entirely.

Multi-Limbed Droids. Some war droids have 4+ manipulators. Roll for limb hit (Right or Left Manipulator) then 1d to determine which specific limb if more than two are present. Crippling additional manipulators reduces capability progressively but doesn’t disable the droid.

Droid Immunities & Vulnerabilities SW

Universal Droid Traits. Droids do not take shock penalties (no nervous system) — apply damage but no per-turn skill penalty. Droids cannot be poisoned (tox damage = 0 unless specifically anti-droid). Droids ignore Mental Stun completely. Droids do not roll HT for organic Major Wounds — replace with ”system stability” check (effectively HT for game purposes, but it’s the chassis stability, not biological).

Ion Vulnerability. Ion weapons (Doc III Part I) treat droids as taking massive damage — armour divisor 5+, large damage multiplier. The standard anti-droid armament. Some droids have ion-shielded systems that resist with system-stability HT.

Electromagnetic Effects. Strong electromagnetic pulses (EMP) and certain Force powers may temporarily shut down droids without dealing HP damage. Roll system-stability vs the effect. Failure = droid offline for the duration.

Droid Crippling Recovery. Crippled droid locations don’t heal naturally — they require Mechanics skill repair (not Physician). Repair times vary by complexity (1-24 hours typical) and may need replacement parts. Some droids have self-repair routines that slowly restore HP (1 per day).

Droid HP Track — How It Differs HOUSE

HP Level Droid Effect
HP > ⅔ HPmaxFully operational. No penalties.
HP ≤ ⅓ HPmax — System StressMove and DX-based rolls at −1. Subsystem damage rolls become more frequent (see below).
HP ≤ 0 — InertDroid shuts down. No consciousness check — the droid is simply offline. Repair to ≥1 HP automatically restarts (no HT roll needed).
HP ≤ −1× HPmaxSignificant structural damage. Repair possible at Mechanics −2; requires workshop facility.
HP ≤ −2× HPmaxHeavy damage. Mechanics −4. Significant time investment.
HP ≤ −5× HPmaxEffectively destroyed for combat purposes. Salvage useful parts; rebuild requires majority new construction.
HP ≤ −10× HPmaxScrap. Not recoverable.

No Death Checks. Critically: droids do NOT roll death checks at successive HP multiples. A droid at −3×HPmax is heavily damaged but theoretically repairable — it doesn’t ”die” the way an organic does. The droid is simply inert. Restoration depends on time, parts, and a competent technician.

Salvage and Cannibalisation. A heavily damaged droid (−2× HPmax or beyond) is often more valuable as parts than as a repair candidate. Field commanders make a cost/time calculation: rebuild for hours-to-days, or scrap and use the parts elsewhere.

Subsystem Damage HOUSE

When a droid takes damage that penetrates DR but doesn’t cripple a location, the GM rolls 3d on the subsystem damage table for additional functional effects. This represents the kind of mechanical fault that doesn’t destroy the droid but degrades its capability. Roll once per significant hit (GM judgment — generally damage above ⅙ HPmax in one strike).

3d Subsystem Fault Effect (until repaired or timer expires)
3–4Sensor Flicker−2 to Per and ranged attacks for 1d turns.
5–6Comm FaultLong-range communication offline for 1d minutes. Cannot coordinate with networked droids.
7–8Power FlickerLose 1d Power Cell units (see below). If at 0 Power: −1 to all rolls for 1 turn.
9–10No specific subsystem effectJust the HP damage; no additional fault.
11–12Manipulator Stutter−1 DX with hands for 1d turns. Drop on a critical failure.
13–14Locomotion GlitchMove −1 for 1d turns. Fall prone on critical failure.
15–16Servo LockRandom joint locks for 1d turns. HT (system stability) to break free; otherwise that limb is rigid.
17–18Critical FaultHT (system stability) or shutdown until repaired by Mechanics roll. On success: −3 to all rolls for 1d turns.

Why This Matters. Subsystem damage means a droid taking blaster fire isn’t just losing HP — its sensors might flicker, its weapon arm might stutter, its comms might cut out. The fight feels more like fighting a malfunctioning machine than fighting a biological creature taking damage.

Power Cell Management HOUSE

Power Cells Replace FP. Droids have a Power Cell rating instead of FP. Combat operation drains roughly 1 Power per minute of active combat (so 10-30 minutes is a typical combat-day). Idle/standby drains negligibly. Small droids (mouse droid, protocol) typically have Power 10-20; medium droids (security, war) 20-40; heavy droids (battle, military) 40-80.

Low Power Thresholds. Power Cell ≤ ⅓ of max: low-power mode kicks in, −1 to all skills. Power Cell ≤ 0: emergency shutdown sequence triggers; HT (system stability) to delay by 1 minute or shutdown immediately. Below −1× max: deep shutdown, requires external recharge to restart.

Recharge. External power source recharges fully in 1 hour. Power cell swap: 15 minutes if spare available. Trickle-charge solar collectors: 1 unit per hour in sunlight. Field generators or portable power packs: 4-8 hours full charge. Cannot recharge without a working power input (Sensor Head or Chassis must be intact).

Heat & Overheating HOUSE

Heat Accumulation. Sustained heavy operation generates heat. The GM tracks Heat as a percentage of a droid’s heat capacity. Combat at full effort adds ~10% per minute. Sustained fire adds heat per burst. Most commercial droids have efficient passive cooling and rarely overheat; war droids and heavy combat droids overheat more readily.

Heat Effects. Heat 0–50%: no effect. Heat 50–75%: −1 to all skills (heat warning, sensor noise). Heat 75–100%: −2 to skills; HT (system stability) each minute or take 1d burn damage from internal overheating. Heat 100%: forced shutdown (recommended), or take 2d burn damage and continue at −3.

Cooling. 1 heat unit per minute of inactivity in normal temperature. Cold environments cool faster (additional +1 per minute in sub-zero). Active cooling (Mechanics roll to vent heat): 1d minutes per +1 heat shed. Damaged cooling system: heat builds permanently until repaired.

Hydraulic & Coolant Leaks — The Droid Bleeding Analog HOUSE

When Leaks Trigger. A droid taking cutting or impaling damage ≥ HP/3 to any limb or chassis location rolls HT (system stability) vs the damage. Failure: a hydraulic line, coolant line, or power conduit is severed.

Leak Effects by Type. Hydraulic leak: lose 1 manipulator-strength unit per turn; Move −1 per turn until sealed. Coolant leak: Heat gain doubled until sealed (system overheats far faster). Power conduit leak: rare. Lose 1 Power Cell unit per turn. GM rolls 1d to determine which type when leak triggers (1-3 hydraulic, 4-5 coolant, 6 power).

Sealing the Leak. Mechanics skill roll. Success: leak sealed, no further fluid loss. Failure: leak continues; another attempt may be made (no penalty stacking). Once sealed, refill the lost fluid: field refill 5 minutes per pool, workshop refill 1 hour for complete recheck and refill. Until refill, the droid operates at reduced capacity.

Lightsaber and Cauterisation. SW Lightsabers slice clean through hydraulic and power lines. The cut is too clean to seal in the field — full workshop repair required (or modular component replacement). This is why droids fear lightsaber wielders.

EMP & Electronic Warfare HOUSE

EMP Pulse. Electromagnetic pulses, ion grenades, and electronic disruption weapons affect droids without dealing HP damage. The droid rolls HT (system stability) vs the EMP strength rating. Failure: droid offline for the duration (1d turns to 1d minutes depending on EMP type). Success: droid suffers −2 to all rolls for 1d turns from system jitter.

Ion Damage Targeted Effects. Ion damage applied to a specific droid location causes additional disruption: ion to Sensor Head → blinded 1d turns. Ion to Manipulator → drop everything, locked 1d turns. Ion to Comm Array → silenced 1d minutes. Ion to Locomotion → immobile 1d turns. These effects stack on top of normal ion HP damage.

Faraday-Shielded Droids. Some military droids include EMP/ion shielding in their chassis. Treat as Damage Resistance 5+ vs ion damage specifically (in addition to normal DR), and HT at +5 vs EMP pulses. Adds significantly to droid cost; common only in high-value combat units.

Part IV

Wound Effects

Wound multipliers · major wounds · location-specific effects · bleeding · cauterisation

Penetrating damage becomes injury by applying the damage type’s wound multiplier, possibly modified by the hit location. The result tells you how much HP the target loses on this hit. The next questions are: is this a Major Wound? Does the location have a special effect? Is there bleeding? Did the target survive the strike?

Wound Type Trigger Effect
Normal WoundPenetrating damage with multiplier appliedHP loss. Shock penalty on next turn (−1 per HP, cap −4).
Major WoundSingle hit injury > HP/2HT roll or stun + knockdown. Crit fail: unconscious.
Major Wound to Head/FaceSingle hit injury > HP/2 to Skull, Face, EyeHT at −5 (Face) or −10 (Skull/Eye) or stun + knockdown.
Crippling WoundSingle hit injury ≥ HP/X to limbLocation crippled — useless until healed.
SeveranceCutting damage that meets cripple thresholdLocation amputated. Permanent without restoration.
DestructionDamage to a location ≥ 10× cripple thresholdPermanent destruction. Bionic / regrow / lost forever.
Bleeding WoundNeckV/A or significant cut/impHT each turn or lose 1 HP. Continues until first aid.
Cauterised Wound SWLightsaber, blaster bolt (sometimes), fireNo bleeding. Cripple/sever permanent at moment of injury.

Wound Multipliers in Detail

Standard Multipliers. By damage type: cr ×1, cut ×1.5, imp ×2, pi- ×0.5, pi ×1, pi+ ×1.5, pi++ ×2, burn ×1, tib-burn ×1, cor ×1, tox ×1. These apply to TORSO hits as baseline; other locations modify.

Location Modifiers (multiplicative). Skull/Brain: ×4 on penetrating damage (any type that penetrates the skull DR 2). Eye: ×4, ignores skull DR. Vitals/Heart: ×3 for imp / pi / pi+ / pi++ / tib-burn (no effect for crushing or cutting unless the GM rules massive impact). Neck: ×1.5 for cutting (and ×2 for cutting if explicitly severing) / ×1.5 for crushing (snap/strangle). NeckV/A: ×1.5 + bleeding effect. Limb cap: pi+ / pi++ / imp limited to ×1 (excess damage is lost).

Compounding Multipliers. Multipliers compound: a sword thrust (imp ×2) to the Vitals (×3) does 2 × 3 = 6× rolled damage. Wait — RAW is that you replace the base multiplier with the location’s multiplier (not multiply them). So Vitals + imp = ×3 (not ×6). Use the LARGER of the two multipliers. The location multiplier overrides the type multiplier in most cases. Reading the location entry in the table: the multiplier listed IS the final multiplier to use for that location.

Major Wounds

Major Wound Definition. A Major Wound is a single hit that inflicts injury greater than the target’s HP/2 in one strike. Calculate after applying DR, wound multiplier, and location adjustments — what hits the target’s HP track is what matters. A character with HP 12 takes a Major Wound on any single hit exceeding 6 HP.

Major Wound Effect. The target rolls HT immediately. Success: the wound hurts but the target keeps fighting (still suffers HP loss and any other effects). Failure: stunned (lose next turn, defenses −4, HT each turn to recover) AND if standing, also HT or knocked prone. Critical failure on the HT: unconscious. High Pain Threshold negates the HT roll — never stunned by major wound (still loses HP, still falls prone if HT fails for prone-specific reasons).

Location-Specific Major Wounds. Face: HT at −5 to avoid stun. Skull, Eye: HT at −10 (brain damage). Groin: pi/imp/cut causes auto major wound regardless of damage amount. Heart: any penetrating damage causes auto major wound. Vitals: penetrating damage = major wound. These location-specific MWs trigger even on low damage.

Crippling & Severance

Cripple Threshold. Each location has a fraction-of-HP threshold for crippling: Arm/Leg HP/2, Hand/Foot HP/3, Eye HP/10, Joint HP/4. When a single hit injury meets or exceeds the threshold, the location is crippled — useless until healed. A HP 12 character’s leg crippling at 6+ HP from one hit; hand at 4+; eye at 1+ (HP/10 = 1.2 → 1).

Crippling Effects. Crippled arm: drop everything held, no attack/parry/grapple with that limb, two-handed weapons unusable. Crippled leg: Move 1, fall prone (DX to avoid), cannot stand unaided, no kick. Crippled hand: drop held weapon, cannot grasp. Crippled foot: Move halved, −2 DX-based footwork defenses (no Acrobatic Dodge, no Retreat bonus). Crippled eye: blinded that side, lose monocular depth perception, −3 to ranged. Crippled joint: locks in place, effects per parent limb. Crippled spine: cripples both legs (lower spine) or both arms (upper).

Severance vs Crippling. Crippling means the limb is non-functional but still attached. Severance means the limb is gone — physically separated from the body. Only cutting damage crossing the cripple threshold causes severance (RAW). Crushing damage cripples but cannot sever. Impaling damage cripples but cannot sever a limb (it can sever the head with enough damage to the neck, since the spine is severable). Lightsaber damage SW cuts cleanly — treat as severance on cripple (consistent with on-screen behaviour).

Destruction. If a single hit’s injury exceeds the cripple threshold by 10× or more, the location is destroyed — permanently lost, even with later healing or surgery. A leg with HP/2 = 6 cripple threshold is destroyed at 60+ HP single-hit injury. Destruction requires prosthetic, regrowth (rare without Force healing), or doing without.

Bleeding

Bleeding Wounds. Certain hits cause active bleeding — HP loss continues each turn until first aid stops it. Bleeding triggers: hit to NeckV/A (cut/imp/pi/tib-burn), any cut damage exceeding 1/3 HP/limb (GM call). Bleeding rate: HT roll each turn; failure: lose 1 HP; success: no loss this turn. After 5 minutes of bleeding (combat-time bleeding lasts the engagement), passive shock and HP loss continue.

First Aid Stops Bleeding. First Aid skill roll (default IQ-based, sometimes Physician). Success: bleeding stopped, no further HP loss. Failure: bleeding continues; another attempt may be made. This is the standard battlefield ”patch them up” action.

Graduated Injury — Levels of Function Loss HOUSE RULE

Standard RAW treats a location as either healthy or crippled (with shock penalty next turn from the damage). For grittier feel, this house rule adds graduated impairment based on the largest single hit taken to a location relative to its cripple threshold. The impairment lasts until the location heals (return to full HP, or significant medical attention).

Single-Hit Injury Function Loss
Light (1 HP to ¼ of cripple threshold)Shock penalty next turn only. No lasting impairment.
Moderate (¼ to ½ of threshold)−1 to actions involving that location, lasting effect (see per-location table below).
Heavy (½ to ¾ of threshold)−2 to actions involving that location; reduced function.
Severe (¾ to threshold)−3; major function loss. One step short of crippling.
Crippled (at or beyond threshold)Per existing rules: location useless, drop weapons, Move 1, etc.

Tracking the Impairment. Use the LARGEST single hit to a location since combat began — not accumulated injury. If a foe takes multiple light leg hits, the leg is still only lightly impaired (largest single hit defines impairment level). If a later hit is heavier, the impairment level upgrades. Multiple separate moderate hits do not stack into a heavy impairment.

Healing the Impairment. Once the location’s accumulated HP injury heals back to less than the impairment level’s threshold, the impairment downgrades one step. Full HP recovery resets all impairments to zero. Bacta or kolto immersion may downgrade impairment by one step per day of immersion.

Per-Location Impairment Effects

Location Moderate (−1) Heavy (−2) Severe (−3)
Arm−1 to attack / parry / grapple with this arm−2 to attack / parry; −1 to swing damage with this arm−3 to attack / parry; −2 to swing damage; cannot fully extend (−1 reach with this arm)
Leg−1 to Move (e.g. Move 6 → 5)−2 to Move; no Retreat bonus when stepping with this leg; −1 to Acrobatic DodgeMove halved; HT−2 to avoid falling when sprinting; no Acrobatic Dodge or Sideslip/Slip
Hand−1 to DX-based skills using this hand (fine work, weapon attacks)−2 to DX with this hand; drop held items on critical failure of attack/defense−3 to DX; cannot use two-handed weapons; cannot operate fine controls (lockpicks, computers, syringes)
Foot−1 to Acrobatic Dodge / Sideslip / Slip with this foot leading−1 to Move; −2 to Acrobatic Dodge; no Retreat bonus−2 to Move; no Acrobatic Dodge; no footwork defenses at all (Doc I MA)
Eye(HP/10 threshold leaves little graduated range — use directly:) any injury short of cripple gives −2 to ranged with this eye, −1 to Per-vision——
Torso(Does not cripple — but reeling at HP/3 covers graduated function loss for torso wounds. Major Wound mechanic covers the rest.)——
Head (general)−1 to IQ / Per for 1d hours after major wound to head; concussion symptoms−2 to IQ / Per for 1d days; possible amnesia of immediate prior events−3 to IQ / Per; HT or fall unconscious; possible permanent IQ damage (GM call)
Joint−1 to actions per parent limb (HP/4 threshold so light range = 1 HP only)−2 per parent limb; joint stiffening (cannot fully extend)Joint locks intermittently; HT each round of activity or limb stutters (−2 DX one second)

Worked Example. A character with HP 12 takes a 4 HP hit to the leg from a vibroblade swing. Leg cripple threshold = HP/2 = 6. Hit is 4/6 = 67% of threshold, falling in the Heavy range (½ to ¾). Result: −2 to Move (Move 6 → 4), no Retreat bonus when stepping with this leg, −1 to Acrobatic Dodge. Plus the normal shock penalty next turn (−4, since 4 HP > 4 cap).

Interaction with Crippling. If the same character later takes another 4 HP hit to the same leg, the largest single hit is still 4 (not 8 — they don’t stack into crippling). Leg remains Heavy impaired. A third 4 HP hit doesn’t cripple either. Only a single hit of 6+ HP cripples per RAW. The graduated system is for impairment SHORT of crippling, not a path to crippling.

Why the House Rule. Pure RAW means a leg shot of 5 HP and a leg shot of 0 HP have the same functional effect (no impairment). This rule introduces realism: a 5 HP leg shot is meaningfully different from a graze. GMs preferring lighter book-keeping can ignore this rule and use only the crippled/not-crippled binary.

Lightsaber Cauterisation SW

Cauterisation Rule (Campaign Standard). Lightsaber wounds are cauterised by the plasma blade. The hit does its full damage and triggers all normal effects (major wound HT, crippling), but: no bleeding HP loss, and any cripple/sever effect is finalised at the moment of injury (no regrowth without Force healing or prosthetics). NeckV/A struck by lightsaber: ×1.5 damage as normal, but no per-turn bleeding HT. Vitals/Heart killed normally. Severance by lightsaber: the limb is permanently gone, neatly cut.

Blaster Bolt Cauterisation. Blaster bolts also cauterise to some extent, but less reliably than lightsabers. GM call. Standard: bolt wounds to NeckV/A bleed at half rate (HT each turn, but only lose 1 HP every 2 turns). Doesn’t apply to slugthrower wounds — those bleed normally.

Part V

HP, Shock
& Consciousness

The HP track · shock penalties · reeling · death checks · FP fatigue · stuns

HP represents the character’s physical reserve. Above HP/3 a healthy combatant fights without penalty; below HP/3 they’re reeling; at 0 or less they’re at risk of unconsciousness; far below 0 they’re dying. The HP track interacts with shock penalties on the next turn after damage, with consciousness checks each turn at 0 or below, and with death checks at successive HPmax multiples. Fatigue Points have a parallel track for stamina, stun damage, and Force-power costs.

HP Level Effect Notes
HP > ⅔ HPmaxHealthy; no penaltiesCombat normal.
HP ≤ ⅓ HPmax — ReelingMove and Dodge halved (round up)Skill unaffected. Persists until HP returns above ⅓.
HP ≤ 0 — Consciousness CheckHT each turn at start; fail = unconsciousSuccess = may still act. Crit fail = unconscious + die check (severe).
HP ≤ −1× HPmax — Death CheckHT or dieCrit fail on the HT = die instantly. Hard to Kill grants +N to roll.
HP ≤ −2× HPmax — Death CheckHT or dieSame.
HP ≤ −3× HPmax — Death CheckHT or dieSame.
HP ≤ −4× HPmax — Death CheckHT or dieSame.
HP ≤ −5× HPmax — Auto-DeadDies automaticallyNo save. No resuscitation under normal medicine.
HP ≤ −10× HPmax — Body DestroyedBody utterly destroyedEven Force-spirit / clone restoration unlikely.

Shock Penalty

Shock. On the turn AFTER taking HP damage, the wounded character suffers a shock penalty to all DX, IQ, and skill rolls. Penalty = −1 per HP lost on that turn, cap −4. So a character who takes 6 HP this turn suffers −4 next turn; one who takes 2 HP suffers −2 next turn. Brain damage (Skull / Eye / mental) caps at −8 instead of −4.

Fit & Very Fit (House Rule). By RAW, Fit and Very Fit give effective HT for HT rolls and let you recover FP twice as fast, but they do not raise your FP total. In this campaign they also add directly to the FP pool — Fit grants +1 FP and Very Fit +2 FP, on top of the faster recovery — so a fit character has more stamina to spend, not just quicker to refill. The companion sheet folds this into both the “Effective HT” and the FP lines of the Scaled Summary, and into the FP pool used in combat.

High Pain Threshold. High Pain Threshold (advantage) negates shock penalties entirely. The character feels the wound but it doesn’t impair function. Many Mandalorians and battle-hardened veterans have HPT; common in this campaign era.

Shock from Multiple Hits. Multiple hits in one turn: shock penalty is based on the SINGLE HARDEST HIT (per RAW), not the sum. A character taking three small hits each of 2 HP doesn’t suffer −6; they suffer −2 (the single largest hit was 2 HP). This is a quirk of the rule but it’s what RAW says — though some campaigns house-rule to sum the hits.

Reeling

Reeling Threshold. When current HP drops to HP ≤ ⅓ HPmax, the character is reeling. Move halved, Dodge halved (round up in both cases). Other skills unaffected — you can still shoot well, but you can’t dance well. Persists until HP returns above ⅓ by healing.

Tactical Implications. Reeling combatants struggle to retreat or chase. A reeling sniper still snipes effectively; a reeling brawler is in trouble. Reeling combined with leg crippling drops Move to 0 or 1.

Consciousness Checks

At 0 HP or Below. At the start of every turn while at HP ≤ 0, the character rolls HT. Success: may take a normal turn this round. Failure: unconscious; cannot act. Critical failure: unconscious AND begin checking for death (treat as if at the next HP multiplier).

Critical Success on Conscious Check. Adrenaline. Character recovers 1 HP at no cost. Combat continues. Rare but useful when at low HP.

Healing Out of 0 HP. First Aid or natural healing can lift the character above 0 HP, ending the per-turn consciousness checks. Once HP positive, the character is exhausted but functional.

Death Checks

Death Thresholds. At HP ≤ −1×HPmax (i.e. HP = −HPmax), roll HT or die. At −2×, −3×, −4× HPmax, roll HT again. Critical failure on any death check = die instantly. At HP ≤ −5×HPmax, automatic death; no further saves. At HP ≤ −10×HPmax, body is destroyed beyond identification.

Hard to Kill. Hard to Kill (advantage) adds +1 per level to the HT roll for death checks AND for consciousness checks. Common for elite warriors; very common for Wookiees and Mandalorians in this campaign. Plus on death check; doesn’t help against auto-death at −5×HP.

Hard to Subdue. Hard to Subdue (advantage) adds +1 per level to HT for consciousness checks and to recover from stun. Doesn’t help with death checks. Often paired with Hard to Kill on combat-built characters.

Fatigue Points & FP Track

FP Overview. Fatigue Points represent stamina. Spent on Extra Effort, certain skills, swimming/climbing, holding breath, and most importantly Force powers SW in this campaign. FP recovers naturally — 1 FP per 10 minutes of rest. FP damage (fatigue / fat damage type, including stun bolts) goes to FP.

FP Thresholds. FP ≤ ⅓ FPmax: Move and Dodge halved, ST halved for damage purposes, all skills at −2. FP ≤ 0: Roll HT to act each turn; DX and IQ halved (round up). FP ≤ −1×FPmax: Unconscious. Cannot wake until FP recovers above −1×.

Recovering FP. 1 FP per 10 minutes of rest (no strenuous activity). Sleep: full FP recovery overnight (8 hours). Stimulants (caf, certain SW drugs): may temporarily restore FP at GM-determined cost. Eating: a meal restores some FP. Most combat is too short for FP recovery to matter — manage FP at start of an encounter.

Stuns

Physical Stun. Triggered by major wounds (HT fail), severe shock, head trauma. Effect: lose your next turn (Do Nothing maneuver only); active defenses at −4 each turn. Recover by spending Do Nothing maneuver AND rolling HT successfully. Combat Reflexes adds +1 to recovery rolls.

Mental Stun. Triggered by surprise, fright checks, certain Force effects (Mind Trick, etc.), and very high pain. Effect: lose your next turn, active defenses at −4. Recover by rolling IQ each turn. Combat Reflexes: +6 to recover from mental stun (huge bonus). Combat Reflexes ALSO means you never ”freeze” in surprise — you act normally on the first turn of surprise.

Stun vs HPT. High Pain Threshold does NOT protect against stun rolls — only against shock penalty. A major-wounded HPT character still rolls HT to avoid stun. However, HPT often gives a +3 to that HT (RAW). Check the advantage’s full text.

Part VI

Crippling
& Severance

Threshold mechanics · what crippled means · restoration · prosthetics · the SW catalogue

Crippling separates lethal combat from disabling combat. A trained soldier can break a foe’s arm or destroy a sniper’s weapon hand without killing. A Jedi cuts off an opponent’s saber hand to end the fight without taking a life. The mechanics: single-hit injury crossing a fraction of HP threshold cripples the location. Cutting damage that crosses the threshold severs. Damage 10× the threshold destroys. Lightsabers do all of this with surgical precision and no follow-on bleeding.

Location Cripple Threshold Destroyed Threshold Effects When Crippled
ArmHP/2HP × 5Useless. Drop everything held. No attack / parry / grapple with that limb.
LegHP/2HP × 5Move drops to 1, fall prone (DX to avoid), cannot stand unaided, no kick.
HandHP/3HP × 10/3Drop weapon, cannot grasp. Two-handed weapons need both hands.
FootHP/3HP × 10/3Move halved, −2 DX-based footwork defenses, no Acrobatic Dodge / Retreat bonus.
EyeHP/10HPBlinded that side, −3 ranged actions, lose monocular depth perception.
Arm / Leg JointHP/4HP × 5/2Joint locks. Effects per parent limb (often functions as crippled arm/leg).
SpineHP/2HP × 5Cripples both legs (lower spine) or both arms (upper). Severance = paralysis below.
Vitals——Cannot be crippled. Major wound triggers HT, kills outright at high damage.
Heart——Cannot be crippled. Penetrating = auto major wound, often fatal.
Skull / Brain—HP × 4Cannot be crippled (death precedes). Brain damage causes lasting issues.

How Crippling Works

Single-Hit Threshold. A single attack’s injury must cross the threshold in one hit. Accumulated damage from multiple hits does NOT cripple — it just kills if you take enough. A character with HP 12 has Arm cripple threshold 6: one shot delivering 6+ HP injury to the arm cripples it; three shots of 2 HP each do not.

Calculation. Apply DR, wound multiplier, and location modifier to determine final HP injury. THIS is the value compared to the threshold. A blaster bolt rolling 8 damage against an arm wearing light armour (DR 4), tib-burn ×1 (limb cap applies for some types, not tib-burn): 8 − 4 = 4 penetrating, ×1 = 4 HP injury. The target’s Arm HP/2 = ~5 HP threshold. Not crippled.

Damage Cap on Limbs. When a limb is hit and the injury exceeds the cripple threshold, only the threshold’s worth of damage actually transfers to the character’s HP track — the rest is ”lost” (the limb absorbed the excess). A character with HP 12 takes a 20 HP hit to the arm. Threshold = 6. The arm is crippled. The character loses 6 HP (cap), not 20 — they DON’T take 20 HP of total damage; they take 6 HP plus a crippled arm.

Severance — Cutting Cripple = Amputation

Cutting Damage Severs. When cutting damage causes a crippling injury, the limb is severed — physically separated from the body. Same threshold (HP/2 for arm/leg, HP/3 for hand/foot, etc.). The character loses the limb. This is permanent without restoration (prosthetic, regrowth via Force healing).

Other Damage Types and Severance. Crushing damage cripples but does NOT sever the limb (it’s still attached, just broken). Impaling damage similarly: cripples but the limb remains attached. Piercing damage: same — cripples, doesn’t sever. SW Lightsaber damage (imp + burn) severs cleanly on cripple — campaign-standard ruling consistent with on-screen Jedi combat.

Severance vs Major Wound. Severance includes major wound effects. The HT roll for stun + knockdown still applies. A severed arm is itself a major wound regardless of damage amount. Bleeding from severance is severe (multiple HP per turn) without immediate cauterisation. Lightsaber severance is the exception — wound is cauterised; no bleeding.

Destruction — Permanent Loss

Destruction Threshold. When single-hit injury exceeds the cripple threshold by 10× (so HP/2 limb at HP 12 → destroyed at 60+ injury), the location is destroyed. Same as severance but more severe: the location is reduced to organic debris. Cannot be reattached, cannot regenerate, requires complete replacement (prosthetic or cybernetic).

Damage Threshold Examples. A character with HP 12: Arm/Leg cripple at 6, destroyed at 60. Eye cripple at 1, destroyed at 12. Hand/Foot cripple at 4, destroyed at 40. Most personal weapons can’t reach destruction thresholds in one hit; heavy weapons, vehicle weapons, and Force powers (Doc IV) can.

Restoration SW

Surgical Repair. A severed but-recovered limb can sometimes be reattached: Physician skill (or Surgery sub-specialisation) at appropriate penalty, with a medbay’s equipment. The era’s medical tech can reattach within ~1 hour of severance with viable tissue. Lightsaber severances are easier (clean cuts) but the cauterisation hampers regeneration. Force healing in Doc IV provides a cleaner path.

Prosthetics. Cybernetic replacements. Typical era-appropriate prosthetic: arm or leg with DR equivalent to light armour, full ST/DX function (sometimes better). Hand: precise manipulators, may include built-in tools. Eye: full visual replacement, often with HUD overlay options. Prosthetics require Mechanics/Cybernetics skill for fitting and an appropriate medical or workshop facility. Fitted prosthetics behave like natural limbs for crippling/destruction; can be re-damaged. Specific models and costs belong in the equipment document.

Force Sense and Cybernetics. SW Force-sensitive characters using Force Sense may have difficulty sensing through extensive cybernetics. The GM may rule a −2 to Force Sense rolls for characters with 25%+ cybernetic body mass. This is a common trope of the era.

Regrowth via Force Healing. SW Specific Force healing techniques can regrow limbs over time (Doc IV). Generally requires high Force training, significant FP cost, and time (weeks to months). Reserved for Force-trained healers; common Jedi rarely regrow rather than accepting a prosthetic.

Permanent Wounds (Without Restoration)

Living With a Crippling. A character with a non-restored crippled limb continues to live but with permanent function loss. Crippled arm = no-attack-with-this-limb permanently. Crippled leg = movement permanently halved. Crippled eye = blind that side permanently. The campaign may include moments where this matters — the Jedi who lost a hand and chose not to replace it carries that decision visibly.

Replacement Options Summary. Live with the loss · use a prosthetic (Mechanics skill + appropriate facility, possibly Force Sense difficulty) · regrow via Force healing (rare, expensive, time-consuming) · clone replacement (extreme, generally outlawed). The campaign treats prosthetics as the common default replacement — widely accepted in this era.

Part VII

Critical Hits
& Misses

The critical tables — hit, miss, & defense · location-specific criticals

Critical successes and failures break the normal probabilities. A natural 3 or 4 is always a critical success regardless of skill — the moment when a 10-skill swordsman lands a perfect hit. A natural 18 is always a critical miss regardless of skill — the moment when even a Force Master fumbles. Under campaign house rule, critical hits may still be defended against — but only by the defender scoring a critical defense success of their own. Critical misses cause secondary damage, weapon loss, even self-injury. This part has the critical hit and critical miss tables, plus the defense criticals (success on defense and the off-balance penalty inflicted).

Roll Trigger Notes
Crit Success — AttackNatural 3 or 4. Natural 5 if effective skill ≥ 15. Natural 6 if effective skill ≥ 16.HOUSE Defender may attempt active defense but only succeeds on a critical defense of their own. Otherwise critical hit lands. DR still applies; damage rolled and modified per Crit Hit Table.
Crit Failure — AttackNatural 18. Natural 17 if effective skill ≤ 15. Any roll where margin of failure ≥ 10.Roll on Crit Miss Table. Attack does no damage to target.
Crit Success — DefenseNatural 3 or 4. Natural 5 if defense ≥ 15. Natural 6 if defense ≥ 16.Auto-defends. Attacker is off-balance (−2 DX / defenses until they spend a Ready to recover).
Crit Failure — DefenseNatural 18. Natural 17 if defense ≤ 15. Margin of failure ≥ 10.Auto-fails. Attacker may also roll on the Crit Hit Table.

The Critical Hit Table

Roll 3d on this table when an attacker scores a critical success. Apply the entry’s effect IN ADDITION to normal damage. Damage is rolled normally; DR still applies; the table’s effect is the bonus from the critical.

3d Effect on Critical Hit
3Damage is rolled normally; the attack does max damage AND inflicts an automatic Major Wound, regardless of damage amount. Defender’s HT roll is at −5 to avoid stun.
4Triple damage. Roll damage as normal; multiply final injury by 3. (Or use the GM’s preferred max+normal+extra calculation; the simple ”×3” suffices for most tables.)
5Double damage. Multiply final injury by 2.
6Damage ignores DR. The attack penetrates armour fully. Normal damage roll, full penetration.
7Defender is stunned (HT each turn to recover; defenses −4 while stunned). Damage normal.
8Normal damage. No extra effect. HOUSE Defender may attempt active defense but only succeeds on a critical defense of their own.
9–10Normal damage. No extra effect.
11Defender drops weapon (or held item). Recover with Ready maneuver. Damage normal.
12Knockdown. Defender falls prone. Future attackers gain prone bonuses. Damage normal.
13–14Defender is stunned (HT each turn to recover). Damage normal.
15–16Automatic Major Wound regardless of damage amount. Defender rolls HT or stunned + knockdown.
17Defender rolls HT at −5 or dies. If passes, takes normal damage.
18Outright kill. Defender dies regardless of HP, HT, or armour. (The classic.)

The Critical Miss Table

Roll 3d on this table when an attacker scores a critical failure. The attack does NOT damage the target; instead the attacker suffers the listed effect.

3d Effect on Critical Miss
3Stab / hit yourself, full normal damage from your own weapon. Apply to the hand or arm holding the weapon (or whichever location the GM judges most appropriate).
4Drop weapon AND trip prone. DX − 4 to avoid falling. Recover prone via Change Posture; recover weapon via Ready.
5Hit yourself for half damage. Apply to the hand or arm wielding the weapon.
6Drop your weapon. Ready maneuver to recover. No injury.
7Whiff — lose your attack. Turn passes; no damage to anyone.
8–10Lose your attack. Turn over without effect.
11Drop your weapon. Recover via Ready maneuver.
12Fall prone. DX or Acrobatics roll at no penalty to avoid; otherwise hit the ground.
13–14Hit a random ally in melee range. Roll your normal attack on the random target. If no ally is in range, treat as 8-10 (lose attack).
15–16Weapon damaged. Roll HT for the weapon (typically 12) or it breaks on next use.
17Weapon broken. The weapon is destroyed in the strike. (For lightsabers SW: hilt damaged but blade still functions; or roll separately for the hilt.)
18Catastrophic. Weapon broken AND attacker falls prone AND self-injury (1d damage to the attacker’s weapon arm).

Critical Defense Outcomes

Critical Defense Success. Defender rolls a natural 3 or 4 (or 5 with defense ≥ 15, 6 with defense ≥ 16). The defense automatically succeeds — no margin needed. The ATTACKER is left off-balance: −2 DX, −2 to all active defenses, until the attacker spends a Ready maneuver to recover their stance. Off-balance penalty applies to skills as well as defenses.

Defense Critical Bonus Effects. Each defense type gets a bonus effect on critical success: Critical Parry with weapon: treat as free Aggressive Parry — defender’s weapon thrust damage to attacker’s attacking limb. Critical Parry unarmed (Karate/Judo): treat as free Aggressive Parry — thrust damage to attacking limb. Critical Dodge: +1 hex of free movement on top of normal Step / Move. Critical Block (shield/cloak): attacker driven back; shield/cloak takes no damage from the attack.

Critical Defense Failure. Defender rolls a natural 18 (or 17 if defense ≤ 15, or margin of failure ≥ 10). Defense auto-fails. Attacker may roll on the Critical Hit Table as if they themselves had critted. This is brutal — the defender’s bad roll grants the attacker a critical hit.

The Critical Defense Reference Table

Outcome / Bonus Trigger Effect
Crit Defense SuccessNatural 3 or 4. Nat 5 if defense ≥ 15. Nat 6 if defense ≥ 16.Defense automatically succeeds — no margin needed. Attacker is left off-balance (−2 DX, −2 to all active defenses, applies to skills as well) until the attacker spends a Ready maneuver to recover. Defense-type bonus also applies (see below).
Crit Defense FailureNatural 18. Natural 17 if defense ≤ 15. Any margin of failure ≥ 10.Defense auto-fails. Attacker may roll on the Critical Hit Table as if they themselves had critted.
— Defense-Type Bonuses on Critical Defense Success —
Crit Parry (weapon)Critical success with a weapon ParryTreat as free Aggressive Parry — defender’s weapon thrust damage to attacker’s attacking limb.
Crit Parry (unarmed)Critical success with Karate / Judo / Boxing ParryTreat as free Aggressive Parry — thrust damage to attacker’s attacking limb.
Crit DodgeCritical success on Dodge+1 hex of free movement on top of normal Step / Move.
Crit BlockCritical success with shield or cloak BlockAttacker driven back 1 hex. Shield / cloak takes no damage from the attack regardless of penetration.

Critical Hits Allow Counter-Crit Defense HOUSE

The Rule. When an attacker scores a critical hit (per the Crit Hit trigger criteria), the defender may still attempt an active defense — but the defense succeeds only on a critical defense success of its own (nat 3-4, or nat 5 at def ≥ 15, or nat 6 at def ≥ 16). Any other result fails: the critical hit lands. This replaces the RAW rule that critical hits bypass all active defense.

Why This Rule. RAW makes critical hits an unstoppable spike — when one happens, the defender simply takes the wound. The campaign treats moments of perfection symmetrically: an attacker’s perfect strike can be answered by a defender’s perfect parry. The math keeps criticals dangerous (defender needs nat 3-6 to save themselves), but allows the dramatic moment where a master duellist matches a master swordsman’s killing blow.

Resolution. Attacker rolls critical hit; defender declares an active defense and rolls. If the defender’s roll qualifies as a critical defense success, the defense succeeds and the Critical Defense Reference Table effects apply (auto-defend, off-balance attacker, defense-type bonus). Otherwise, the attacker rolls on the Crit Hit Table and applies the result. DR still applies in either case if the hit lands.

Multi-Defense Penalty. The defender may try multiple defenses against a critical hit if their character has the slots, each at the cumulative multi-defense penalty (−4 RAW; −2 with TbaM/WM trained on the defense weapon — Doc I house rule). Each defense roll only succeeds on its own critical success. Acrobatic Dodge, AoD variants, and movement defenses all permit their normal modifiers; only the crit-roll requirement changes.

Lightsaber Parry vs Critical Bolt. SW When a blaster bolt critical hit comes at a Force-sensitive with Lightsaber Parry available (Doc II Part VII), the same rule applies: defender rolls parry at the appropriate Deflect / Redirect / Reflect penalty, succeeds only on a critical of their own. On a critical Reflect: the bolt re-attacks the original shooter as a critical hit (defender’s crit ports to the reflected shot).

Location-Specific Critical Hits

Critical Hit to Head. Critical hits that land on the head (Skull/Face) are even more lethal. Some GMs use a ”Critical Head Blow” table — RAW B557. Common entries: knock target unconscious (HT or sleep), brain damage (permanent IQ loss), scarring, eye loss. Reduces the campaign-appropriate severity GM-side. Default: apply the regular Crit Hit Table effect, but with HT rolls at additional −5 for head locations.

Critical to Vitals/Heart. Penetrating critical hit to Vitals or Heart often = instant death without HT save (especially Heart). GM judgment; the RAW already makes these locations deadly. Crit Hit table results 15-18 are nearly always lethal at vital targets.

Critical to Limb. Critical hits to limbs may auto-cripple or auto-sever (cutting) regardless of damage amount. GM call; particularly cinematic outcomes. A critical to the weapon-hand of an opponent is a common ”disarm them dramatically” Jedi outcome.

Critical Hits with Special Weapons

Lightsaber Criticals. SW Lightsaber criticals are spectacular. Standard table applies, but cauterisation rule means: no bleeding follow-on; cripple/sever is permanent in the moment. Critical roll of 18 (outright kill) with lightsaber against a foe: clean decapitation, severed torso, or similar — the iconic Jedi finishing move.

Blaster Criticals. SW Blaster critical hits at close range often cauterise the wound (no bleeding follow-on). Critical to Vitals: cleanly through-and-through, often instant death. Critical to weapon arm at moderate range: disarm without killing — Han Solo’s signature move.

Stun-Bolt Criticals. SW Stun bolt criticals: target rolls HT at −5 or unconscious immediately. The ”instant takedown” common in Sith detention sequences. Damage is FP, so no permanent injury — they wake up later in a cell.

Droid-Specific Critical Hits HOUSE

Critical hits against droids follow the standard Crit Hit Table but with droid-specific overlays. Where the table specifies a biological effect (stun, HT or die), substitute the droid-mechanical equivalent. Specific Crit Hit results have distinct droid flavour:

Crit Hit Droid-Specific Effect
3Severe component damage. The struck location’s primary subsystem is destroyed (sensor blinded that side, manipulator drops everything permanently, locomotion drive seized — pick by hit location). Plus full damage.
4Power surge — triple damage AND visible electrical arcing for 1d turns (+2 for allies to target this droid).
5Double damage. Standard. May knock the droid prone if struck on locomotion.
6Penetrates seam — full damage ignoring DR. Often through an armour joint or sensor housing.
7System stagger (droid version of ”stunned”). Droid loses 1 turn while diagnostic routines run. Defenses −4 until resolved (HT each turn).
11Drop manipulator-held items (droid version of ”drop weapon”). Reset via Ready maneuver.
12Knockdown — chassis loses balance. DX to stay upright; otherwise fall prone (no biological injury, but takes 1 turn to recover).
13–14System stagger as roll 7. Diagnostic phase.
15–16Critical fault. HT (system stability) at −5 or droid shuts down for 1d minutes. On success: −3 to all rolls for 1d minutes from diagnostic load.
17HT (system stability) at −5 or droid is destroyed (chassis breaks down catastrophically). On success: heavy damage, droid at HP/2.
18Catastrophic destruction. Droid disintegrates from the strike. Power core breach possible — roll Power Core Critical sub-table below.

Power Core Critical Hits — Sub-Table HOUSE

When a critical hit lands on a droid’s Power Core (target only at −3), roll 3d on this sub-table:

  • 3–6: Power surge. Extra damage to attacker (1d burn arc to anyone adjacent to the droid, including the attacker). Visible arcing for 1 turn.
  • 7–10: System fault. Droid shuts down (HT system stability to delay 1 turn, otherwise immediate). Manual restart required (Mechanics roll).
  • 11–14: Catastrophic shutdown. Droid offline until major repair (workshop, 1+ hours).
  • 15–18: Power core breach. Small explosion: 1d burn damage to all within 2 hexes, plus droid permanently destroyed.

Programming Glitches — Critical Sensor Head Damage HOUSE

A critical hit to a droid’s Sensor Head can cause persistent programming corruption. After a normal Crit Hit Table roll applied to the droid, also roll 3d for programming effect on a Sensor Head hit:

  • 3–5: Personality drift. Droid behaves erratically for the encounter — refuses orders, follows odd priorities, processes tasks out of expected sequence. GM call on specifics.
  • 6–9: Standard sensor damage per the location entry — −2 to Per and ranged. Programming intact.
  • 10–13: Friend-Foe confusion. Droid targets allies for 1d turns. Combat AI compromised. Recovers via Mechanics (AI/Programming) roll.
  • 14–16: Emergency protocol activation. Droid follows its built-in emergency programming (often shutdown for self-protection; sometimes a more aggressive last-stand routine).
  • 17–18: Full corruption. Droid is hostile to all parties or completely disabled. Requires Mechanics (AI/Programming) at −3+ to restore. May lose personality permanently.

Why This Is Different. Organic critical head hits cause biological trauma — concussion, brain damage. Droid critical head hits corrupt the programming. The character of the droid may change. This is one of the more dramatic mechanical differences between fighting droids and organic enemies.

Part VIII

Environmental
Damage

Falls · fire · drowning · vacuum · cold · poison · radiation · the SW hazards

Not every wound comes from another being. Falling damage, fire, drowning, exposure, poison, radiation, vacuum — the universe has many ways to injure. This part is the reference for hazards that arise from environments, traps, accidents, and the unique threats of the Star Wars galaxy (hyperspace mishaps, lava worlds, depressurisation, exotic poisons).

Hazard Damage Type / Effect Notes
Falling1d−3 cr per metre of fallCapped at terminal velocity (200 m/s). Acrobatics/DX to reduce.
Fire (engulfed)1d−1 burn per turn engulfedPlus equipment damage. HT or stunned by fire pain.
Lava (immersed)6d burn per turn immersedMustafar territory. Almost instant death without specialised gear.
DrowningHT roll every 10 secEach fail = lose 1 FP. At 0 FP, lose 1 HP per turn until rescued or drowned.
SuffocationHT every 15 sec / breathTime scales with held breath. Each fail = 1 FP. At 0 FP, 1 HP/turn.
Vacuum SW1d−1 burn (decompression) per 5 secPlus suffocation. Sealed armour negates.
Cold (frigid)HT every minuteEach fail = 1 FP. At 0 FP, take HP. Frostbite at HP/4 cold damage to extremities.
Heat (extreme)HT every 10 minEach fail = 1 FP / 1 HP. Dehydration ×2 rate.
Dehydration1d HP per day without waterCritical fail on HT = collapse.
RadiationToxic damage per rad/doseLong-term: cancer, sterility, organ failure. Reactor sites and atomic ruins.
Poison (ingested)Tox damage at delayed intervalsHT to resist each stage. Antidote skill rolls negate.
Poison (injected)Tox damage immediately or scheduledFrom venoms, kolto-syringes (Doc IV), assassin’s needles.
AcidCorrosion damage on contactContinued damage from pooled acid: 1 HP per turn × 1d turns.
Electricity1d burn per shockHT or stun. Droids: ion damage if voltage exceeds shielding.
Explosion / ConcussionDamage scales by distance from centerCr or burn type. Knockback applies. Shock penalty.
Hyperspace Mishap SW1d burn per second exposedRare. Damaged hyperdrive can vent. Crew survival HT vs death.

Falling

Falling Damage. Roll 1d−3 crushing damage per metre of fall. Terminal velocity for a human is ~200 m/s; falls of 200+ m do max-damage equivalent (~ 60+ HP). DX or Acrobatics or Judo (best of these) to reduce: success halves damage; critical success quarters it; failure full damage. Falling damage hits multiple locations as the body impacts; the GM may roll random locations or treat the impact as torso.

Reducing Fall Damage. Skills that help: Acrobatics (roll to convert fall into a controlled landing, reducing damage), Judo (breakfall — reduces ground impact damage), Climbing (catch on surfaces during a fall). Specific gear: jetpacks (mitigate or eliminate), parachutes, repulsorlift devices, Force Push down to slow descent. SW Force Jump and certain Force powers (Doc IV) make falls trivial.

Falling Into Water. Reduces damage. From low height: half damage. From great height: still significant due to terminal velocity. Acrobatics or Swim roll to enter cleanly (further reduce). Falling into shallow water: full impact damage (the bottom hits hard).

Fire & Heat

Engulfed in Flame. 1d−1 burn damage per turn engulfed. Clothes catch fire (additional damage source). HT roll each turn or stunned. Roll Stop, Drop, and Roll: DX or Athletics to put fire out. Force Push SW can extinguish flame from outside.

Lava. 6d burn damage per turn immersed (Mustafar, certain alien worlds). Most armour ignores at this level. Lightsabers SW can briefly part the surface but the heat still ablates the wielder. The ”lava death” common in Sith locations.

Heat (Atmospheric). Extreme heat (deserts, mining caverns, engine rooms). HT roll every 10 minutes; failure = 1 FP. Reaching 0 FP = lose 1 HP per failed roll. Dehydration accelerates: ×2 rate if also dehydrated.

Drowning & Suffocation

Drowning. Submerged with no held breath. HT roll every 10 seconds (after initial held-breath duration). Failure = lose 1 FP. At 0 FP, take 1 HP per turn. At −1×FP = unconscious; HP continues to drop. Death likely within 1-3 minutes for untrained, longer for trained swimmers.

Holding Breath. Most humans hold breath ~30 sec to 1 min comfortably. HT-based extension: HT × 4 seconds of safe holding. Roll HT to extend further; each failed roll forces a breath (in air, no problem; underwater, drown).

Suffocation. Lack of oxygen (sealed environment, vacuum, gas atmosphere). Same as drowning but slower (15 second intervals). Sealed armour with life support negates.

Vacuum & Space Hazards SW

Vacuum Exposure. Unarmored or unsealed in space: 1d−1 burning damage per 5 seconds (decompression of bodily fluids and rapid heat loss). Plus suffocation begins immediately (no air to breathe). Sealed environment suit or armour with life support negates damage and suffocation.

Hyperspace Mishap. SW Damaged hyperdrive may vent into the cabin — 1d burn per second of exposure to hyperspace radiation. Engineers may roll Engineering (Hyperspace) to seal. Crew rolls HT or worse — much worse — outcomes.

Depressurisation. Sudden loss of cabin pressure (ship hit by enemy fire). Per the rapid-decompression rules: 2d−2 crushing damage to all inside (rapid pressure differential). Sealed armour negates the damage but the wearer must still hold on against the outflow.

Cold

Frigid Conditions. Sub-zero temperatures, ice planets (Hoth, Csilla, etc.). HT roll every minute; failure = 1 FP. Frostbite at HP/4 cold damage to a location: location crippled until thawed. Hypothermia at 0 FP: HP loss; unconscious soon.

Cold Weather Gear. Insulated cold-weather gear (parka, sealed suit with thermal lining): negates routine cold checks. Heavy power-armour with thermal regulation: full negation. Note: highly conductive metal armour in extreme cold requires an insulating undersuit, or the metal conducts heat away from the wearer faster than it protects.

Poisons & Disease

Poison. Many types: ingested (slow onset), injected (immediate), contact (skin absorption), inhaled (gas). HT roll to resist (the toxin lists its resistance number, e.g. HT−2).
