Failure: take listed tox damage. Multi-stage poisons: each stage is its own HT roll, separated by listed time. Antidote rolls (Physician skill or specific item) can stop progression.

Disease. Incubation period, then symptoms, then resolution. Roll HT vs disease’s onset; if failed, character is infected. Each day or appropriate interval, HT vs disease severity: failure = symptoms progress, HP/FP loss, possibly other effects. Successful Physician treatment can shorten or end disease progression.

SW-Era Pathogens. SW The galaxy has numerous era-specific bio threats — engineered bioweapons, exotic plagues, parasitic infections. The GM picks era-appropriate diseases as the campaign requires.

Radiation

Radiation Sources. Damaged reactors, old atomic ruins, certain Sith holocrons, certain stellar phenomena, hyperspace anomalies. Damage scales by exposure intensity (rads): mild = HT every hour vs 1 FP; severe = HT every minute vs HP. Long-term: cancer, sterility, organ failure — campaign-defining outcomes.

Anti-Rad Gear. Sealed environment suits with radiation shielding negate or reduce. Anti-Rad consumable tabs SW grant +5 to HT vs radiation for 1 hour.

Acid & Chemical Burns

Acid (cor damage). Cor damage type. Each 5 cor damage that penetrates DR also reduces target DR by 1 permanently at that location. On Face: HT roll or blinded. Pooled acid: 1 HP per turn × 1d turns continuing damage, until cleansed.

Star Wars Acid Creatures. SW Various creatures across the galaxy produce corrosive secretions — acid-spitters, certain venomous predators, alchemically engineered beasts. Severity varies; GM call.

Explosions & Concussion

Explosion. Detonation damage scales by distance from blast centre. Listed at the centre: e.g. grenade 6d damage at centre, 3d at 1 hex out, 1d at 2 hexes out, halved each hex beyond. Damage type usually crushing (concussion) or burning (incendiary). Knockback applies. Anyone in radius rolls Dodge or Acrobatics to dive away.

Concussion Stun. Even non-injuring explosions stun those nearby. HT roll vs the centre damage; failure = stunned (lose next turn). Brain damage cap (−8) applies if very close.

Grenades and Explosives. SW Common categories: concussion / fragmentation grenade (typical 6d cr at center, scaling down by distance); high-yield compact military explosive (6d×3 cr at center — devastating; rare and tightly controlled); ion-emission grenade (6d ion damage, droids only); gas grenades (varies by agent). Specific models and stats belong in the equipment document.

Part IX

Recovery
& Healing

First aid · natural healing · Physician · bacta · kolto · medical droids · prosthetics

Damage taken is damage healed. The campaign uses gritty default RAW recovery: 1 HP per day natural healing (with successful HT), accelerated by first aid and Physician care, and accelerated further by the medical technology of the Star Wars galaxy — most notably bacta (galactic standard) and kolto (in this current time, the superior alternative). Force healing exists but lives in Doc IV. Major wounds, lost limbs, and severed locations require more involved restoration (Part VI’s prosthetic and surgical options).

Method Skill / Item Effect Notes
First Aid (immediate)First Aid skillStop bleeding · +1 HP recoveredBattlefield. Quick. Once per injury.
Natural HealingHT roll daily+1 HP per day on successRAW default. Long.
Physician CarePhysician skill, daily+2 to +5 HP per dayDoctor or medic; consumes their time.
Medical DroidVarious models+2 HP per day; better than human medic for routineReliable, never tires. Common in clinics and aboard ships.
Kolto patch SWField consumableHeals 1d6+4 HP over 3 turns (rolled once, applied across the three turns)The basic field dressing. Cheap, common, Manaan kolto.
Standard med pack SWField consumableHeals 2d6+6 HP over 3 turnsThe adventurer’s standard. A proper field kit.
Military med pack SWField consumable · restrictedHeals 3d6+6 HP over 3 turnsIssue-grade trauma pack. Republic and Mandalorian forces.
Stim Pack SWField consumableTemp +ST/+HT for 1 hour; FP cost afterBattlefield combat enhancer.
Bacta Tank SWStandard medical facilityHP recovery × 5; works on cripplingThe galactic standard of later ages. Expensive.
Kolto Tank SWManaan / this era’s equivalentHP recovery × 6; better than bacta in this eraOnly Manaan produces. Sole source of kolto.
SurgeryPhysician + medbayReattachment of severed limb (if recovered)Within ~1 hour of severance. Long recovery after.
ProstheticMechanics skill + facilityReplacement limb / organFunctions as natural limb. Specific stats in equipment doc.
Force Healing SWForce-sensitive onlyVarious — see Doc IVFP cost. Regrowth, crippling reversal, etc.
Anti-Rad Tabs SWConsumable+5 HT vs radiation × 1 hourCommon adventurer kit.

Using Healing Packs. Kolto patches and med packs are this era’s fast, field-applied consumables. Applied as a single action, each heals its rolled total spread across the next 3 turns (roll once; spread it evenly or apply it all on the third turn). Three hard limits keep them from trivialising combat: (1) Once per hour — one pack per character per hour; a second inside that window does nothing. (2) Diminishing returns — within a single fight, each pack after the first heals a step less (GM guidance: −1 die or roughly half each time). (3) No miracle repair — packs restore ordinary lost HP only; they do not mend grievous or crippling wounds (severed limbs, destroyed organs, dismemberment), which need a Kolto/Bacta tank, surgery, or Force healing. Stim packs and combat enhancers are separate and stack on their own track.

First Aid

First Aid Skill. A trained roll to immediately help a wounded character. IQ-based default (Easy). Used to: stop bleeding (failure of HT-vs-bleeding stopped on success), recover +1 HP immediately (success), stabilise a 0-HP character (success means they pass their consciousness check). Roll once per injury; if you fail, others may try.

Battlefield First Aid. Most field medics carry medpacs — consumable healing items. Apply medpac: First Aid roll at no penalty, +1d HP recovered on success. Critical success: +1d+2. Crit failure: medpac wasted (and the patient might worsen by 1 HP, GM call).

Stabilising a Dying Character. A character at HP ≤ −1×HPmax (death-check territory) can be saved if a teammate reaches them in time. First Aid roll at appropriate penalty: success means the dying character stops bleeding and stops dropping HP — but doesn’t necessarily restore HP. Requires Physician or medbay for actual HP recovery.

Natural Healing

RAW Default. Once per day, a healing character rolls HT. Success: recover 1 HP. Failure: no recovery that day. Critical success: 2 HP. Critical failure: no recovery + lose 1 HP from infection. Days where the character is reeling or below 0 HP: no recovery (HT to maintain).

Rest & Conditions. Recovery rolls assume rest — sleeping properly, eating, drinking. Bad conditions reduce: −1 to roll for harsh environment, −2 for stress, −3 for active combat days. Good conditions help: +1 with proper medical care, +2 in medbay/clinic, +3 in bacta tank. Force healing rolls in Doc IV provide additional bonuses.

Healing Crippled Limbs. A crippled (but not severed/destroyed) limb heals when the character returns to ⅔ HP and rests with care. Limb regains function naturally over 1-4 weeks depending on severity. Without rest, the cripple may become permanent — chronic injury.

Physician Care

Physician Skill. IQ-Hard skill for trained medical professionals. Used to provide ongoing care, treat severe injuries, perform surgery (sub-specialisation), diagnose unusual conditions. Daily care under Physician: +2 HP per day (skill 12 minimum); +4 HP per day (skill 15+); +5 HP per day (skill 18+). Critical success: +1 bonus HP that day.

Surgery. Sub-specialisation of Physician (Surgery). Required for limb reattachment, organ damage repair, complex injury surgery. Long sessions (hours) and surgical equipment. An era-standard medbay can perform most operations; field surgery is harder. Crit fail on surgery: patient loses HP / dies / location worsens. Crit success: ideal recovery, no complications.

Recovery Time. Even with Physician care, severe injuries take days to weeks. A character at −HPmax (death-check territory) who survives needs 2-4 weeks of recovery before returning to full duty. Reeling lasts until HP/3 cleared. Major wounds linger. Mental shock from trauma: GM call; sometimes weeks.

Bacta & Kolto SW

Bacta. The galactic standard healing substance of the modern galaxy. In this current time, bacta exists but is less common; kolto is the era’s standard. Bacta-immersion tanks: full HP healing rate ×5 (so 1 HP per day naturally becomes ~5 HP per day in bacta). Single-use bacta patches: heal 1d HP immediately.

Kolto. SW In this current time, the galaxy’s superior healing substance, produced only on Manaan. More effective than bacta: tank-immersion ×6 healing rate. Kolto can address crippling injuries — partial limb function restoration on a successful Physician roll with kolto. Single-use kolto packs: heal 1d+2 HP immediately, restore mild cripple (GM call).

Tank Immersion. Submersion in bacta or kolto for hours-to-days. Patient floats in nutrient suspension; injury heals at accelerated rate. Available at major medical clinics, Republic medbay vessels, and hospital ships (kolto only on Manaan).

Medical Droids & Medpacs SW

Medical Droids. Various series serve as automated medics, surgical assistants, and triage units. Mechanically: treated as Physician at the droid’s rated skill (typically 13-15). Reliable, never tire, available 24/7. Many ships and clinics carry one. Specific models in the equipment document.

Medpacs. Field consumable healing device. Used: First Aid roll, on success the user heals 1d HP immediately (1d+1 if Physician trained). Single use. Standard adventurer kit; most parties carry several.

Stim Packs. Combat-grade stimulant. One-shot inject for +1 to +2 ST and HT (and a small Move bonus) for 1 hour. After the hour: FP cost (equivalent to the bonus, lost as fatigue). Repeated use exhausts the user. Republic and Sith special forces carry stim packs as standard issue.

Prosthetics & Cybernetics

Replacement Limbs. When a limb is severed or destroyed and cannot be naturally regrown, a prosthetic replaces it. Era-appropriate prosthetics provide DR equivalent to light armour, ST equivalent to natural (sometimes better), and full DX function. Cybernetic hands include precise manipulators (sometimes with built-in tools); cybernetic eyes include HUD overlay options. Specific models and quality tiers in the equipment document.

Cybernetic Side-Effects. Cybernetic limbs feel ”cold” to many users. Force-sensitive characters may have reduced Force Sense through extensively cybernetic bodies (Doc IV). Mechanically: a character with one cybernetic limb has no penalty; 25%+ cybernetic body mass = −1 Force Sense; 50%+ = −2; full cyborg = −3 (with cybernetic intelligence taking over much of the personality).

Cybernetic Damage and Repair. Cybernetic limbs take damage like natural limbs (HP equal to natural, DR per design). When crippled, repair requires Mechanics skill (not Physician) with appropriate tools and parts. Some cybernetics self-repair slowly (1 HP per day). Major cybernetic damage may require a full clinic visit.

Long-Term Care & Permanent Effects

Convalescence. Severely wounded characters may need weeks of rest. The campaign should accommodate this — downtime for healing is a story tool. Force healers can accelerate but not eliminate the time. Some psychological injuries (fright check failures, PTSD) require similar recovery time.

Permanent HP Loss. Severe trauma can permanently reduce HPmax (lost 1 HP permanently per critical injury at high penalty). Restored by spending character points (1 HP per 2 points) or by extreme Force healing (Doc IV).

Mental & Spiritual Wounds. Combat takes a psychological toll. The GM may apply Fright Checks after particularly horrific experiences. Force-sensitives may suffer from Dark Side temptations after lethal combat (Doc IV). Long-term: nightmares, PTSD, reduced confidence (Will roll penalties for a time). Recovery: Psychiatry skill, social support, time, sometimes Force Heal.

Droid Repair SW HOUSE

Droids do not heal — they’re repaired. The mechanics parallel organic healing but use different skills, different tools, and different time scales. The fundamental difference: a droid’s damage is mechanical, not biological. There’s no immune system to fight infection, no body to recover. Instead, every component is potentially replaceable, every system diagnosable. The skilled mechanic can fix what looks unfixable; the unskilled can scrap a barely-damaged droid through botched intervention.

Repair Action Skill / Tools Effect
Field Patch (combat)Mechanics skill, Ready maneuverStop fluid leak; +1 HP if positive; stabilise at current HP if at or below 0.
Stabilise Inert DroidMechanics rollPrevent further deterioration. Holds droid at current HP until proper repair.
Reset / RestartMechanics rollWake an inert droid (HP ≤ 0). Restart sequence: 1d turns.
Workshop Repair (light)Mechanics 12+, workshop+2 HP per hour. Lighter damage (less than ¼ HPmax).
Workshop Repair (heavy)Mechanics 12+ at −2, workshop, parts+1 HP per 2 hours. Heavier damage (¼ to ½ HPmax).
Critical Damage RepairMechanics −4, workshop, partsSevered limbs, destroyed modules. 4-12 hours plus parts acquisition.
Modular Limb ReplacementMechanics 12+ with parts30 min (arm) / 1 hr (leg) / 2 hr (sensor head) / 4 hr (power core).
Subsystem ResetMechanics rollClear a subsystem fault (sensor flicker, manipulator stutter, etc.). 1-3 turns.
Seal Hydraulic / Coolant LeakMechanics rollStop fluid loss. Cannot seal lightsaber cuts in field — workshop required.
Power Cell RechargeExternal power sourceFull recharge: 1 hour. Field swap: 15 min if spare available.
Active Heat VentingMechanics rollShed heat faster: 1d minutes per +1 heat unit shed.
Programming RepairMechanics (AI / Programming)Restore corrupted programming from Sensor Head critical hits. 1-4 hours.
Self-Repair RoutineBuilt-in (if equipped)Slow regeneration: 1 HP/day (basic) to 1 HP/hour (advanced) or 1 HP/turn (rare).

Droid Repair Skills — at a Glance

Which skill. Droid repair uses Droid Mechanic (with Electronics Repair for circuitry and comms, and Mechanic for general mechanical work) — the skills themselves, their difficulties, and their specialisations live in the Skills book. This section covers only how they apply to damage. As a rule of thumb, skill 12+ handles routine repair, 15+ combat-grade work, and 18+ cutting-edge or exotic systems.

Repair Critical Failures. Crit fail on a repair roll: damage worsens by 1d HP, or a subsystem develops a new fault, or the droid’s programming becomes corrupted. The mechanic-as-saboteur is a familiar trope. Important repairs should have a backup plan.

Field Repair (Combat-Time)

Field Patch. The droid analog of First Aid. A character with Mechanics skill spends a Ready maneuver to attempt a field patch. Success: stop one active fluid leak OR restore 1 HP if droid is positive HP, OR stabilise at current HP if droid is at or below 0. Critical success: +1d HP if positive. Only one patch per ”wound” — a leak once sealed stays sealed; a stabilised droid stays at current HP until further repair.

Without Parts. Field repair without proper tools: −2 to skill (field expediency). With a repair kit (compact parts and tools): +0. With a full droid repair kit: +1 (specialised tools and replacement parts).

Combat Stabilisation. A droid at HP ≤ 0 needs stabilising before it’s left for ”later repair”. Mechanics roll to stabilise — success prevents further deterioration. Without stabilisation, the droid continues to degrade (1 HP per hour from coolant loss, system stress, etc.). This is the droid analog of stabilising a dying character.

Workshop Repair

Workshop Required. Serious repair needs a workshop — replacement parts, diagnostic tools, fabrication equipment, often a power source. Field repair handles emergencies; workshop repair handles complete restoration. Ship medbays, military repair bays, and droid shops all qualify.

Repair Time by Damage. Light damage (less than ¼ HPmax of damage): 1-2 hours per HP restored, Mechanics at +0. Heavy damage (¼ to ½ HPmax): 2-4 hours per HP restored, Mechanics at −2. Critical damage (severed limb, destroyed module): 4-12 hours minimum, plus parts cost, Mechanics at −4. Programming corruption (from critical Sensor Head hits): 1-4 hours of Mechanics (AI / Programming) work.

Parts and Sourcing. Most workshop repairs include parts in the time estimate. Specialty parts (rare droid models, custom components) may take days to source. Field commanders often improvise — repurpose a similar component from another droid, or fabricate a workable substitute at penalty.

Modular Component Replacement

Modular Design. Droids are designed for replacement, not repair-from-scratch. Limbs, sensor heads, manipulator tools, power cores — all are designed to be swapped out as units when damaged beyond field repair. This is one of the most important practical differences from organic healing.

Replacement Times. Manipulator (arm): 30 minutes with available parts. Locomotion drive (leg / wheel / tread): 1 hour. Sensor head: 2 hours (delicate alignment). Power core: 4 hours (Mechanics [Electronics]). All times assume parts on hand; sourcing adds delay.

Severed by Lightsaber. SW A lightsaber-severed droid limb cannot be reattached in the field — the cut is too clean and the alignment is destroyed. Modular replacement is the only path. Without replacement, the droid operates without that limb (heavily impaired).

Self-Repair Routines

Built-in Maintenance. Many droid models include self-repair routines that slowly restore HP between activations. Common levels:

  • Self-Repair Level 1: 1 HP per day. Common in maintenance and utility droids.
  • Self-Repair Level 2: 1 HP per hour. Rare; some war droids include this feature.
  • Self-Repair Level 3: 1 HP per turn. Cutting-edge tech, rare even in elite military units.

Self-repair restores HP only — does not fix critical faults (severed limbs, destroyed modules, programming corruption). Those require an external technician.

Heat Management & Power Recharge

Heat Venting. Droids cool naturally at 1 heat unit per minute of inactivity in normal temperature. Active venting (Mechanics roll to open vents and divert coolant): 1d minutes per +1 heat shed. In cold environments cooling is faster (+1 per minute in sub-zero). Damaged cooling system: heat builds permanently until the cooling component is repaired.

Power Recharge. Standard external power source: 1 hour full recharge. Field generator / portable power: 4-8 hours. Spare power cell swap (if available): 15 minutes. Solar trickle: 1 unit per hour in direct sunlight. Without a working power input (intact Sensor Head or Chassis component), recharge is impossible — the connection point is gone.

Coolant & Hydraulic Refill. After a leak is sealed (Mechanics roll), the lost fluid must be refilled. Field refill from carried reserve: 5 minutes per fluid pool. Workshop refill with complete pressure recheck: 1 hour. Until refilled, the droid operates at reduced capacity (manipulator strength reduced, hydraulic stiffness, etc.).

Part X

The Damage
Playbook

Quick-reference tables · damage formulas · location summary · status effects · medical actions

The comprehensive lookup for Doc III. Use during play when a specific rule or value is needed: damage type effects, location penalties, status durations, healing rates. All Star Wars-tagged entries are setting-specific; the rest is GURPS 4e RAW.

① Damage Type Quick Reference

Type Mult Locations Allowed Special Effects
Crushing (cr)×1All locations except Eye, Vitals, Heart, NeckV/A, JointsKnockback. ×1.5 to Neck (snap). Male groin: ×2 shock.
Cutting (cut)×1.5All except Eye, Vitals, HeartSevers at cripple threshold. ×2 to Neck (sever).
Impaling (imp)×2All locations×3 to Vitals/Heart. Limb cap ×1.
Piercing pi-×0.5All locationsHalf multiplier. Slugthrowers.
Piercing pi×1All locations×3 to Vitals/Heart.
Piercing pi+×1.5All locations×3 to Vitals/Heart. Limb cap ×1.
Piercing pi++×2All locations×3 to Vitals/Heart. Limb cap ×1.
Burning (burn)×1All except Eye, Vitals, Heart (unless tib-burn variant)Sets fires. Damages gear.
Tight-Beam Burn (tib-burn) SW×1All locations (like imp)Blasters. ×3 to Vitals/Heart. Cauterises.
Corrosion (cor)×1All locationsReduces armour DR (−1 per 5 damage). Blinding on Face.
Toxic (tox)×1Bypasses physical DR partiallyHT roll to resist. Often staged onset.
Fatigue (fat) SWFP onlyStun bolts. No HP damage.Knock unconscious at FP ≤ −1× FP max.

② Hit Location Quick Reference

Location To-Hit Cripple Multiplier & Notes
Skull / Brain−7—×4 wound mult, DR 2 innate. Major wound HT at −10.
Face−5—Crit Hit Table reads one row better. Cor → blind.
Eye−9HP/10×4 mult, no DR. imp/pi/tib-burn only.
Neck−5—×1.5 cr (snap), ×2 cut (sever).
NeckV/A−8—×1.5 dmg, HT/turn vs bleeding. cut/imp/pi/tib-burn.
Vitals−3—×3 for imp / pi / pi+ / pi++ / tib-burn. Major wound trigger.
Heart−5—As Vitals + auto major wound.
Groin−3—cut/pi/imp = auto major. Male cr ×2 shock.
Spine−8HP/2Cripples both legs/arms. Cr/cut/imp/pi/tib-burn.
Arm−2HP/2imp/pi+/pi++ capped at ×1.
Leg−2HP/2imp/pi+/pi++ capped at ×1.
Hand−4HP/3imp/pi+/pi++ capped at ×1.
Foot−4HP/3imp/pi+/pi++ capped at ×1.
Arm/Leg Joint−7HP/4Joint locks. cr/cut/imp/pi/tib-burn.

③ HP Track & Status Effects

Status / HP Level Effect
Shock penalty (next turn after damage)−1 per HP lost, cap −4 (brain −8). HPT negates.
Major wound (single hit injury > HP/2)HT or stun + knockdown. Face −5, Skull/Eye −10.
HP ≤ ⅓ HPmax — ReelingMove & Dodge halved. Other skills unaffected.
HP ≤ 0 — Conscious checkHT each turn start. Fail = unconscious.
HP ≤ −1× to −4× HPmaxHT or die at each threshold. Crit fail = die.
HP ≤ −5× HPmaxAuto-dead. No save.
HP ≤ −10× HPmaxBody destroyed beyond identification.
FP ≤ ⅓ FPmaxMove & Dodge halved, ST halved for dmg, skills −2.
FP ≤ 0HT to act each turn. DX & IQ halved.
FP ≤ −1× FPmaxUnconscious. Recovers when FP returns above −1×.
Physical stunLose turn, defenses −4. HT each turn to recover.
Mental stunLose turn, defenses −4. IQ each turn (CR +6).
Off-balance (after crit-defended)−2 DX & defenses until Ready maneuver clears.
Bleeding woundHT each turn or lose 1 HP. First Aid stops.
Crippled locationUseless until healed. Permanent loss without restoration.
— Droid-Specific Status —
Droid Inert (HP ≤ 0)Droid offline. NO consciousness check. Restart via Mechanics roll on repair to ≥1 HP.
Droid System Stress (HP ≤ ⅓)Move & DX rolls −1. More frequent subsystem damage rolls.
Subsystem Fault (post-hit roll)Sensor flicker / comm fault / power flicker / manipulator stutter / locomotion glitch / servo lock / critical fault. Per Part III table.
Power Cell ≤ ⅓Low-power mode. −1 to all skills.
Power Cell ≤ 0Emergency shutdown. HT to delay 1 minute or shutdown immediately.
Heat 50-75%−1 to all skills.
Heat 75-100%−2 to skills; HT/min or 1d burn dmg internally.
Heat 100%Forced shutdown OR 2d burn dmg + continue at −3.
Hydraulic / Coolant LeakContinuous fluid loss until sealed by Mechanics roll. Reduced function.
EMP / Ion DisruptionHT (system stability) vs strength. Failure: offline 1d turns to 1d min.
Programming CorruptionPersistent. Requires Mechanics (AI / Programming) repair, not field-fixable.

④ Critical Outcomes Quick Reference

Trigger Roll Outcome
Attack crit successNat 3-4; nat 5 at skill 15+; nat 6 at skill 16+HOUSE Defender may defend but only succeeds on a crit defense of their own. Roll Crit Hit Table 3d.
Attack crit failureNat 18; nat 17 at skill ≤15; margin of fail ≥ 10Roll Crit Miss Table 3d. No damage to target.
Defense crit successNat 3-4; nat 5 at def 15+; nat 6 at def 16+Auto-defend. Attacker off-balance (−2 DX/def). Defense-type bonus applies (see below).
Defense crit failureNat 18; nat 17 at def ≤15; margin of fail ≥ 10Auto-fail. Attacker may roll Crit Hit Table.
— Defense-type bonuses (always apply on crit success) —
Crit Parry (weapon)—Free Aggressive Parry — defender’s weapon thrust damage to attacker’s attacking limb.
Crit Parry (unarmed)—Free Aggressive Parry — thrust damage to attacker’s attacking limb.
Crit Dodge—+1 hex of free movement on top of normal Step/Move.
Crit Block—Attacker driven back. Shield/cloak takes no damage.

⑤ Environmental Damage Quick Reference

Hazard Damage / Effect Resistance
Fall1d−3 cr per metreAcrobatics / DX / Judo to halve.
Fire engulfed1d−1 burn per turnStop-Drop-Roll: DX/Athletics. Sealed armour negates.
Lava6d burn per turnVery few materials resist. Death likely.
DrowningHT each 10 sec; fail = 1 FP; at 0 FP = 1 HP/turnSwim skill, scuba gear, sealed armour.
Vacuum SW1d−1 burn per 5 sec + suffocationSealed armour + life support negate.
Cold (frigid)HT per minute; fail = 1 FPCold-weather gear; sealed armour negates.
RadiationTox per rad/dose; long-term cancerSealed armour. Anti-Rad Tabs +5 HT.
PoisonTox damage staged. HT to resist.Antidote (Physician skill) or specific item.
AcidCor damage. Pooled: 1 HP/turn × 1dSealed armour; rinse/cleanse.
Electric shock1d burn. HT or stun. Droids: ion.Insulated armour; grounded boots.
ExplosionScales by distance from center. Knockback.Cover. Dive for cover.
Hyperspace mishap SW1d burn per sec. Engineering to seal.Sealed hyperdrive systems.

⑥ Healing & Medical Reference

Action / Item Skill / Cost Effect
First AidFirst Aid skill+1 HP, stop bleeding. Once per injury.
Stabilise dyingFirst Aid rollStop death-check progression. Stabilises at current HP.
Natural healingHT daily+1 HP per day on success. 0 on fail.
Physician carePhysician skill 12++2 HP/day. Skill 15+ = +4/day. Skill 18+ = +5/day.
Medpac SWConsumable device+1d HP immediately. First Aid roll. Single use.
Stim pack SWConsumable injector+1 to +2 ST/HT for 1 hour. FP cost after.
Bacta tank SWMedical facilityRecovery rate ×5.
Kolto tank SWManaan facilityRecovery rate ×6. Better than bacta. Manaan only.
SurgeryPhysician (Surgery)Reattach severed limb (within ~1 hour). Repair organ damage.
Prosthetic limbMechanics fitting + facilityFull function replacement.
Force healing SWFP cost (Doc IV)Restore HP, reverse crippling, regrow limbs (rare).
Anti-Rad Tab SWConsumable+5 HT vs radiation × 1 hour.
Medical droid SWVarious modelsPhysician skill 13-15. 24/7 availability.
— Droid Repair (parallel to organic healing) —
Field Patch (combat) SWMechanics skill + ReadyStop leak / +1 HP / stabilise. Droid analog of First Aid.
Reset Inert Droid SWMechanics rollRestart droid at HP ≤ 0. Sequence: 1d turns. No HT roll needed.
Workshop Repair SWMechanics 12+, workshop+1 to +2 HP per hour. Penalty by damage severity.
Modular Replacement SWMechanics + parts30 min arm / 1 hr leg / 2 hr sensor head / 4 hr power core.
Programming Repair SWMechanics (AI / Programming)Restore from Sensor Head critical hit corruption. 1-4 hours.
Power Cell Recharge SWExternal powerFull: 1 hr. Field swap: 15 min. Solar trickle: 1 unit/hr.
Self-Repair (built-in) SWDroid featureL1: 1 HP/day · L2: 1 HP/hr · L3: 1 HP/turn (rare).

⑦ Damage Calculation Cheat Sheet

Step 1 — Roll Damage. Roll the weapon’s damage dice. Note the total.
Step 2 — Apply Armour Divisor. Effective DR = target DR ÷ AD. (Lightsaber swing AD 5, thrust AD 10.)
Step 3 — Subtract DR. Penetrating damage = Damage rolled − Effective DR. If ≤ 0, no penetration (subject to blunt trauma if flexible armour).
Step 4 — Apply Wound Multiplier. Use the LOCATION’S multiplier (from table); if none specified, use damage type’s standard multiplier. Skull ×4, Vitals ×3, Eye ×4, etc.
Step 5 — Apply Limb Cap. imp/pi+/pi++ damage to a limb capped at ×1 effective multiplier. Excess injury lost (the limb absorbed it).
Step 6 — Final HP Injury. This is what hits the HP track. Check: major wound (> HP/2)? Crippling threshold (> HP/X for that location)? Destruction (10× threshold)?
Step 7 — Resolve Wound Effects. Shock penalty next turn. HT for major wound. Cripple/sever/destroy. Bleeding if applicable. Status effects.

Cross-Document References

For attack mechanics and how damage is delivered, see Doc I (melee) and Doc II (ranged). For Force-related damage and healing (Force Lightning, Force Push impact, Force healing, Mind Trick stunning), see Doc IV. For specific weapon damage stats by model, see the Equipment Compendium (Doc VII). For vehicular damage and starship combat, see Doc VI.

Document IV

The Living Force

The books of Force tradition · powers, forms & the bonded blade (its own signature, not a book)
The Codex of the Living Force

The Common Ground

The rules shared by every tradition — read once, true of all eight books.
How the Force Works · Alignment · Universal Rulings · Point Costs
The Living Force · The Common Ground

How the Force Works

Read once. Everything here is true of every tradition in the eight books that follow — so the books need not repeat it.

The Force is the energy that binds the galaxy. A rare few are born able to feel it, and fewer still are trained to bend it. This document opens with the rules common to all Force use — how a power is cast, where alignment sits, how a power is resisted or dodged, and what it costs in points to learn. Each tradition (each book) then opens with its own short header: its place on the light–dark axis, its tier ladder, its capstones, and the price of every step.

The Force, in Brief

Every Force-user has one Force attribute — the trait their gift flows through (ST, DX, IQ, HT, Will, or Per; most traditions favour IQ or Will) — and a set of Force skills, each tied to that attribute. Casting a power is an ordinary 3d6 roll against the relevant Force skill (see Three Dice, One Rule), paid for in Fatigue Points (FP). Roll at or under your effective skill and the power takes; the margin and the dice resolve the rest.

A character picks one tradition as their Primary — that book is their home, and its rules govern how deep they may go. Depth is measured in tiers (I–V); rising a tier is proven by capstones (earned deeds) and paid for in character points.

The Words We Use

A power is a single learned application of the Force — a thing you do (Force Push, Force Heal, Mind Trick). It has a Force skill, an action, a range, and an FP cost.

A tradition is a school of Force practice with its own philosophy, alignment, powers, and capstones. Each book below is one tradition; you pick one as your Primary.

A tier (I–V) marks depth within a tradition, from Initiate to Legend. It is gated by capstones and points — never by your alignment.

TermWhat it means at the table
Force attributeThe one attribute your gift runs on. Most powers in your book roll against a skill based on it.
Force skillA learnable skill (usually Hard or Very Hard). Powers are cast on a roll against it.
FPThe fuel. Spent to cast; recovered with rest. At 0 FP you cannot cast (barring extra effort).
TierI Initiate · II Knight · III Master · IV Grandmaster · V Legend (names vary by book).
CapstoneThe deed that proves a tier and unlocks the next. Detailed in each book's header.

Alignment — the Light, the Dark, and the Grey

A gauge of where a character stands — and nothing more.

Every Force-user sits somewhere on a single axis running from −100 (wholly Dark) through 0 (Balanced) to +100 (wholly Light) — the same slider the chronicles of Revan's age used. Deeds move it. It is a pure reference point: a mirror of the choices a character has made, read by Force-senses and felt by those they meet. It grants no bonus and no penalty of its own. The benefits of the light, the dark, and the grey are the books — their powers, their capstones, their requirements. The meter only tells you, and the galaxy, where someone stands.

+100 0 −100
+90 to +100Luminouslight made manifest — a beacon
+70 to +90Radianta paragon of the light
+50 to +70Devoteda true servant of the light
+30 to +50Alignedwalks the light in earnest
+10 to +30Attunedleaning to the light
−10 to +10Balancedtrue neutral — the grey center
−30 to −10Shadowedthe dark, tasted
−50 to −30Taintedgiven to the dark in earnest
−70 to −50Corrupteda true servant of the dark
−90 to −70Malignanta horror in the dark
−100 to −90Fallenthe dark made manifest
A deed…moves the meter
Minor — a small mercy; a petty cruelty±1 – 2
Significant — sparing a beaten foe; harming an innocent; domination±3 – 6
Defining — a great sacrifice; an atrocity±8 – 15
Leaning hard on a power against your nature∓2 (GM may waive for a true grey)
A gauge, not a gate — and not a bonus

Alignment never modifies a roll, an FP cost, or a power. What a character may learn is set by their book's requirements (the Living Path expects the light; the Unchained, the dark) and earned through capstones + points. A book may require a certain standing to enter or to climb — that requirement is printed in the book's header — but the meter itself spends nothing and grants nothing. The GM nudges it between sessions; players read it to know where they stand.

Where the dark's power and price live

The dark side is the stronger hand in raw power — and it always costs. But that strength and that cost are written into the dark books (the Unchained, the Hollowing): their powers, their capstones, the corruption they demand. The meter only reflects how far someone has gone; it is not the source of either the gift or the toll.

The Common Ground · II

Universal Rulings

Casting, range, defence and reactions — true for every power in every book.

Reading a Power

Every power in the books is written to the same stat line, so you can read any of them at a glance:

Example — Force Push (Common Force, Tier I)

Roll ST  ·  Action Attack  ·  Range 8 hexes (line-of-sight)  ·  Defence Dodge if aware, else ST contest  ·  FP 1 per hex  ·  Reaction? Yes — a defensive shove out of turn.

FieldReads as
RollAttribute (Skill) you roll 3d6 against.
ActionWhat it costs you: Attack, Concentrate, Ritual, Quick (free), or Reaction.
RangeSelf · Touch · N hexes · Line-of-sight · AOE r=N · AOE r=N at range N (see below).
DefenceHow a target escapes it: Resist (attribute/skill), Dodge, Tutaminis / lightsaber parry, or None.
FPFatigue spent.
Reaction?Can it be used out of turn (once, as a defense)? Yes or No. See Reactions.

Range, Line of Sight & Area

Range is stated on every power and means one of:

TagMeans
Self / TouchYou, or a target you can lay a hand on.
N hexesA hard cap. Beyond it the power simply does not reach.
Line-of-sightAny target you can see and clearly perceive — effectively unlimited at the GM's discretion, but blocked by walls, smoke, and the like. Used for sense, mind, and far-reaching powers.
AOE r=NAn area effect centred on you (or the target), filling every hex within radius N.
AOE r=N at range NA placed area: you set the centre up to its stated range, then it fills its radius. Both numbers are listed when they differ; a burst that can only come off your own body says centred on self.

Where falloff applies (damage thinning toward the edge of a blast), the power says so; otherwise an area effect is uniform within its radius.

Resisting & Avoiding a Power

There are three ways to escape a power, and which apply depends on the power's nature. Each power's Defence field tells you which:

Nature of powerHow it's avoided
Mind & internal
fear, domination, mind tricks, internal harm		Resisted only — a contest against Will, HT, Mind Block, or another mental-based skill, as the power states. You cannot dodge a thought. If it catches you unaware, resist at −6 (a blindside — see the Awareness Gate).
Kinetic
Force Push/Pull, hurled objects, telekinetic strikes		Dodge if aware (see the gate). Cover or a braced stance blunts the knockback. A lightsaber can cut a hurled object out of the air — a parry — if it is small enough to slice; a flung speeder or boulder is too large and must be dodged or weathered.
Energy
redirected bolts, Force lightning, energy projection		Tutaminis (bare hand — a neutral power, see the Common Force book) or a lightsaber parry/deflect (blade); a Force resistance roll where stated; and a Dodge in some cases — a slow bolt or a telegraphed gout can be sidestepped if you are aware (GM's call).
The Awareness Gate — being blindsided

Whether you can answer an incoming power depends on whether you saw it coming. Obvious tells — a shouted word, a flung hand, a slow ritual, a hurled boulder — leave you aware, for free. A fast or hidden cast forces a Perception roll (a sensitive may use a Force-sense skill) to notice it.

If you are caught unaware you are blindsided — exactly like a surprise attack: you get no Dodge at all, and any resistance roll is at −6. Mind & internal powers can never be dodged regardless, but they too take the −6 when they catch you unready.

When a Dodge is allowed, range is the deciding modifier — a shove in your face is near-impossible to slip; one from across the field is easy to read and step from:

Range to casterDodge modifier
Close (1–2 hexes)−4
Short (3–5)−2
Medium (6–10)0
Long (11+)+2

Force Push & Kinetic Powers — the Shove

Treat Force Push and its kin as a kinetic shove: on a hit, the target is knocked back per the knockback rules (see Damage), scaled to the power's strength. A successful Dodge avoids it; a braced stance or solid cover reduces the knockback even on a hit; an object can be interposed to take the shove in the target's place; and a small flung object may be cut from the air with a blade rather than dodged. Hurled objects and telekinetic strikes follow the same logic.

Reactions

A reaction is an ability used once, outside your own turn — usually to defend, or however the ability works. A power's Reaction? tag simply says whether it can be: Yes (it may also be triggered out of turn) or No (your turn only). A handful of powers are reaction-only — their Action line reads Reaction, meaning they exist purely to answer something in the moment.

Defences themselves — resist, dodge, Tutaminis or a parry — are always available when you're targeted, whatever the attacker throws; the Reaction? tag only governs whether an offensive or utility power can be used out of turn. (Ordinary, non-Force reactions come later.)

Retired — Force Deflect

The old neutral power Force Deflect is removed. Turning a blaster bolt with a lightsaber is a lightsaber parry (see Ranged Combat → Bolt Deflection); turning one bare-handed is Tutaminis, the neutral power detailed in the Common Force book. Nothing is left for a separate power to do.

The Common Ground · III

Buying the Force with Points

Every tier, power, form and stance has a price.

Force ability is bought with the same character points as everything else. The economical path is to go deep in one tradition — buy a tier and you gain that tier's powers wholesale. If a player would rather take just one ability — their own book, a neutral art, a minor tradition, or a cross-tradition power their primary allows — they may buy that single power individually, at a higher price per power, because they are skipping the ladder.

What you're buyingT-IT-IIT-IIIT-IVT-V
Buy into a tradition tier (grants that tier's powers)816305080
Single power, bought individually (your book / neutral / minor / cross)48142028
Lightsaber Form (priced by its tier)48142028
Battle Stance (by potency — some are stronger)Lesser 3  ·  Standard 5  ·  Greater 8

Lightsaber techniques (Saber Throw and the like) are not a separate category — they live in the Common Force (Neutral) book and are bought as single powers, above.

Buying a tier is cheaper per power than buying powers one at a time — commitment is rewarded. Buying powers individually exists so a swordmaster can take the one Force trick that fits, or a Jedi can dip a toe in a neutral art, without taking a whole book. Cross-tradition buying is allowed only as far as your primary book's light/dark requirement permits: a Living Path Jedi can never buy dark powers, while a neutral art is open to all.

How to Read a Book

With the common ground covered, each of the eight books opens with a short, standard header — its identity and flair, then the things you need before you spend a point. Here is the shape, using The Living Path as the example:

The Living Path — the Jedi Way

Discipline, compassion, and the serenity of the light. The broad road, and the most forgiving.

Alignment req.Enter at Attuned+ (+5); the deeper tiers expect Devoted (+45+). Dark powers are barred.
Force skillIQ-based; serenity favours a cool head.
TiersI Initiate · II Knight · III Master · IV Grandmaster · V Legend.
Capstone & CrossingEach tier-up is proven by a deed of restraint or sacrifice — refusing the dark when it was offered. (Full deeds listed at each tier.)
To buyTier costs 8 / 16 / 30 / 50 / 80; single powers priced individually as above.

Every book that follows wears this same header — alignment requirement, skill, tiers, capstone, cost — so a reader always knows the price of admission before reading a single power.

End of the Common Ground. The rules above apply to every tradition. What follows are the shared specifics — capstones and learning — and then the eight books themselves.

Part One

Tiers & Capstones

Having the power is not the same as earning the right to wield it

Power alone does not raise you in a tradition. To climb from one tier of a tradition to the next, you must complete that tier’s Capstones — named, earned, role-played achievements with a cost in time and (if you would rather buy past them) in character points. A capstone is the moment the rules say now you have truly become this.

How Capstones Work

The Two Kinds of Capstone

The Crossing Capstone — one per tier-up. The threshold act that commits you to the next depth: a deed, a vow, a sacrifice, or (for the dark) a piece of yourself given up. The Crossing is, by its nature, a point of no easy return.

Mastery Capstones — the proof you have actually learned the tier, not just qualified for it. Demonstrations of the tier’s powers under real conditions, taught or self-discovered, each with a training-time cost.

How many capstones? It scales with depth

The shallow tiers ask little; the deep tiers ask everything. Every tier-up needs exactly one Crossing capstone (the committing act) plus a growing number of Mastery capstones:

  • Reaching Tier II: 1 Crossing + 1 Mastery
  • Reaching Tier III: 1 Crossing + 2 Mastery
  • Reaching Tier IV: 1 Crossing + 3 Mastery
  • Reaching Tier V: 1 Crossing — and at this height the Crossing IS the mastery; the legendary deed proves itself.

The strict traditions (Concordance, Hollowing, Inscribed) may add one extra condition at a tier where the fiction demands it — noted in their tables.

The Three Gates of a Tier-Up

To rise from Tier N to Tier N+1 in any tradition, all three must be true:

  1. Prerequisites. You hold any traits the tradition requires at that tier (advantages/disadvantages), and you keep to the tradition’s discipline where it has one — the Concordance’s Code, the Hollowing’s hunger.
  2. Capstones. You complete that tier’s Crossing + Mastery capstones (2.3+). Each takes training time; capstones may be pursued in parallel but each Mastery capstone needs its own focused practice.
  3. Cost. You pay the tier’s character-point cost for the new powers — OR, if your group plays capstone-only, the capstones are the cost and points are waived. (Full point schedule: Part 3.) A player in a hurry may also buy past a single Mastery capstone by paying its listed point surcharge instead of doing the training — but the Crossing capstone can never be bought; it must be earned in play.

Training time, at a glance

Each capstone lists a training time — the in-fiction weeks of focused work (Part 2 details study-session length and how attribute-alignment halves it). As a rule of thumb, capstones deeper in a tradition cost more time:

Tier reachedMastery capstone training (each)Crossing capstone
Tier II~2 weeksAn event in play (no fixed time)
Tier III~4 weeksAn event in play
Tier IV~8 weeksAn event in play, usually momentous
Tier V~16 weeks / a season of lifeOnce-in-a-saga

Attribute-aligned traditions (your primary Force attribute matches the tradition’s governing attribute) halve Mastery training time, per the Codex’s standing rule.

Dual Practice & the Leaning Grey

Most Force-users walk one road. A rare few hold the Concordance as their Still Point and channel a single poled tradition through it — and this is the only way anyone wields a tradition of the light or the dark without being dragged toward its pole. The clearest example is a Concordant who has chosen the works of the light. Its antithesis — a grey practitioner who has chosen to consume — is the same machine run in reverse.

Why only a Concordant can do this

The Concordance’s signature is this: emotion does not steer a true Concordant — they steer themselves. Healing a friend does not pull a true Concordant toward the light, because nothing pulls them; they chose it, and choosing what they already meant to choose does not pull them anywhere. This is the whole trick. A Jedi who heals is moved toward the light; a Sith who feeds is dragged toward the dark; a Concordant who does either stays grey while their hands do the work. The Still Point holds the centre; the companion tradition is only what the hands have chosen to practise.

Because of this, a Concordant does not need to meet the companion tradition’s usual prerequisite. A Concordant need never be a devoted servant of the light to wield the Living Path — their Code, held intact, is their qualification. The Code overrides the companion tradition’s gate. Break the Code and the whole arrangement collapses at once (below).

The Two Leanings

LeaningStill PointPermitted companionHard-barred
The Luminous Grey
light-leaning Concordant		Concordance of FeathersThe Living Path (Light) — chosen works of healing, defence, and guidanceThe Unchained Path & the Hollowing — utterly. The Code forbids it.
The Hollow Grey
the antithesis		Concordance of FeathersThe Hollowing — chosen works of consumption, the Force devoured by deliberate willThe Living Path — utterly. The chosen hunger forbids it.

There is no ”Unchained” leaning — the dark side of passion is the opposite of the Concordance in method (it surrenders choice to emotion), so a Concordant cannot wield it at all. The Hollowing is the dark mirror precisely because, like the Concordance, it is a path of will rather than passion — it is choosing to consume, coldly, which the grey discipline can hold. Hate cannot be held grey; hunger, terribly, can.

The Rules of the Leaning Grey
  1. Concordance is primary. You advance it as your Still Point, holding your Code. Your Concordance tier is your true rank.
  2. The companion caps one tier below your Concordance tier. Dividing one discipline two ways has a price: if you are Concordance Tier III (Sentinel), you may reach Living Path / Hollowing Tier II, and no higher, until your Concordance rises. A pure single-path user always out-climbs you in raw power on that pole — your strength is the breadth, not the height.
  3. The companion does not compromise you. So long as your Code holds, the mere act of using your companion tradition costs you nothing — no drift, no taint (the deeds still answer for themselves — feeding on an innocent is still an atrocity — but the tradition itself does not corrupt you the way it would anyone else).
  4. The companion’s own prerequisite is waived — replaced by your held Code. You never need the light’s devotion to heal, or the dark’s hunger to feed.
  5. The opposite pole is hard-barred. A Luminous Grey cannot learn, convert, or use any Unchained or Hollowing power — the attempt itself is a Code violation. A Hollow Grey cannot touch the Living Path. This is absolute; there is no dabbling across the centre.
Collapse — when the Still Point breaks

The arrangement is a discipline, not a permanent state, and it fails hard if its conditions fail:

  • If you break the Code, your Concordance climb pauses and — more dangerously — your companion tradition stops being a free choice. Until you return to the Code, using it carries the companion tradition’s ordinary cost, like anyone else’s. The grey discipline only protects you while you hold it.
  • If you break your Code, you lose the Concordance’s steering entirely until you atone (a Crossing-level act): emotion sets your direction again like an ordinary Force-user, and the companion tradition’s drift comes with it. A Luminous Grey who breaks faith may find themselves sliding — and, cut off from the discipline that barred the dark, newly able to fall toward it.
  • The antithesis is more fragile still. Holding the Hollowing grey is a knife’s edge: the hunger wants to become passion, and every feeding tests whether the Concordant still chooses it coldly or has begun to crave it. The first time craving replaces choice, the Still Point breaks — they become an ordinary Hollowing faller, and the grey is lost, usually for good.
Part Two

Learning & Training

How long it takes to learn what you know — by the hour

Power earned is not power given. A character may buy ability with points (Part 3), but in play most things are learned — at a desk, on a training floor, in the field, or alone in meditation with a crystal that only answers slowly. This part sets the hours. It governs three different things in three different ways: ordinary Skills, which can be studied or learned by doing; Force powers, Forms, and techniques, which must be learned deliberately and never level up just from use; and the special acquisitions — the Inscribed Voice’s conversions chief among them.

The Hour Standard

The base unit

All deliberate learning runs on study-hours. The campaign standard:

200 study-hours = 1 character point earned

A character point spent on a skill or ability is identical whether bought outright (Part 3) or earned at the desk — the hours simply convert. What changes from one thing to the next is how many points a level costs (its difficulty), how fast you may absorb the hours (session caps), and whether you may learn it by doing at all.

Learning Skills — Study Hours by Difficulty

A skill’s GURPS difficulty sets how many points each level costs, and therefore how many study-hours. Higher levels cost more because each step up the column is dearer.

DifficultyPoints for level 1 / 2 / 3 / 4+Study-hours to reach that level (cumulative)
Easy1 / 2 / 4 / +4 each200 / 400 / 800 / +800
Average2 / 4 / 8 / +4 each400 / 800 / 1600 / +800
Hard4 / 8 / 12 / +4 each800 / 1600 / 2400 / +800
Very Hard8 / 12 / 16 / +4 each1600 / 2400 / 3200 / +800

”Level” here means relative skill (attribute−1, attribute, attribute+1…). The point costs are standard GURPS; the hours are simply points × 200. Once a skill reaches attribute+1, every further +1 costs a flat 4 points (800 hours) regardless of difficulty — mastery plateaus the same for everyone.

3.2a · Attribute Scaling — the gift of a high stat

A naturally gifted mind or body learns faster in its element. Apply the better of these to study-hours for a skill:

ConditionEffect on study-hours
The skill’s governing attribute is 13–15−10% study-hours
The skill’s governing attribute is 16–18−20% study-hours
The skill’s governing attribute is 19+−30% study-hours
You hold a relevant Talent (e.g. a combat or science Talent covering the skill)−5% per level of Talent (max −20%, stacks with the above)
You have a living teacher with the skill at a higher level than your target−25% study-hours (the classic advantage of a master)
No teacher, no manual — pure self-teaching by trial+50% study-hours, and you cannot exceed attribute+0 without a teacher or text

3.2b · Study-Session Length — how much you can absorb at once

Hours do not stack infinitely. The mind saturates; hours studied past the daily cap are wasted. The cap depends on the skill’s difficulty (harder material tires you faster) and on IQ.

DifficultyMax productive study per dayWith high IQ (14+) / Single-Minded
Easy8 hours/day10 hours/day
Average6 hours/day8 hours/day
Hard4 hours/day6 hours/day
Very Hard3 hours/day4 hours/day

A single sitting tops out at 4 hours before a break is needed (1 hour rest resets it); the daily cap is the most useful hours, not raw clock time. Intensive study (a dedicated academy, nothing else in your life) lets you hit the cap every day; an adventurer studying between jobs typically banks 1–3 hours a day at best. Worked example: a Hard skill at attribute level (800 hrs), studied 4 hrs/day at an academy, takes ~200 days; the same skill with IQ 16 (−20% → 640 hrs) and a teacher (−25% → 480 hrs) at 6 hrs/day takes ~80 days.

In-Use Learning — Levelling by Doing (Skills Only)

You do not only learn at a desk. A skill used hard, repeatedly, under real stakes, improves on its own — the body and mind bank the lesson without spending a point. This applies to ordinary Skills only. Force powers, Forms, and techniques never level this way (2.4).

The Practice Track

Each skill quietly accumulates Use-Hours when used meaningfully — in genuine challenge or combat, not idle repetition. The GM tallies them loosely (a tense scene of lockpicking under fire is worth far more than an hour of practice locks). When a skill’s Use-Hours reach the threshold for its next level, it rises one level, free of points:

  • In-use threshold = ½ the study-hours for that level (doing teaches faster than reading — but only to a point).
  • Hard ceiling: in-use learning can raise a skill only up to attribute+2. Beyond that, true mastery requires deliberate study or points — the field teaches competence, not artistry.
  • Only meaningful use counts. The GM may rule a scene grants anywhere from a few minutes to several ”Use-Hours” of credit by how much it genuinely tested the skill.
  • Only one level per skill per real in-game month may be gained this way — the lesson needs time to set.

This is how a soldier becomes deadly without a classroom, and why a character’s most-used skills creep upward over a campaign. It rewards the way a character actually plays. (Optional grit: a critical success on a high-stakes use grants bonus Use-Hours; a critical failure that you survive and learn from grants a few as well — pain teaches.)

Learning Force Powers, Forms & Techniques

The Force is not banked by doing. You may use Force Lightning a thousand times and never learn a new power from it — the Force gives only what is deliberately reached for and understood. These are learned by training time alone, never by in-use levelling, and most carry prerequisites.

The Force-Learning Standard
  • A Force power’s learning time is set by its tier, not a difficulty column: Tier I ≈ 1 week, Tier II ≈ 3 weeks, Tier III ≈ 6 weeks, Tier IV ≈ 12 weeks, Tier V ≈ a season or a singular event (Legendary powers are usually earned by a capstone deed, not trained — Part 1).
  • Attribute alignment halves it: if the power’s governing attribute is your primary Force attribute, halve the learning time (the standing Codex rule).
  • A teacher who knows the power halves it again (stacks with alignment — a well-taught, aligned power learns in a quarter of the time). Self-teaching a power with no teacher and no text adds +100% and requires a successful Force roll at −4 to even begin.
  • You must meet the power’s prerequisites (2.4a) and your tradition tier must already permit that tier of power (Part 1). You cannot learn a Tier III power as a Tier II practitioner, taught or not.
  • No in-use levelling, ever. A power is known or it is not; it does not sharpen with use. (Your Force skill — the roll behind all your powers — is an ordinary skill and does follow 3.2/3.3. The powers it fires do not.)

3.4a · Prerequisites & Per-Ability Nuances

Many powers and techniques gate behind something specific. The pattern, by category:

CategoryTypical prerequisite to learnNuance
Light / Dark / Common powersTradition tier permits the power’s tier; lower-tier ”feeder” power where the Codex chains them (e.g. Force Heal before Revitalize).Some powers explicitly require another known first; the Codex notes these in the power’s entry.
Concordance workingsHeld to the Code + Concordance tier; a quiet mind (Meditation skill) for the perceptive workings.The reading workings (Surface/Deep Read) require Mind Block as a defensive counterpart before they can be learned.
Hollowing workingsHollowing tier; having fed at the matching scale (the act, not a roll, opens the next).No teacher possible — learning time is doubled and always ”self-derived,” reflecting the path’s solitude.
Inscribed VoiceThe ghostfire crystal; the working found in the Book of Darkness; the conversion ritual (2.5).Entirely specific to the Inscribed adept — see 3.5.
Saber FormsLightsaber skill threshold for the tier (14/16/18/20/22+); the previous Form-tier mastered.Learning a Form tier ≈ 4 weeks of drilling; the signature demonstration (Part 1) is what confirms it. A new Form from scratch (not a tier within one) ≈ 8 weeks.
Form-Neutral TechniquesListed prereq per technique (e.g. Saber Split requires Saber Throw); Lightsaber at a stated level.Each is one learnable unit, ~2–4 weeks. Trakata is forbidden: no teacher exists outside the two who use it, so self-teaching it costs ~6 months and a Lightsaber roll at −6 to begin — most who try simply injure themselves.
Bonded Blade signaturesA completed bonded saber whose crystal accepts you (a Sith forces it) + your matching Force primary.Not ”learned” over weeks — gained in a single deep bonding meditation (1 day) once the blade is bonded. One per character.

The Conversion of the Inscribed Voice

An Inscribed adept does not learn their workings the way anyone learns anything else. They convert them: take a dark Nightsister ritual out of the grimoire and re-derive it through a ghostfire crystal until it answers to the adept’s Will instead of to blood and bone. The full conditions are in Part 1 (1.7); here is the time and the procedure.

The Conversion Procedure
  1. Find it. The working must exist in the Book of Darkness as a Nightsister ritual. If it is not in the Book, it cannot be converted — only authored anew, and only at Clan Mother (Tier V, Part 1).
  2. Convert it. A focused ritual of Ritual Magic or Holocron Crafting, ~1 day per tier of the working (Tier II ≈ 2 days, III ≈ 3, IV ≈ 4), at the crystal, expending the Inscribement reagents and geometry. Roll the governing skill at the end; success converts the draft.
  3. Prove it. The converted working is a draft until cast successfully in earnest once — the crystal must hear it work. Only then is it truly known.
  4. On failure: a failed conversion is a damaged draft, not lost time — re-attemptable after one week at −2 (cumulative −2 per failure until success, then reset).
  5. The crystal is the engine. Lose the ghostfire crystal and every converted working goes inert until a new crystal is carved (months; Codex Book V). The adept’s knowledge is preserved but unusable until then.

This is deliberately slower and stranger than ordinary Force learning, because it is: the adept is the practitioner of a tradition being rebuilt by hand. Each working is a small act of creation, not a lesson — which is exactly why the Inscribed tier is gated by what has been converted and proven, not by any band or teacher.

Quick Reference — Time to Learn, at a Glance

WhatHow it’s learnedRough time (typical)In-use levelling?
Ordinary SkillStudy-hours by difficulty, or by doingEasy lvl1 ~200 hrs → Very Hard lvl3 ~3200 hrs (before scaling)Yes, to attribute+2
Force skill (the roll itself)As a Very Hard skill (study or use)Standard skill hoursYes (it is a skill)
Force power (I–V)Training time by tier; prereqs~1 wk (I) → a season/event (V)No
Saber Form tierDrilling + skill threshold + signature~4 wks/tier; ~8 wks a new FormNo
Form-Neutral TechniqueTraining + listed prereq~2–4 wks (Trakata ~6 months self-taught)No
Bonded Blade signatureOne bonding meditation~1 dayNo
Inscribed workingConvert from the Book via crystal~1 day per tier of the working + prove in playNo
Quick Reference

The Path Playbook

Everything above, condensed to the table edge — for the table

The whole framework at a glance. These tables summarise the rules detailed in Parts 1–3; where a quick line and the full text seem to differ, the full text governs.

QR-1 · The Capstone Gate — Consensus

To rise one tier in any tradition you must clear all three Gates: Standing, Capstones, and Cost. Capstone count scales with depth.

Reaching tierCrossingMastery capstonesTraining time (each Mastery)Buy-past?
Tier II1 (the committing act)1~2 weeksMastery only, by point surcharge
Tier III12~4 weeksMastery only
Tier IV13~8 weeksMastery only
Tier V1 — the Crossing is the mastery—A season / once-a-saga deedNever — must be earned in play

The Crossing capstone can never be bought — it is always a deed done in play. Mastery capstones may be skipped by paying their point surcharge (Part 3) instead of the training. Attribute-aligned traditions halve Mastery training time. The strict traditions (Concordance, Hollowing, Inscribed) may demand one extra condition at a tier, noted in their tables.

QR-2 · Training & Learning — Time by Type

WhatHow learnedTypical timeLevels in use?
Skill (Easy→Very Hard)200 hrs = 1 pt; by difficulty, ± attribute scalingEasy L1 ~200 hrs → V.Hard L3 ~3200 hrs (before scaling)Yes, to attribute+2
Force skill (the roll)As a Very Hard skillStandard skill hoursYes (it is a skill)
Force power I–VTraining time by tier + prereqs~1 wk (I) · 3 wk (II) · 6 wk (III) · 12 wk (IV) · season/event (V)No
Saber Form tierDrill + skill threshold + signature~4 wk/tier · ~8 wk a whole new FormNo
Form-Neutral TechniqueTraining + listed prereq~2–4 wk · Trakata ~6 months self-taughtNo
Bonded Blade signatureOne bonding meditation~1 dayNo
Inscribed workingConvert from the Book via the crystal, then prove~1 day per tier of the working + cast in earnestNo
Learning modifiers, in one line
  • Attribute alignment (power’s attribute = your Force primary): halve learning time. Teacher who knows it: halve again (skills: −25%).
  • High stat (skills): −10% (13–15) / −20% (16–18) / −30% (19+) study-hours. Self-taught, no text: +50% (skills) / +100% & a roll (Force).
  • In-use levelling (skills only): meaningful Use-Hours = ½ study-hours per level; caps at attribute+2; max one level per skill per month.
A consolidated reference of every Force tradition charted in this campaign — from the Living Force of the Jedi to the unrestrained passion of the Sith, the philosophical balance of Kreia’s Way, and the re-derived practice of the Holocron-Saber tradition. Includes the seven lightsaber Forms and the form-neutral techniques that bind them.
Old Republic Era  ·  GURPS 4E COMPANION  ·  The Eight Books, One Volume

Force Conditions & Bonds

Conditions of the Living Force — what a being is born into, and the ties that bind them.

Not every relationship with the Force is a matter of training. Some beings are born already shaped — open, closed, deafened, or tethered — and no amount of study moves them far from how they entered the galaxy. This section gathers those innate conditions, and the lasting effects that sit beneath any tradition, including the bonds that link one living being to another.

Conditions People Are Born With

Force-Blind (the Many). The overwhelming majority of sapient life. A Force-Blind being feels nothing of the Current, cannot be trained in any tradition, and registers to Force senses only as the ordinary warmth of a living thing. This is the baseline, not a flaw — most of the galaxy lives and dies without ever touching the Force.

Force-Sensitive (Born Open). A being born with the latent capacity to feel and, with training, wield the Force. Sensitivity alone grants only a faint passive awareness — a prickle before danger, a cold note at a nearby death — until a tradition is learned; it is the prerequisite every book in this volume assumes. Sensitives glow faintly to a practiced reader and can be picked out of a crowd.

Force-Muted (Deaf to the Current). A rare being born sensitive yet sealed: the capacity is present but walled off behind silence. A Force-Muted character can neither learn nor use Force powers — but is correspondingly almost impossible to touch through the Force. They gain +4 to resist any power that targets the mind, senses, or spirit, cannot be sensed, read, tracked, or located by Force means, and do not register on Force detection at all. Some are born this way; some are made so by deep trauma, a severance, or a Hollowing feeding. It is a fixed condition rather than a skill — once Muted, always Muted, unless a Legend-tier working (the Living Path’s Force Restoration, or the deeper rituals of the Inscribed) is spent deliberately to re-open them.

Force-Wound (Born in a Scar). A being conceived or born amid great Force-death — a massacred field, a murdered world, a dark nexus — can carry the scar inward. They read as a small cold absence rather than a living glow, unsettle animals and sensitives at close range (−1 to others’ reactions nearby), and gain +2 to resist fear and mind-affecting powers. Whether that hollowness is a wound or an armour is for the table to decide.

Born Bonded. Twins, rare siblings, or a parent and child who enter the galaxy already sharing a single thread of the Force. A Born Bond behaves exactly as a Force Bond (below) that was never chosen and cannot be ritually released — only severed, with every consequence that severance carries. Born-bonded pairs feel one another’s strong emotion and mortal danger across any distance, from their first breath.

Force Bonds

A Force Bond is a sustained, two-way tie between two living beings, woven through shared experience rather than hierarchy. Unlike the Master–Padawan or training bonds of the old Orders, the bonds described here are bidirectional and equal — and they carry real weight, and real risk. Any Force-sensitive may form them; the Born Bonded condition above is simply the case where a being never had the choice.

Force Bonds — In Play

The Practice. Like Meetra Surik (the Jedi Exile), a bond-former possesses an unusual capacity for forging permanent two-way Force Bonds with willing companions. Unlike Master–Padawan or training bonds, these are not hierarchical — they are bidirectional, sustained by shared experience, deepened by trauma or trust. Forming a bond requires 1 week of shared travel and a final shared meditation, or a single shared moment of life-threatening crisis where both parties consciously reach for each other through the Force.

Carrying Capacity. A bond-former may hold up to Will ÷ 6 active bonds simultaneously. Bonds may be voluntarily released through ritual (no consequences) or severed through betrayal (1 month of Force disturbance and −2 Will until reconciled or fully released).

Passive Effects of Bonds. While bonded, both parties share faint emotional resonance over any distance: a rough sense of well-being, alarm if the other is in mortal danger, and the ability to find each other within 10 km without other aid. Force-sensitive bonded parties may attempt to send single sentences across the bond at a cost of 2 FP (sender) and a Will roll.

Active Uses. Bonds enable: Mother’s Calling (summoning of living bonded); Bound Voice may sometimes channel a bonded living companion’s expertise (GM call); Inscribed Future may use a bonded ally’s action as a trigger condition.

The Risk of Bonds. When a bonded companion dies, the survivor takes 1d mental fatigue and is Shaken for 1d turns if conscious, regardless of distance. If she is the direct cause of their death, the consequence is worse: the same effect plus a permanent −1 to Will until she has formed three new bonds to replace the one she shattered.

The Eight Books

Each section traces one tradition’s approach to the Force.
◆The Bonded BladeAttribute Signatures · opening · one per Force primary
IThe Living PathLight side powers · Tiers I–V
IIThe Common ForceNeutral powers · available to all
IIIThe Concordance of FeathersKreia’s Way · light-neutral · the Force passes through
IVThe HollowingThe path of hunger · dark-neutral · the Force devoured
VThe Inscribed VoiceNightsister practice re-derived · holocron and saber
VIThe Unchained PathDark side powers · Tiers I–V Legendary
VIIThe Nine FormsLightsaber combat philosophies · Initiate to Battle Master
VIIIThe Form-Neutral TechniquesSaber Throw, Blade Barrier, Trakata, and the rest
+Minor TraditionsIktotchi foresight, Baran Do sky-craft & Zeltron empathy · the smaller books, one conglomerate
OPENING

THE BONDED BLADE

Attribute Signatures
One signature for each Force primary attribute. A character has one primary — and so commands one of these, the way their connection to the Force expresses itself through a bonded blade.
ST · WILL · DX · HT — ONE PER WIELDER

The Bonded Blade

Attribute Signatures · one per Force primary

The Bonded Blade — Attribute Signatures

Before the paths and the powers comes the blade itself.

Alignment req.None — open to any wielder; the blade answers to nature, not doctrine.
Force skillYour signature rides your Force primary attribute — ST, DX, Will, or HT. You command the one that matches it, and only that one.
TiersNone. This is not a studied Book — it has no tiers.
Capstone & CrossingNone. Granted whole by the bond, not earned in steps.
To buy5 points. Prerequisite: a bonded lightsaber built around a crystal that has accepted you — for a Sith, a crystal bent to obey (a bled or synthetic crystal forced to listen) — and a Force primary attribute. Yours once both are met; dormant if the blade is lost.

Before the paths and the powers comes the blade itself. A Force-user who bonds a lightsaber to their connection finds that the bond expresses their primary Force attribute in a single signature manner — a way of fighting that belongs to no Form and no tradition, but to the wielder’s own nature.

There are four, one for each possible Force primary: Force Torrent (ST), Force Vessel (Will), Blade Velocity (DX), and Tempered Blade (HT). A character has exactly one Force primary, and therefore commands exactly one of these signatures. It is theirs from the moment the blade is bonded, underpinning everything that follows in the books — it is granted by the bond itself, not studied as one of them.

How it is gained. The signature is not a Book — it costs a flat 5 points, not tiered study. It unlocks once two things are true: you carry a bonded lightsaber whose crystal has accepted you (a Sith instead forces the crystal to listen — a bled or synthetic crystal bent to obey), and you have a Force primary attribute (ST, DX, Will, or HT). The primary determines which of the four signatures is theirs — exactly one, never more. Lose the bonded blade and the signature goes dormant until a blade is bonded again. On the character sheet it lives in its own Bonded Blade panel, separate from the studied Books, and appears the instant the bond and primary are set.

Force Torrent

While you hold your bonded blade, your own muscle drives it: add your ST swing damage to a swung saber strike, and your…
RollST — Quick Contest vs the target’s STActionPer swing; Saber Bash is reactive on a ParryRangeMeleeDefenceA Dodge avoids the grind entirely; meeting the blow with any guard invites the ST contestFP2 per swing; 4 for a Saber BashReactionNo — passive; Saber Bash rides a Parry

Effect — Strength in the blade. While you hold your bonded blade, your own muscle drives it: add your ST swing damage to a swung saber strike, and your ST thrust damage to a thrust, on top of the blade’s own damage. This is the single exception to the rule that lightsabers take no ST-based swing or thrust — the bond carries your strength into the cut. Always on; no FP, no roll.

The grind. On a melee attack, spend 2 FP. If the target meets it with anything physical — a Parry, a Block, or any held weapon, shield, or object — make a Quick Contest of ST. Win and that defence is −1 from now on, stacking on top of the normal penalty for repeated defences (any parrying tool counts: sword, vibroblade, staff, braced shield). Repeat it every swing to pile the penalty up.

REACTIVESaber Bash. When an enemy strikes you in melee and you Parry successfully, spend 4 FP and make a Quick Contest of ST. Win and you interrupt their attack; if they also fail, you knock them back 1 hex; beat them by more than 10 and you disarm them as well.

Where another duelist seeks the gap, the Torrent makes one — every blow lands a fraction harder than the last, until the guard itself begins to fail. No trick to it: only weight, and the will to keep applying it.

Limits. The grind does nothing against a Dodge — the blade never touches a guard, so the 2 FP is spent for nothing. Saber Bash fires once per turn cycle, and not at all on a cycle you spent in All-Out Attack.

Force Vessel

Bank activation or end-effect powers inside the blade — maintained passives can’t be stored.
RollWill to store; each released power then rolls its ownActionStore at casting; release on any swing or a successful ParryRangeMelee (on release)DefenceEach released power’s own resistance, at a flat −5 (deeper with imbued FP)FPthe stored power’s own at storage, plus extra at releaseReactionNo — released on a swing or Parry

Effect — Store. Bank activation or end-effect powers inside the blade — maintained passives can’t be stored. Stored workings are invisible to Force Sense and keep for up to 6 hours with no concentration rolls; fight, run, sleep, or cast other powers freely while they wait. They shake loose against your will only if the saber is disarmed involuntarily or the blade is damaged.

Release. On any swing of yours (no hit needed), or the instant your blade meets an attack on a successful Parry, the target is hit by as many stored powers as you choose to spend. All fire at once, each rolling its own resistance.

Penalties. Every released power is resisted at a flat −5. On top of that you may pour extra FP into one named power before the strike — each 1 FP is a further −1 to resist that power, with no cap. Only one power may be imbued per release; declare which power and how much FP before you swing or parry.

The blade hums with nothing anyone can sense — then it touches a guard, and three workings unfold at once from what looked like only steel. The patient duelist does not announce their power; they carry it sheathed until the blade speaks for them.

Limits. Passives can’t be stored — activation and end-effect powers only. An involuntary disarm or a damaged blade spills everything held. The −5 and any imbued FP must be declared before the strike resolves.

Blade Velocity

Your Step is 2 hexes instead of 1 on any maneuver that includes a Step (Step-and-Attack, Attack with a Step,…
Roll— (no roll; always on)ActionPassive while the bonded blade is heldRangeSelf / meleeDefence—FPNone (a released defense still pays its own cost)ReactionNo — passive

Effect — Doubled Step. Your Step is 2 hexes instead of 1 on any maneuver that includes a Step (Step-and-Attack, Attack with a Step, Move-and-Attack). Always on while the bonded blade is held; no FP, no roll.

Second defence. Once per turn you may make one extra Force-powered active defence — a second Parry or Block — free of the usual penalty for stacking defences.

The blade is never quite where the enemy expects, because its wielder is always already a half-step elsewhere. Economy, not flourish — every motion bought, none wasted.

Limits. The extra defence is once per turn and grants no extra attack; you still pay the FP for the defence’s own effect. The Step doubling itself is free.

Tempered Blade

When you Parry an energy attack (blaster bolt, lightsaber, Force Lightning, any burn or shock), reduce the damage that…
RollHT-based (see effect)ActionConcentrate to raise, then maintainedRangeSelfDefence— (pure-defensive self-buff)FP3 to activate, then 1 per turnReactionNo

Effect — Bleed. When you Parry an energy attack (blaster bolt, lightsaber, Force Lightning, any burn or shock), reduce the damage that leaks through by HT ÷ 3, rounded down. Catching Force Lightning on a lit blade forms a Lightning Lock (Doc I §Saber Lock): Tempered Blade grants +1 to that sustained contest and still bleeds any leaked damage by HT ÷ 3.

Anchor. +3 to resist Disarm, Saber Bind, Sundering Strike, knockback, and forced movement (Force Push / Grip / Whirlwind, grappling). You may also use HT in place of ST — at that same +3 — on any roll made in your own defence: resisting or shaking off a Strength-based effect on you (a shove, a grapple, being moved), and on Force rolls that would otherwise key off ST. It never applies to a contest you start against someone else — grappling, shoving, or forcing another still uses your true ST — and it never covers Will- or DX-based resistance.

Hold. Ignore the first shock penalty each turn, and take +3 HT to stay conscious, resist stun, and shrug off knockdown.

Endure. The buff needs no concentration to hold: the damage-while-concentrating penalty doesn’t apply, and being hit never forces a Will roll to keep it up. Nothing short of falling unconscious shakes it loose.

Some blades cut. This one simply does not yield — the wall that will not fall outlasts the storm not by answering it, but by refusing, breath after breath, to break.

Limits. Pure-defensive — it adds nothing to your attacks. The HT-for-ST swap is for your own resistance and Force rolls only; it never powers a contest against another, and never replaces Will or DX.

BOOK I

THE LIVING PATH

Light Side Powers
There is no emotion, there is peace.
There is no ignorance, there is knowledge.
There is no passion, there is serenity.
There is no chaos, there is harmony.
There is no death, there is the Force.
— The Jedi Code
TIERS I · II · III · IV · LEGEND

The Light Side

A path of patience, restraint, and the Living Force

The Light Side — the Living Path

Discipline, compassion, and the serenity of the light. The broad road, and the most forgiving.

Alignment req.Enter at Attuned+ (+5); the deeper tiers expect Devoted (+45+). Dark powers are barred.
Force skillIQ-based; serenity favours a cool head. A power whose attribute matches your Force primary is learned in half the time.
TiersI Initiate · II Knight · III Master · IV Grandmaster · V Legend.
Capstone & CrossingThe light is gated by character, not power — each tier-up is a deed of restraint or sacrifice, and the deep tiers demand a Trait Gate and a chosen Path of Light (below).
To buyTier costs 8 / 16 / 30 / 50 / 80; single powers priced individually by tier.

Capstones & the Paths of Light

The light is gated by character: you cannot climb on power alone, you climb by becoming the kind of person the light answers to. There are branching paths of devotion — choose the trait-shape of your light.

Trait Gate — the cap on how high you climb
  • To reach Tier III (Master): you must hold at least Pacifism (Reluctant Killer) or Charitable or Empathy — a demonstrable kinship with life. Without one, you cap at Tier II no matter how strong you are.
  • To reach Tier IV (Grandmaster): reluctance is not enough. You must take a binding Code of Honor (Jedi), Sense of Duty (all living things), or a Vow — and pick a Path of Light below, which shapes (and caps) your highest powers.

The Paths of Light (choose at Tier IV)

PathDemandsGrants / caps
The GuardianSense of Duty (the innocent); will fight to protectFull access to Barrier, Valor, Wall of Light; healing powers cap one tier lower.
The ConsularPacifism (Cannot Kill) or (Self-Defence Only)Full access to all healing, Stasis, mind-soothing, Restoration; offensive light (Electric Judgment) capped.
