The Hollow Crown

You wear your emptiness like a crown, and everything living within reach kneels to the dread of it.
AffinityWillActionRitual (minutes to set), then sustainedRange20 hexes, no line-of-sightDefenceContest Will vs your Force skill each turn (fail: can’t attack or approach you, −4; pass: −2 & a fright check); allies feel it tooFP10, then 3/minReactionNoPrereqTier IV · The Wound That Walks

Effect. You wear your emptiness like a crown, and everything living within reach kneels to the dread of it. While the Crown holds, every being that fails the Will contest is overcome by the certainty that resisting you is futile — they cannot attack you, cannot flee toward you, and suffer −4 to all rolls made in your presence. Those who pass still feel it: −2 and a fright check on first exposure. The weak-willed may simply fall to their knees. This is not mind control; it is the animal knowledge that a predator beyond argument has entered the room.

SCALABILITY. +5 FP extends the crown to everyone who can hear of you within the hour (a besieged garrison's nerve breaking before you arrive); a being you have personally drained with the Maw is at an additional −3 against the Crown forever.

You do not raise your voice. You do not raise your hand. You simply let them feel, all at once, the size of the hole you have become — and the room stops being a room full of soldiers and becomes a room full of prey deciding whether to run.

Sustaining the Crown radiates dread onto allies too, who take the −2 (though they may act); few followers stay loyal to a thing they cannot stop being afraid of. Maintaining it is an open, screaming beacon in the Force — any adept within a system feels exactly where you are.

Unbecoming

The Abyss can be turned inward.
Roll—ActionReaction (or 1 turn) — escape any one effectRangeSelfDefenceNone — you simply weren’t there; paid not in FP but in permanent self (an attribute point, a skill level, a memory, a relationship — your choice)FPnoneReactionYes — uses your Reaction; also a 1-turn action on your turnPrereqTier IV · Eclipse of the Self

Effect. The Abyss can be turned inward. By unmaking a piece of your own existence you buy an absolute escape from any single effect: a killing blow, a mind-domination, a Force binding, a Sealed working — you simply were not there to be subject to it. Declare Unbecoming and the thing fails to find you. The cost is paid not in fatigue but in self: each use permanently spends one of the following, your choice — a point of an attribute, a level of a Force skill, a memory the table treats as gone, or a relationship that simply ceases to have happened in your mind. The Force does not refund it.

SCALABILITY. There is no scaling. There is only how much of yourself you are willing to stop being. A character may Unbecome as many times as they can bear; most cannot bear it twice.

SELF. Something that was you a moment ago is now a smooth, painless absence — a name you no longer answer to, a skill your hands have forgotten, a face you cannot picture. You do not grieve it. That is the worst part.

The blow that should have ended you passes through the place you stopped occupying. You are still standing. You are also a little less — and you cannot quite remember what.

Unbecoming is the dark mirror of sacrifice: where the Light gives the self for others, the Abyss spends the self to preserve itself, and the GM should track each loss as a step toward becoming the Wound That Walks in full — a being with nothing left to lose because there is nothing left of it to lose.

The Last Silence

The capstone of the whole tradition, and the thing the Order most fears the Hollowing can produce.
AffinityWillActionRitual (a long stillness), then self-feedingRangecentred on you, widening one hex per turn; no line-of-sightDefenceContest Will vs your Force skill (cumulative penalty as it grows) — severed for the scene, or forever on a crit fail; the caster is not immuneFP15 to begin, then feeds itselfReactionNoPrereqTier IV · all other Devouring Abyss workings

Effect. The capstone of the whole tradition, and the thing the Order most fears the Hollowing can produce. You open a Silence — a growing sphere in which the Force itself is consumed. Inside it, no power can be cast, no Force-sense functions, no living connection to the current persists; Force-users feel as if they have gone deaf and blind in a sense they did not know they had. Each turn the sphere widens by a hex and every Force-user inside must win the Will contest (at a cumulative penalty as it grows) or have their connection to the Force severed for the rest of the scene — and on a critical failure, possibly forever. To non-sensitives it is only a terrible cold and a dread; to the Force-using, it is a small, spreading death of the soul.

SCALABILITY. Pour in more of yourself (FP, then HP, then Unbecomings) and the Silence grows faster and reaches further; a Silence fed without limit at a Force Nexus is how legends say a world goes quiet.

You sit down in the middle of the room and begin, very gently, to stop. The light does not go out — the meaning does. One by one the bright, humming presences around you flicker and then are simply not there, and the quiet that replaces them is the quietest thing that has ever been.

The Last Silence is the single most Monstrous act in the common books — an atrocity against the Force itself, and an instant, maximal fall to the dark for anyone who completes it. The caster is not immune: hold the Silence too long and your own connection begins to gutter inside it, and a Hollowing adept has more than once become the first permanent casualty of their own great working. This is the act that ends who you were — the Crossing of the dark, paid in the death of every spark within reach.

The Endless Mouth

Roll your Force attribute. A consuming void opens, widening 1 hex/minute; everything living inside takes 1d injury/minute (HT halves)…
AffinityWillActionRitual (minutes), or instinctive at a death-rich siteRangeRegion — a void widening one hex per minuteDefenceResisted by HT (halves); 1d injury/min to all living insideFPvariableReactionNo

Effect. Roll your Force attribute. A consuming void opens, widening 1 hex/minute; everything living inside takes 1d injury/minute (HT halves) and feeds you. A lesser Feast of Worlds.

BODY. A whispered un-name and the jaw left hanging open; at a death-rich site it can open near-instinctively, without the full ritual.

A door left open, and what comes through is appetite.

Once open it feeds on all life within — friend, and self, if they linger; closing it is far harder than opening it.

Eat the Name

Roll your Force attribute vs Will. On success the target's Force is permanently severed — they become Force-Muted (see Force Conditions…
AffinityWillActionConcentrate (2 turns)RangeTouch or 4 hexesDefenceResisted by Will (or Force skill if higher); on a win the target is permanently Force-MutedFP18ReactionNo

Effect. Roll your Force attribute vs Will. On success the target's Force is permanently severed — they become Force-Muted (see Force Conditions & Bonds) — and you gain 3d FP.

BODY. A hand laid over the victim's mouth and two full turns of silence; the hand stays and the silence holds, or the working fails.

You take the one thing that cannot be given back.

What you sever cannot be restored by any ordinary means — they are Muted, perhaps forever.

BOOK V

THE INSCRIBED

Voice
Nightsister practice severed and re-derived through ghostfire crystal and bound holocrons.
A HOLOCRON-SABER TRADITION · 25 WORKINGS

The Inscribed Voice

A Severance from Dathomir · Re-Derivation Through Saber and Holocron

The Inscribed Voice — a Severance from Dathomir

Nightsister workings re-derived through a ghostfire crystal and bound holocrons — dark practice converted into something new. A working is this tradition’s word for a single casting of a Force power — one worked piece of Force-craft.

Alignment req.None — the crystal does not judge. A grey, dark-capable craft (its necromantic workings are barred at Light nexuses); gated not by allegiance but by what you have converted and proven.
Force skillWill-based. Each working names its own Resist contest — HT, Will or Mind Block, Lightsaber parry, ST, or IQ, per the target.
TiersI Initiate · II Adept · III Witch · IV High Priestess · V Clan Mother.
Capstone & CrossingEach tier-up converts a deeper Nightsister working through the crystal and proves it in earnest (full conditions and deeds below).
To buyTier costs 8 / 16 / 30 / 50 / 80; single workings priced individually by tier.

The holocron economy. Every working has a base form and a stronger bolstered form fed by consuming an inscribed holocron face. The ghostfire saber is the conduit, the holocron the page; lose the crystal and the whole codex goes inert.

Capstones, Conditions & the Crystal

The Inscribed Voice is the rarest of paths — typically walked by a lone practitioner, and its progression is therefore unusually specific: the adept re-derives Nightsister grand-ritual workings through a ghostfire crystal and bound holocrons, converting dark practice into something new. Their tier is not gated by allegiance at all — the crystal does not judge — but by what has been converted and proven.

The Specific Conditions of the Inscribed Voice
  1. The source: a Nightsister grimoire. The adept must hold a Nightsister grimoire (a ”Book of Darkness”). Each new working must first be found in the Book as a dark Nightsister ritual, then converted — re-derived through the ghostfire crystal so it answers to the adept’s Will-based Force skill rather than blood-and-bone sorcery.
  2. The conversion ritual. Converting one working requires a focused ritual: (Ritual Magic or Holocron Crafting) work of roughly 1 day per tier of the working being converted (Tier II ≈ 2 days, Tier III ≈ 3, Tier IV ≈ 4), performed at the adept’s crystal, expending reagents per the Inscribement geometry. A failed conversion is not wasted time but a damaged draft — re-attemptable after a week, at −2.
  3. The proof. A converted working is not ”known” until cast successfully in earnest once — the crystal must hear it work. Until then it is a draft inscribed but unspoken.
  4. The crystal is everything. Lose the ghostfire crystal and the entire Inscribed tradition goes inert until a new one is carved (a months-long undertaking, Codex Book V). The adept’s tier is preserved, but unusable, until then.
ReachCrossing capstoneMastery capstones
II · Adept of the VoiceThe First Conversion — successfully convert and cast one Tier-II Nightsister working from the Book through the crystal.(1) Bind a defeated spirit cleanly and draw on its bound voice in a real fight.
III · Witch of the CrystalThe Geometry Mastered — perform a full Grand Ritual (the eight-component, three-ring geometry) without flaw, converting a Tier-III working.(1) Remove a key foe from a fight with Coffin Mist. (2) Convert a working at a Force Nexus, halving its cost.
IV · High PriestessThe Lattice Unleashed — channel a converted working at Unleashed scale (e.g. a star-system Battle Meditation via Ritual Enhancement), the crystal and bound holocrons singing as one.(1) Convert a Tier-IV working. (2) Bind a powerful spirit and hold it. (3) Survive losing and re-carving understanding of the crystal’s limits.
V · Clan MotherThe Voice Made One’s Own — author an original working that exists in no Nightsister book — pure re-derivation, the tradition no longer borrowed but the adept’s own.The original working is the mastery; the adept has become a source, not a student.

These workings were once an inheritance from Dathomir — death-rooted, ichor-veined, sung in the green dark of the Nightsisters. The practitioner who carries this codex has severed yourself from that line. You did not lose the shape of what you knew. You re-derived it.

What you carry now runs through two media: a ghostfire-crystal lightsaber — whose blade is invisible to most eyes but pulses teal when you channel — and the small holocrons you imbue through ritual meditation. The saber is the conduit. The holocron is the page.

Every small holocron has three outward faces. Each face can be bound, through a full meditative ritual, to bolster a single specific working. When you draw on a face, you consume the binding for the bolstered version of that working; the face remains dim until re-inscribed during your next ritual cycle.

One holocron is set apart from the rest: the Soul Bind holocron. It holds up to four mindless soul-tethers, drawn from those who fell to your hand and would not return to the Force on their own. The bound do not speak. They acknowledge. They resonate. They make the deeper workings possible.

All workings here are governed by you Will-based Force skill. Resistance contests follow GURPS standard practice (Characters p. B349): the target rolls the most relevant attribute or skill — HT against physical drain, Will or Mind Block against mental intrusion, the Lightsaber skill against energy parry, ST against grappling effects, IQ against perception trickery, and so on. Each working specifies the contest in its Resist line.

Critical strategy: most of these workings were ritual at their root. They still answer best when prepared. Imbue holocron faces before the moment of need, not after.

Holocron & Saber Mechanics

Quick Reference

The Saber as Conduit. CONVERTED   Non-bolstered workings require the saber ignited to channel. Bolstered (face-consumed) workings do not require the saber lit — the holocron carries the channel itself. A drawn but unlit saber will not channel.

Reading the Stat Block. Somatic = the motion, word, gesture, or focus the working physically requires (a free hand, the voice, eye contact, a full ritual). Activation = the maneuver required to begin (Instant, Concentrate N turns, Ritual). Range = how far the working’s effect reaches. Casting Point = where the effect originates (the practitioner, a hex you can see, touch, etc.). Duration = how long the effect persists. Resist = the contest the target uses to resist, per GURPS standard contest rules. Holocrons = the number and kind of holocrons required to perform or bolster the working. Reagents / Site = where ritual workings demand them, ingredients consumed and conditions of the place.

The full economy of holocron fragments, small and large holocrons, face inscription rules, the Soul Bind holocron, and the ritual ladder is detailed in the following two sections.

Holocrons — The Currency of the Craft

Fragments · Small · Large · The Soul Bind Holocron

Holocron Fragments. RARE   Holocrons of any size are crafted from holocron fragments — shards of resonant crystalline lattice harvested from broken ancient holocrons, mined from rare Lothal cave-formations, or pulled from the wreckage of Sith temples. Fragments are very rare: a single fragment is worth roughly a starship’s payload of spice on the black market, and Force-sensitives in the know will trade favours or lives for them. The GM should treat finding a fragment as a meaningful event.

Construction. RITUAL   Holocrons are built from fragments and frames, fused in a forging ritual.

Small holocron: 10 fragments + 1 holocron frame, fused over a 7-day forging ritual. Has 3 outward faces available for inscription.

Large holocron: 50 fragments (or a complete holocron kit) + 1 kyber crystal (carved into small seed-pieces) + 5 holocron frames, fused over a 30-day forging ritual at a prepared site. Either forging ends with a Ritual Magic (or Holocron Crafting) roll: success fuses the lattice; failure damages the matrix — the fragments are not lost, but the forging must be re-attempted after a week of repair, at −2. Has 7 outward faces. The carved kyber seeds are what give the large lattice its capacity for multiple distinct harmonic signatures — and its ability to anchor a Mist-Fold link indefinitely (see Mist-Fold, Tier IV).

The Soul Bind holocron is always a small holocron whose faces are sealed and replaced with 4 tether-slots (it cannot also hold face-inscriptions; the tether function and face function are mutually exclusive). Holocron frames are manufactured lattice-housings — less rare than fragments, available from specialist artificers or salvaged from inert holocrons, but still not something found in a general market.

Specialisation of Small Holocrons. RULE   A small holocron may only be imbued with one type of working across all its faces. All three faces of a given small holocron bolster the same working — multiple charges of the same effect, but no diversification. This is a structural property of the small lattice: it can hold one harmonic signature deeply, or none at all. A practitioner with two small holocrons therefore carries two distinct working-types, each available up to three times. A small holocron may be wiped clean over a 24-hour reset meditation if you wish to switch its bound working.

Versatility of Large Holocrons. RULE   A large holocron’s 7 faces may each hold a different working — the deeper lattice can carry up to seven distinct harmonic signatures simultaneously. This is the principal mechanical reason to seek a large holocron despite its enormous cost: not just more faces, but the freedom to mix.

Face Inscription. RITUAL   Each face binds to one specific working as its bolstered version. Inscription is a Ritual Magic session — 1 hour per face, uninterrupted, in a place suitable for meditation. A face once bound stays bound until consumed (one bolstered activation) or deliberately wiped during meditation (1 hour to wipe a face, no FP cost).

Face Consumption. RESOURCE   Activating a bolstered working consumes the face. The face stays dim until re-inscribed in the next ritual cycle. The base version of any working is always available without face consumption.

RULE Drawing from Another's Holocron. Any working channeled into a holocron face may be activated by someone other than its creator, using the holocron as the medium: the drawer holds the lattice, speaks the working, and pays the working's full FP cost themselves (bolstered cost if the activation consumes a face). Two ways in: ① Willing attunement — the creator opens the lattice to a named person (a minute of joint meditation); attuned users simply draw, no roll. ② Force the lattice — an unattuned drawer rolls a Quick Contest: their Force attribute vs the crafter's Ritual Magic skill — the holocron defends with its maker's craft, even from across the galaxy or beyond the grave. Win: the working activates. Lose: the lattice stays sealed and the attempt costs 2 FP. Critical failure: the face discharges at the drawer (GM applies the working's effect against them, or 1d Force backlash if that's meaningless). Soul-tethers cannot be drawn by anyone but their binder; a shell has nothing to give. Non-Force-sensitives feel only cold glass.

Soul Bind Holocron. NECRO   A specific small holocron set aside to hold up to 4 mindless soul-tethers (see Soul Bind, Tier III). Tethered souls power Bound Voice, contribute to Echo Shriek, anchor Holocron Sentry and The Borrowed Face, and may be drawn upon by Mother’s Calling. Soul-tethers do not occupy face slots — the Soul Bind holocron’s faces are repurposed for the tether function. A second Soul Bind holocron may be prepared at any time, but no single practitioner may carry more than two; tether-slots are not pooled across holocrons (each holds its own 4).

Shell Holocrons — Anchors and Links. RULE   A small holocron with no working imbued on any face is a shell — an empty lattice. Shells are not wasted: they are the anchors of the Mist-Fold portal working (Tier IV), and they serve as the inert link-points a Mist-Fold ritual binds together. A shell holds a portal-link the way the Soul Bind holocron holds a tether — it is a slot for a connection rather than a charge of power. A shell may be inscribed with a working at any time (a 24-hour reset is not needed to fill an empty face, only to wipe an existing one), at which point it ceases to be a shell and can no longer anchor a portal-link until cleared again. Carrying several shells is how you keep multiple Mist-Fold destinations prepared.

RULE Portal charge & the font of power. A Mist-Fold transit drains its anchors: small holocrons used in a portal must be re-linked and recharged after the fold (full preparation — 1 hour of Ritual Magic per link) before they will carry another. The exception is a large holocron set up at a font of Force power (a nexus): it feeds on the place, holds its link indefinitely, and any small holocron folded to it needs no recharge afterward. You may fold to the large holocron itself as a destination. Links may be re-arranged — break a small holocron's link and bind it to the large one (or to a different small) with the same full preparation; a lattice holds one link at a time. The fold window remains 3 seconds, whatever the anchor.

The Ritual Ladder

GURPS Ritual Magic — Time, Site, and Sacrifice

RULE Joined Workings — bringing friendlies into the circle. Workings whose effect is protective, perceptive, or concealing (not necromantic, not offensive) may be extended to willing allies you include in the ritual: each participant must remain inside the circle for the entire casting and may do nothing else while it runs. Each ally beyond yourself costs +2 FP (bolstered: +3 FP) and the working may carry at most Ritual Magic ÷ 4 (round down) recipients including you. One roll covers the whole circle — a failure fails everyone, and a critical failure picks its victim at random. Qualifying workings include Witchsight, Phantom Stride, Resonant Touch, Etched Counter, Inscribed Future (one shared omen, read once), and riding a Mist-Fold (passengers were always permitted — this formalises it). Necromantic workings (Soul Bind, Coffin Mist, Curse of the Lingering, Ghostfire Horror) and The Borrowed Face follow their own targeting text instead. The GM may admit or refuse edge cases by the same test: would a Light nexus smile on sharing it?

Workings tagged RITUAL require uninterrupted ceremonial effort — not the brief Concentrate maneuver. Per GURPS Ritual Magic conventions (B242), any interruption restarts the ritual at zero progress, and certain ceremonial elements must be honoured throughout: the practitioner cannot eat, sleep, or leave the inscribed site without breaking the ritual.

Brief Rituals. 1–4 hours, 1 small holocron, simple meditation. Inscribing a face. Holocron Tracing. Inscribed Future’s base form.

Major Rituals. 8–24 hours, 1–2 small holocrons or 1 large, marked circle (chalk, blood, or inscribed holocron-runes), specific reagents, a fixed site. Soulforge. Holocron Sentry (briefer cousin: 10 minutes + corpse + tether).

Grand Rituals. Days to a week, multiple holocrons, an inscribed site with holocron-runes arranged in a specific geometry, reagents that must be gathered specifically (rare components, sometimes living, sometimes from the target), bound to the prepared site — if the practitioner leaves the marked circle before completion, the ritual cancels and the FP, reagents, and time are lost.

Holocron-Runes. Geometric inscriptions made by carving, drawing, or burning patterns matching the practitioner’s holocron-lattice into the ground or walls of the ritual site. Each rune takes 1 hour per square metre to inscribe properly; a Grand Ritual’s site typically needs 12–30 such runes arranged in concentric figures. Once inscribed, the runes remain dormant until the ritual activates them; afterward, they fade over 1d days unless re-inscribed.

Sacrifice. The most demanding workings require something given up — a soul-tether consumed, a holocron face permanently destroyed (not merely consumed), a permanent point of FP, or a meaningful object. Each working specifies its own.

Ritual Enhancement

Folding a Working’s Power Through the Lattice

The Principle. What an ordinary Force-user achieves through long meditation, a holocron-adept achieves through ritual. Ritual Enhancement is the Inscribed Voice’s general method for taking any Force ability — your own workings, or any Force power you know from any tradition — and, in the space of a prepared ritual, folding it into a markedly stronger, sharper, or larger version of itself. Where the Common Force’s Meditation Enhancement trades time for power, Ritual Enhancement trades ritual intensity — holocron faces, inscribed runes, and your ghostfire crystal as catalyst — for the same result, usually faster, and at the high end, further than meditation alone can reach.

Activation. RITUAL   A Ritual Enhancement is a minimum 1-hour ritual, performed with the ghostfire crystal as catalyst and at least one holocron face dedicated to the working being enhanced. The hour may be extended for greater effect (see Scaling). At completion, the enhanced version of the chosen ability is “held” — ready to release — for a window the GM sets (typically until next used, or until your next full rest), much as a bolstered holocron face holds a charge.

What It Does. A Ritual-Enhanced working may, at your choice during preparation:

Double its output. Whatever defines the ability — range, duration, damage, healing, area, lift capacity, resistance penalty — is roughly doubled. A doubled Force Push hurls a speeder; a doubled heal knits an organ; a doubled Battle Meditation reaches across a battlefield.

Focus and sharpen it. Instead of doubling raw output, the working becomes more precise and harder to resist — tighter targeting, reduced collateral, a meaningful penalty (−2 to −4) to any resistance roll the target makes, or the removal of a normal limitation (a touch-range power gains short range; a single-target power affects two).

Scaling. RULE   The base 1-hour ritual delivers one enhancement (double or focus). You may push further by investing more:

More time. Each additional hour (up to a few) allows either a second enhancement option to stack (double and focus), or a single deeper effect (e.g. triple instead of double for the grandest workings, at the GM’s discretion).

More ritual. Consuming additional holocron faces, inscribing a full rune-circle, or working at a Force Nexus that accepts you can substitute for time — achieving the deeper effect in the base hour, or pushing past what time alone permits. At an accepting Nexus, the usual halving of cost and time applies here too (see Force Nexus Workings).

Trade either way. A field caster with no holocrons trades hours for power; an adept with a holocron lattice trades ritual intensity for the same gain in less time — or combines both to reach scales (a star-system-wide Battle Meditation, an Unleashed working) that neither time nor ritual could reach alone.

Cost and Strain. A Ritual Enhancement costs the FP of the underlying working plus half again (round up) at release, and ties up the dedicated holocron face until the held charge is spent. Stacking multiple enhancements multiplies the strain — the GM may require a Will or HT roll to hold a doubled-and-focused grand working without fatigue. Critical failure during preparation: the working inverts or misfires on release (GM’s call), and the dedicated face is burnt dim for 1d days.

The Saber Unmade

The Ghostfire Crystal as Ritual Catalyst

The Unmaking. For any working tagged RITUAL, the practitioner’s lightsaber is disassembled at the start of the ritual and its components are arranged around the inscribed site. The hilt-shell, the focusing assemblies, the power cell, the emitter matrix, and the secondary harmonic guides are each laid at specific points within the rune-pattern. The ghostfire crystal itself is set at the centre of the inscribed circle as the ritual’s catalyst — the working channels through it rather than through an ignited blade.

Why It Matters. The saber components form an extension of the holocron-rune pattern: they are pre-tuned to your own Force-signature and act as living anchors for the working. A complete saber laid out across a Brief Ritual’s site adds nothing mechanically; a complete saber across a Major or Grand Ritual’s site is structurally required — the ritual cannot complete without it, because the components are absorbing and redirecting the rune-pattern’s flow.

Component Roles by Tier.

Brief Rituals. The saber sits intact across your knees or on the ground beside you. No disassembly required. The ghostfire crystal pulses in time with your breath through the hilt.

Major Rituals. The saber is partially disassembled — emitter matrix and power cell removed, hilt opened. The ghostfire crystal is exposed and held in your hand or set on a small inscribed pedestal at the centre. The other components rest at the cardinal points of the rune-pattern.

Grand Rituals. Full disassembly. Eight or more components laid out in specific geometric relation to the runes and any cardinal-point braziers. The ghostfire crystal sits in the geometric centre, sometimes suspended on a thin chain or resting on a small holocron face acting as a plinth. The crystal will glow visibly throughout the ritual — teal, then bronze-teal at the climax — even to non-Force-sensitive observers.

Vulnerability During Ritual. While the saber is disassembled, you cannot ignite it as a weapon. If you are attacked during a Major or Grand Ritual, you must defend with bare hands, the Force, or whatever you can reach — the saber components are scattered across the site and reassembly requires a full minute of focused work (Mechanic / Armoury (Lightsaber) roll, default DX−3). This is the deliberate cost of the ritual’s power: you are unarmed in the most material sense while the most material workings happen.

The Crystal’s Survival. The ghostfire crystal survives all rituals intact — even those that destroy small holocrons (e.g. Soulforge). It is the constant catalyst; only the components and holocrons fold to the working. If the crystal itself were ever lost or destroyed, the entire codex would be rendered inert until you carved a new one (an undertaking measured in years, not days).

Grand Ritual Layout — the diagram below illustrates the standard arrangement for a Grand Ritual. Component placement varies slightly by working (Soulforge replaces the centre crystal-plinth with the working item beneath the suspended crystal; Mother’s Calling replaces it with the Soul Bind holocron pressed to your chest), but the geometry of eight components around concentric rune-rings is constant.

The practitioner stands at the centre, the ghostfire crystal level with your chest (suspended on a thin chain for Soulforge and Mother’s Calling, or resting on a small holocron plinth for Wither). Components are placed before you begin; runes are inscribed days earlier; braziers are lit at the start of the invocation.

Force Nexus Workings

When the Place Itself Lends Its Strength

The Principle. A Force Nexus is a place where the Force gathers thicker than elsewhere — a battlefield where thousands died, a long-empty temple, a planetary convergence point, the heart of a vergence. Performing a ritual at a Force Nexus that accepts the practitioner allows the place itself to bear part of the working’s weight. Costs and durations halve, and certain workings become possible at scales otherwise out of reach.

Mechanical Benefits at an Accepting Nexus.

Cost. All FP costs (initial and maintained) for the ritual halve, rounded up. A 50 FP Grand Ritual becomes 25 FP at an accepting nexus.

Time. Ritual preparation and invocation times halve. A 7-day Mother’s Calling becomes 3.5 days at an accepting nexus. The mandatory holocron-rune inscriptions still take their full physical time to draw, but you do not need to remain awake throughout the preparation in the same disciplined manner; the nexus holds the working’s progress during your brief rest.

Reagents. Reagents are not reduced. Holocron-rune count is not reduced. Holocrons consumed by a ritual are still consumed. Sacrifices are still demanded. The nexus lends Force, not material.

Resistance and Reach. Workings cast at the nexus gain +2 to your Force skill for contests and double their stated effect range or radius (your choice at activation, not both).

Acceptance. A nexus must accept the practitioner for the bonus to apply. Acceptance is not given freely. The first time you attempt a ritual at a given nexus, you roll a Quick Contest of your Will vs the nexus’s own Will (GM determines — typically Will 15–40 for minor nexuses, 50+ for major). Modifiers may apply: alignment match between your intent and the nexus’s nature (+2 to +5), a relevant offering or appropriate ritual gesture (+1 to +3), prior relationship or guardianship (+2 to +6), open antagonism or trespass against the nexus’s domain (−5 or outright refusal). On success, the nexus accepts you for that ritual; on critical success, the acceptance is durable and applies to subsequent visits. On failure, no bonus; on critical failure, the nexus actively resists you (see Hostile Nexus below).

Light, Grey, and Dark Nexuses. CORRUPTION   Most Force Nexuses are aligned. A Light nexus — a Jedi temple, a place of healing, a verdant sanctuary — will outright reject any necromantic working performed against an unwilling target. This includes Soul Bind, Holocron Sentry, The Borrowed Face (drawing on an unwillingly bound soul), Curse of the Lingering, Coffin Mist, and Force Horror when cast against a non-consenting being. The ritual cannot complete at a Light nexus — the place withdraws its acceptance the moment your intent becomes clear, and the working fails (all FP, time, and reagents are still lost). If the target consents — a dying ally choosing Soul Bind to remain useful to you, a volunteer accepting Holocron Sentry, a willing recipient of a Soulforged item drawn from their own willingly-released soul — the Light nexus permits the working, and grants you the standard accepting-nexus bonuses. Non-necromantic workings of constructive or protective intent (Witchsight, Resonant Touch, Verdant Surge, Etched Counter, Inscribed Future, Mother’s Calling restricted to the living-bonded portion) are welcomed at Light nexuses without restriction.

A Grey or neutral nexus has no alignment-modifier for any working tagged here. A Dark nexus — a Sith temple, a site of mass atrocity, an old Sith battleground — accepts all of the workings in this codex readily (+3 to acceptance roll), but exacts a corruption price: at the end of any ritual performed at a Dark nexus, you roll Will or Mind Block vs the nexus’s Will. Failure imposes 1d−1 Corruption (minimum 1); critical failure imposes 1d+1 Corruption and a temporary trait (Bad Temper, Bloodlust, or Callous, lasting 1d weeks). Major rituals at Dark nexuses compound the risk — the bigger the working, the deeper the seductive offer.

Hostile Nexus. If you fail the acceptance contest critically or trespasses against a nexus’s nature, the place actively resists you. Treat as: the ritual proceeds at doubled cost (the opposite of an accepting nexus), runs at doubled time, and at completion you must roll Will or take 3d mental fatigue plus 1 Corruption as the place’s rejection bleeds into you. You may abandon the attempt before completion and lose only the time and reagents invested so far.

Recognising a Nexus. Force-sensitives may attempt a Will or Per-based Force skill roll to perceive a place’s nature on arrival — success reveals it as a nexus and indicates its rough alignment; critical success reveals its approximate Will and any known historical associations. Witchsight functions normally at a nexus and tends to reveal nexus-residue clearly (ancient bonds, lingering presences, geological Force-imprints).

I  ·  INITIATE

The First Faces

Foundational workings · saber-primary · 1–5 FP base · 3–7 FP bolstered

The earliest re-derivations from the conversion period. These rely most directly on the saber as conduit — most don’t require holocron binding to function, though every one of them benefits measurably from a consumed face. This is the scene-setting toolkit: reposition, perceive, mark, charge, curse with small things.

Phantom Stride

Teleport from your current position to any visible hex within 8 hexes.
AffinityWillActionMove, instant (or a Reaction Blink)Rangeany hex you can see, up to 8 (16 bolstered)DefenceNone (self)FP2 (3 bolstered)ReactionYes (the Blink) — uses your Reaction; also a Move on your turn

Effect. Teleport from your current position to any visible hex within 8 hexes. As a Reaction defence (a “Blink”): +2 to Dodge against one incoming attack, displacing up to 2 hexes from origin. Bolstered: range becomes 16 hexes; Blink gives +4 Dodge and ignores follow-up reaction attacks against you for 1 turn.

The ghostfire blade flickers once. Teal mist pools briefly at your feet and you dissolve — not blurring, simply absent. Arrival is the reverse: mist gathers, the saber pulses, and you stand there.

Must perceive the destination hex by sight or by Witchsight. Cannot stride through Force-sealed barriers or into a Force Void. Critical failure: you scatter — arrives 1d hexes off target in random direction, prone.

Witchsight

Vision shifts. Living things glow with a faint internal fire (the Force in them); bonds between living things show as…
AffinityWill (or Per, worst)ActionConcentrate (1 turn), then maintainedRange20-hex radius (100 bolstered)DefenceForce Stealth resists (Quick Contest); reads signatures, not mindsFP2 (3 bolstered), then 1/minReactionNo

Effect. Vision shifts. Living things glow with a faint internal fire (the Force in them); bonds between living things show as visible teal threads; recent paths of movement linger as faint footprint-ghosts for up to 10 minutes after passage; emotional residue in a place shows as low colour-mist (rage red, fear pale, grief blue-grey). Sees through ordinary illusions and tracks any creature you have marked or knows by Force-signature. Bolstered: range becomes 100 hexes (line of sight), you can see Force-bonds across any distance you have previously walked, and footprint-ghosts linger 1 hour.

You close your eyes and reopens them. The world is the same, except for what now sings beneath it. The saber dims its pulse so the world’s own light can be heard.

Hidden Force-users may resist with Force Stealth. Does not read minds — reads signatures, residues, and bonds. Bolstered version requires the saber ignited. Critical failure: you see too much — 1d turns blinded by overlapping signatures.

Hex of Wandering

A minor curse settles on the target like fine dust.
AffinityWillActionConcentrate (1 turn)Range10 hexes, line-of-sightDefenceResisted by Will or Mind Block; never dodgedFP3 (5 bolstered)ReactionNo

Effect. A minor curse settles on the target like fine dust. For the duration, they suffer accumulating small misfortunes at the GM’s discretion: keys slip from pockets, knots loosen, words come out wrong, footing fails at the worst moment. Mechanically: −1 to all DX, IQ, and Per rolls, and on any critical failure roll, the result is read one row worse on the GURPS critical tables. Bolstered: −2 to all DX/IQ/Per rolls; duration 24 hours; the target also reacts at −2 to all social rolls (others find them slightly off-putting without knowing why).

You extend two fingers in their direction and turns them in a slow half-circle, as if winding a thread. The saber pulses once, low. Nothing visible happens to the target. Then, over the next hour, every small thing goes a little wrong.

Subtle but cumulative; the target may not realise they are hexed for the first 10 minutes. Force-sensitives within 5 hexes may notice the casting with a Per-based Force roll. Critical failure: the hex reflects — you suffer it yourself for 1d hours.

Resonant Touch

Imbue a mundane item with a temporary Force charge.
AffinityWillActionConcentrate (1 turn)RangeTouch (an item in your hands)DefenceNone (object)FP3 (5 bolstered)ReactionNo

Effect. Imbue a mundane item with a temporary Force charge. Item gains +1 to one DX-based skill check when used (declared at activation: blaster pistol → +1 to hit, lockpick → +1 to Lockpicking, comlink → +1 to Electronics Operation, etc.). One charge per item, single-use. Bolstered: bonus becomes +3, duration 24 hours, item may be charged with two uses.

You hold the item between your palms. A teal mist gathers between your fingers and the object — brief, then absorbed. Whoever holds it next will feel the saber’s harmonic answering faintly in their hand.

Cannot stack on already-Force-imbued items. Charge is single-use; the item returns mundane afterward. Cannot enhance lightsabers or weapons with native Force properties.

Coldfinger

Brief grave-cold passes from your touch into the target.
AffinityWillActionAttack — a touch (DX roll to land it)RangeTouchDefenceResisted by HT; the touch itself can be dodged or parried at −1FP2 (4 bolstered)ReactionNo

Effect. Brief grave-cold passes from your touch into the target. They feel the bone-chill of death itself for the duration: −2 to DX (shivering), −1 to Will against any subsequent fear or intimidation effects, and they cannot perform Fine Manipulation tasks (locks, surgery, electronics) until it passes. Also chills 1L of liquid to near-freezing, or extinguishes any open flame you touch. Bolstered: −4 DX, −3 Will vs fear, target must roll HT or be Mentally Stunned 1 turn at the initial touch.

You brush a fingertip against their collar, their cheek, the back of their hand. The mist that follows your finger is barely visible — teal, dispersing in a breath. The target feels suddenly as if they have stepped into a tomb.

Requires actual touch (combat: a DX roll to touch, the target may dodge or parry the touch attempt at −1). Living targets only; droids and constructs are immune. Critical failure: you touch yourself in the gesture — you suffer the effect for 1 turn.

Spirit Sight

Sense spirits, curses, Force echoes, lingering emotions, and supernatural presences within 10 hexes.
AffinityWill (or Per, worst)ActionConcentrate (1 turn), then maintainedRange10-hex radius (30 bolstered)DefenceNone — passive senseFP2 (3 bolstered), then 1/minReactionNo

Effect. Sense spirits, curses, Force echoes, lingering emotions, and supernatural presences within 10 hexes. You perceive the residue of strong events (death, terror, ritual), the threads of active curses or bindings, and the presence of the dead or the bound — whether or not they wish to be seen. A narrower, spirit-focused cousin of Witchsight: where Witchsight reads the living Force and bonds, Spirit Sight reads what lingers after. Bolstered: range 30 hexes; you can also read the rough age and emotional character of an echo (how long ago, and what it felt like).

You let your eyes unfocus and breathes the teal in. The room fills with afterimages — a death here, an old grief soaked into that wall, a thin curse-thread wound around a stranger’s wrist. The dead are rarely as gone as the living believe.

Overwhelming sites (mass graves, battlefields, old Sith temples) may force a Will roll or leave your Shaken for 1d turns from the sheer density of echoes. Does not compel or banish what it senses — it only sees.

Spirit Mend

Knit flesh through the Force. Restore 3 HP per 2 FP spent to a touched living target.
AffinityWillActionConcentrate (1 turn)RangeTouchDefenceNone (willing target)FP2 per 3 HP (2 per 4 HP bolstered)ReactionNo

Effect. Knit flesh through the Force. Restore 3 HP per 2 FP spent to a touched living target. Healing can mend damaged internal organs and critical wounds, not merely surface injury — the Force does not distinguish between a cut and a punctured lung, only between the living and the dead. Bolstered (face-charged): restore 4 HP per 2 FP and automatically stop all bleeding. Where the source document calls for a vial of blood, the bolstered face-charge replaces it — the holocron carries the enhancement, not a spent reagent.

You lay your hand over the wound and the teal sinks in rather than curling away. Beneath your palm, torn things find their shape again. It is not gentle — healing this fast never is — but it is whole.

Cannot regrow fully severed limbs or restore the dead. Crippling injuries: mendable only if the crippling is soft-tissue or recent (a crushed hand, yes; a limb amputated days ago, no — see On Healing sidebar). Cannot heal your own wounds and another’s in the same action. Overuse in a day risks your own fatigue (GM may require HT rolls past 20 FP healed in an hour).

On Healing — What the Force Can and Cannot Mend. RULE

Force healing (Spirit Mend, Resonant Touch, and any borrowed light-side healing you learn) does not distinguish between a shallow cut and a torn organ. It can fully restore damaged internal organs and close critical wounds — a punctured lung, a ruptured artery, internal bleeding, a cracked skull — provided the target is still alive when you begin. HP restored is HP restored, wherever the damage sits.

Crippling injuries are the grey area. A recent or soft-tissue crippling — a crushed hand, a shattered (but present) limb, a severed tendon, fresh nerve damage — can be mended if treated before the body sets into its damaged state (GM’s call, typically within hours to a day). A fully severed limb cannot be reattached by healing alone (the Force closes the wound, but will not regrow what is gone), and a crippling that has already healed wrong (an old amputation, a limb set crooked weeks ago, scar-locked tissue) is beyond simple mending — it would require a Grand Ritual-scale working or proper cybernetic replacement.

What healing never does: raise the dead, regrow fully lost limbs or organs, cure disease or poison (separate workings), or remove a curse, compulsion, or psychological trauma (those are severed or cleansed, not healed).

II  ·  ADEPT

The Hexing Hand

Hexes, severings & spirit-work · saber-and-voice · 3–15 FP per working

The conversions of the second band, where the re-derived workings stop merely echoing the saber and begin to bite. The Adept no longer borrows the dead Nightsisters' faces but their methods — the sever, the wither, the hex laid with intent. Costlier than the Initiate's first faces, and the first workings on this path that can do lasting harm.

Spirit Sever

Briefly cut the target’s connection to their own motor control.
AffinityWillActionAttackRange1 hex (2 bolstered)DefenceResisted by Will or HT, worst (unaware −4, engaged +4); never dodgedFP3 (6 bolstered)ReactionNo

Effect. Briefly cut the target’s connection to their own motor control. Combat: stunned 2 turns (defence −4, lose maneuver, HT each turn to recover). Out of combat: unconscious 1 minute per point of margin. Unaware targets resist at −4; actively engaged targets resist at +4. Bolstered: stun lasts 4 turns at −6 defence; out-of-combat duration trebled; awareness modifier doubled (±8); range extends to 2 hexes.

Your free hand makes a flat, horizontal cutting motion in the air. The saber’s blade pulses sharply. A teal ripple distorts the air around the target’s head — their eyes flash teal once and they slacken.

Fails against beings with no nervous system (droids, pure-energy entities). Critical failure: the ripple bounces and you are stunned 1 turn. Cannot be used on targets stun-immune via Iron Will or equivalent.

Crystal Tether

Project sticky filaments of teal crystal-light at one target limb within range.
AffinityWillActionAttack, then maintainedRange6 hexes, line-of-sightDefenceResisted by ST (or Will if Force-sensitive, worst); the threads can be cut (HP = your Will)FP2, then 2/turn (4 + 2/turn bolstered)ReactionNo

Effect. Project sticky filaments of teal crystal-light at one target limb within range. Bound limb is grappled at you Will as effective ST. While bound, that limb is at −4 DX on top of the grapple. Each subsequent turn you may channel +2 FP to extend the binding to one additional limb (max 4 limbs over 4 turns; fully bound = helpless). Bolstered: starting ST is your Will +6; targets resist at −2; additional limbs still cost the extra channel FP (the discipline is not in the cost — it is in the binding).

You extend an open hand. Threads of teal — half-light, half-mist — spool from your fingertips and wrap the chosen limb. Each thread sings faintly in the saber’s frequency. The target moves as if through clinging water.

Targets larger than SM +2 resist the initial bind at +2. Cutting damage to the threads (saber, vibroblade) severs them — DR 0, HP equal to your Will. Range refers to the initial bind; once bound, you may move freely up to 12 hexes from the target before the threads strain (each hex beyond 12: +1 FP/turn to maintain). Critical failure: threads bind your own arm instead for 1 turn.

Verdant Surge

Accelerate plant growth in a 3-hex line, width, or 3-hex radius from the chosen casting hex (your choice of shape at…
AffinityWillActionConcentrate (1 turn); Instant bolsteredRange8 hexes (a 3-hex line or area from the target hex)DefenceCaught creatures resist with DX vs your Will; needs growable groundFP3 (5 bolstered)ReactionNo

Effect. Accelerate plant growth in a 3-hex line, width, or 3-hex radius from the chosen casting hex (your choice of shape at activation). Effective ST of the growth equals 1.5× your Will. Targets in area: DX to escape unentangled; failed roll = grappled, must Break Free vs the ST. Scaled: +3 FP per additional 3 hexes of area. Bolstered: area doubles, ST becomes 2× your Will, growth happens instantly with no concentration turn.

You point to the chosen ground. The saber’s faint pulse syncs with the heartbeat of the soil. Vines, roots, and stalks erupt teal-veined from the earth — they are real plants, not Force constructs, and they remain after you leave.

Requires growable substrate at the casting point — fails on durasteel, polished stone, sterile environments. Sparse vegetation gives half effect (ST halved). Casting point must be a hex you can see or has previously walked. Critical failure: growth turns hostile to you too, 1d turns.

Weighing of Hearts

Vision shifts to monochrome grey.
AffinityWillActionConcentrate (1 turn), lasts 1 minRange15-hex radius, no line-of-sightDefenceWill or Mind Block to conceal intent (+4 if Mind Block ≥ your Will); a readFP4 (7 bolstered)ReactionNo

Effect. Vision shifts to monochrome grey. Hostile or deceitful sentients within 15 hexes glow teal — intensity proportional to malice. You gain +2 to attack rolls against highlighted targets (reads their intent); alternatively, you may choose to ignore all field-of-view penalties on defence (no rear-hex or Side facing penalty against highlighted attackers, who you defend against from any direction at full active defence). Bolstered: also reveals each highlighted being’s primary hostile intent in one sentence (GM provides); attack bonus becomes +3.

The saber dims to a steady low pulse. The world greys out around you — colour drained from everything except sentients whose hearts you now reads. Those who mean you harm shine teal at the chest, and you see the shape of their next intended act ghosted in front of them.

Fails against targets with Mind Block at your Will or higher (their resistance roll is at +4). Critical failure: you see false hostility from harmless beings for the duration.

Wither

A working of accelerated decay performed across the ritual site, then released outward as a slow wave.
AffinityWillActionMajor Ritual (8 hrs, marked site)Range100 m radius from the site (300 m bolstered)DefenceResisted by HT — living things in radius (Iron Will/Force Veil may use Will); holocrons, sabers, crystals, cortosis & Resonant-Touched items are sparedFP15 (24 bolstered)ReactionNoHolocrons1 small, 2 faces (1 large, 3 bolstered)

Effect. A working of accelerated decay performed across the ritual site, then released outward as a slow wave. At completion of the 8-hour ritual, the 100m radius around the inscribed circle is touched by the working’s wake. Within that radius for the next 7 days: plants wither and die (vegetation greys, leaves drop, food crops fail), perishable food and unsealed liquids spoil within hours, mundane organic materials accelerate decay (wood softens, leather cracks, rope frays, cloth thins). Living things in the radius at activation: HT roll or take 3d6 fatigue, plus visibly age by 1d months (no mechanical effect but obvious to anyone who knew them); failed HT by 5+ ages by 1d years instead. Animals and most sentients flee the area within hours; the place gains a tangible wrongness that lingers months after the working ends. Bolstered: radius 300m; effect persists 30 days; living things resist at −3; ageing failures of 5+ become 1d decade instead of 1d years.

What It Will Not Wither. Holocrons, lightsabers, focusing crystals, cortosis-weave, items previously Resonant-Touched (the harmonic shields them), Force-imbued artefacts. Beings with Iron Will or active Force Veil at activation may roll Will instead of HT.

The site you choose is usually no-one’s land — an old farmstead, an abandoned outpost, a moor at moon-fall. You spend a full day inscribing runes into the ground in concentric arcs. The vial of grave-water marks the centre; ash from a dead fire marks the cardinal points; your own wound, drawn deliberately and dripped into the soil, is what tells the working whose work this is. Then you take the saber apart — emitter, power cell, harmonic guides set at the cardinal runes, the hilt-shell laid open beside you. The ghostfire crystal sits on a small inscribed pedestal at the very centre. For eight unbroken hours you meditate within the circle. At the end the crystal — bare, no blade to ignite around it — pulses once, deeply, bronze-teal. The mist that rolls outward is the colour of old bronze. Nothing in its path stops growing; everything in its path stops being alive.

A signature working. Unmistakable to anyone with Force-sensitivity within 10km when activated — a wave of unmistakable necromantic intent. The withered area is identifiable to Witchsight (or equivalent) for months afterward as your work. Critical failure: the working folds back on you — you age 1d years cosmetically, takes 5d fatigue, and the inscribed site becomes hostile to you (−2 to all Force rolls if you return within 1d months). Cannot be cast within 1 km of an inhabited settlement without immediate severe consequences (witnesses, hostile reactions from any Force-user in the region).

Witch Smoke

Conjure dense, spirit-tainted teal smoke.
AffinityWillActionAttack (conjure)Range10 hexesDefenceNone — area obscurement (1-hex radius per FP; 1 turn per FP)FP1 per hex radius + 1 per turnReactionNo

Effect. Conjure dense, spirit-tainted teal smoke. Area: 1 hex radius per FP spent on size. Duration: 1 turn per FP spent on duration. All beings inside are visually obscured; creatures outside the smoke attacking targets inside attack blindly (−10, or as the GM’s blind-attack rules). Beings inside attacking each other suffer the same. The smoke does not impede your own Witchsight or Spirit Sight — you see through your own working. Face-charged (bolstered): the smoke also lightly chills and unsettles — non-allies inside roll Will or take −1 to attack from the spirit-murmur within it; no reagent required.

You exhale and the breath does not stop — it pours, thickening, until a bank of teal fog rolls across the ground faster than smoke should move. Inside it, shapes move and whisper that are not quite there.

Where cast without a face-charge, the working consumes its reagent: a handful of dead or dried plants crushed at the casting point. With a holocron face-charge, the reagent is unnecessary — the lattice supplies what the dead plants would have. Wind disperses the smoke faster (GM’s call). It obscures you too, unless you rely on Witchsight.

Hex of Agony

Wrack one target with phantom agony.
AffinityWillActionConcentrate (1 turn)Range10 hexesDefenceResisted by HT (Quick Contest); High Pain Threshold halves it; never dodgedFP1 per −1 (split between depth and duration)ReactionNo

Effect. Wrack one target with phantom agony. For each FP spent, the target takes −1 to all DX-based and IQ-based actions (attacks, defences, skills, spell/Force concentration) for the duration. Duration: 1 turn per FP spent. You choose the split between penalty depth and duration at casting. The pain is real to the nervous system but leaves no physical wound — medics find nothing. Bolstered (face-charged): the target also suffers −1 to Will-based actions, and the hex resists being shaken off (any attempt to push through costs the target an extra action).

You close your hand slowly, as if gripping something at a distance. The target’s body locks — not struck, not bound, simply hurting, everywhere at once, for no reason their own eyes can find.

Extended Exposure: a body hexed again and again learns the agony before it arrives. Each repeated use on the SAME individual within days builds a cumulative −1 to their Will resistance (max −4), and the pain takes hold faster — eventually the mere clench of your hand, telegraphed, is enough to stagger them.

Resisted by Will (Quick Contest vs your Force skill). High Pain Threshold halves the penalty; immunity to pain or a fully cybernetic body negates it. Critical failure: the hex rebounds — you suffer half its penalty for 1 turn.

III  ·  WITCH

The Reach Beyond

Soul-binding · holocron-tracing · 5–14 FP per meaningful use

The territory once dominated by Nightsister necromancy. Here range begins to matter, ritual time begins to matter, and consequences begin to bite back if mishandled. Soul Bind is the cornerstone — everything from Tier IV onward draws on the tethers it produces.

Holocron Tracing

Read an entire holocron’s stored knowledge in a single ritual session, without consuming the holocron or triggering its…
AffinityWillActionBrief Ritual (1 hr)RangeTouch (the holocron)DefenceContest a sealed holocron's gatekeeper Will (−3 bolstered); else noneFP8 (12 bolstered)ReactionNoHolocrons1 of your faces + the source

Effect. Read an entire holocron’s stored knowledge in a single ritual session, without consuming the holocron or triggering its single-use depletion. The contents transcribe themselves into your memory as a trace — a perfect recall available to you for 24 hours, after which the trace fades. During the 24-hour window you may write the contents down in full to a datapad, paper, or another medium (this transcribed record is permanent and mundane — readable by anyone). Bolstered: trace persists 1 week; you may trace a damaged or partially-depleted holocron and recover lost content; you may attempt to trace a sealed holocron (Sith, Jedi-secured) by winning a Will contest against the gatekeeper at −3.

What Tracing Does Not Do. Tracing reads content. It does not impart understanding or Force-skill. If the holocron teaches a Force power, you gain the description of how to use it, but cannot use it via the Force unless you go through the proper training to learn it (skill points, time, GM permission). A holocron containing a Sith technique grants you the same knowledge a scholar would have from reading a transcription of it — you knows it exists, what it does, and broadly how it is performed, but you cannot channel it. You may, however, transcribe the contents and pass them to another Force-user, or use the knowledge to recognise the technique when used against you.

Tracing a Sith Holocron. Reading a Sith holocron through tracing is meaningfully safer than the alternative — learning from a Sith holocron carries severe corruption risk. Tracing still carries some risk: on activation, roll Will or Mind Block (your best). On a failed roll, take 1d Corruption (typically 1–3, vs the 5–10 from full commitment to learning). On a critical failure, the Sith gatekeeper reaches back through the trace for 1 turn — resolve as a single Force-attack at the holocron’s original creator’s Will. A successful Will roll on a Sith trace gives the full content with no corruption penalty.

You press your brow to the holocron. For an hour the saber lies across your thighs and pulses in time with your breath. By the end you know what the holocron knows — not as someone told you, but as if you had walked through every page yourself. The holocron remains intact, undimmed.

Standard holocrons are essentially single-use — reading them depletes them. This working reads around that limitation by copying the lattice without consuming it. If the ritual is broken (interruption, hit, forced to move), the trace is incomplete and the holocron is dimmed partially. Critical failure: the holocron and the saber’s crystal both crack visibly; the trace is corrupted (1d items of misinformation); ritual cannot be re-attempted on this holocron for 1d days.

Corporeal Phase

Become teal mist and pass through up to 1 metre of solid matter — a wall, a door, a closed bulkhead — emerging in the…
AffinityWillActionMove, instantRangeSelf — phase through up to 1 m of matter (3 m bolstered)DefenceNone — but you are helpless while phasedFP6 (9 bolstered)ReactionNo

Effect. Become teal mist and pass through up to 1 metre of solid matter — a wall, a door, a closed bulkhead — emerging in the same orientation. May move up to your Basic Move while phased. Cannot phase through Force-sealed barriers, cortosis-weave, or your own holocrons. Bolstered: phase up to 3 metres of matter and remain phased up to 2 turns; enemies cannot attack you while phased (you also cannot attack).

Your form drops to teal vapour in one breath — the saber suspended in the mist, still pulsing softly. The mist threads through the matter’s structure rather than tearing it. On the other side, you reform with the saber already in hand.

Helpless while phased and cannot attack. Lightsaber-blade contact during the phase severs the mist — you emerge where the blade cut you, taking 2d damage. Critical failure: partial reform inside the barrier — 3d crush damage, HT or fall unconscious.

Echo Shriek

Project the resonant cry of a bound soul or recorded harmonic through your saber.
AffinityWillActionConcentrate (2 turns)Range15 hexes (a 10-hex-radius burst from the chosen point)DefenceResisted by HT (DR applies; deaf immune); hits allies unless bolsteredFP3 (6 bolstered, needs a soul-tether)ReactionNo

Effect. Project the resonant cry of a bound soul or recorded harmonic through your saber. Two turns of concentration build the harmonic; on the second turn, the shriek bursts from the chosen casting hex within 15 hexes (it does not require LoS to you once the casting point is set, only that you had LoS at activation). 10-hex radius from the casting point. All within take 3d6 fatigue damage (treat as physical sonic — DR applies, earplugs help). Scaled: +3 FP per additional 3d6 (max 12d6 at 12 FP). Bolstered: damage becomes 4d6 base; non-resisting targets also stunned 1 turn; you may exclude up to 4 named individuals from the effect.

The saber’s pulse becomes a vibration you can feel in your teeth. For two turns you hold it. On the second turn the chosen point — a doorway, a corner, the middle of a corridor — releases the harmonic. A single sound, neither voice nor instrument but a soul’s compressed grief, explodes outward. Teal mist briefly fans where the wave breaks.

Affects your allies unless bolstered. Sound-deafened targets take half damage. Cannot be used while Force Veil is active. The 2-turn build is interrupted by any damage to you (Will roll or lose the working and the FP).

Soul Bind

Two contests resolved in sequence.
AffinityWillActionConcentrate (2 turns)Range2 hexes (near-touch)DefenceResisted by HT and Will, worst; binds the soul only if they fall to 0 FPFP5 (8 bolstered)ReactionNoHolocronsthe Soul Bind holocron (a free tether-slot)

Effect. Two contests resolved in sequence. FP drain: 2d FP siphoned; you regain half as healing (FP, or HP if margin ≥3). Soul tether: if the target falls to 0 FP or below from the drain, you may bind their soul to your Soul Bind holocron — they become mindless after the binding (Bound Soul: corpse-like, alive, no will, cannot answer questions, cannot be rethralled). Up to 4 tethers per holocron. The body remains animate but vacant. The soul-signature powers Echo Shriek, Holocron Sentry, Bound Voice, the Borrowed Face, and Mother’s Calling. Bolstered: 4d FP drain; you heal the full amount; soul-tether contest at −2 to target’s resistance.

You step close. The saber low and pulsing once per heartbeat. Teal threads stretch between your free hand and the target’s chest — drinking, then drawing tighter. If they fall, the saber pulses sharply twice and the holocron at your belt catches the last bright thing in them. After, the body breathes, but the eyes will never focus again.

This is off-putting. NPCs who witness it (allies included) react: −3 to −6 to Reaction for any subsequent interaction. Tethered souls cannot be released back into their original body. Voluntary release dissipates the soul into the Force — they do not return. Critical failure: backlash — you take 2d mental fatigue and cannot use this working again for 24 hours.

Etched Counter

Over an hour of meditation, etch a counter to one specific Tier I–III power onto a holocron face; it fires automatically when that power is used against you.
AffinityWillActionBrief Ritual (1 hr) to inscribe; then triggers as a ReactionRangeSelf at trigger (3-hex radius bolstered)DefenceCounters the one named power (as Equilibrium/Rebuke at +2; +4 bolstered)FP5 to inscribe; trigger freeReactionYes — uses your Reaction (pre-inscribed counter)Holocrons1 face, consumed at trigger

Effect. Over an hour of meditation you etch the signature of one specific Force power (Tier I–III) onto a holocron face — a Counter-Inscription keyed to that one power. When that exact power is later used against you by anyone, the face triggers automatically as your Reaction: the inscribed counter resolves and the incoming power is cancelled, exactly as a Force Equilibrium / Rebuke would (you win the contest at +2, no FP spent at the trigger). The face is spent once it fires. Bolstered: the counter also protects any ally within 3 hexes, fires at +4, and — if it loses the contest — is not consumed, leaving one retry.

You spend an hour with the holocron, the saber laid across your knees, etching the chosen power’s signature into one face as a geometric pattern only you can read. When the trigger comes no gesture is needed: the face flares teal, the saber pulses, and the incoming power dies on arrival.

One Counter-Inscription per face, and it must match the incoming power exactly — the wrong power triggers nothing. A critical failure while inscribing wastes the face and the FP. Counter-Inscriptions cannot stack: a face holds this or a bolstered version of another power, not both.

IV  ·  HIGH PRIESTESS

The Greater Workings

Reanimation · misdirection · phantom-craft · 8–18 FP per use

Heavy investment, heavy consequence. Workings at this tier reach into combat-shaping scale and pull on more than your own Force reserves — they pull on bound souls, on holocron-stored energy, on the saber itself as anchor. Most rely on at least one tether in your Soul Bind holocron to reach their full potential.

Holocron Sentry

Animate a corpse (or soul-bound vacant body) as an obedient sentry construct.
AffinityWillActionMajor Ritual (4 hrs)RangeTouch (the corpse)DefenceNone — an obedient construct (ST 14, HP 25; 150 pts; max Will÷10 at once)FP8 (12 bolstered)ReactionNoHolocronsa Soul Bind tether (spent only when the sentry is destroyed)

Effect. Animate a corpse (or soul-bound vacant body) as an obedient sentry construct. Base stats: ST 14, DX 11, HT 14, HP 25, DR 1, no Will/IQ, follows simple combat commands. 150 base points of combat ability. Maximum simultaneous sentries: Will ÷ 10. Bolstered: 250 points; ST 18, HP 35; gains one assigned Talent (a single skill at your Will level — e.g. Stealth, Climbing, Beam Weapons). The sentry retains faint body-memory of the bound soul — a former soldier remembers how to hold a rifle.

The body lies still. You kneel beside it, the saber partially disassembled around you in a small ring — emitter at the corpse’s head, power cell at its feet, harmonic guides at the hands, the hilt-shell opened to your left. The ghostfire crystal you hold in your palm directly over the corpse’s chest, pulsing teal in time with your breath. A thread of mist runs from the holocron at your belt through the crystal in your hand and into the body beneath. The corpse sits up slowly, eyes vacant teal — and then it is yours. You reassemble the saber with three practised motions while the sentry rises.

Sentries are obviously uncanny. Untrained observers may roll Will to recognise them as wrong (+2 if seen close); recognised use carries Reaction penalties (−2 to −4 minimum). Critical failure: the body rises briefly hostile, attacks for 1d turns, then collapses. Loss of the soul-tether (release, holocron destruction) collapses the sentry instantly.

Crystal Phantom

Project a semi-solid warrior, beast, or chimera of teal crystal-light.
AffinityWillActionConcentrate (2 turns), then maintainedRange8 hexes to summon; leashed within 8 of youDefenceNone — a 200-pt phantom (350 bolstered); dissolves if it drifts past 8FP10 (14 bolstered), then 3/turnReactionNo

Effect. Project a semi-solid warrior, beast, or chimera of teal crystal-light. 200 points of combat ability (your design at activation). Phantom moves 6 hex/turn, attacks on your turn but doesn’t consume your maneuver. Cannot exceed 8 hexes from you — drift further and it dissolves. Bolstered: 350 points; advanced racial or beast advantages (terror-aura, claws, regeneration); two attacks/turn if Rapid Strike-capable; if “killed” while bolstered, dissipates harmlessly and may be re-summoned the next round at half FP.

You close your eyes. The saber rises before you, blade pulsing in time with something you are shaping in the air ahead. Teal mist gathers — column, then form, then armour or claw or face. The shape is briefly transparent, then solid enough to swing.

Concentration broken: phantom dissipates in 1d turns. If you are stunned or unconscious, phantom collapses immediately. Cannot enter Force Voids or pass through cortosis. Cannot be summoned in Force Veil.

Bound Voice

Call one named bound soul into brief partial coherence beside you — manifests as a teal silhouette visible to you and…
AffinityWillActionQuick (free) — needs a soul-tetherRangeSelf — the soul manifests at your shoulderDefenceNoneFP6 (10 bolstered)ReactionNoHolocronsa Soul Bind tether (+1 Force skill where the soul's expertise applies)

Effect. Call one named bound soul into brief partial coherence beside you — manifests as a teal silhouette visible to you and to other Force-sensitives. The soul cannot speak (Soul Bind rules — mindless), but: (a) indicates yes/no by light intensity; (b) reacts to imagery you show them mentally; (c) provides faint resonance: +1 to your Force skill for the duration where the soul’s living expertise applies. Bolstered: the soul achieves brief coherence — speaks one full sentence of its own choosing (possibly accusatory, possibly useful). Resonance bonus becomes +2 and lingers an additional 3 turns.

The Soul Bind holocron at your belt pulses, teal mist curling outward in a slow halo. A vague figure resolves at your shoulder — barely a person, mostly an impression. It looks where you look. It feels what you feel. For a moment, what they were comes back through you.

Openly visible to other Force-sensitives within 10 hexes — they perceive the manifestation clearly. Critical failure: soul achieves full coherence for 1 turn and attacks you or flees with your holocron; resolve based on the original soul’s nature.

Coffin Mist

Teal mist coalesces around the target’s head and shoulders.
AffinityWillActionConcentrate (1 turn), then maintained (max 5 turns)Range10 hexesDefenceWill or Mind Block to resist, then HT each turn to break free; never dodgedFP8, then 2/turn (12 bolstered)ReactionNo

Effect. Teal mist coalesces around the target’s head and shoulders. To everyone else, it is a brief curl of mist that dissipates. To the target, the mist becomes opaque earth pressing in on all sides — they are convinced, beyond reason, that they have been buried alive. Phantom claustrophobia and the smell of grave soil. The target is physically unaffected but mentally paralysed: cannot maneuver other than Do Nothing, cannot use Force powers, defends at −4. Each turn after the first, the target rolls HT to push past the panic and recover (success: shake off the working). Bolstered: mist also blinds the target to all real sensory input for the duration (they see only the imagined grave); HT recovery rolls at −3; affects up to 3 targets simultaneously if you pay full cost per target.

You make a small downward-press gesture, palm flat. The saber pulses once, then settles to a low steady glow. Around your chosen target’s head, teal mist briefly curls — gone in a breath to onlookers. To the buried, it is centuries long.

Mind-affecting; immune: droids, beings with no concept of burial (some non-humanoids), Iron Will-equivalent traits. Targets who survive the working and learn what was done to them react at −4 to you forever. Critical failure: you bury yourself in the working — suffers the effect for 1 turn.

The Borrowed Face

You draw on one bound soul-tether and briefly wears their appearance: face, voice, mannerisms, build (within reason —…
AffinityWillActionConcentrate (2 turns)RangeSelf, or one willing ally by touch — the working is wornDefenceClose observers who knew the original roll Per −3 (−5 bolstered) to see through itFP8 (14 bolstered)ReactionNoHolocronsa soul-tether resembling the desired face

Effect. You draw on one bound soul-tether and briefly wears their appearance: face, voice, mannerisms, build (within reason — mass shifts by up to ±30 kg, height by ±15 cm). The disguise is Force-level convincing — mundane observers automatically accept it; suspicious close observers (someone who knew the original well) roll Per at −3 to spot inconsistencies (your own mannerisms bleeding through). You know what the original sounded like, walked like, gestured like — the soul guides you even as it remains mindless. You do not gain the original’s skills, knowledge, or languages — you still must bluff anything domain-specific. Duration: 1 hour base; ends early if you dismiss it or the wearer is rendered unconscious. Worn by another: you may lay the face onto one willing ally instead — the ally must be touched and present for the full casting; the build limits (±30 kg, ±15 cm) measure from the wearer’s frame, and it is the wearer’s mannerisms that bleed through for observers’ Per rolls. You feel a faint tug from the worn face while it lasts and knows the moment it drops. Bolstered: duration 2 hours total; the wearer gains 1 random language the original spoke; close observers’ Per rolls at −5; you may swap the worn face to a different bound soul mid-working with a Will roll (otherwise locked to the chosen face).

You hold the Soul Bind holocron to your chest. Teal mist gathers and clings to you like a second skin. When it settles, your own features are gone — in their place, the face of someone who once looked out at the world. Your voice, when you speak, is theirs.

Worn faces are still your body — you still bleed where you bleed, take wounds where you take wounds (a tall borrowed face on your short frame is suspicious to anyone looking carefully). If the original is recognisable (a known figure), this becomes a major secrecy risk. Critical failure: the soul resists the wearing — the wearer’s face fractures between their own and the borrowed; 1d hours of obvious wrongness, the borrowed identity exposed (and an unhappy ally, if it wasn’t your own face at stake).

Mist-Fold

The signature Nightsister teleportation working — also called the Bone Portal or the Mist-Way — an instantaneous fold…
AffinityWillAction1-hr ritual to link two holocrons; Instant to open the fold (open 1 turn)RangeUnlimited — between the two linked holocrons, anywhere in the galaxyDefenceNone — you choose who and what passesFPby mass: 3 (self) / 8 (+~500 kg) / +4 per extra ~500 kgReactionNoHolocrons2 linked (shells, or a large holocron as a permanent Nexus anchor)

Effect. The signature Nightsister teleportation working — also called the Bone Portal or the Mist-Way — an instantaneous fold in space between two linked holocrons, anywhere in the galaxy. Establishing a link is a 1-hour ritual performed with both holocrons present (or, for a remote anchor, with one holocron and a previously-prepared partner). Once linked, opening the fold is Instant and the portal stays open for 3 seconds — long enough for those adjacent to step through, but not a standing doorway. You decide who and what passes. The linked holocrons are not consumed and not left behind: at activation you choose whether each holocron ends up on the entry side or the exit side, and they travel (or stay) as you will.

Multiple links, one active. You may keep several links prepared at once (each held in a separate shell holocron), but activating any one link deactivates all the others — only a single fold may be live at a time. Re-activating a different prepared link is Instant, but switching mid-turn is not (one fold per turn).

FP and mass. Folding follows Phantom Stride’s cost structure, scaled for what crosses: self only as Phantom Stride bolstered (3 FP); self + adjacent allies/cargo up to ~500 kg at 8 FP; each additional ~500 kg or each additional 2 passengers at +4 FP. The distance does not affect the cost — across a room or across the galaxy is the same fold. Only mass matters.

The large-holocron anchor. If the link’s far end is you large holocron stationed on a Force Nexus that accepts you, that link is permanent and self-sustaining — it persists as an existing link as long as the large holocron remains on the accepting Nexus. Folding back to the large holocron from the field means the small holocron you carried out does not need re-charging or re-linking — the large anchor holds both ends. If the large holocron is not the anchor, then any small holocron used to make a fold must afterward be physically carried back and re-linked (another 1-hour ritual) before it will fold again.

Re-anchoring in the field. If the large holocron is currently linked to a small holocron, you may — with full 1-hour preparation — set up a new shell holocron on your person, break the large holocron’s link with the old small one, and establish a fresh link to the large holocron with the new shell. You may instead do the reverse and re-anchor through the old small holocron. You may also break the large-holocron link entirely and instead establish a direct link between two small holocrons on your person. Every new or changed link requires the full hour of ritual magic — there is no shortcut.

You press two empty holocrons together at your brow and breathes the working into them for an hour, until they hum the same note. After that, distance forgets itself between them. When you open the fold, teal mist pours from the lattice and the air folds like cloth — and for three seconds there is a doorway between a cave on Dathomir and the deck of a ship three systems away. Then the mist thins and the doorway was never there.

Requires the full 1-hour ritual to establish or change any link — this cannot be rushed and cannot be done in combat. The 3-second window is unforgiving: anyone not adjacent and ready when the fold opens is left behind. A fold cannot open into a Force Void, through a Force-sealed barrier, or to a holocron that has been destroyed or had its shell-link overwritten with a working.
