Mastery of a Form means more than knowing its strikes — it means seeing combat through the Form’s logic, breathing in its rhythm, ending fights in the shape it carves.

The Order recognises Forms I through VI plus Vaapad. Juyo, the unrestrained dark variant of Form VII, is forbidden but persists; its terminal tier is the Dark Warrior. Beyond the seven classical Forms stand two advanced disciplines — Jar'Kai (Form VIII) and Saberstaff (Form IX) — each built on a different blade entirely, and each requiring Grandmaster mastery of a single-blade Form before the body can be retrained to them. Battle Master is the rarest of titles: Grandmaster proficiency in every disciplined Form, with seamless transitions between them mid-combat.

Each Form provides a Core Buff, a permanent Flaw that does not change with mastery, and at certain tiers a named Technique. Tier advancement requires GM approval and character-point investment. Form flaws are permanent — mastery deepens the strengths, never erases the cost.

Technique action costFREEFree action — rides another action; no slot usedFREE ATKFree bonus attack — triggered by a hit/parry/step; 1/turnATTACKIs your Attack maneuver this turnFORCEForce-power rider on a saber attack — costs FPFREE·FORCEFree Force action declared with your attack — costs FPREACTIVEOut-of-turn reaction (when hit / when a power is used on you)FULL·DEFTakes your whole turn as a defensive stanceFULL TURNFull action — uses your entire turnPASSIVEAlways-on / automatic — no action to use
FORM I

Shii-Cho

The Determination Form · Form of the Sarlacc
Permanent Flaw — −3 to hit in 1v1 duels. No Targeted Attacks.

Example — a Shii-Cho Sword Master is swarmed by five battle-droids. Their multiple-attack penalty is cut by 4 per extra target, so cutting at three of them costs far less than usual, and he is at +2 Parry for facing 2+. Drag him into a one-on-one duel and the −3 dueling flaw bites — the form was never built for a single foe.

IInitiate

Core Buff. Multiple Attack penalties reduced by 2 per extra target. +1 to Parry when facing 3 or more foes.

IIKnight

Core Buff. Multiple Attack penalties reduced by 3 per extra target. +1 to Parry vs 2 or more foes.

Technique — FREE ATKSarlacc Sweep: After any successful attack against a foe in a group of 3+, make one additional attack against an adjacent target at −3. Free action. Once per turn.

The sweeps grow more deliberate. You begin reading the crowd as geometry rather than individuals.

IIISword Master

Core Buff. Multiple Attack penalties reduced by 4 per extra target. +2 to Parry vs 2 or more foes.

The Form flows without hesitation. Mastery deepens the group advantage without touching the 1v1 limitation.

IVGrandmaster

Core Buff. Multiple Attack penalties reduced by 5 per extra target. +3 to Parry vs 2 or more foes.

Technique — FREEZone Control: When 4+ foes are within 3 hexes, forgo your Step to force all enemies within 2 hexes to make a ST roll at −2 or be pushed back 1 hex. Free action at start of your turn.

Combat becomes geometry. You are the centre, the blade the radius. You do not fight individuals — you manage the space they occupy.

VBattle Master

Core Buff. Multiple Attack penalties negated entirely. +4 to Parry vs 2 or more foes. (The permanent flaw still holds: −3 in 1v1 duels, and no Targeted Attacks — Shii-Cho never learns the precise strike.)

You stop fighting and start managing. The blade finds the edges of everyone within reach. Each thought they were not the target.

FORM II

Makashi

The Contention Form · Form of the Ysalamiri
Permanent Flaw — −3 Parry vs blasters. −2 to all defenses vs flank and rear attacks.

Example — A Makashi Knight opens a saber duel with a Feint at +3, wins the Quick Contest, and lands the follow-up before the defender can bring their blade across. Put her in front of a blaster line instead and the −3 Parry vs blasters is exactly why Makashi duelists fear a gunfight.

IInitiate

Core Buff. +3 to Feint, Disarm, or Beat. +1 to Parry vs lightsabers. Negates the standard −1 Lightsaber parry penalty.

IIKnight

Core Buff. +3 to Feint, Disarm, or Beat. +2 to Parry vs lightsabers.

Technique — FREEThe Courtly Bind: On a successful Parry vs a lightsaber, immediately attempt a free Beat or Disarm at full skill. Once per turn.

The bind flows so smoothly it looks rehearsed. Your opponent's blade goes where you want it before they understand what happened.

IIISword Master

Core Buff. +4 to Feint, Disarm, or Beat. +2 to Parry vs lightsabers.

Technique — FREE ATKMakashi Riposte: After a successful Parry vs lightsaber where you did not use the Courtly Bind, make an immediate counterattack at +2 to hit as a free action.

The riposte arrives before the opponent has finished their stroke. You were already there. You had already decided.

IVGrandmaster

Core Buff. +5 to Feint, Disarm, or Beat. +3 to Parry vs lightsabers.

Technique — PASSIVECho Mai: on a successful Disarm winning the Quick Contest by 4+, the opponent's weapon-hand is crippled instead of disarmed. Target makes HT at −3 — failure means hand crippled for 1d hours. No Targeted Attack penalty.

You fight like water. Every attack slides past something that is no longer there.

VBattle Master

Core Buff. +6 to Feint, Disarm, or Beat. +4 to Parry vs lightsabers.

You do not need to disarm them. You simply make it clear the weapon is already forfeit. Sometimes they drop it before you touch it.

FORM III

Soresu

The Resilience Form · Form of the Mynock
Permanent Flaw — No All-Out Move & Attack. −2 damage on all hits.

Example — A Soresu Initiate under fire Parries three blaster bolts in one turn before any cumulative penalty, Stepping while in All-Out Defense to stay in cover. The −2-damage flaw never matters while she is weathering the storm — but it also means she cannot All-Out Move & Attack to close when she finally wants to strike.

IInitiate

Core Buff. +2 to Parry. Can Parry 3 blaster bolts per turn before cumulative penalties apply. Can Step while using All-Out Defense.

IIKnight

Core Buff. +2 to Parry. 4 blaster bolts before cumulative penalties. Step while using All-Out Defense.

Technique — FREEPatient Fortress: When using All-Out Defense and not moving more than a Step, gain +3 to all defenses until your next turn. Stacks with the standard All-Out Defense bonus.

You stop moving entirely and attacks find less and less. It stops feeling like defence and starts feeling like gravity.

IIISword Master

Core Buff. +3 to Parry. 5 blaster bolts before cumulative penalties. Step while using All-Out Defense.

Technique — FULL·DEFIron Ward (full-turn posture): declare one adjacent ally your ward and spend your turn guarding them — no offensive actions, Step only to keep adjacency. Any attack on your ward within reach may be intercepted with a full Parry at no multi-defense penalty, regardless of facing. Blaster bolts intercepted by margin 3+ are reflected at the shooter (torso) at full Lightsaber skill. Soresu's −2-damage flaw is irrelevant while guarding. Ends on your choice, if the ward leaves reach, or if you are Stunned.

The blade becomes a wall. You stop being a fighter and become a guarantor.

IVGrandmaster

Core Buff. +4 to Parry. 7 blaster bolts before cumulative penalties. Can Step during any defensive posture.

Technique — PASSIVEUnbroken Circle: While you have not been hit this encounter, your Soresu Parry bonus grows by +1 for each consecutive turn spent not taking damage (maximum +3 additional, total +7 at this tier). Being hit resets to zero.

The longer you hold the more it costs them. There is a calculation happening in their head — and the numbers are not working out.

VBattle Master

Core Buff. +5 to Parry. Unlimited blaster bolts with no cumulative penalty. All defensive postures allow a Step.

For those watching, it stops looking like a fight. It looks like a philosophical argument the other person is losing.

FORM IV

Ataru

The Aggression Form · Form of the Hawk-Bat
Permanent Flaw — Costs 1 FP per turn while in active combat. Stunned for 1 turn if FP reaches 0.

Example — An Ataru Knight spends the Step that rides his Attack as a Force Leap, crossing eight hexes to reach a sniper — and because he closed that distance fast, his strike lands at +1 to hit while the sniper defends at −1, arriving from an angle and a speed the man cannot cleanly read. It costs the leap's FP plus the form's standing 1 FP per turn — let his pool hit zero mid-fight and he is Stunned for a turn.

IInitiate

Core Buff. On your turn, when you take a Step as part of a maneuver — such as the Step that accompanies an Attack or All-Out Attack — you may spend it as an instant movement-based Force power (Force Step, Force Leap, Phantom Stride, or similar) in place of the normal 1-hex Step, crossing the full distance that power allows (up to 8 hexes for a Force Leap or Phantom Stride). You still pay the power's FP cost; because it rides the Step itself, it needs no extra maneuver or Quick Action. It cannot be used on a defensive Step (such as the free Step from All-Out Defense) or on any movement taken in reaction to another's action. Encumbrance is handled by the movement ability itself — there is no separate Ataru schedule: it falls as a flat reduction on the total distance exactly as that power dictates, so a teleport such as Phantom Stride is barely troubled by a heavy load while a Force Leap simply lands you shorter the more you carry. Momentum Strike — if you moved 2+ hexes this turn before attacking (which the Step-channelled movement power makes trivial), that attack is at +1 to hit and the target defends against it at −1: you arrive from an angle and a speed they cannot cleanly read.

IIKnight

Core Buff. Step movement-power unchanged. Momentum Strike +1 to hit / −1 to the target's defence.

Technique — FREE ATKHawk-Bat's Dive: Once per turn, when you channel a Step through a movement power, you may make one melee attack against a single enemy you pass adjacent to during the move — the strike lands in passing and you continue on to your destination without stopping. No extra maneuver, no Move-and-Attack penalty; the attack counts as having moved for the damage bonus. Costs only the movement power's FP.

The jumps are longer than they should be. The Force is not hidden anymore.

IIISword Master

Core Buff. Step movement-power unchanged. Momentum Strike +2 to hit / −2 to the target's defence.

The Form stops being exhausting and starts being a pressure system.

IVGrandmaster

Core Buff. Momentum Strike +2 to hit / −2 to the target's defence.

Technique — FREESlipstream: When you channel a Step through a movement power you bring others with you — carry one sentient passenger of up to 500 kg, or a non-sentient object of up to 20 kg, plus your own gear, and every willing ally adjacent to your starting hex is caught in your wake and carried to open hexes adjacent to your destination — their travel triggers no free attacks or reactions of any kind, and there must be room to set them all down. You also ignore the encumbrance penalty to leap distance: however laden you and your passengers are, the wake still carries the full reach. Once per turn; costs only the movement power's FP.

You move, and the space behind you grips whoever is closest and drags them along. Pull a friend out of the fire — or into it.

VBattle Master

Core Buff. Momentum Strike +3 to hit / −3 to the target's defence. Movement powers channeled through a Step cost 1 FP less.

Technique — FULL TURNWhirlwind of the Hawk-Bat: spend 6 FP and make an attack against every enemy within 2 hexes — each at −2 to hit (instead of multi-attack penalties). Once per encounter.

Six hexes. One blur. The still points are where it started and where it ended.

FORM V

Shien

The Perseverance Form · Form of the Krayt Dragon (classical variant)
Permanent Flaw — −2 to Dodge. If forced to Dodge, suffer −2 to all skills next turn.

Example — A Shien Sword Master deflects two blaster bolts and, with Returned Volley, throws both back in one action — split across two shooters at +4 each, no multiple-attack penalty. Force her to Dodge a melee blow rather than Parry it, though, and she is at −2 to all skills next turn.

IInitiate

Core Buff. Reflected blaster bolts hit at +2 to reflected attack roll. After a successful Parry, gain Riposte opportunity on next attack this turn: +2 damage.

IIKnight

Core Buff. Reflected bolts: +3 to reflected attack roll. Riposte: +2 damage.

Technique — FREE ATKPress After Parry: After landing a Riposte, spend 1 FP to make an immediate follow-up attack at −2. Once per turn.

The riposte flows into the follow-up before the opponent can reset.

IIISword Master

Core Buff. Reflected bolts: +4 to reflected attack roll. Riposte: +3 damage.

Technique — FREE ATKReturned Volley: when you deflect two or more blaster bolts in a turn, you may redirect them all in a single action — each bolt is its own ranged attack at this tier's reflect bonus, massed on one target or split among several, at no multiple-attack penalty. Once per turn.

Their fire becomes your quiver. You give it all back at once.

IVGrandmaster

Core Buff. Reflected bolts: +5 to reflected attack roll. Riposte: +4 damage. After any successful Parry followed by a landed Riposte, move up to 2 hexes toward the opponent without triggering free attacks.

You walk through the fight like you are crossing a room. Attacks become stepping stones.

VBattle Master

Core Buff. Reflected bolts: +6 to reflected attack roll. Riposte: +5 damage. Every defensive action succeeding by 3+ generates a free counterattack at −2.

Technique — PASSIVEStorm-Eye: while at half HP or lower, all reflected blaster bolts hit at +2 to attack roll and may be redirected to any target within range (not just the original shooter).

You are not fighting back. You are making them fight for you.

FORM V

Djem So

The Perseverance Form · Form of the Krayt Dragon (martial variant)
Permanent Flaw — −2 to hit any foe faster than you (higher Basic Speed). Every Djem So counterattack costs 1 FP and stacks a cumulative −2 to all your active defenses until the start of your next turn — counter twice and your guard is at −4, three times −6, and so on. A counter is raw telegraphed power: it is always a plain strike and may never be a Deceptive Attack, Rapid Strike, All-Out Attack, or Targeted Attack.

Example — the counter: a Sword Master Parries a Mandalorian's vibroblade and answers at full skill for weapon damage +5 (the +2 core buff and the +3 counter stack on one swing). It costs 1 FP and drops his active defenses −2 until his next turn. A second Mandalorian strikes; he Parries at that −2 and counters again, now −4 and down a second FP. He can answer the whole line — but each reply leaves him more open and emptier, so a Djem So master spends his counters rather than spraying them. No counter may ever be a Deceptive Attack or Rapid Strike.

The martial answer to Shien. Where Shien turns blaster fire back on the shooter, Djem So was built later for the duel — block, parry, and answer at once. It uses the opponent's own aggression against them: every defence is the windup for an overwhelming counter-blow. The power form of the strong and the immovable.

IInitiate

Core Buff. +1 damage on all saber hits (raw power behind the blow). After any successful Parry or Block you may make an immediate counterattack against that attacker — there is no limit on how many times per turn, but each costs 1 FP and stacks the defensive penalty above. The counterattack is made at −2 to hit, +1 damage.

You do not give ground. You take theirs — and pay for every inch.

IIKnight

Core Buff. +1 damage on hits. Counterattack at −1, +2 damage. Your blocks and parries answer ranged and melee equally well.

Technique — FREE ATKFalling Avalanche: an overhead power strike. After an Evaluate or a won Beat, your next attack is +2 damage and knocks a wounded foe back 1 m. Once per turn.

The blade comes down like a collapsing cliff.

IIISword Master

Core Buff. +2 damage on hits. Counterattack at full skill (+0), +3 damage.

Every block is a question. Every counter is the answer.

IVGrandmaster

Core Buff. +2 damage on hits. Counterattack at +0, +4 damage.

Technique — REACTIVEPower Hurl (Saber Throw): once per turn, hurl the lit blade as a thrown attack at full Lightsaber skill (Strength-based throwing range); it returns to your hand at end of turn. The telegraph penalty applies on the throw turn.

The Krayt does not always wait for you to come to it.

VBattle Master

Core Buff. +3 damage on hits. Counterattack at +0, +5 damage; a counter answering a defence you won by 3+ is made at +2 to hit.

Technique — PASSIVEUnstoppable: when a counterattack wounds a foe, they roll ST or are driven back 1 m and suffer −1 to their next attack.

They break themselves on you, again and again, until there is nothing left to break.

FORM VI

Niman

The Moderation Form · The Diplomat's Form
Permanent Flaw — Diminishing Returns: the 1st Force Power Follow-up in a turn costs its normal price; the 2nd costs +1 FP, the 3rd +2 FP, the 4th +3 FP (maximum +4 extra). This surcharge is added after any tier discount and is never reduced or waived — no discount, at any tier, can lower a Follow-up to zero. Resets at the start of each new turn.

Example — A Niman Knight lands a saber hit and immediately rides a Force Push as a Follow-up for its own base FP (−1 at this tier), no extra action. A second Follow-up the same turn still works, but Diminishing Returns adds +1 FP on top of it, and a third would add +2 — the hybrid never comes for free.

IInitiate

Core Buff. +1 to all Force Skill rolls. Can use any Instant Force Power immediately after a saber HIT as a Follow-up — it costs the power's own base FP (the saber attack itself is unaffected), no additional action. The Follow-up power must be an Instant cast — any ability requiring 'do X in same attack' must be Instant.

IIKnight

Core Buff. +2 to all Force Skill rolls. A Follow-up power costs −1 FP off its own base cost (minimum 1); the Diminishing Returns surcharge is then added on top. Follow-ups may also trigger after a successful Parry.

Technique — FREE·FORCEForce Feint: Spend 2 FP for a Quick Contest of Force skill vs opponent's Will as a free action before your attack. On win: opponent at −2 to Parry your next strike this turn.

The Force arrives slightly before the blade. They parry the disruption and forget the attack.

IIISword Master

Core Buff. +3 to all Force Skill rolls. A Follow-up power costs −2 FP off its own base cost (minimum 1); the surcharge is still added on top.

Technique — FORCEConvergence: Once per turn, combine a melee saber attack and one Initiate-tier Instant Force Power in a single action. Both resolve simultaneously, each using their own contest. The Force Power must be an Instant cast. Cost: the power's own FP +1, and this counts as a Follow-up for Diminishing Returns.

The saber and the Force stop being two things. Your opponent sees one attack and receives two.

IVGrandmaster

Core Buff. +4 to all Force Skill rolls. A Follow-up power costs −3 FP off its own base cost (minimum 1); the surcharge is still added on top.

There is no gap between the blade and the Force. One intention, two expressions, one moment.

VBattle Master

Core Buff. +5 to all Force Skill rolls. A Follow-up power costs −4 FP off its own base cost (minimum 1); the surcharge is then added on top. The discount climbs every tier, but the surcharge guarantees no Follow-up is ever free.

Technique — FORCESynthesis: once per turn, a single saber hit may trigger two Instant Follow-ups instead of one. Each rolls its own contest and pays its own discounted base cost plus the running Diminishing Returns surcharge — so the second of the pair carries the +1, and chaining further still climbs. The capstone leans into the flaw rather than escaping it: more follow-ups means more surcharge.

One swing. One moment. One consequence — arriving from every direction you know how to use at once.

FORM VII

Juyo

The Ferocity Form · The Vicious Path
Permanent Flaw — Will roll required each turn or lose 2 FP and −1 to mental defense (cumulative). Juyo and Vaapad share Tier 1 — practitioners commit to a path at Tier 2.

Example — A Juyo Initiate makes a Rapid Strike at only −2 instead of −6, both blows carrying Armour Divisor 2 so they cut through a trooper's plate. At the top of every turn, though, he rolls Will or loses 2 FP and takes a cumulative −1 to mental defense — the frenzy always asks to be paid.

IInitiate

Core Buff. Rapid Strike at −2 instead of −6. All attacks have Armor Divisor (2). Overwhelming Tempo — each attack you make this turn that a foe defends against cracks their guard: −1 to that foe's active defences against each of your subsequent attacks this turn (cumulative, max −2). The aggression is structural, not learned — Juyo opens the door.

IIKnight

Core Buff. Rapid Strike at −2. Armor Divisor (2). On a failed Will roll: lose 2 FP and −1 mental defense (cumulative). The frenzy begins to feel useful.

Technique — PASSIVEFirst Stack: when you make 2+ attacks in a turn and at least one hits, gain +1 to your next attack roll this encounter. Stacks up to +3.

You have learned to steer the current rather than fight it. The aggression still exists — now it has something resembling a throttle.

IIISword Master

Core Buff. Rapid Strike at −2. Armor Divisor (2). Overwhelming Tempo cap rises to −3: each foe defends at up to −3 against your stacked attacks this turn.

Technique — PASSIVEStacking Rage: a successful Will roll channels the fury into focus — +2 to your next attack. A failed roll still costs 2 FP and −1 mental defense; the flaw is never turned into a reward.

Two strikes land and something in their decision-making breaks briefly. Not pain — tempo. The pace you set is faster than they can process.

IVDark Warrior

Core Buff. Rapid Strike at −2. Armor Divisor (3). Overwhelming Tempo cap rises to −4: each foe defends at up to −4 against your stacked attacks this turn.

Technique — FREE·FORCEDark Channeling: spend 4 FP on an attack to add +2d damage to that single strike. Each use: 1 Dark Side compulsion check.

You stop trying to control it. What you become is what the Form was always shaping you toward.

FORM VII

Vaapad

The Way of the Vornskr · Channelling the Dark
Permanent Flaw — Will roll required each turn or lose 2 FP and −1 to mental defense (cumulative). Vaapad and Juyo share Tier 1 — paths diverge at Tier 2.

Example — Facing a Sith hurling dark lightning, a Vaapad Sword Master uses Current Mirror — a Quick Contest of Will that halves the power's effect and grants +2 to his next strike. He still owes the form's per-turn Will roll: Vaapad rides the current, it never silences it.

IInitiate

Core Buff. Rapid Strike at −2. Armor Divisor (2). Identical to Juyo at Tier 1 — the paths diverge at Tier 2.

IIKnight

Core Buff. Rapid Strike at −2. Armor Divisor (2). On a failed Will roll the mental-defense penalty is halved (−½ instead of −1) — the practitioner is learning to process rather than absorb. The 2 FP loss on a failure still applies. Channel the Current — each time an opponent attacks you this turn (hit, miss, or parried), bank +1 to hit against that opponent on your next strike (cumulative, max +2); it resets when you attack. Their aggression sharpens you.

The aggression is the same. But there is something different in the eyes. A Vaapad practitioner studies what triggers the Will roll.

IIISword Master

Core Buff. Rapid Strike at −2. Armor Divisor (2). The cumulative mental-defense penalty no longer escalates — it is capped at −1 however many times you fail — but each failed Will roll still costs 2 FP. The current can be ridden, never ignored. Channel the Current cap rises to +3.

Technique — REACTIVECurrent Mirror: when an opponent uses a dark-side Force Power against you, Quick Contest of Will rolls. On win: reduce that power's effect by half, gain +2 to hit on your next attack, and impose −2 on the dark-sider's defence against that countering strike. Once per turn.

Dark-side attacks do not land cleanly. Something in the practitioner captures part of what was thrown and redirects it.

IVGrandmaster

Core Buff. Rapid Strike at −2. Armor Divisor (3). A successful Will roll channels the current: +2 to hit on your next attack. A failed roll still costs 2 FP, and the mental-defense penalty remains capped at −1 — mastery shapes the overflow but never makes failing it a gain.

Technique — REACTIVEAbsorbed Fury (Reactive Quick Action): once per encounter, when hit for 5+ damage, spend 3 FP as a Reactive Quick Action to convert the impact. Gain +3 to hit and +3 damage on your next attack, reduce damage taken by 2.

Hits seem to make you faster rather than slower. The opponent's force does not disappear. It comes back.

VBattle Master

Core Buff. Rapid Strike at −2. Armor Divisor (4). A successful Will roll grants +2 to hit on your next attack, and the mental-defense penalty never escalates past −1. But a failed roll still costs 2 FP — the Vornskr always takes its due, no matter how complete the mastery. Channel the Current cap rises to +4.

Technique — PASSIVELiving Counter: against a dark-side opponent specifically, every successful Parry adds +1 cumulative bonus to your next attack against them and an equal cumulative penalty to that foe's active defences. Resets at end of encounter.

It no longer looks like a fight. It looks like a conversation — between the practitioner and every dark thing that has ever tried to reach them.

FORM VIII

Jar'Kai

The Way of the Twin Suns · The Two-Blade Discipline
Advanced Form — requires Grandmaster proficiency in any single-blade Form before training may begin. Built on the dual-wield, not the single blade.
Permanent Flaw — Requires two working, lit sabers in hand. Confers nothing if a blade is lost, sheathed, or thrown, and forbids the use of a shield, a free hand, or a two-handed grip. A character reduced to one blade fights at that blade's base skill with none of this Form's benefits. This cost is live at every tier — even a Battle Master is one disarm away from an ordinary swordsman.

Example — A Jar'Kai Sword Master makes a Twin Strike — both blades at full skill on one foe, who must defend against the second blade at −3 — and keeps a spare off-blade Parry in reserve for a second attacker. Disarm one saber and she drops to that blade's bare skill with none of the form's gifts.

IInitiate

Core Buff. One extra Parry each turn at no cumulative penalty — and it may guard a different attacker than your first Parry, the off-blade answering one foe while the lead blade answers another. Off-hand attack penalty reduced to −2.

IIKnight

Core Buff. Off-hand attack penalty reduced to −1. A Dual-Weapon Attack forces the defender to split attention — the second blade's defense is rolled at −2.

Technique — ATTACKTwin Strike: make one attack with each blade against the same foe at no extra Multiple-Attack penalty beyond the off-hand value. The defender defends against the second blade at this tier's second-blade penalty. Once per turn.

Two points of light cross and re-cross. The eye that tracks one blade loses the other.

IIISword Master

Core Buff. Off-hand penalty eliminated — both blades strike at full skill. The second-blade defense penalty inflicted on foes rises to −3, and Twin Strike becomes at-will — the once-per-turn cap is lifted.

The two suns no longer take turns. They burn at once, and the difference between lead hand and off hand has dissolved.

IVGrandmaster

Core Buff. Two extra Parries each turn at no penalty, each able to guard a different attacker. Dual-Weapon Attacks force the second-blade defense at −4.

Technique — FREE ATKCascade: on any turn you successfully Parry with one blade, you may immediately riposte with the other at full skill as a free action. Once per turn.

Defense and attack stop being separate decisions. The blade that turns a stroke is already answering it.

VBattle Master

Core Buff. Cannot be flanked — the twin guard answers every angle, negating all flank and rear defense penalties. Three extra Parries per turn at no penalty, each guarding a different attacker. Second-blade defense penalty on foes reaches −5. Capstone: once per turn a Dual-Weapon Attack adds a third blade-cross strike at no extra penalty — the blades pass and return in a single motion.

A wall of light with no seam in it. To stand behind this fighter is no safer than to stand before them.

FORM IX

Saberstaff

The Way of the Spinning Wheel · The Double-Blade Discipline
Advanced Form — requires Grandmaster proficiency in any single-blade Form before training may begin. Built on the double-bladed saber, not the single blade.
Permanent Flaw — Clinch: a foe in your 1-hex zone costs you the spin's second attack roll against them — the weapon is too long to bring to bear that close. On a critical miss the staff strikes the wielder for normal damage. Mastery eases the clinch but never closes it: the second strike always dies that close, at every tier including Battle Master.

Example — A Saberstaff Initiate’s single Attack maneuver yields two strikes at full skill against adjacent foes, while the constant spin grants +1 Parry and immunity to flanking. Let a foe close to within 1 hex and the staff is too long to bring round — that foe costs you the second strike, at every tier, forever.

IInitiate

Core Buff. The spin strikes twice on every swing — each Attack maneuver yields two attack rolls against the same or adjacent targets, both at full skill (a built-in Rapid Strike with no penalty). The constant motion is itself a guard: +1 to Parry, always on. Cannot be flanked. Clinch: a 1-hex foe costs you the second attack roll and your Parry against them.

IIKnight

Core Buff. Twin strikes continue; the +1 Parry holds. Clinch eases: a 1-hex foe no longer costs your Parry — but still costs the second attack roll.

Technique — FULL TURNWhirlwind Guard: as a defensive option, spend your turn spinning the staff — until your next turn, gain +3 to all Parries and reflect deflected blaster bolts at −2 to a chosen target. Once per turn.

The blade becomes a disc of light. Bolts that meet it leave at angles their shooters did not choose.

IIISword Master

Core Buff. The Parry bonus rises to +2. The two attack rolls per swing may target two different foes within reach. Clinch: a 1-hex foe still costs the second attack roll (your Parry is unaffected).

The wheel learns to choose its spokes. Two enemies, two ends of the staff, one motion.

IVGrandmaster

Core Buff. The Parry bonus holds at +2. Three attack rolls per turn when surrounded by 2+ foes. Clinch: a 1-hex foe still costs the second attack roll (Parry unaffected) — the spin can never quite reach a clinch.

Technique — FULL TURNReaping Spin: once per turn as a full action, spend 3 FP to attack every foe within reach (1 hex) at full skill in a single rotation; each defends independently at −2.

The wheel widens until it has no outside. Everyone within reach is inside the cut.

VBattle Master

Core Buff. The Parry bonus reaches +3. The double strike applies on every swing; cannot be flanked. Clinch (permanent): a 1-hex foe still costs you the second attack roll against them — the spin never reaches a clinch, even now.

There is no longer a gap in the wheel. The blade is everywhere it needs to be, and the close fighter who once owned that hex now shares it with an edge.

The Battle Master Signatures

Ultimate Techniques · Once per Encounter

A Battle Master — Grandmaster proficiency across Forms I–VI plus Vaapad — carries one signature technique from each Form. These are the rarest distinction in the saber tradition: a fighter who can read what a battle demands and bring the right Form to it without hesitation. A practitioner who has also mastered the advanced disciplines carries their signatures too — the twin-blade and the spinning staff each answer a battle in their own voice.

FormSignatureWhenEffect
Form I · Shii-ChoThe Sarlacc's Reach FULL TURN Once per encounter as a full action, spend 5 FP to make a single Lightsaber attack at full skill targeting every enemy within 2 hexes simultaneously. Each defends independently at −2. Requires 3 or more enemies in range.
Form II · MakashiCho Mok Precision PASSIVE Once per encounter, on a successful Disarm or Beat winning the Quick Contest by 4+, the opponent's weapon-arm is crippled instead of disarmed. Target makes HT at −3 — failure means limb crippled for 1d hours. No Targeted Attack penalty.
Form III · SoresuEye of the Storm FULL·DEF Once per encounter, spend 8 FP for 3 turns of absolute defense. All attackers roll at −6 to hit. Cannot be moved, Force-Gripped, or affected by area attacks. Cannot attack or use Force powers. On the turn it ends, make one attack at +4.
Form IV · AtaruThe Hawk-Bat's Stoop FULL TURN Once per encounter as a full action, spend 6 FP to charge up to 6 hexes in a straight line. Make one attack against every enemy whose hex you pass through or end adjacent to — each at full skill, each defends independently at −2, each hit deals full damage plus movement bonus.
Form V · ShienThe Living Counter FULL TURN Once per encounter, spend 5 FP for 2 turns where every attack against you — hit or miss — generates a free counterattack at full Lightsaber skill at −2. No Parry or Dodge required from you.
Form VI · NimanThe Rancor's Weight ATTACK Once per encounter, spend 8 FP to make a single saber attack that triggers every Initiate-tier Instant Force Power you know as simultaneous Follow-ups against the same opponent. Each requires its own resistance roll. FP at half price. Diminishing Returns flaw still applies.
Form VII · VaapadThe Living Circle FULL TURN Once per encounter, spend 8 FP for 3 turns. Roll twice on all attack rolls and take the better result. Dark-side Force Powers used against you are resisted at +4. Successfully resisting one grants +2 to hit until end of turn.
Form VIII · Jar'KaiDance of the Twin Suns FULL TURN Once per encounter, spend 7 FP for 2 turns. Every Attack maneuver becomes a full Dual-Weapon Attack at no off-hand penalty, and each blade's strike forces its own defense at −3. Any turn you Parry, you riposte with the other blade at full skill as a free action. Requires both sabers lit and in hand.
Form IX · SaberstaffThe Unbroken Wheel FULL TURN Once per encounter as a full action, spend 7 FP to spin into a single sustained rotation: make one attack at full skill against every foe within reach (1 hex), each defending independently at −3, and until your next turn gain +3 to all Parries with no flank or rear penalty. The 1-hex dead zone does not apply during the Wheel.
BOOK VIII

THE FORM-NEUTRAL

Techniques
Saber techniques bound to no single Form. The shared vocabulary of every blade-user.
EIGHT TECHNIQUES · AND TRAKATA

The Form-Neutral Techniques

The shared vocabulary of every blade-user

The Form-Neutral Techniques — the shared vocabulary

Techniques that belong to no Form — learned from any teacher, Jedi, Sith, or independent. They presuppose no philosophy.

Alignment req.None. Each technique is learned individually; most are common, Trakata is forbidden. All work alongside whatever Form you practise.
UsesLightsaber skill for attack rolls unless noted. Attribute favours: DX-primary +4 to attacks; ST-primary +4 to Lightsaber Quick Contests; Will-primary +4 where focus overrides motion.
To learnNo tier ladder — each technique is a single learnable unit with its own prerequisite. Trakata is the exception: taught by almost no one.

Slot badges. Each technique’s action cost is tagged in its header — ATTACK, FREE, FREE·FORCE, FULL·DEF (full legend in the Nine Forms).

These belong to no Form. They are the techniques a saber-wielder might learn from any teacher — Jedi, Sith, Mandalorian, or independent. They presuppose no philosophy. Some are common (Saber Throw, Blade Barrier); some are forbidden (Trakata). All work in conjunction with whatever Form the user practices.

Use Lightsaber skill for attack rolls unless otherwise noted. Attribute favours: DX-primary Force-users gain +4 to attack rolls with these techniques; ST-primary gain +4 to Lightsaber Quick Contests; Will-primary gain +4 in contexts where focus and intent override raw motion.

Saber ThrowATTACK

Throw at full Lightsaber skill at any target within 10 hexes.
RollLightsaberActionAttack (your attack maneuver)Range10 hexes, line-of-sight (thrown, returns)DefenceDodge or Parry; you cannot Parry while the blade is in flightFP4 (returns next turn) or 6 (same turn)ReactionNo

Effect. Throw at full Lightsaber skill at any target within 10 hexes. Blade travels straight. Base 4 FP: returns at start of next turn. 6 FP: returns same turn at end of action. While in flight: no saber Parry. Force-sensitive target catches/intercepts (Quick Contest): return delayed 1 turn.

You release the blade with a flick of the wrist and a pulse of Force energy. It screams outward in a flat arc, trailing a faint hum. It was always coming back.

No Parry while blade in flight. Blade travels straight — anything in path must dodge or be hit at −3. Favours: DX-primary +4 to throw roll.

Blade BarrierFULL·DEF

Spin blade at high speed creating defensive field.
RollLightsaberActionFull-turn defensive stance (Concentrate to start, then maintained)RangeSelf — a 1-hex spinning guardDefenceNone to cast — it lets you Parry ranged attacks from any direction; you cannot attack while it holdsFP2, then 1/turnReactionNo

Effect. Spin blade at high speed creating defensive field. All ranged attacks can be Parried regardless of direction — all directional penalties removed. Cannot attack while maintaining — all actions defensive. Any attempt to attack ends Blade Barrier.

The blade becomes a constant blur — a disc of light rather than a line.

Cannot attack while active. Favours: DX-primary +4 to Parry rolls while active.

Saber BindFREE

On successful Parry vs melee weapon, spend 2 FP to Force-lock the blades.
RollLightsaberActionFree action, on a successful ParryRangeMelee — weapon contactDefenceA Quick Contest of Lightsaber each turn to hold or break; both fighters are lockedFP2ReactionNo — Free action on a successful Parry

Effect. On successful Parry vs melee weapon, spend 2 FP to Force-lock the blades. Neither fighter can attack until the bind breaks. Winner of each turn's Quick Contest may attack that turn; loser remains bound. While bound: Disarm at +4 as your action.

Your blade catches theirs and holds — not by strength but by Force-assisted adhesion.

You are also locked. Losing LoS ends bind automatically. Favours: ST-primary +4 to Lightsaber in binding contest.

Guided StrikeFREE·FORCE

Spend 2 FP before attacking. Ignore up to −4 worth of cover/obstacle penalties.
RollLightsaberActionFree Force action with your attackRangeMelee (or a target within 2 hexes with no LoS, at −6)DefenceDodge; ignores up to −4 of coverFP2ReactionNo

Effect. Spend 2 FP before attacking. Ignore up to −4 worth of cover/obstacle penalties. May target opponent without direct LoS (within 2 hexes) at −6 to hit.

The blade curves slightly mid-swing — not by physics but by intent.

None — pure technique. Favours: Will-primary +4 to attack roll when targeting without LoS.

Sundering StrikeFREE·FORCE

Declare before attacking. If attack contacts opponent's weapon/lightsaber: Quick Contest of Lightsaber.
RollLightsaberActionFree Force action, declared before your attackRangeMelee — must contact the opponent's weaponDefenceA Lightsaber Quick Contest on contact; the loser is at −4 defences for the turnFP3 (wasted if no weapon contact)ReactionNo

Effect. Declare before attacking. If attack contacts opponent's weapon/lightsaber: Quick Contest of Lightsaber. Loser: −4 to all defenses for rest of turn cycle AND Will roll at −2 or lose current Form bonus until next turn. If dodged or no weapon contact: 3 FP spent, no effect.

The strike doesn't aim for the body — it aims for the guard.

FP lost if opponent dodges or avoids weapon contact. Favours: ST-primary +4 to Lightsaber Quick Contest.

Saber SplitATTACK

Throw saber on path through two adjacent opponents.
RollLightsaberActionAttack (your attack maneuver)Range10 hexes — two targets within 2 hexes of each otherDefenceEach defends independently; you attack each at −2; no Parry while the blade fliesFP6ReactionNoPrereqSaber Throw

Effect. Throw saber on path through two adjacent opponents. Attack each at −2. Both defend independently. Returns next turn. Requires Saber Throw known.

One throw, two arcs.

No Parry while blade in flight. −2 to hit both. All Saber Throw conditions apply. Favours: DX-primary +4 to both attack rolls.

Force CadenceFREE

Combine two attack modifiers in a single attack without All-Out Attack.
RollLightsaberActionFree action, declared with your attackRangeSelf / meleeDefenceAs the attacks you combine; you still pay both modifiers' penaltiesFP4ReactionNo

Effect. Combine two attack modifiers in a single attack without All-Out Attack. Examples: Deceptive + Rapid Strike (both apply simultaneously); Feint + immediate attack same turn; Telegraphic + Rapid Strike. All rules of both modifiers apply. Both modifiers must be valid for the attack type. Melee-only modifiers still only apply to melee. Once per turn.

Two techniques, one motion.

You still pay all penalties of both attack types. Once per turn. Favours: Will-primary +4 to Lightsaber skill for the attack action.

TrakataATTACK

The Forbidden Cut. Trakata is a saber technique, not a Force ability — it falls under no Form and is not taught in any…
RollLightsaberActionAttack maneuver, or set as a Wait triggerRangeMeleeDefenceA sharper Deceptive Attack (−1/−1); on repeats against a foe who has seen it, a Lightsaber Quick Contest firstFP0 — a saber technique, not a Force powerReactionNo

Effect. The Forbidden Cut. Trakata is a saber technique, not a Force ability — it falls under no Form and is not taught in any standard curriculum. It converts a Deceptive Attack into an Instant attack at an improved ratio: −1 to hit / −1 to the opponent’s defense (standard Deceptive is −2/−1). The blade deactivates mid-swing and reignites at a different angle; the cognitive gap between the vanishing blade and its reappearance exploits the mind’s expectation of continuity.

Usage modes. Direct attack: use Trakata as your Attack maneuver this turn; your Quick Actions for that turn are forgone entirely (full concentration goes into the technique). It does NOT interrupt or end any maintained Concentrate power. Wait maneuver: declare Wait with Trakata as the trigger; you may still use Quick Actions in the turns before it fires, and when the trigger condition is met Trakata resolves as your attack at its full −1/−1 ratio. Also does not interrupt maintained concentrations.

Repeat use — the mind adapts. On each subsequent Trakata attempt against the SAME target this encounter, make a Quick Contest of Lightsabersmanship before the attack resolves. Attacker wins: the −1/−1 Deceptive ratio holds. Defender wins: the attacker is thrown off — their defense is at −6 against the next attack that comes their way this turn cycle.

Witnessing the trick. The adaptation is not limited to those struck by it. Any other combatant who clearly SAW a Trakata attack resolve and COMPREHENDED what happened (GM’s call — a Force-sensitive, a trained duelist, or anyone with line of sight and the wits to read it) has likewise learned its rhythm: they too make the Quick Contest of Lightsabersmanship the first time Trakata is used against them, exactly as if they had already been struck by it. A foe who never saw it coming gets no such contest on the first use; the gap in their expectation of continuity is still intact.

The blade is there, then it is not, then it is — a hand’s-width left of where the eye swore it would be. Most who train the saber consider the trick dishonourable, or simply do not know it exists. Only a handful of practitioners across the galaxy are known to use it (tradition credits Atton Rand among them).

Forbidden or unknown to the Jedi Order, the Sith, and most independent Masters — using it openly carries a reputation cost among traditionalists. A foe who has weathered it once begins to read the rhythm; the Quick Contest on repeat attempts is the blade’s honesty catching up with the trick.

Document V

Force Training

The discipline of growth · study-hours & regimens · mentors, trials & the climb through the tiers
Document V · Force Training

How a Force-User Grows

Power is not given; it is practiced. Every tier climbed, every power unlocked, every Form mastered is the product of hours at the work — meditation, sparring, study, and the slow re-shaping of the self. This book gathers the training rules scattered through the Codex into one place and adds the regimens, mentors, and trials by which a student becomes an adept and an adept a master.

The Study-Hour

The campaign’s base unit holds here as everywhere: 200 hours of study or practice = 1 character point, and a point costs the same whether bought at creation or earned at the desk. A Force-user spends those hours differently than a soldier — in meditation, kata, and contemplation — but the arithmetic is identical.

Power Affinity — what the attribute on a power actually does. Every power block lists an Affinity attribute (IQ, Will, ST…). That attribute is not what you roll — using any power is always rolled against your Force attribute (the Force Primary chosen on your sheet), modified per the power. The Affinity is how the power wants to be learned: if it matches your Force Primary, you learn that power in ¼ the study hours; otherwise ½. (Both stack with a mentor's 150-hour rate — a matched power under a master is ~38 hours per point.) Where older text says "Roll IQ" or "Roll Will" for a power, read it as your Force attribute; printed target resistances (… resists HT, vs Will) are unchanged.

What an hour buys. Track hours toward the next point on the relevant skill (a Force power-skill, a Form, or an ordinary skill). At 200 logged hours, gain the point; the skill or power rises exactly as if purchased. Partial hours carry over. The character sheet’s Skill Training tab does this bookkeeping automatically.

Faster & slower. The 200-hour figure is the campaign default. A GM may rule that a gifted teacher, a holocron gatekeeper, or an intensive retreat shortens it, or that distraction and poor conditions lengthen it. The sheet’s training rate is adjustable for exactly this reason — but 200 is the standard, and nothing here assumes otherwise.

The one thing hours cannot buy. The Crossing — the committing deed at the heart of each tier — is never earned at the desk. Points buy capability; they never buy the turning of the soul. A student may train every skill to its cap and still not advance a tier until the Crossing is done, in play.

What You Are Training

A Force-user’s growth has four separate tracks, each trained on its own hours:

TrackWhat it isTrained byTypical time to first level
Power-skillsThe Very Hard skills your power rolls are made against (governed by IQ, Will, etc.).Meditation, guided practice, repeated use.~1–3 weeks of focused practice per early point.
PowersThe fixed-cost permissions a tier unlocks — once owned, attempted whenever skill allows.Instruction from a master, a holocron, or hard-won discovery.Tier I ~1 week; deeper tiers far longer (see 5.3).
FormsThe lightsaber combat disciplines (Doc IV), each a five-tier ladder at 4 pts per tier.Sparring and kata under a Form-master.~2–4 weeks per Form tier.
The selfThe Crossing and the capstones that gate each tier.Deeds, not drills — lived in play.As long as the story takes.

Climbing the Tiers

Advancing a tier in a tradition is the central act of Force training. It requires three things, in this order:

TierTraining time (guideline)Capstone gateThe Crossing
I · Initiate~1 weekNone — the door is open.The first commitment to the path.
II · Knight~3 weeks1 Mastery, or a +10-pt buy-past.A deed that proves the tier.
III · Master~6 weeks2 Masteries, or a +15-pt buy-past.A harder deed; the cost rises.
IV · Grandmaster~12 weeks3 Masteries, or a +20-pt buy-past.A profound act; the self is reshaped.
V · LegendA season; once-a-sagaThe Crossing itself — unbuyable.The defining deed of a life.
Masteries. Each Mastery capstone is a focused achievement within the tradition — a power driven to its limit, a trial passed, a teaching embodied. They may be pursued in parallel, but each needs its own practice. A player may pay past a Mastery’s training with points; the Crossing is never for sale.

Training Regimens

How the hours are spent shapes what is learned. These regimens are flavour-with-teeth: a GM may grant the listed edge when a character commits to one between adventures.

RegimenPracticeEdge while training
Meditative RetreatSeclusion, stillness, contemplation of the Force.Hours count at full value even under hardship; +1 to resist corruption that season.
Sparring CircleDaily combat practice with a partner or master.Form and Lightsaber hours count 25% faster; risk of training injury on a critical miss.
ApprenticeshipStudy under a named master of higher tier.Power-skill hours count faster (see 5.5); the master may set a Crossing.
Holocron StudyCommunion with a gatekeeper’s recorded mind.Access to powers no living teacher offers; the gatekeeper’s agenda is its own.
Trial by OrdealDeliberately seeking a hardship that tests the path.Can be a Crossing if survived with the path intact.
Wandering PracticeTraining amid ordinary life and travel, no formal setting.No bonus, no penalty — the reliable default; hours always count.

Mentors & Apprenticeship

A teacher of higher tier accelerates a student’s growth and opens doors that solitary practice cannot.

The teaching bonus. Under a willing master at least one tier above you in the relevant tradition, power-skill and Form practice hours count at 150 hours per point rather than 200 (the GM may improve this for a truly great teacher). The master must share the tradition; a Sith cannot teach the Living Path.

The master’s price. No master teaches for nothing. A Jedi master sets trials and expects the Code honoured; a Sith master demands service and will test loyalty through cruelty. A holocron gatekeeper bargains in knowledge and may pursue an agenda centuries old. The bond between master and student is a story hook, not just a multiplier.

Teaching as a master. A character may teach others, using the Teaching skill (Doc IX) alongside their own tradition rank. Guiding a student through a Crossing is itself a Mastery-worthy act for the teacher.

The Training Playbook

At a glance, for the table:

  • To raise a power-skill or Form: log practice hours (200/point, 150 under a master). The sheet tracks it.
  • To learn a new power: meet the tier gate (rank, capstones or buy-past), then pay the power’s point cost. A teacher or holocron may be required for the rarest.
  • To advance a tier: put in the training time, satisfy the capstone gate, and — the part no points can skip — do the Crossing in play.

Worked Regimens SW

From Hours to Days. The hour-count is the engine; a regimen is how a Force-user actually spends their days. Each row below is a realistic schedule, with daily hours that feed the 200-per-point math directly. A Tier-I student on the Open Hours (4 hrs/day) makes a power-skill point in roughly 50 days; the same student under a master cuts that to ~37 (150-hour rate). The Crucible is a brutal pace and the Sith use it precisely because it shortens the road — at a cost in burn-out and lost time that show up at the table.

RegimenDaily HoursMixSuits
— Initiate (Tier I) regimens — first weeks of practice —
The Open Hours4 hrs / day2 hr meditation · 1 hr Form kata · 1 hr studyBeginners with leisure; the standard Jedi schedule.
The Working Apprentice2 hrs / day1 hr meditation · 1 hr Form kataFor students with other duties; nearly everyone in this era.
Battlefield Initiate1 hr / day, every day30 min meditation · 30 min Form (1 hr full kata on rest-day)Wartime sensitives, line soldiers learning under fire, anyone with no spare hours but discipline.
— Knight (Tier II) regimens — deeper work, longer hours —
The Devoted Knight6 hrs / day3 hr meditation/study · 2 hr Form · 1 hr power practiceTython-residents; the new Order’s default.
The Travelling Knight3 hrs / day, on the move1 hr meditation · 1 hr Form · 1 hr study (variable)Field knights, smuggling crews’ Jedi members.
The Crucible8 hrs / day, brutal4 hr power · 2 hr Form · 2 hr forced trialHard schools that shorten the road through pain. Dark traditions overwhelmingly favour it.
— Master (Tier III) regimens — the great workings —
The Master’s Path8 hrs / day, sustained3 hr power · 2 hr study · 2 hr Form · 1 hr teachingStandard Master regimen; teaching counts.
The Long Retreat12 hrs / day, weeks at a stretch6 hr meditation · 4 hr power · 2 hr FormHolocron retreats, deep cave-work, sanctum vigils.
The Grey Discipline4 hrs / day, lifelongEqual mix; balance is the practice itselfConcordance, Bonded Blade, dual-practice paths.

A character is not locked to one regimen — circumstances change. Use the GM’s adjustable training-rate (the sheet’s training tab) to reflect a regimen change for a week, a tour, or a retreat. Burn-out: sustained regimens above 8 hrs/day call for a weekly Will roll at −1 per extra hour beyond 8; failure means burnout — the week’s progress is lost.

Mentors — Who Can Teach What SW

A Master Is a Tier Plus a Tradition. The 150-hr/point teaching rate of 5.5 needs a willing master, and a master is defined by two simple things: how far up the tradition they have climbed (their capability tier, 5.3), and which tradition that climb belongs to. A teacher can teach up to one tier below their own in their own tradition, can sometimes assist in adjacent ones, and cannot teach what they have not themselves crossed.

The Iron Rule. A student cannot reach Tier N in a tradition without instruction from a teacher who has themselves reached at least Tier N — or an equivalent gatekeeper-holocron (5.9). This is why Grandmasters are rare: each one represents an unbroken chain. Break the chain and the tier is closed until a new Grandmaster crosses.

By Capability Tier

Master’s TierCan Teach Up ToTypically FoundTypically Demands
— Teaching by Capability Tier —
Tier I · InitiateCannot formally teach. Can mentor pre-Initiates in basics and good practice.Anywhere learners gather.Companionship and shared work.
Tier II · KnightCan teach Tier I content reliably (their tradition’s opening capstones, the early powers, the first Form tiers).Field practitioners, garrison sensitives, travelling adepts.Service, usefulness, sometimes a duel.
Tier III · MasterCan teach up to and including Tier II content. Sets formal trials. Eligible to oversee a Crossing they have themselves crossed.Established schools, hidden sanctums, holocron archives.A bond of trust; the tradition’s code honoured; often a long apprenticeship.
Tier IV · GrandmasterCan teach up to Tier III. Their personal practice rewrites the tradition’s upper reaches; their students inherit lineage.Rare, often retired or hidden; some hold council seats.A test only the master can set, and a vow only they can witness.
Tier V · LegendEffectively anything within their tradition; their teaching itself becomes part of the canon.One or two in a generation. Frequently dead and known only through holocrons.To be sought, not asked. The teaching costs the student something they did not yet know they had.

By Lightsaber Form

The Form Gate. Saber Forms (Doc IV, §1.9) climb on the same ladder. To learn the next tier of a Form you need a Form-master at least one tier above you in that Form. A Form V master cannot teach you Form VII — even though they may rank higher overall. The Form is its own lineage.

TeacherCan TeachWhereDemands
— Teaching Lightsaber Forms — the same gate, narrower —
Form-master (a tier above the student in that Form)Can teach the next Form-tier — never beyond their own.Wherever sabers are practised seriously.Sparring under their eye, often for years.
A holocron of the FormCan teach up to the level the holocron was recorded at — no further.Old archives, hidden vaults, recovered relics.Gatekeeper’s test; sometimes a tradition’s code.
Self-study from cross-Form analogyUseful for cross-pollination; cannot raise a Form’s own tier.Reading and sparring with other Form practitioners.Time. The slowest path; no rate bonus.

By Tradition

Teacher / Student PairingWhat Can Pass BetweenWhereCost
— Teaching by Tradition — who can take a student down which path —
Same tradition, higher tierThe full 150-hr/point teaching rate; can oversee Crossings.The standard pairing.As the tradition demands.
Cross-tradition within a Path (Light or Dark)Can teach shared techniques and the Common Force; cannot teach tradition-specific powers.Same Path, different tradition.Mutual respect; alignment compatibility.
Across the Light/Dark lineCannot teach the other side’s powers at all. May teach the Common Force or saber-craft only.Rare and watched.Heavy. Crossing this line is itself a story event.
Grey teaching either PathA Grey practitioner can teach Light or Dark powers as Grey — restrained, balanced, and never as a turn to the dark.Concordance, Bonded Blade, and other Grey lineages.The student must accept the Grey discipline alongside the lesson.

No master teaches for nothing — the “demands” column is a story hook, not a transactional fee. Service rendered, codes honoured, oaths sworn, debts owed: these are the actual currency. A teacher who asks only credits is rarely worth what they charge.

Holocrons & Alternative Teachers SW

When No Living Hand Will Teach. A holocron is a master sealed in crystal — a curated record made by a Force-user. It carries the same Iron Rule (5.8): a holocron made by a Tier-III master can teach up to Tier-II content, no more. The gatekeeper enforces this, and many gatekeepers are more exacting than their living counterparts because they have nothing left to lose.

Working from a Holocron. Where a willing gatekeeper accepts the student, the 150-hr/point teaching rate applies as if a living master taught. Where the holocron resists, the student studies at the slower 200-hr rate or not at all. Dark-side holocrons are dangerous in proportion to what they teach — the risk column below is the campaign-level cost, not a one-roll affair.

Holocron / SourceTraditionTeaches Up ToCostDanger
— Light-side & Grey holocrons —
Knight-tier Jedi holocronLight PathUp to Tier I content reliably; some Tier II under a tradition matchPatient study; tradition’s code honouredLow. Asks of the student before it teaches.
Master-tier Jedi holocronLight Path; specific traditionUp to Tier II content; rarely Tier III gatesA formal trial set by the gatekeeperLow–moderate; the gatekeeper may withdraw.
Grandmaster Jedi holocronLight Path; lineage-specificUp to Tier III content; very rare Tier IVA vow only the holocron will witnessModerate; the student is changed by the encounter.
Grey holocron (Concordance or Bonded Blade)Grey disciplineTier I–II Grey practice; the dual-practice disciplineA long pilgrimage and a deedModerate; tests the seeker by silence and obstacle.
— Dark-side holocrons (do not handle alone) —
Knight-tier Sith holocronDark PathTier I Dark powers; saber-craft of crueltyA piece of the self per lessonSevere. Corrupts the unprepared.
Master-tier Sith holocronDark PathUp to Tier II Dark powers; the deeper FormsWill −1 per lesson to resist its pullSevere. Long contact corrupts.
Grandmaster Sith holocronDark Path; lineage-specificUp to Tier III content; teaching a way of beingWill −2 per lesson to resist; lasting changesExtreme. The maker’s personality may attempt to overwrite the student.
Hollowing holocron (any tier)The HollowingThe path of the predatorDeepening hunger each lesson; lasting changesCatastrophic. Most do not survive a full curriculum.
— Alternatives when no master and no holocron —
Solo studyWhatever the user already hasUp to current rank only; never Tier-up200 hrs/point baselineLow. Slow.
Self-discovery (in play)Any path the GM rules earnedA Crossing — never a Form-tier or power without oneA Crossing-worthy actVariable. The honest path.

The Trials & the Crossings SW

The Acts That Make a Knight, a Master. The Crossing (5.3) is the campaign-defining deed at each tier — the part no points can buy. The table below is the working list of trials and Crossings the era recognises, drawn from Jedi tradition, Mandalorian custom, and the Sith schools that survive. A character does not need to pass this exact trial; the row is a model. The GM’s job is to recognise when a campaign event is a Crossing — and to honour it.

Trial / CrossingTierWhat it asksHow it’s resolved
— Initiate → Knight (Trial of Courage) —
The First SaberTier I → IIA real fight in which the saber must be drawn for a stake the character believes inA scripted encounter or a campaign moment; the Crossing is the decision to commit to the blade.
The First Hard ChoiceTier I → IIA moral split where path-fidelity mattersIn play; a deed that proves the alignment.
— Knight → Master (Trial of the Flesh / Insight) —
The Trial of the FleshTier II → IIIEndure real cost — a wound, a loss, a refused easy pathOften a Crippling injury (Doc III) accepted rather than avoided.
The Trial of InsightTier II → IIISee past a deception — the master’s, the Order’s, the self’sA riddle, a holocron-trial, an investigation that reveals the lie.
— Master → Grandmaster (Trial of the Spirit) —
The Trial of the SpiritTier III → IVConfront the dark mirror — usually a vision of the self given to the darkIn play; resolution is the Crossing.
The Long VigilTier III → IVA retreat at Ilum, Tython, Dxun, or a tomb — weeks of silenceResolved by the GM; usually advances a capstone, nothing more.
— Sith & Dark trials —
The Test of CrueltySith Tier I → IIInflict deliberate harm on a being who trusted the apprenticeLasting darkening; the Crossing into the path.
The Master’s ReplacementDark Tier III → IVSupplant one’s own teacher — in some Dark traditions the only way to advanceKilling, exile, or formal challenge; the bond is broken either way.
The Hunger’s TrialHollowing Tier II → IIIFeed on a living Force-user and survive what you becomeSevere; few return as anything recognisable.

For a campaign that wants a more mechanical Crossing, frame it as a Quick Contest: the highest power-skill in the character’s tradition vs an effective 14 + tier number, with circumstantial modifiers. But the test rewards story; a clean roll without the deed is not a Crossing.

Sanctums & Training Sites SW

Where the Work Is Done. A regimen needs a place. The sites below are the working roster of the post-Accords galaxy — the sanctioned schools, the wild refuges, the dark places that teach in spite of themselves. A long retreat at one of these (5.7) typically lifts the per-point rate to 100 hrs (a great teacher and a sacred place compound), and several offer training otherwise unavailable. Read the access column carefully — not every door opens for credits or letters.

SiteTradition / AffinityOffersAccess
— The Restored Order’s sites —
An Order seat (e.g. Tython)The local established OrderFull curriculum to Master tier; sitting council; library and forgeOpen to chartered members; visitors by invitation. The era’s political settlement secures it.
IlumCrystal pilgrimageThe crystal caves — bonded saber-crystal; trials of the caveOpen to Initiates under the Order; informal to Grey. Cold work.
Dantooine Enclave (ruins)Old Order memory; Concordance refugeSilent meditation; the past’s lessons; rare archive fragmentsOpen and watched. Anyone may sit here; many do.
— Mandalorian & Grey sites —
Mandalore (clan training-yards)Bonded Blade; clan combatSaber-and-blaster doctrine; beskar discipline; honour-duelsBy clan invitation. Outsiders prove themselves first.
A wild war-moon (e.g. Dxun)Bonded Blade and battlefield traditionsBattlefield Force; survival under hardship; saber-and-blaster doctrineSurvive the moon. Many do not.
The Concordance groves (hidden)Concordance of FeathersThe dual practice; balance under constant testFound, never sought. The Concordance finds the seeker.
— Dangerous & dark sites —
Korriban — Valley of Dark LordsSith ancestryTomb-trials; holocrons; the temptation of the HollowingOpen and lethal. The dead defend the dead.
Malachor VWound in the Force; the TrayusThe deepest Sith and the longest silence; for those who would understand what was brokenOfficially forbidden. Reaching the surface is itself a trial.
Yavin IV (Massassi temples)Old Sith remnantsAncient Sith ritual; the Massassi survivors’ broken loreJungle and ghost; alone, unprepared, you do not return.
Lehon (Rakata Prime)The Builders’ lost knowledgeRakatan-era Force lore; Star Forge fragmentsThe way there is lost. Finding it is a campaign in itself.

— End of Force Training —

Document VI

Vehicles & Starship Combat

Personal · vehicle · starfighter · capital scale · hyperspace · boarding

Overview

What this covers. Scale and cross-scale damage, dogfighting, hyperspace, boarding actions, vehicle and ship damage, repair, and the full ship catalogue.

How to use it. Start with Scale & Scope to understand how personal and vehicle numbers talk to each other, then jump to the chapter your scene needs.

Part I

Scale & Scope

The four scales · cross-scale damage · crew positions · the GURPS Spaceships skeleton

Four scales of combat exist in this document, each with its own hex size, turn length, and damage range. The personal scale you already know from Docs I-III: 1-second turns, 1-metre hexes, weapons dealing single-digit dice. Above that lies the vehicle scale (speeders, walkers, light tanks), then the starfighter scale (fighters and bombers), then the capital scale (frigates through battleships). Combat within a scale uses standard rules. Combat across scales requires the conversion rules below, and one universal principle: the higher scale wins by a large margin.

Scale 1 Hex = Turn Length Hull HP Typical DR Typical Weapons
Personal1 m1 second8-200-20Pistols, rifles, blades, light explosives
Vehicle10 m5 seconds15-251-30Vehicle cannons, heavy blasters, light missiles
Starfighter100 m10 seconds25-404-19Laser cannons, ion cannons, proton torpedoes
Capital1 km1 minute280-2,40056-500+Turbolasers, heavy ion cannons, capital missile racks

Cross-Scale Damage

The Core Principle. Each scale up gives ×10 effective HP and DR. A weapon designed for a lower scale typically cannot meaningfully damage a higher-scale target — its damage is too small compared to the target’s armour and structure. A weapon designed for a higher scale devastates lower-scale targets — one solid hit is generally lethal.

Personal vs Vehicle. Personal weapons (rolling 1-5d typical) face vehicle DR 20-80. Most rolls don’t penetrate. Specific exceptions: anti-vehicle launchers (rocket launchers, missile tubes), heavy disruptors, dedicated AT munitions. Lightsabers SW are the universal exception — they cut through any DR over time (see Part VIII).

Vehicle Weapons vs Persons. A vehicle cannon’s listed damage (often 6d×2 to 6d×5 of burning or impaling) doesn’t roll out against a person — it instantly kills them on any hit. The GM may roll for visual effect, but no organic survives a direct hit from a vehicle-scale weapon barring extraordinary circumstances (heavy cover absorbing most of the blast, miraculous deflection by Force powers, etc.). Treat as auto-death on hit.

Starfighter Weapons vs Vehicle. A starfighter laser cannon (~6d×3 burning) versus a ground vehicle: roll, subtract vehicle DR (~6–16); the penetration vastly exceeds the vehicle’s Hull HP — destroying the struck system outright (usually the whole vehicle). Same as vehicle-vs-person scaling: don’t roll fine detail past obvious destruction.

Capital Weapons vs Starfighter. A turbolaser shot at a starfighter is an instant-kill on any hit. This is why fighters dance around capital ships rather than absorbing fire. The fighter’s job is to evade, not to tank.

Defending Across Scales

Higher-Scale Targets. A higher-scale target gets large Size Modifier (SM) bonuses to be hit. A starfighter is SM +5 to +7 (much easier to hit by a person aiming a personal weapon — though the damage is irrelevant). A capital ship is SM +10 to +20 (an aircraft carrier is hard to miss). These SM bonuses interact with the standard ranged combat rules from Doc II.

Lower-Scale Targets. Persons attacked by vehicle weapons get small SM penalties (a human is SM 0 from a vehicle’s perspective). The vehicle gunner still rolls Gunnery skill normally; SM is just a single modifier on top. A vehicle missile targeting a single person typically hits, but the question is whether the person can take cover.

Crew Positions & Skills

Single-Pilot Vehicles. Speeder bikes, swoops, starfighters: one pilot handles everything. Pilot skill (specific to vehicle type) covers movement, evasion, sometimes weapons. Some single-pilot fighters have a gunner seat or astromech assistant; in those cases the gunner has Gunnery skill independently.

Multi-Crew Vehicles. Walkers, gunships, frigates, capital ships: multiple crew positions, each with distinct skill rolls. Standard positions include Pilot/Helm, Gunner (Gunnery skill, possibly multiple gunners), Engineer (Mechanics / Engineering), Sensor Operator (Electronics Operation [Sensors]), Communications, and on capital ships also Captain (Tactics / Leadership), Damage Control (Mechanics).

Skill Distribution. Each crew position rolls their own skill each turn. The Captain may make a Tactics roll for the ship’s overall combat position; the Pilot rolls Pilot for movement; the Gunners roll Gunnery for attacks. Each station’s roll is independent. The whole ship’s effectiveness is the sum of individual crew performances.

Astromechs & Droid Crew. SW Astromech droids (R-series, etc.) commonly assist starfighter pilots — they handle navigation calculations, repair small damage, manage subsystems. Treat as having Mechanics, Navigation, and Electronics Operation skills at the droid’s rated level (typically 12-15). Astromechs can attempt repair rolls during combat at the pilot’s command.

Starship Combat

A ship is a big, fast character on a wide hex. If you can run a firefight, you can run a dogfight — the turn, the maneuvers, the defences and the wounds are the ones you already know. Only three things grow: the hex, the hull, and who rolls the dice.

The whole of it, in one breath

A starfighter is a character whose DX is its Handling, whose HP is its hull, and whose skills belong to its crew. It moves on a 100-metre hex, attacks and defends with the same maneuvers as a soldier, and dies when its hull pool runs out. Learn nothing new — read the same sheet, scaled up.

The Ship Block

A ship is statted like a person, in the same boxes, read the same way. The only renamed lines are the three a hull needs that a body doesn't.

Aurek-class Strikefighter — Republic line interceptor · starfighter scale · SM +4
Handling +1 · HT 12 · SR 4  — Handling is the ship's DX; it flies and dodges on this Hull HP 30 · Move 12 · Basic Speed 6.00 · Dodge 8 · SM +4 Hull DR 8 (energy/kinetic) · Shields SP 240 (recharge 24 / calm turn) Crew — Pilot: Piloting (Starfighter) · Gunner: Gunner (Beams) · Astromech: Mechanic, Astrogation Weapons — 2× Heavy Laser 60 (6d×3), Acc 14 · 4× Concussion Missile 80 (6d×4), lock-fire, ×2 vs shields, ammo 4
What is renamed, and what is not
On a characterOn a shipand so
HPHull HPthe wound track; whittle to 0 and she is crippled
DXHandling (Hnd)the chassis' competence — it flies and dodges on this
DR + armourHull DR + Shields (SP)shields soak first, then hull DR — the same subtraction
Basic SpeedBasic Speedunchanged — initiative
DodgeDodgeunchanged — 3d6 ≤ it to evade
MoveMoveunchanged — hexes per turn (1 hex = 100 m)
Size ModifierSMunchanged — large ships are easier to hit
Damage · Acc · RoFDamage · Acc · RoFunchanged — read the guns like a character's
your skillsthe crew's skillsthe ship has none of its own

The crew, not the ship. A hull has no skills. The Pilot flies and dodges on Piloting; the Gunner shoots on Gunner; the Engineer or astromech repairs on Mechanic; the jump is Astrogation. The same Aurek is deadly under an ace and clumsy under a cadet, because every roll the ship makes is a person's roll. Build the one habit: find the station, roll its skill.

Moving & Maneuvering

The hex is 100 metres; the turn is one second. Everything else about movement is the ground rule unchanged. A fighter's Move (8–14 for most) is how many 100-m hexes it crosses in a turn; a freighter manages 5–6, a capital 2–4. Heavy hulls turn and accelerate slowly — that sluggishness is low Handling, and it is the whole of what "maneuverability" means here.

The maneuvers are the ones you know. Move, Attack, All-Out Attack, All-Out Defense, Aim, Wait — identical to Doc I. A strafing pass is Move and Attack: −4 to hit, skill capped at 9, exactly as on foot. All-Out Attack trades your defences for a bonus; All-Out Defense buys a better Dodge; Aim feeds Acc onto next turn's shot. Nothing here is a new verb.

"Getting on their tail" is just Facing. The thing every space battle is about — position, the six-o'clock, the clean firing solution — is already your Facing & Flanking rule (Doc I §1.6). Manoeuvre into a foe's rear hex face and they get no active defence against your shot: no Dodge, nothing. To contest it, the two pilots make a Quick Contest of Piloting; the winner takes (or keeps) the rear arc. There is no bespoke "position track" — it is hexes and facing.

The old aerobatics are optional flavour. Immelmanns, Split-S, scissors and yo-yos return as optional Piloting techniques a pilot may buy and roll for an edge — they sit on top of the core like Acrobatic Dodge sits on top of Dodge. A table that wants them has them; a table that doesn't loses nothing. They are never required to fly.

Attacking & Dodging

The shot. A gunner rolls 3d6 ≤ Gunner + SM + range band + Aim, with the modifiers below. The size of the target helps you; the range hurts you; aiming a turn erases the range with the weapon's Acc.

Range is a ruler, named in bands. Gunnery does not count metres. Read the Speed/Range ruler re-anchored to 100-m hexes, with the band names on top as a convenience: Knife ±0 · Close −4 · Medium −6 · Long −8 · Extreme −12. Every gun lists an Acc; one turn of Aim adds it to the next shot, so a patient gunner shoots through the band penalty.

The dodge. A ship evades on Dodge = 3 + Piloting∕3 + Handling — recognisably Dodge, rolled 3d6-under like any character's. The pilot's skill is most of it; Handling separates a nimble interceptor (+2) from a barge (−2) flown by the same hand. All-Out Defense (Increased) buys +2 as ever; flying evasive is −2 to the gunners but a steadier dodge. A foe on your rear face gets no dodge at all.

Modifiers — the whole of them
SituationEffect
Target's Size Modifier+SM to hit (fighter +4 … capital +10/+20)
Range band±0 / −4 / −6 / −8 / −12
Aimed last turn+Acc
Firing while evasive−2 to your gunners
On the target's rear facetarget gets no active defence
Capital gun vs fighter-size−6 (too slow to track)
Wide-arc turret, RoF 5++1 to +2 to hit (Doc II RoF)

Damage & the Hull HP Pool

One subtraction, in order. A hit resolves exactly as on the ground, in three steps: ① Shields soak first, off the SP pool. ② Hull DR reduces the remainder 1:1, less any armour-piercing (AP cuts DR before the subtraction). ③ What is left comes off the Hull HP pool. A centre-mass hit does nothing fancier — it whittles the pool. That is the entire damage step.

Ship guns hit flat — the one real change from ground combat

At ship scale a weapon does its flat number — no damage roll. A heavy laser does 60; take 60 off the shields and move on. Thirty guns on a capital ship resolve as thirty subtractions, not thirty handfuls of dice, and a furball actually plays.

This loses nothing the fight wanted. The HP pools were tuned to average damage to begin with, so flat numbers make the fight-length math exact rather than lucky — and the swing still lives where it belongs: the to-hit roll still crits, and a called shot still spikes. You keep the drama; you drop the arithmetic.

Against people and droids, you roll. Each gun also carries a dice form — the ×3 family — for when a ship weapon is brought down onto personal scale. A clean hit on an unarmoured being is auto-death regardless, so the dice only come out against something durable enough for DR to matter: a war droid, beskar, a personal shield. The block prints both: Heavy laser — 60 (6d×3). Ship play reads the 60; the dice wait for the ground.

Lasers are the floor. Every weapon does a flat, consistent number, and the one axis that ever modifies it is the shield axis. Lasers are the baseline — always reliable, always available, infinite ammo, lowest damage, full value against shields. Any weapon that out-damages a laser pays for it in lock-and-flight time, ammunition, credits, or range. Nothing out-classes the laser for free.

Shields, and the two things that cheat them. Shields are an ablative buffer that soaks before hull. Exactly two families beat them. Ion counts ×2 against shields and does nothing to hull — a shield-stripper and system-killer, never a hull-breaker. Warheads (missiles, torpedoes) count ×2 against shields too, but carry through to the hull behind them. When a hit drops the shields mid-strike, the leftover damage spills onto hull at face value. Screens recharge 10% of maximum per calm turn (halved on a damaged emitter, nothing on a destroyed one).

When the hull starts to go

Systems break on thresholds, not on every hit. The first time the pool falls to ½, and again at ¼, roll 3d on the Systems Strain table: one random system goes ◐ damaged. At ¼ the ship is wallowing — −1 to all crew rolls. This is the smoke-in-the-cockpit middle of a fight: the warning before the kill, and the window to break off, divert power, or board.

3dSystem strained → ◐3dSystem strained → ◐
3–4Weapon mount11–13Sensors
5–7Sublight engines (Move −)14–15Manoeuvring (Hnd −1)
8–10Shield generator (regen cut)16–18Reactor (fire) / Hyperdrive

Crippled, then destroyed. Hull HP 0 = Crippled — adrift and helpless, shields down, drive and guns dead, but the hull intact and salvageable: board it, capture it, or finish it (a great many prizes are taken exactly here). Below −½ maximum = Destroyed — structural breakup or reactor breach, 1d rounds for the crew to reach the pods. A single hit exceeding the whole pool still vaporises a fighter; a Reactor or Hull ✕ from a called shot ends a ship whatever the pool reads.

Hull HP is set by class — no bookkeeping. Like Move and SM, the pool comes from the hull's class, so no ship needs its own number tracked. Speeders/walkers 15–25 · fighters/interceptors/bombers 25–40 · shuttles/freighters/gunships 90–200 · corvettes 280–420 · frigates/cruisers 750–1,100 · capitals 1,600–2,400.

The Weapon Ladder

The one rule of ship weapons

Every ordnance tier out-damages every laser per hit — and counts shields at half — but needs a lock, arrives one to three turns later, can be shot down by point-defence, and can be dodged again on arrival. Lasers are reliable; torpedoes are the payoff. Ion is not in the damage race at all — it strips shields and kills systems. Choose between a sure thing now and a heavy thing later.

Ship-scale damage (flat) · personal-scale roll in parentheses
WeaponShip dmgvs shieldsvs personTrade-off
Light laser40full4d×3 burnreliable · no lock · Acc, RoF
Medium laser50full5d×3 burnthe workhorse
