Heavy laser60full6d×3 burnenergy ceiling — the top beam
Ion cannon40×24d×3 ionstrips shields / kills systems · 0 hull
Concussion missile80×26d×4entry ordnance already beats the top laser · lock · +1 turn · ammo
Proton torpedo100×26d×5lock · +1–2 turns · scarce · the trench-run shot
Heavy torpedo120×26d×6lock · +1–3 turns · rare · capital-killer

Lock, flight, intercept. A warhead needs a sensor lock (a Sensor Ops or Gunner roll on the target) before it fires; loosed without one it is dumb-fire at a heavy penalty. It does not arrive the turn it is fired — a missile lands +1 turn later, a torpedo +1 to +3 by range. In flight it can be shot down by point-defence and dodged again when it arrives. The torpedo is a heavy bet, not a guaranteed hit — and that, not its damage, is its only cost.

Called Shots & System Damage

A called shot is a targeted attack. Aiming for a specific system — engines, a shield emitter, a weapon mount, the bridge — is the targeted attack of Doc I, at the same skill penalty. Penetrating damage then lands on the named part instead of the pool: ◐ damaged on any penetration, ✕ destroyed if it beats the part's outward DR twice over. This is how a sharp gunner ends a bigger ship without grinding the whole pool down — knock out the hyperdrive so it can't run, the shield emitter so the screens stop, the bridge so the crew rolls collapse. It is also where the variance the damage roll "lost" actually went: to the spot the player chooses.

Crossing Scales

Up the scale, you die; down the scale, you don't roll. The hull/DR bands already do the work the old "×10 per scale" rule did, so that rule is retired. What remains are the rulings that keep cross-scale fights fast: a ship gun on a person is auto-death on any hit — roll only if the target is armoured, droid, or shielded enough that its DR could change the answer, using the weapon's dice form. A fighter's guns on a capital sting only its exposed mounts (called shots); its hull pool laughs off centre-mass beam fire. A capital gun on a fighter is auto-kill, which is why fighters live by the Dodge and the rear arc, not the hull.

Legacy stat blocks. Hull DR is quoted net — the old armour-divisor baseline (÷5) is baked in, so play is 1:1. If you meet an old card with hundreds of DR on a fighter, divide by 5 once and write it down. The Damage Threshold (DT) stat is gone; toughness is the Hull HP pool.

A Turn, Played Through

Setup. A pilot flies the Aurek above (Gunner 14, Piloting 13); a Sith fighter (SM +4, similar block, 240 SP) crosses at Close band.

Turn 1 — position, then beams. Both pilots make a Quick Contest of Piloting; the pilot wins and slides onto the Sith's rear face — the Sith gets no dodge. The gunner fires both heavy lasers: 3d6 ≤ 14 + 4 (SM) − 4 (band) = 14, lands. Flat damage: 60 + 60 = 120 straight off the Sith's shields — 240 → 120 left. No dice, two subtractions, done.

Turn 2 — the Sith breaks free and bets heavy. The Sith wins the Piloting contest, shakes the tail, and — out of clean beam range — takes a concussion missile lock on the Aurek (Gunner roll: good) and fires. The missile does not arrive yet. It is in the air.

Turn 3 — the warhead lands. The Aurek's point-defence tries to swat it (misses); the pilot dodges — 3 + 13∕3 + 1 = Dodge 8, rolls 9, fails. Impact: 80, ×2 vs shields = 160 off the Aurek's screens — 240 → 80 left. One missile has done what nearly three heavy-laser hits would, because it punched the shields at double. But it cost the Sith a lock, a turn of flight, a point-defence pass and a dodge to land — the heavy bet, paid in full.

From here it is more of the same: lasers grind the screens off, ion drops them in half the passes, a second warhead cracks a naked hull, and a called shot to the engines ends the run. Every number is a subtraction; every roll is a person's skill.

The detailed reference that follows — vehicle handling, ramming, cover, sensors, atmospherics, bridge crew, capital weapons and hyperspace — remains in force for the cases this core does not spell out. Where the two differ on the combat math itself, this Starship Combat section governs.

The GURPS Spaceships Skeleton

⚠ Superseded by Starship Combat (above) for the combat math. Retained below for reference and detail.

RAW Reference. Where useful, this document draws on the GURPS Spaceships series for ship classifications, system definitions, and combat resolution at starfighter and capital scales. The fundamentals — pilots roll Pilot, gunners roll Gunnery, damage scales by 10× per scale — are consistent across the books. Star Wars-specific modifications (hyperdrives, blaster cannons, beskar-plating, Force interactions) overlay the base mechanics where noted with the SW tag.

Abstraction at Capital Scale. Combat at the capital scale (1-minute turns, 1 km hexes) is necessarily abstracted. Each bridge officer rolls once per turn for their station’s contribution; the GM resolves outcomes narratively rather than tracking individual weapon shots. This contrasts with starfighter combat which still resolves per-attack at finer grain.

Force-Sensitive Pilots. SW Force-sensitive characters piloting vehicles may have specific Force-related abilities and bonuses defined in Doc IV. Until then, Force-sensitive pilots use their base Pilot skill normally. The GM may permit minor cinematic bonuses (a Force-sensitive pilot anticipates an attack by half a second, +1 to a single Dodge roll once per encounter, FP cost) at the GM’s discretion. Major Force-piloting techniques are deferred to Doc IV.

Part II

Personal &
Vehicle Scale

Speeders · swoops · walkers · ground combat vehicles · ramming · swoop racing

Ground-and-air combat at vehicle scale: speeder bikes, swoops, landspeeders, airspeeders, walkers, and light combat vehicles. Hex size is 10 m, turn length 5 seconds. Most personal weapons cannot damage a vehicle. Most vehicles can damage persons trivially. The interesting combat happens vehicle-vs-vehicle.

Vehicle Class Hull HP DR Move Crew Typical Weapons
Speeder bike152-440-60 hex (400-600 m/turn)1Light blaster cannon (optional)
Swoop151-350-80 hex (500-800 m/turn)1None typical (racing) or light blaster
Landspeeder (civilian)153-520-30 hex (200-300 m/turn)1-4None typical
Airspeeder (civilian / patrol)184-730-50 hex (300-500 m/turn)1-2Optional light cannon
Combat speeder206-1030-40 hex2-3Blaster cannons, light missiles
Light walker (scout class)208-1210-20 hex (slow)1-2Twin blaster cannons
Heavy walker (assault class)2512-205-15 hex (slow)3-6Heavy cannons, missile racks
Light combat tank2510-1615-25 hex2-3Main cannon, secondary blasters
Heavy combat tank2516-3010-20 hex3-5Heavy cannon, ion cannon, missile launcher

Vehicle Handling & Maneuvers

The Pilot Roll. When a vehicle attempts something challenging — tight turn at speed, evasive maneuver, threading through obstacles, jumping a gap — the pilot rolls their relevant Pilot skill specialisation (Pilot [Speeder Bike], Pilot [Walker], Pilot [Tank], etc.). The vehicle’s Handling (Hnd) modifier applies: maneuverable craft give Hnd 0 to +2, sluggish craft give Hnd −1 to −3. Critical failures roll on the vehicle’s mishap table (typically: lose control briefly, side-swipe an obstacle, or in the worst case crash).

Stability & Recoil. Vehicles have a Stability Rating (SR) — similar to a person’s Rcl for ranged combat. Multi-shot weapons (auto cannons) have their accuracy degraded by SR each shot beyond the first. Stable platforms (heavy tanks) have SR 1; nimble craft (speeder bikes firing while in motion) have SR 3-5.

Movement & Acceleration. Listed Move is top speed in hexes per turn at vehicle scale. Acceleration: a vehicle can change speed by Move/5 per turn (roughly 20% per turn). Slamming to a stop or full-throttle from rest takes 4-5 turns for most vehicles. Speeder bikes and swoops accelerate faster (Move/3 per turn) — that’s part of why they’re hard to chase.

Ramming & Collisions

Ramming Damage. Vehicle ramming damage = (vehicle HP / 5) × (relative speed in hexes per turn / 10), as crushing damage. Damage applies to both vehicles. A speeder bike (HP 40) ramming at 40 hex/turn into a stationary target: 40/5 × 40/10 = 32 damage. The bike takes 32 damage too — likely destroying itself. Ramming is a desperation tactic.

Glancing Collisions. Side-swipes, scrapes, partial impacts deliver half ramming damage (round down). DX or Pilot roll to convert a head-on collision into a glancing blow.

Obstacles & Terrain. Hitting a wall, tree, or building at speed: treat as ramming a stationary object. Treat the obstacle’s HP as effectively infinite — the vehicle takes full ramming damage, the obstacle is undamaged or minimally damaged. Soft obstacles (mud, brush): reduce damage by half.

Pedestrian Impact. Hitting a person with a moving vehicle: vehicle ramming damage applies fully (rare for the person to survive). The person rolls DX or Acrobatics at a steep penalty to dive aside (DX−2 at high speed, DX−4 at extreme speed). Successful dive: take half-damage at most. Failed dive: full vehicle ramming damage.

Cover & Concealment

Vehicle Cover for Persons. A vehicle provides excellent cover for persons taking shelter behind it — DR equivalent to the vehicle’s DR for cover purposes. A speeder’s flank: DR 15. Behind a walker: DR 60. Some vehicles also have specific gunner ports / hatches that limit incoming fire to a much smaller angle.

Cover for Vehicles. Vehicles can use terrain features as cover — buildings, hills, dense forests. A vehicle in cover is at +2 to hit (partial concealment) to +4 (good cover) to its opponents.

Vehicle-vs-Vehicle Combat

Standard Resolution. Vehicle combat uses the standard Doc I-II resolution: attacker rolls Gunnery skill, defender rolls the vehicle’s Dodge — 3 + (Pilot ÷ 3, round down) + Hnd (the single canonical formula; see Ship-to-Ship Defence, Part VIII). Damage resolves as a pool: shields soak first, hull DR (minus the weapon’s flat AP, if any) reduces what remains, and the penetrating damage comes off the vessel’s Hull HP — systems break at integrity thresholds or under a called shot (see Hull Integrity in Part VIII). Critical hits to vehicles roll on a vehicle critical table (see Part VIII).

Gunnery vs Piloting. Vehicle Dodge is 3 + (Pilot ÷ 3, round down) + Hnd — for most craft a little lower than personal Dodge, and for capital hulls (Hnd −4 to −5) effectively none. Speedy maneuverable vehicles are harder to hit because they’re moving fast through hexes; slow heavy vehicles must rely on armour. A speeder bike’s effective Dodge is similar to a person’s; a heavy tank’s is much lower (it’s a sitting target — but it has DR 80+).

Position Advantage. A pilot who maneuvers to a target’s rear or flank gains +2 to Gunnery rolls and the target’s Pilot/Dodge is at −2. Standard ”behind the enemy” engagement. Won by Pilot vs Pilot Quick Contest each turn until position is established.

Swoop Racing SW

Race Mechanics. Swoop racing is a popular if dangerous sport — high-speed circuits through canyon courses, urban infrastructure, or open desert. Each race turn (~10 seconds), each racer rolls Pilot (Swoop) at modifiers for course conditions: −1 to −4 for difficult sections, +0 for clear straightaways. Winning the round = advance position. Critical success = pass two rival racers; critical failure = mishap (crash, off-course, deduction).

Course Hazards. Tight turns (Pilot −2 to navigate at speed), low ceilings (DX or strike head), other racers’ wake (−1 to Pilot for 1 turn), terrain hazards (rocks, debris). The GM rolls 1d for hazards per turn — on a 6, a random hazard interrupts. Pilots roll vs the hazard’s specific challenge.

Sabotage & Foul Play. Common in unsanctioned races. A rival may attempt to bump (Pilot vs Pilot for a ramming-style collision at low relative speed), spike a tire (Mechanics roll pre-race), or sabotage equipment (Stealth + Mechanics). The Hutts’ races on outer-rim worlds are notorious for foul play; sanctioned circuits on the Core Worlds enforce strict no-sabotage rules.

Betting Culture. Most swoop races have heavy betting attached. Win/place/show, head-to-head, prop bets. Race fixers run odds and skim. Some characters become race regulars to fund their main activities. Treat as a wealth/social skill subgame.

Part III

Starfighter
Scale

Single-pilot ships · dogfighting · shields · sensors · critical hits to systems

Most ship-to-ship combat happens at starfighter scale. Single-pilot fighters and bombers, 100-metre hexes, 10-second turns. Personal-scale combat skills don’t transfer directly — Pilot (Starfighter) and Gunnery (Starfighter) are separate specialisations. Sensor Ops becomes critical here; finding the enemy through jamming and debris is half the fight.

Starfighter Class Hull HP DR Move Shields Typical Loadout
Light interceptor255-850-70 hex (5-7 km/turn)20-50 SPTwin laser cannons
Standard fighter307-940-60 hex40-80 SPLaser cannons, light torpedoes
Heavy fighter3510-1230-45 hex80-150 SPHeavy lasers, ion cannons, proton torpedoes
Bomber4011-1920-35 hex100-200 SPConcussion missiles, proton torpedoes, ion cannons
Gunship12011-1725-40 hex120-250 SPMultiple cannons, missile racks, turret guns

Pilot, Gunnery & Sensor Ops

Pilot (Starfighter). The fundamental skill for all flight, evasion, and maneuvering. IQ-Hard. Each turn the pilot rolls Pilot for whatever maneuver they’re attempting — pursuit, evasion, position gain, recovery from damage. Pilot also covers the ship’s Dodge value — 3 + (Pilot ÷ 3) + Hnd, the canonical formula.

Gunnery (Starfighter). Separate skill for the ship’s weapons. In single-pilot fighters, the pilot also acts as gunner — but the rolls are separate, and the pilot can only fire weapons when not maneuvering at full effort (no Gunnery on a turn with maximum-evasion Pilot rolls).

Electronics Operation (Sensors). Reading the sensor display, identifying contacts, detecting jamming attempts, breaking through ECM. Sensor Ops is the critical skill for awareness — without it, you’re flying blind. In single-pilot ships the pilot also operates sensors; in multi-crew ships there’s a dedicated sensor station.

Astromech Assistance. SW A starfighter with an astromech droid socket: the droid handles navigation and minor repairs, freeing the pilot’s attention. Astromech provides +1 to Pilot rolls in atmosphere (the droid handles steering adjustments) and can attempt 1 repair per minute during combat (Mechanics roll, see Part IX).

Dogfighting Maneuvers

⚠ Superseded by Starship Combat (above) for the combat math. Retained below for reference and detail.

Position & Initiative. Dogfighting is a continuous battle for advantageous position. The pilot behind their opponent (six o’clock) has a clean firing solution; the pilot facing forward only sees the opponent occasionally. Each turn, pilots roll Pilot vs Pilot Quick Contest to gain or maintain position. Winner gains position advantage for the next turn.

Position Advantage Effects. When you have position on the foe: +2 to Gunnery (clean firing arc), foe’s Dodge at −2 (you’re hitting their less-protected aspect). Loss of position: opponent gains the +2/−2 swap.

Maneuver Effect
Tight TurnPilot at −2 to rotate 90° in one turn. Useful for evading pursuit. Success: gain rear arc on pursuer.
Barrel RollPilot at −1. Adds +2 to Dodge against next attack. Pilot only — one attack defended.
LoopPilot at −2. End the turn 180° reversed. Loses momentum (next turn Move halved).
ImmelmannPilot at −3. Quarter-loop with half-roll at top. Reverse direction AND end up over the opponent. Position advantage on success.
Split-SPilot at −2. Half-roll then half-loop. Reverse direction quickly without losing speed.
ScissorsPilot vs Pilot QC. Defender at −2 (forced into the chase). Winner gains position. Repeats until one pilot escapes.
Head-On PassBoth pilots Pilot rolls + Gunnery rolls. Either may damage the other. Pure damage trade; no position advantage gained.
Yo-YoPilot at −2. Pull up to reduce relative motion, then come back down for shot. Gains gunnery +2 next turn at cost of position.

Range & Engagement Bands

⚠ Superseded by Starship Combat (above) for the combat math. Retained below for reference and detail.

Range Bands. Starfighter combat plays out in range bands rather than fine-grained hexes. Knife-fight: ≤1 km (≤10 hex). Most cannon fire effective; missiles awkward at this range; mid-air collisions possible. Close: 1-5 km. Standard dogfight range; cannons and missiles effective. Medium: 5-15 km. Missiles and torpedoes excel; cannons less effective. Long: 15-50 km. Torpedoes and long-range weapons; cannons drop off. Extreme: 50+ km. Long-range weapons only; very hard to hit; sensors marginal.

Range Penalties — the Band Modifier. Ship gunnery does not count metres: apply the band’s flat modifier to Gunnery. Knife-fight ±0 · Close −4 · Medium −6 · Long −8 · Extreme −12. Every ship weapon lists an Acc rating from its fire-control computer; Aim for one turn and add Acc to the next shot — patient gunners erase the band penalty. (This replaces the old “multiply km by 1,000 and read the Speed/Range Table” rule, which produced unrollable −16s and was never how the table played it — see the Balance & Errata Log.)

The Gunner’s Procedure — at the table. ① Pick a mount · ② target class sets SM · ③ pick the band · ④ did you Aim last turn? (+Acc) · ⑤ roll 3d6 ≤ Gunner + SM + band + Aim − 2 if you’re flying evasive (capital guns −6 against fighter-size; RoF 5+ adds its bonus) · ⑥ the defender rolls Dodge = 3 + Pilot÷3 + Hnd (−2 vs guided warheads) · ⑦ on a hit, resolve on the target’s card: shields soak (ion ×2) → hull DR − AP → Hull HP (a called shot lands on that system instead).

Worked example. A gunner (Gunner 14) brings the Aurek to Close band on a Sith fighter (starfighter, SM +4) and snaps a shot — no Aim: 14 + 4 − 4 = 14. He rolls 11 — hit by 3. The Sith pilot jinks: Dodge 3 + (Piloting 12)÷3 + Hnd 1 = 8, evasive +2 = 10 — rolls 11, misses the jink. The laser cannon rolls 6d×3 = 63 burn. The fighter’s screens are fresh — SP 240: the shields soak all 63 (177 left — two more passes like that and it flies naked, less whatever its ⟳ recharge claws back). Later, shields down: 63 − hull DR 10 = 53, straight off the Hull HP pool. The Aurek only carries HP 30, so 53 blows clean through it — and because one hit exceeded the fighter's whole pool, it vaporises (fighters that drop their screens do not last). Against a freighter (HP ~120) that same 53 is nearly half the hull: two such hits cross the ½ and ¼ thresholds, each forcing a Systems Strain roll as a system flickers ◐ — and the ship fights on. That buffer is what the pool buys you.

Shields

Shield Mechanics. Most starfighters carry deflector shields — energy fields that absorb damage before hull. Shields have Shield Points (SP) separate from HP. Damage reduces SP first; once SP reach 0, remaining damage hits hull. Shields recharge at 10% of maximum per turn spent out of direct fire (the sheet’s ⟳ tool; halved while the emitters are ◐ damaged, nothing once ✕ destroyed) — routing power to shields doubles that turn’s recovery.

Shield Facing. Starfighter shields often have facings — fore, aft, port, starboard. Each facing has its own SP. The pilot may route power to one facing (gains the other facings’ SP into that one, leaving others at 0 — risky but tactical). Routing takes 1 turn; full reroute returns 1 turn after.

Shield Harmonics. SW Some specialised weapons (ion cannons, certain proton torpedoes) bypass or reduce shield effectiveness via wavelength matching. Treat as Armour Divisor 5+ against shields specifically. Most ”anti-shield” weapons are heavier and slower-firing than standard cannons.

Shields vs Atmospheric Weapons. Shields work against energy weapons normally. Against kinetic/physical attacks (proton torpedoes, missile warheads), shields work but at reduced effectiveness (treat shields as DR ½ vs kinetic). Against atmospheric pressure waves and gravitational effects: shields ineffective.

Sensors & Detection

Standard Sensors. Each starfighter has a sensor suite with rated range and resolution. Active sensors (broadcasting) detect more reliably at long range but reveal the broadcaster. Passive sensors (listening only) avoid detection but reduce range significantly. Sensor Ops roll to interpret contacts.

Jamming & ECM. Electronic countermeasures degrade enemy sensor effectiveness. A sensor operator running ECM rolls Electronics Operation (Sensors) vs opponent’s same roll each turn. Winner detects, loser is jammed (no sensor reading on that target for the turn). Specialised ECM equipment adds bonuses (+2 to +5).

Sensor Lock for Missile Fire. Most starfighter missiles and torpedoes require a sensor lock before firing — a successful Sensor Ops roll on the target. Once locked, missiles guide themselves on the lock. Breaking lock requires evasive maneuvering: opposed Pilot vs the original Sensor Ops roll. Missiles fired without lock are ”dumb-fire” (much lower hit chance).

Stealth Hulls. SW Some specialised craft have radar/sensor-reducing hull coatings (effective DR vs detection rolls of +5 to +10). Rare; usually military or smuggler tech. Doesn’t make ships invisible — just harder to detect at range.

Atmospheric vs Space

Atmospheric Flight. Most starfighters can fly in atmosphere. Atmospheric maneuvering is slower (Move reduced 25-50% due to drag). Tight turns are easier (atmosphere provides lift to bank against). G-loading matters: tight maneuvers cost 1-2 FP per turn for the pilot (or stim/medical equivalent). Some craft are atmosphere-only or vacuum-only.

Vacuum. Space flight has no drag — vehicles maintain momentum. Direction changes require thruster fire. Pilots must think in three dimensions and use vector physics. The ”feel” of dogfighting in space differs from atmosphere; experienced pilots specialise.

Re-entry & Atmospheric Entry. Coming down from orbit: heat plates and atmospheric flares. Pilot roll to enter cleanly; failure = damage from buffeting. Re-entry from extreme angles or speed: critical risk of breakup.

Critical Hits to Starfighter Systems

Critical Systems. When a critical hit lands on a starfighter, the GM rolls 3d for which subsystem is damaged: 3-4 Engines (Move halved), 5-6 Weapons (one weapon disabled), 7-8 Shields (1 facing destroyed, no regen), 9-10 Hull (extra damage), 11 Sensors (no detection, fly blind), 12-13 Comms (silent), 14-15 Life Support (HT roll or pilot suffers), 16-17 Pilot wounded (pilot takes personal damage; ejection countdown), 18 Catastrophic (fuel rupture or hyperdrive breach).

Hyperdrive Damage. SW A critical hit may target the hyperdrive. Damaged hyperdrive: cannot jump (or jumps at half speed). Destroyed hyperdrive: stranded until repaired. Some critical hits to the engines may cascade into hyperdrive failure (1d turn delay; possible explosion). Hyperdrive repair is a specialty (Part IX).

Bailout / Ejection. When a fighter is hit critically: pilot may attempt to eject (DX or Pilot roll, takes a turn). Successful: pilot in escape pod/seat, fighter falls away. Failed: pilot stays with ship until next attempt. In space: ejection seat with limited life support (1d hours). In atmosphere: parachute or repulsorlift descent.

Part IV

Capital Ship
Scale

Bridge crew · abstracted combat · sections & subsystems · turbolaser exchanges

At capital scale, combat necessarily becomes abstract. Frigates, cruisers, dreadnoughts. Hexes are 1 km, turns are 1 minute, and each bridge officer contributes a skill roll per turn rather than tracking individual shots. The Captain’s Tactics roll positions the ship; the Pilot/Helm executes; gunnery batteries trade fire as broad volleys; sensors and damage control resolve as ongoing background. Individual fighters operate inside this scale at their own pace; their interaction with capital ships is one-sided unless the fighters carry capital-class ordnance.

Capital Class Hull HP DR Move Crew Notes
Patrol corvette35056-9620-30 hex (20-30 km/min)10-30Light turbolaser, ion cannons, often single shield generator
Light frigate750100-16015-25 hex50-200Multiple turbolasers, point-defense, full shield matrix
Heavy frigate / destroyer900130-21010-20 hex200-800Heavy turbolasers, missile racks, hangar for fighters
Cruiser1,100176-2568-15 hex1,000-5,000Capital weapons throughout, hangar, large crew
Dreadnought / battleship2,000300-5005-12 hex (slow)5,000-30,000Capital weapons, multiple hangar bays, command-grade systems
Capital flagship / war station2,400500+3-8 hex (very slow)30,000+Massive armament, planet-affecting capability in some cases

Bridge Crew Positions

Captain. Overall command. Rolls Tactics (Naval) or Leadership each turn for ship’s combat positioning, orders to subordinate stations, broad strategic decisions. Captain’s roll may give +1 to +3 bonus to specific crew stations for that turn (focus fire orders, evasive maneuvers, full damage control, etc.).

Executive Officer (XO). Second-in-command. Relays orders, manages crew details, takes over if Captain incapacitated. May contribute Tactics or Leadership rolls to support the Captain’s position.

Pilot / Helm Officer. The conn — actually flies the ship. Rolls Pilot (Capital Ship). Capital ships maneuver in 1-minute turns; what the pilot does this turn determines the ship’s position next turn. Pilot also handles atmospheric entry if relevant, planetary approach, jump initiation.

Tactical Officer. Manages weapons batteries. Rolls Gunnery (Capital) for each weapon group fired. Capital weapons are usually grouped into batteries — turbolaser bank, ion cannon battery, missile launcher rack. Each battery gets one Gunnery roll per turn for its fire group.

Sensor Officer. Detection, identification, ECM. Electronics Operation (Sensors) at capital-scale equipment. The sensor officer may attempt to detect hidden ships, identify hyperspace approach, jam enemy sensors.

Communications Officer. Comms, broadcasts, interception. Electronics Operation (Communications). Critical for fleet coordination, intercepting enemy comms, sending out distress signals or false signals.

Engineering. Power management, system performance. Engineer (Capital Ship) or Mechanics with appropriate specialty. May reroute power between systems (boost shields at cost of engines, etc.), manage hyperdrive, handle critical repairs.

Damage Control. Mechanics-led teams that handle real-time hull breaches, fires, power conduit damage. Roll Mechanics each turn that damage occurred to mitigate or repair small portions of damage in flight.

Abstracted Combat Resolution

⚠ Superseded by Starship Combat (above) for the combat math. Retained below for reference and detail.

Each Turn (1 Minute) Sequence. (1) Pilot announces movement intent. (2) Captain rolls Tactics for positioning bonus. (3) Sensor Officer rolls for any detection / jamming. (4) Tactical Officer rolls Gunnery for each weapon battery firing this turn. (5) Damage Control rolls for any damage from previous turns being managed. The GM resolves outcomes narratively — capital ship combat is a continuous flow, not turn-by-turn skirmish.

Weapon Battery Damage. A turbolaser battery firing once does (say) 6d×8 damage per shot (in the 30-50 range after shields and DR), with shot count multiplied by the battery’s listed RoF — typically 2-5 shots per turn. Damage rapidly accumulates. Multiple batteries firing in coordinated volleys can destroy a frigate in 5-10 turns.

Shield Matrix. Capital ships use a deflector shield matrix that wraps the entire hull. Shield Points are large (1,000+) and regenerate at a rate of 50-200 per turn from shield generators. Generators are critical subsystems — if destroyed, shields collapse and the ship is vulnerable.

Hull Sections. Capital ship hulls divide into sections — forward (bow), aft (stern), port (left), starboard (right), dorsal (top), ventral (bottom). Each section has its own HP and may have local subsystems (a hangar bay on the ventral, a bridge on the dorsal, etc.). Damage to a section may destroy local systems independent of the ship’s overall HP.

Heavy Capital Weapons

Turbolasers. The standard heavy capital-grade weapon. Damage 6d×6 to 6d×20+ burning, 1:1 vs hull DR. Fires in batteries; coordinated volleys. Slow tracking — limited to broadside engagement against most ship sizes; bow/stern arcs are weaker.

Heavy Ion Cannons. Anti-shield and anti-droid (vs droid fighters) weaponry. Damage as turbolaser but targets shields preferentially (ion: ×2 vs shields). Can also affect electronic systems.

Missile / Torpedo Racks. Launcher pods. Concussion missiles, proton torpedoes, ion torpedoes. Slower-firing but high damage and harder to evade. Some have planetary-attack variants. Sensor lock required (as starfighter scale, but at longer range).

Point-Defense Lasers. Anti-fighter, anti-missile defense. Small turret weapons mounted across the hull. Their job is to shoot down enemy fighters and incoming missiles before they reach the capital ship. Roll Gunnery against incoming attackers; success destroys the missile or damages the fighter.

Charric Batteries. SW The Chiss capital-grade weapon — phased-plasma in place of turbolaser bolts. Damage 6d×6 to 6d×20+ burning · AP 20 (a penetration edge over turbolasers; cuts shields and hull alike). Chiss fire-control and sensor superiority give +1 to hit and a longer effective range than comparable batteries. Smaller fighter, gunboat and turret mounts — charric cannon and charric turret — do the same at vehicle scale. Fired in coordinated batteries.

Breach Ordnance. SW Chiss armour-piercing missiles and torpedoes — breach missiles (fighter and gunboat scale) and breach torpedoes (capital scale). Treat as missile / torpedo racks, but built to crack armour rather than shields: +2 AD over the standard warhead for a slightly tighter blast. Sensor lock required, but Chiss targeting makes the lock faster.

Heavy Capital-Grade Specials. SW Some flagships and war stations carry specialized weapons: large-scale ion projectors, superlaser systems (rare; usually reserved for fortress installations), mass driver kinetic projectors, gravitic anomaly generators. These are scenario weapons rather than standard combat resolution.

Capital Ship Critical Systems

Critical Hits at Capital Scale. Critical hits to capital ships strike at major subsystems. The GM rolls 3d on the capital crit table: 3-4 Bridge (officers wounded or killed; command disrupted), 5-6 Reactor (power loss, half ship systems offline), 7-8 Shield Generator (shield matrix degraded or destroyed), 9-10 Weapons Battery (one battery destroyed), 11-12 Engines (sublight propulsion crippled), 13-14 Hyperdrive (cannot jump; possible explosion), 15-16 Hangar (fighter complement destroyed), 17-18 Catastrophic (cascade failure; ship destroyed within 1d minutes).

Reactor Breach. SW A reactor breach is the spectacular finish of capital ship combat. After enough damage to engineering systems, the reactor goes critical. The GM declares 1-3 minutes to ship destruction; the crew may attempt to eject (escape pods), abandon ship, or flee in fighter complement. Bridge officers face hard choices.

Boarding Vulnerability. When shields collapse, capital ships become vulnerable to boarding actions. See Part VI. A coordinated boarding party can disable a much larger ship from within — a classic Mandalorian tactic.

Part V

Hyperspace
Navigation

Astrogation · jump types · hyperlanes · interdictors · mishaps · navicomputers & astromechs

Hyperspace navigation is the connective tissue of the galaxy. Without it, the Republic is a few core worlds and a vast unreachable rim. With it, courier ships span the galaxy in hours and fleets project power across thousands of light-years. The mechanics are technical and unforgiving — a mis-calculated jump can throw a ship into a stellar mass or strand it in deep space. This part covers everything from quick microjumps to the lore of the great hyperlanes.

Jump Type Range Calculation Time Astrogation Modifier Mishap Risk
Microjump (in-system)Within a star system10 seconds - 1 minute+2 (short, easy)Low
Standard Jump (known route, sector)System to system within a sector1-10 minutes0Standard
Long Jump (hyperlane to hyperlane)Across multiple sectors via main lanes10-60 minutes−2Moderate
Galactic TransitAcross the galaxy via established lanes1-24 hours of calculation; days of transit−4High if route unfamiliar
Blind Jump (unknown destination)To uncharted spaceHours of calculation; outcome uncertain−8Severe
Emergency JumpAny direction, away from dangerSingle turn (rushed)−6 to −10Severe

The Astrogation Skill

Skill Mechanics. Astrogation is the IQ-Hard skill for calculating hyperspace jumps. Each jump requires an Astrogation roll, modified by jump type (see above), the ship’s hyperdrive class (faster drives = +1 to +2), the navigator’s familiarity with the route (+1 to +3), local hazards (gravity wells, debris, recent stellar activity), and time pressure (emergency jumps suffer huge penalties).

Calculating a Jump. The navigator declares destination. The GM determines the jump type and applies modifiers. The navigator may spend extra time for bonus (each +1 to skill costs 1 doubling of the time). Roll Astrogation. Success: jump calculated correctly, ready to engage hyperdrive. Failure: calculation has errors — either re-calculate (more time) or attempt jump with mishap risk. Critical Success: jump shaves 10-20% off transit time. Critical Failure: serious miscalculation; jumping triggers a mishap (see below).

Astrogation Skill Levels. Skill 10-12 is competent: can handle standard jumps reliably. Skill 14-16 is good: can attempt long jumps and unfamiliar routes. Skill 18+ is expert: can attempt blind jumps with reasonable chance, calculate emergency jumps without disaster. Smugglers, military officers, and dedicated navigators tend toward the higher end.

Hyperdrive Classes

Class Rating. Hyperdrive classes are inverse — lower number = faster. Class 0.5 (top military / racing): ~24 hours to cross the galaxy on main lanes. Class 1.0 (premium commercial): ~48 hours. Class 1.5 (standard commercial): ~72 hours. Class 2.0 (mid-grade): ~96 hours. Class 4+ (older / cargo): days to weeks. Specific times vary by route and astrogation skill.

Backup Hyperdrives. Many ships carry a slower backup hyperdrive (typically Class 8 to 12). If the primary fails, the backup gets the ship home — slowly. This is the smuggler’s safety net.

Hyperdrive Failure in Hyperspace. If the hyperdrive fails mid-transit, the ship reverts to realspace at the current point along the route. This is usually deep space, far from any settlement. Distress calls go out; rescue may be days away.

Hyperlanes & Routes

The Major Lanes. SW Four to five major hyperlanes span the galaxy: Hydian Way (eastern spinward), Perlemian Trade Route (north spinward), Corellian Run (south spinward), Rimma Trade Route (south rim). These are well-mapped, well-traveled, and ships traveling on them gain +2 to Astrogation rolls for familiar routes. Most galactic commerce uses these lanes.

Secondary Lanes. Hundreds of secondary hyperlanes branch from the major lanes, connecting smaller worlds. Less well-mapped, less reliable, but still navigable for experienced astrogators.

Smuggler’s Routes. SW Off-the-map routes used by smugglers and pirates. Not in standard navicomputer databases. Astrogation at −2 to −4 to navigate. Faster than going the long way but risky (more mishaps). Smugglers may know specific shortcuts that shave hours off transit; these are valuable secrets.

Unknown Regions. Vast stretches of the galaxy remain uncharted — particularly the Unknown Regions beyond the rim. Blind jumps into these areas are extremely dangerous. Only a handful of explorers and Sith expedition forces venture there in this era.

Hyperspace Mishaps

Mishap Triggers. A mishap occurs on critical failure of Astrogation, on attempting a jump with too-large penalties, or when navigating through dangerous space (interdiction fields, recent gravitic anomalies). The mishap table is rolled by the GM in case of failure:

3d Hyperspace Mishap
3Catastrophic re-entry. Ship exits hyperspace into a stellar mass or planet’s atmosphere. Crew rolls HT or take massive damage. Likely ship destruction.
4–5Off-course arrival. Ship arrives at the wrong destination (different system, possibly hostile). Astrogation to re-calculate; possibly emergency jump.
6–7Hyperdrive damage. Hyperdrive shorts during transit; 1d HP of mechanical damage to the drive. Cannot jump again until repaired (Mechanics roll, Part IX).
8–10Transit delay. Arrival time increased 25-50%. No other damage. Annoying but not dangerous.
11–13Sensor blackout. Ship arrives but sensors are disoriented; 1d minutes before full readings available. Vulnerable during this window.
14–15Power conduit failure. Hyperdrive emergency-cuts; 1d turns until ship power restored. May suffer environmental damage (hyperspace venting).
16Mid-transit emergency exit. Ship is forced out of hyperspace mid-route, in deep space. Astrogation to re-plot; may take days to reach destination.
17Hyperspace storm. Ship encounters a rare hyperspace anomaly. Lost for 1d days; arrives at destination eventually with full crew. Some crew may experience hallucinations or precognitive flashes (Force-sensitives more susceptible).
18Unknown destination. Ship arrives somewhere completely outside the original plan. May be charted but distant, or completely uncharted (Unknown Regions). Survival is a story arc on its own.

Fuel, Range & Running Costs SW

Two Tanks, Two Fuels. SW A starship tracks two separate reserves. Reactor fuel (RF) — ordinary fuel cells — feeds the sublight drive, the reactor, shields and weapons; it burns by the day while cruising and by the hour in a hard fight. Hyperfuel (HF) — hypermatter and coolant — is spent only to jump, a real gulp each time. Note both on the sheet and tick them down; sitting idle in dock costs almost nothing of either.

What Burns Hyperfuel Faster. A jump’s HF cost climbs with the route length (a galaxy-crossing run costs four times a short hop) and with a faster hyperdrive (a Class 0.5 racing drive burns half again what a standard one does; an efficient Class 3–4 cargo drive sips). See the jump table below.

What Burns Reactor Fuel Faster. Combat. A corvette sips 30 RF a day cruising but drinks 40 RF an hour under battle power — shields up, guns hot, hard evasive burns. A thirsty engagement can cost more RF than a week of travel.

Running Dry. SW Most ships carry many jumps’ worth, so fuel is light bookkeeping — but miscount on a smuggler’s route far from a depot and you revert to realspace stranded (Hyperspace Mishaps). Refuel at every port; never spend the backup drive’s reserve.

Fuel Types — What Burns What SW

Fuel TypePowersSold AsCostLCNotes
Hyperfuel (HF)Hyperspace jumps onlySealed hypermatter charge50 cr / HFLicHypermatter + coolant. Volatile, licensed. The expensive one.
Reactor fuel (RF)Sublight drive, power, shields, weaponsFuel cell10 cr / RF—The ship’s everyday burn; stocked at every port.
Energy cell (EC)Repulsorlifts, droids, powered gearRechargeable cell25 cr new / 2 cr recharge—Scaled-up cousin of a blaster power cell. Rechargeable.
Combustion fuelWheels, tracks, cheap thrustersLitre or fuel slug1 cr / litre—Cheap, dirty, ubiquitous on the Rim & in industry.
Tibanna gasBlaster bolts; hyperdrive coolant top-upCartridge / flasksee Weapon MaterialsLicCross-fuel: also the coolant a hyperdrive sheds on a hard jump.

The master table: hard tank sizes and burn rates by ship class. RF figures are reactor-fuel units (cruise per day / combat per hour); HF figures are hyperfuel units (tank, and the cost of one average jump). Full-refuel cost is simply (RF tank × 10) + (HF tank × 50) cr — it all reconciles.

Ship ClassReactor Fuel
(RF tank)		Cruise
(RF / day)		Combat
(RF / hr)		Hyperfuel
(HF tank)		Avg Jump
(HF)		Jumps
(per tank)		Full Refuel
(cr)
Starfighter
Aurek, Sith fighter		151.5 (~10 d)342~2350
System shuttle / transport
Republic shuttle		401.5 (~26 d)281.5~5800
Light freighter
Ebon Hawk–class		801 (~80 d)2121.2~101,400
Bulk freighter / hauler
Cargo hauler		2501.5 (~165 d)1241.7~143,700
Corvette
Hammerhead–class		90030 (~30 d)40608~7–812,000
Frigate / cruiser
Republic cruiser		3,00050 (~60 d)12020040~540,000
Capital warship
Interdictor, dreadnought		20,000200 (~100 d)6001,000120~8250,000

Endurance in parentheses assumes ordinary cruising with no combat. “Jumps per tank” is the HF tank divided by an average-route jump; long hauls and fast drives cut it sharply (see below). Larger hulls cost far more HF per jump — it takes more hypermatter to push more mass through hyperspace. Spot vs. port price. The Full-Refuel column is the reconciled spot cost (RF×10 + HF×50). Busy core depots sell at spot; remote, blockaded, or monopoly ports routinely mark fuel up 2–3× — budget for it on the Rim.

Hyperfuel per Jump — Route × Drive SW

The Formula. HF spent on a jump = the ship’s Avg Jump HF (above) × the route-length factor × the hyperdrive-class factor. A light freighter (Avg Jump 1.2 HF, Class 1 drive) making a long haul spends 1.2 × 2 × 1 ≈ 2.4 HF; that same ship on a galaxy-crossing run burns 1.2 × 4 ≈ 5 HF — nearly half its 12-HF tank in one jump. Round to the nearest half-unit.

ModifierFactorExample / Note
— Route Length × (applied to the ship’s Avg Jump HF) —
Short hop×0.5In-system or one sector over.
Standard jump×1Cross-sector on a major lane — the listed Avg Jump.
Long haul×2Region to region (Core to Mid Rim).
Galaxy-crossing×4Rim to rim, end of a major lane to the other.
— Hyperdrive Class × (faster drives guzzle) —
Class 0.5–0.75 (racing / military)×1.5Speed costs fuel.
Class 1–2 (standard)×1Baseline efficiency.
Class 3–4 (cargo / old)×0.7Sips fuel; slow.

Transit Times — The Holomap Standard SW

The Galactic Holochart computes journey times with this rule, and table play uses the same math. A jump covers each grid-region of the holomap at a base 16 hours for a Class 1.0 drive; the hard regions — the Deep Core, the Outer Rim, and Wild Space — take 24 hours per region (mass-shadows, thin charts, no beacons). Multiply by hyperdrive class (Class 2 doubles it, Class 0.5 halves it), then by the lane factor: ×0.75 on a major hyperlane, ×1.0 on a minor lane, ×1.25 plotting through open space. The holomap's route-length classes for the fuel formula above map to the same chart: a short hop is ≤1.2 grid-squares, a standard jump ≤4, a long haul ≤9, and anything beyond is galaxy-crossing. Plot any course on the Holochart and it reports the transit time, the HF burn, the reactor fuel for the cruise, and the full fuel bill — all reconciled with this section.

Land, Air & Repulsor Vehicles SW

Why Ground Travel Is Cheap. Most speeders and repulsorcraft run on rechargeable energy cells — a few credits to top up at any power post — which is why a planet-side journey is trivial next to a starship’s fuel bill. Heavy and military repulsorcraft draw on the same reactor cells (RF) as ships; primitive and backwater rigs still burn combustion fuel by the litre. No vehicle on this table uses hyperfuel — HF is for crossing the void, nothing else.

VehicleFuelCapacityRange / EnduranceRefuel / RechargeLC
— Repulsorlift (rechargeable energy cells) —
Speeder bikeEnergy cells1 EC~400 km / a day’s ride4 cr rechargeLic
Swoop bikeEnergy cells1 EC~300 km hard riding4 cr rechargeRes
LandspeederEnergy cells2 EC~600 km / a day+8 cr recharge—
AirspeederEnergy cells2 EC~500 km8 cr rechargeLic
Cargo skiff / sledEnergy cells2 ECa day hauling8 cr recharge—
— Heavy & military (reactor cells) —
Military repulsortankReactor cells40 RF bank~8 hrs combat ops400 crMil
Industrial walker / haulerReactor cells80 RFdays of work800 crLic
— Combustion (cheap & backwater) —
Wheeled / tracked utilityCombustion60 L tank~600 km60 cr fill—
PodracerCombustion + energyrace tankminutes at full powervaries, costlyLic

Operating Costs — Refuel, Docking, Maintenance & Crew

Service / UpkeepTypical CostNotes
— Fuel & Berthing —
Reactor fuel (RF)10 cr / unitSublight, power, combat. Tank sizes above.
Hyperfuel (HF)50 cr / unitJumps only. The pricey reserve.
Docking fee — backwater10 cr / dayDirt strip, no services.
Docking fee — standard port50 cr / dayFuel, basic repair available.
Docking fee — Core world200 cr / dayPremium berth, full services, scrutiny.
Private hangar / berth (month)500–5,000 crSecure, your own lock.
— Maintenance & Upkeep —
Routine maintenance (light freighter, month)3,000 cr~1% of hull value; skip it and accrue malfunctions (Doc VI).
Hyperdrive overhaul15,000 crAfter heavy use or a mishap; restores reliability.
Astromech / droid upkeep (month)600 crParts, lubricant, memory wipes.
Spaceport resupply (food/water, month)900 crPer crew; consumables for the trip.
— Crew (monthly salary) —
Pilot6,000 crSkilled; more for a name.
Mechanic / engineer4,500 crKeeps the maintenance bill honest.
Gunner4,500 crPer turret crewed.
Co-pilot / astrogator5,400 crEarns it on a bad jump.

A ship that earns its keep clears its monthly nut — berth, maintenance, crew, resupply — before profit. For a light freighter with a small crew that is roughly 6,000–9,000 cr a month before fuel; add RF and HF on top of every run. Which is why every captain in the galaxy is chasing one more cargo run.

The Ship Catalogue SW

The previous sections priced fuel and berth, statted the dogfight, and ran the boarding action. This is the catalogue itself — the hulls a campaign actually flies, gets shot at by, or runs from. Stat lines feed directly into the systems above: Hull and Shield DR plug into cross-scale damage; RF and HF tanks plug into the fuel chapter and reconcile to refuel cost; weapons feed dogfighting and boarding. Each named ship is a canonical pattern of the era; the costs are baselines, run them through the Variation System (Trade & Economy doc) for the actual price you pay.

Reading a Row. Hull DR is the ship’s armour after shields drop. Shield SP is the ablative shield pool: it soaks before Hull DR, ion and warheads count ×2 against it, and it recharges 10% per calm turn (see Starship Combat). Hull HP — the wound pool — is read from the ship’s class band (fighters 25–40, freighters 90–200, corvettes 280–420, capitals 1,600–2,400), not listed per ship. Weapons lists the standard fit; modified examples vary. Crew + Pax is operating crew and additional passenger/troop seats. RF / HF is the ship’s fuel reserve in the two-tank model — multiply by 10 and 50 respectively for full-refuel cost (Fuel chapter). Cost is the standard-port baseline.

Starfighters, Bombers & Shuttles

Single-Seat to Squad. Small craft: fighters, interceptors, bombers, and the shuttles and transports that ferry crews up and down. Hyperdrives where present are Class 1–2; the patrol & in-system craft have none and ride a carrier or hyperspace ring. Hull and shield DR sit on the starfighter scale — cross-scale damage in Doc VI applies.

Ship
Role		Hull DRShield SPWeaponsCrew + PaxRF / HF tankHDCost (cr)LCNotes
— Starfighters & Interceptors —
Cartel enforcer fighter
Hutt Cartel — hired-gun fighter		72252× blaster cannon112/31160,000ResCheap, mass-bought fighters flown by cartel pilots and hired guns — mismatched and well-used, but everywhere. Refuel 1,000 cr.
Pirate snubfighter
Pirate — raider fighter		62002× blaster cannon; 2 missiles114/41150,000ResStolen and modified fighters — fast, fragile, flown by raiders who don’t expect to retire. Refuel 1,000 cr.
Nssis-class clawcraft
Chiss Ascendancy — line fighter		82702× charric cannon; 2 breach missiles115/41380,000MilClaw-winged Chiss fighter — phased-plasma cannon and a sensor suite that sees first and shoots first. Class 1 hyperdrive. Refuel 1,100 cr.
Chiss recon fighter

Freighters, Haulers & Civilian Hulls

The Working Hulls. The free-trader’s ladder — from a courier-class light freighter (the Ebon Hawk pattern) through mass-market Corellian designs, the Mandalorian Crusader-freighter, bulk haulers, and the Czerka corporate fleet. These are the ships a PC crew actually owns and flies, and the cargo runs in Trade & Economy assume hulls from this table.

Warships — Corvettes, Cruisers & Capitals

When the Navy Shows Up. Combat ships: from corvettes a determined crew might tangle with up to capital warships they had best run from. Hull/shield DR are on the capital scale; cross-scale damage in Doc VI is what makes a freighter’s pop-gun useless and a capital’s broadside annihilating.
