Faction Fleet Doctrine —amp; Signature Tech

Two fleets of equal tonnage do not fight alike. Each power builds and flies to its own doctrine, and several field technology no one else has. Apply these on top of the hull stats above — they are what makes a Chiss squadron feel nothing like a Mandalorian one across the table.

Galactic Republic — Reliability and Reach. SW Balanced, well-shielded warships from standardised parts, fielded in numbers. Standardised hulls: +2 to all Repair rolls on Republic ships, and any Republic hull can be cannibalised for parts in the field. Strong shields: shield DR runs high for the class — they soak and endure. The Jedi, the Iktotchi Arbiters and most Core worlds fly these same hulls and share the profile.

The Sith Empire — Offense and Terror. SW Wedge-hulled, heavily armoured, built to break a line and a will. Imperial turbolasers: main capital batteries hit one step harder — 6d×N becomes 6d×(N+1). Heavy armour: hull DR runs high; Sith ships tank punishment. Sparse point-defence: the trade is thin anti-fighter cover — enemy fighters and bombers strike Sith capitals at +2 to hit. Bring fighters to a Sith fight. Battle-meditation: a Sith Lord aboard can grant the crew +1 to all rolls for a scene (Doc IV).

The Chiss Ascendancy — See First, Strike First. SW Sleek, sensor-saturated, disciplined, lighter-hulled. Sensor supremacy: a Chiss force cannot be ambushed, detects hostiles far out, and fires first — +2 to hit in the opening round of any engagement. Charric weapons: every Chiss gun is phased plasma at +1 AD over the equivalent bolt, and shrugs off the cold and interference that foul others (Doc VII). Lighter armour: hull DR runs low for the class — they avoid the blow rather than soak it.

The Mandalorian Clans — Beskar and the Boarding Axe. SW Brutal, fast, built to close; beskar-laced hulls and fragile shields. Beskar-laced hulls: reduce an attacker’s AD by 1 against the first hit each turn, and lightsaber and disruptor strikes lose their edge against the plating (Doc VII). Brutal acceleration: +1 to all dogfighting and evasion rolls. Fragile shields: shield DR runs low — so they close fast. Boarding doctrine: magnetic grapples, troop-heavy holds and Basilisk war droids — they board at a marked advantage (Doc VI).

Hutt Cartels —amp; Pirates — Cheap, Hidden, Predatory. SW Mismatched, modified, often stolen; built to take prizes, not win wars. Ion and tractor: raider weapons disable rather than destroy — a crippled ship is worth more than a dead one. Hidden guns: a “civilian” smuggler or barge fires first as a surprise (defender at −2 to react). Unreliable: any ship-systems critical hit is one step worse. Numerous and expendable: they come in numbers and don’t mourn losses.

The Corporations — Whatever Sells. SW Not a navy but a marketplace — Czerka, Corellian Engineering, Rendili, Aratech. Competent, unremarkable hulls sold to anyone: Republic, Hutt, or Rim. Corellian builds the galaxy’s freighters; Rendili and Aratech build warhulls on contract; Czerka builds whatever the quarter’s order demands. They have no doctrine of their own — only customers.

Common Ship Modifications

After the Showroom. No working captain flies stock. The mods below are what every freighter ends up carrying within a year of leaving the yard. Costs are quoted as a fraction of the hull’s base price for the structural mods, and flat credits for accessories. Most need a workshop; field-fits are possible but earn a permanent −1 to the relevant roll until properly bedded in.

ModificationEffectCostNotes
— Hull & survivability —
Reinforced hull (+25% DR)+25% Hull DR15% of hull costHeavier, slightly slower handling.
Upgraded shield generator+25% Shield DR20% of hull costBetter cooling; one extra charge per fight.
Ablative armour plating+1 DR vs ion; +10% Hull DR10% of hull costSlags off under sustained fire; cosmetic damage every fight.
— Drive & range —
Hyperdrive upgrade (Class −1)One class faster (e.g. 1 → 0.75)40% of hull costBurns 1.5× HF per jump (Tool 1c, Fuel chapter).
Backup hyperdrive (Class +2)Backup unit for emergencies15% of hull costSlow, but you get home.
Sublight tune-up+1 Move; +1 Handling10% of hull costSmuggler’s standard first mod.
Expanded fuel reserve+50% RF and HF tanks10% of hull costMore jumps before refuel; heavier hull.
— Weapons & sensors —
Quad-laser upgrade+1d damage per upgraded turret25,000+ cr per turretHeat-sink overhaul needed at high rate.
Concussion missile rackAdd 4 concussion missiles90,000 crReloads need a hangar.
Ion cannon (light)Add 1 ion turret; AD 5 vs droid/ship systems140,000 crThe boarding party’s favourite.
Sensor & ECM suite (military)+2 Sensor Ops; +2 vs target lock75,000 crMil-grade; not legal to civilians on most worlds.
— Cargo, bay & quirks —
Concealed cargo holds10% bay invisible to standard scans45,000 crVital for the smuggler’s actual job.
Expanded cargo bay (+50%)+50% cargo capacity10% of hull costEats one cabin and some shield-generator slack.
Armoured cockpit+2 Pilot vs critical hits60,000 crWorth every credit when the day comes.
Slave-circuits / autopilotRun the ship single-handed (penalties)110,000 crThe lone-wolf option; never quite as good as a crew.

Module Slots — What a Hull Can Carry

No hull has infinite room. Every structural modification (reinforced hull, shield upgrade, hyperdrive class, expanded bay) and every active system (ECM suite, tractor beam, shield booster, cloak, repair drone) takes a module slot, and a hull only has so many. Size sets the budget — this is why a freighter cannot bolt on a frigate's loadout, and why captains agonise over the last slot.

Hull classModule slotsNotes
Starfighter / interceptor / bomber1–2One signature trick — a torpedo bay or a sensor suite, rarely both.
Shuttle / light freighter / gunship3–4The smuggler's playground; concealed holds and a shield booster eat slots fast.
Bulk freighter / corvette5–6Room for point-defence, a tractor, and real cargo.
Frigate / cruiser8–10Carries its own sensor picket, interdiction, and damage-control suites.
Dreadnought / capital12+Fits whatever doctrine demands — and a fighter wing besides.

Slot cost. Most mods cost 1 slot; heavy structural work (reinforced hull, hyperdrive class change, expanded bay) costs 2. Weapons mount in hardpoints, not module slots — a hull's gun count is its own limit (the catalogue lists it). Faction signature gear (beskar plating, charric guns, a Basilisk cradle) is fitted at build and does not spend a slot.

Ship Weapons — Cannons, Missiles & Ordnance

What a fighter, freighter or gunship actually carries into a dogfight. Pick what the hull can mount and the crew can serve. Damage is vehicle-scale and applies 1:1 against ship DR — no dividing. AP N = the weapon ignores N points of DR. (Hull DR catalogue values already include the old armour-divisor baseline.) Capital batteries — turbolasers, heavy ion cannons, charric batteries and breach ordnance — are listed under Heavy Capital Weapons.

WeaponDamage (AP)AccRoFRclBest bandRoleNotes
— Cannons (direct-fire) —
Light laser cannon6d×2 burn631Knife/CloseShort-range dogfightingTwin-linked — high rate, low punch.
Laser cannon6d×3 burn621CloseBalanced primary gunThe fighter standard.
Heavy laser cannon6d×4 burn · AP 5711Close/MedLong-range, hard-hittingSlow tracking; bullies bombers and freighters.
Quad laser cannon6d×3 burn541Knife/CloseWide-arc turretGunner-served — the freighter's teeth.
Rapid-fire / burst laser6d×2 burn582KnifeClose-range suppressionDrains the capacitor fast.
Ion cannon6d×2 ion621CloseAnti-shield / anti-systemDrops shields and saps power; barely marks hull.
— Warheads (missiles, torpedoes, mines) —
Concussion missile6d×4 burn · AP 10511Close/MedGuided anti-shipNeeds a sensor lock; beaten by countermeasures.
Proton torpedo6d×6 burn · AP 20611Med/LongHeavy anti-capitalSlow and devastating — the trench-run shot.
Cluster missile[3d×3] ×4441Close/MedWarhead swarmLocks up to four times; swamps point-defence.
EMP missile— (no hull dmg)511Close/MedArea systems-killDisables shields, drones, mines and systems in the burst (HT or down 1d turns).
Ion missile6d×2 ion511Close/MedAnti-shield + drainStrips shields, slows engines, saps weapon power.
Seismic charge / mine6d×6 burn ex · AP 10311KnifeHuge-radius bomb / mineDropped or laid — catches a whole flight.
Thermite torpedo6d×4 burn · AP 80611MedArmour-melterBurns through plating — beats heavy hull DR.
Sabotage probe—411KnifeAnti-system sabotageLatches on and degrades targeting and sensors.
— Railguns (gunship, long range) —
Slug railgun6d×6 imp · AP 20811LongSniper hull-killerLong charge time; brutal single shots.
Ion railgun6d×3 ion · AP 5811LongShield-stripperDrops a target's shields in one shot, drains power.
Plasma railgun6d×4 burn ex · AP 10711Med/LongBurning area shotSplashes across clustered targets.
— Exotic & illegal —
Neuralyzer array SW— (no hull damage)———CloseCrew-knockout beamBypasses shields entirely. 3-turn sustained lock, then the crew drops — see The Neuralyzer below. Slaver / pirate tech; LC Rare, brazenly illegal.

Reading the table. AP ignores that many points of hull DR (damage is otherwise 1:1 at ship scale — no dividing). Acc is the fire-control bonus: Aim for one turn and add it to your next shot. RoF is shots per turn — RoF 5+ adds the standard rate-of-fire bonus to hit; Rcl is recoil, and every full multiple of Rcl in your margin of success lands an extra hit (a quad turret saturates; a railgun does not). Range is the band table — Knife ±0 · Close −4 · Medium −6 · Long −8 · Extreme −12 — and a turn of Aim erases the band. Ion weapons use the Ion Gunner skill (Gunner defaults at −2, and vice versa). Launchers carry limited warheads — counts are per ship in the catalogue, and a "missile rack" is a 4-shot launcher. The character sheet tracks all of this per mount.

The Neuralyzer SW

Training the beam. The gunner must hold an unbroken lock for 3 consecutive turns: one Gunnery roll per turn (target SM and range mods apply; missing a roll, losing line of sight, or the gunner taking any other action resets the charge). At the end of the third successful turn the array fires automatically — the training was the attack.

Effect. A psychotronic pulse washes through the hull — shields do not stop it and it leaves the vessel undamaged. Every living crew member in the affected volume rolls HT−4: failure = unconscious 1d×10 minutes; success = mentally stunned (IQ roll each turn to recover). A knocked-out bridge means an adrift, intact prize.

Area — why capitals shrug it off. The pulse floods a volume about the size of a light freighter. Vs SM ≤ +6 targets (fighters through freighters): the entire crew. Vs SM +8/+9 (corvettes, frigates): one section — bridge, engineering, or a battery deck (gunner's choice, called-shot penalty −2). Vs SM +10+ capitals: a single compartment; the ship fights on.

Limits & counters. Droids are immune — a droid co-pilot or astromech can fly the ship home. Military neural shielding (rare, expensive) gives +4 to resist. The capacitor takes 1 hour to recharge. No effect through a planet's atmosphere or heavy bulkhead plating (interior compartments beyond the first are safe on capitals). LC Rare; possession is a capital crime in Republic space — this is slaver tech.

Campaign note: the party's Ebon Hawk currently mounts one. The Republic does not know this. Cost if you could buy one: ~250,000 cr, black market only.

Ship Countermeasures, Systems & Utility

The other half of a space fight — staying alive, blinding the enemy, and running them down. Most of these are active systems with charges or cooldowns; a ship can mount only as many as its hull and power plant allow.

SystemEffectNotes
— Countermeasures & EM warfare —
Countermeasure launcher (chaff & flares)Breaks an incoming missile lock — the warhead must re-acquire; +4 to evade it this turnLimited charges (~4 a fight). The standard “flare.”
EMP field projector (EMP disruptor)EM pulse: ship systems, drones and mines within ~5 hexes go down (HT or 1d turns); shields drop, targeting fouls (−4 to hit), and nearby allies gain missile-lock immunity for a turnCooldown between pulses. The bomber’s signature — murders drone and mine swarms.
Sensor jammer / ECM suiteEnemies take −2 to −4 to hit you and to hold a missile lockActive; running it lights you up on passive scans.
Sensor decoy / holo-decoyThrows false contacts — incoming missiles and sensor locks may chase the decoy insteadOne use per decoy.
Targeting beaconMarks a target: allies hit it at +4 and it cannot use countermeasures for several turnsPaint it; let the squadron finish it.
— Stealth & sensors —
Sensor dampening / stealth field−4 to −8 to enemy detection and lock-on; a full stygium cloak hides you outright, but you cannot fire while cloakedCloaks are rare, power-hungry and prized.
Advanced sensor packageGreater detection range; sees through jamming and spots dampened or cloaked hulls soonerChiss ships mount the finest (see Faction Doctrine).
— Defence & survivability —
Distortion / deflector field+active defence (evasion / deflection) for the turnThe scout’s dodge; drinks power.
Shield booster / overchargeSurge shield DR (about +50%) for 1d turnsCooldown — one good burst per fight.
Directional shieldsDouble shield DR on a chosen facing, strip the otherPilot or shield-officer action.
Damage-control / repair droneRepairs ~1d hull, or one disabled system, per turn in flightSupport and bomber standard.
— Mobility & control —
Tractor beam projectorOpposed roll to seize a target — held, it cannot manoeuvre or jumpThe pirate’s snare and the patrol’s arrest.
Interdiction / gravity-well projectorBlocks hyperspace jumps and yanks ships out of lightspeed within its fieldSee Interdictors & Gravity Wells.
Engine booster / afterburnerA turn’s burst of Move and evasion — break contact or close the gapBurns reaction fuel.
Intrusion / slicer suiteSlice a docked or tractored ship: unlock, disable, or seize its systemsPairs with boarding (Doc VI).

Standard Loadouts by Ship Class

The default fit for a generic ship of each class — enough to drop a ready-made hull straight into a fight, or to kit out a buildable one. Named ships in the catalogue carry their own listed armament; this is the baseline everything else flies with.

ClassPrimary WeaponsOrdnance / SpecialsCountermeasures & UtilityTypical Role
Starfighter / Interceptor2× laser cannons4–6 concussion missilesCountermeasure launcher (flares); deflector shields; targeting computerLine dogfighter — fast, balanced, Class 1 hyperdrive.
Scout / Recon2× light or rapid-fire laser cannonsSensor probeAdvanced sensor package; sensor dampening; distortion field; engine boosterEyes of the fleet — finds, shadows, slips away.
Bomber / Heavy Fighter2× heavy laser cannonsProton torpedoes or seismic chargesRepair drone or EMP field; deflector shields; countermeasuresCapital-killer — saturates hulls with ordnance.
Gunship1× railgun + 2× laser cannonsIon cannonSensor jammer; deflector shields; distortion fieldLong-range sniper and shield-stripper.
Shuttle / Transport1–2× light laser turret—Countermeasure launcher; deflector shieldsFerry — lightly armed, not built to fight.
Light Freighter1–2× quad laser turret (often concealed)Concussion missile rack (optional)Countermeasure launcher; shield booster; sometimes sensor dampeningThe do-everything hull — punches above its weight when modded.
Bulk Freighter / Hauler2–4× laser turrets—Point-defence lasers; heavy deflector shieldsCargo — guns ward off raiders, not win fights. Slow.
Corvette / Patrol Ship4–6× laser cannons + 2× ion cannonConcussion missile racksPoint-defence lasers; tractor beam; deflector shieldsFast warship — escort, patrol, boarding.
Frigate / CruiserTurbolaser banks + ion cannonsTorpedo launchersPoint-defence batteries; sensor suite; deflector shields; sometimes interdictionLine warship — anchors a battle group.
Capital / DreadnoughtHeavy turbolaser batteries + heavy ion cannonsTorpedo / missile banksHeavy point-defence; fighter & bomber wing; tractor beams; deflector shieldsFlagship — brings its own air wing and breaks fleets.
CarrierPoint-defence batteries + light guns—Multiple fighter & bomber wings; deflector shieldsMobile airbase — fights through its wings.

Swapping in faction kit. Lay the faction’s signature gear over the baseline (see Faction Fleet Doctrine): charric guns and superior sensors for the Chiss; beskar plating, grapples and a Basilisk for the Mandalorians; heavier Imperial turbolasers for the Sith; ion cannons, tractors and hidden guns for the Hutts and pirates; strong shields and standard parts for the Republic.

Mounts, Hardpoints & Warhead Flight

A hull does not carry guns at whim. Every weapon sits in a hardpoint of a given size, and a frame has only so many of each. Pick a fit the hull can physically mount and the power plant can feed. Four mount sizes, smallest to largest:

MountCarriesWhere they sit
S
Small		Light laser cannon · point-defence laser · rapid-fire / burst laserFighter chin guns, turret stubs, PD blisters — nearly every hull has a few.
M
Medium		Laser cannon · quad laser · ion cannon · concussion / cluster / ion / EMP missile launcher · sabotage probeThe workhorse gun-and-launcher hardpoint — fighters and up.
L
Large		Heavy laser cannon · proton & thermite torpedo · seismic charge · every railgun · plasma launcherBombers, gunships, corvettes and bigger — heavy ordnance and sniper guns.
C
Capital		Turbolaser (light & heavy) · heavy ion cannon · capital missile / torpedo bank · charric batteryFrigate and up only — the line-of-battle weapons.

Larger holds smaller. A mount carries any weapon of its size or below — a Large hardpoint takes a heavy laser, a medium ion cannon, or a small PD stub. A Small mount can never carry a torpedo. Down-fitting wastes tonnage but is legal; up-fitting is not.

Hardpoint budget by class. What a hull of each class can physically mount. Named catalogue ships fly their own listed armament, usually below this ceiling; these numbers are the build limit and the cap for a refit. Utility systems (countermeasures, sensors, tractors) spend module slots, not hardpoints — see the slot table above.

ClassSMLC
Interceptor / starfighter21——
Heavy fighter / bomber221—
Gunship222—
Shuttle / transport21——
Light freighter22——
Bulk freighter / hauler42——
Corvette / patrol442—
Frigate6641
Cruiser6862
Dreadnought / capital81084
Carrier6421

These echo the Standard Loadouts above: a corvette’s “4–6 laser cannons + 2 ion” sits inside 4 S / 4 M / 2 L; a capital’s turbolaser batteries fill its 4 C. A hull rarely flies fully racked — power, crew and credits bite first.

Warhead Flight — guided ordnance takes time

Beams are instant; warheads fly. A laser, ion cannon, turbolaser or railgun bolt crosses the range in the turn it is fired — resolve the hit at once. A guided warhead (missile or torpedo) is a vehicle that has to get there: it launches this turn and arrives a number of turns later, set by how fast it flies and how far it must travel. In the gap it is live on the board — the target keeps its Dodge each turn, point-defence may shoot it down, and countermeasures may break its lock.

OrdnanceSpeedKnife / CloseMediumLongExtreme
Missiles — concussion, cluster, ion, EMPFastthis turn+1+2+2
Torpedoes — proton, thermiteSlow+1+1+2+3
Mines & seismic chargesPlacedLaid or dropped — detonate on proximity, no flight

In flight. Roll the gunner’s attack when the warhead launches (lock and range at launch); it then counts down. Each turn it is in the air a point-defence mount may roll Gunnery to kill it (success destroys it) and the pilot may keep flying evasive. On the impact turn it strikes — apply its damage to the target’s Hull HP (or shields) then. A torpedo loosed at Long range is three turns of jeopardy for the bomber that fired it: this is the trench-run tension, and why escorts and a point-defence screen earn their keep. The companion sheet prints the impact turn on the firing result; the GM console tracks live warheads on its turn clock.

Buying, Owning, Flying

Used Beats New. A working crew almost never buys new. Used and refit hulls run 30–60% of list, often with a story attached — previous owner, war damage, missing logs. The Variation System (Trade & Economy) applies: a Corellian Run dealer sells at near-list; a Hutt-Space lot will sell you the same hull cheaper, with questions you don’t ask answered.

Financing & the IBC. SW The InterGalactic Banking Clan finances most working hulls in the Republic, at 10–15% per year over five to ten years. The Banking Clan owns far more freighters than it appears, on paper at least, and an IBC reposession is one of the era’s great motivating events.

Berth, Crew & the Monthly Nut. Berth, maintenance, crew, resupply, and fuel (Doc VI, Fuel chapter): a light freighter clears 6,000–9,000 cr a month before fuel, plus RF and HF per run. Every captain in the galaxy is chasing the next cargo for the same reason.

Registry & LC. Civilian hulls are normally Lic; military hulls are Mil (and a civilian owning one invites Republic or Sith attention either way). The Czerka and Mandalorian heavy hauls are Res outside their factions. The clearest sign of a hot ship is a transponder code that doesn’t match its paint.

Interdictors & Gravity Wells

Interdiction Field. SW Interdictor cruisers project artificial gravity wells that prevent hyperspace transit. A ship attempting to jump within an interdiction field cannot make the jump (Astrogation auto-fails). Ships traveling through interdicted space drop out of hyperspace.

Detecting Interdiction. A sensor operator rolls Electronics Operation (Sensors) at +2 to detect an interdiction field at long range. Successful detection lets the captain reroute around it. Interdictors are expensive to deploy and rare; usually attached to military fleets pursuing fugitive ships.

Countermeasures. The only way out of an interdiction field is to destroy or disable the interdictor cruiser, or to fly out of its range. Some special hyperdrives can briefly counter weak interdiction fields (rare and military-only).

Navicomputers & Astromechs

Navicomputer. The shipboard computer that stores hyperspace routes and assists the astrogator. Most ships have a basic navicomputer with a few major lanes pre-mapped. Better navicomputers store thousands of routes and have updated stellar position data; these give +1 to +3 bonuses to Astrogation rolls on familiar routes.

Astromech Droids. SW Astromechs (R-series, etc.) supplement navicomputer functions and may calculate jumps on smaller ships that lack a full navicomputer. Astromech provides Astrogation skill at its rated level (typically 13-15) and can run calculations in parallel with the pilot. A small starfighter often has only an astromech for jumps (no full navicomputer).

Memory Routes. Some non-droid races (rare) have natural ability to navigate hyperspace without computer assistance. In the campaign era these are very rare. More commonly, master smugglers memorize specific routes for efficiency without consulting the computer.

Part VI

Boarding
Actions

Catch · breach · combat aboard · defense · the Mandalorian doctrine

A coordinated boarding action can take a ship vastly larger than the boarders’ own. A handful of Mandalorian commandos in a corvette can capture a frigate; a frigate’s crew can capture a cruiser. The technique requires getting close (Stage 1), breaching the hull (Stage 2), winning the corridor combat (Stage 3), and resolving the action — capture, sabotage, or extraction (Stage 4). Each stage has its own mechanics. This is one of the defining combat patterns of the campaign era.

Stage 0 — Setting the Table (component states)

Boarding runs on the component system. The ship you soften is the ship you storm — every ◐/✕ you inflicted in the space fight pays out aboard:

Target stateEffect on the boarding
Shields ✕Required. No breach, tractor lock or airlock clamp through a live shield matrix.
Sublight ✕Target adrift — skip the Stage 1 pursuit contest entirely; approach is automatic.
Sensors ◐ / ✕They don't see you coming: boarders gain surprise at the breach (defenders act last, no defences first turn).
Reactor ✕Dark ship: emergency lighting only (−3 vision penalties), artificial gravity out in 1d sections (DX/Acrobatics per turn), defenders' turrets dead.
Weapon mounts ✕No point-defence fire on the breaching shuttle during approach.
Neuralyzed crew SWThe pulse (see Doc VII, The Neuralyzer) leaves the crew unconscious — the boarding is a walk-through. Check compartments: droids are immune and still on duty.

Stage 1 — Catching the Target

Maintaining Pursuit. Pilot vs Pilot Quick Contest each turn. The pursuer wants to close range; the target wants to escape. Each turn the loser of the contest takes a position penalty next turn (−2 to whatever they try). When pursuer comes within tractor/grapple range (1-5 km), they may attempt a lock.

Tractor Beam Lock. Specialised capital and large fighter craft carry tractor beams — focused gravitic emitters that grab smaller ships. Tractor Beam Operator skill (Electronics Operation or similar) vs target’s Pilot to escape. Once locked, target cannot move under own power; locked ship moves with the tractor’s vessel until tractor disengaged.

Boarding via Airlock. Ships in close proximity may align airlocks for orderly transfer. Pilot rolls from both ships to align safely. Common for inspection, customs, civilian docking. Combatively used when target is disabled or surrendered.

Forced Docking. Aggressive variant. Pursuer rams gently and clamps; target Pilot rolls to evade or to crash-out. Failed evasion = forced dock; pursuer’s airlock now connects to target’s hull. Slow, ugly, often damaging to both ships.

Stage 2 — Breaching the Hull

Lightsaber Cut. SW A lightsaber can cut through any hull plate given time. Mechanics for slow cut: the saber wielder takes a Concentrate maneuver each turn to maintain the cut. Each turn, the wielder cuts through DR equal to Lightsaber skill ÷ 10 (round up) — roughly: 4-6 turns to cut a person-sized hole through fighter-class hull (DR 4-12), ~10 turns for a freighter (DR 10-16), 1-3 minutes for a capital hull section (DR 150-250). Some Force-users can spend FP to accelerate (Doc IV).

Plasma Cutter. Specialised tool. Cuts through DR 20 of hull in about 1 minute (freighters and below; double time per size class above). Brings its own attention (visual signature, heat); the boarding party is exposed during the cut. Common boarding-team equipment.

Breach Charge. Explosive entry. Sets in 30 seconds, detonates with significant noise and flash. Cuts a person-sized hole in anything up to capital plating (DR 250). Loud — alerts the defenders. Fast — boarders can enter immediately. Best for surprise/shock entries.

Docking Ring. Used when both ships consent (or one is disabled). Pneumatic seal extends; airlocks open. Cleanest entry, no hull damage. Doesn’t work against an actively resisting target unless the target’s tractor or airlock systems are damaged into accepting the connection.

Cutting Through Bulkheads. Once inside, boarders may need to cut through internal bulkheads, doors, blast doors. Lightsabers slice through almost everything (slow); plasma cutters work; breach charges do too. Heavy blast doors (capital ship sections, restricted areas) take longer.

Stage 3 — Combat Aboard

Corridor Combat. Interior ship combat is fought in narrow corridors and confined spaces. Movement is restricted. Cover is abundant but limited to one or two characters per cover-spot. Most engagements happen at 5-10 hex range (close to point-blank).

Cover Density. Bulkheads, equipment racks, doorframes provide cover. DR 8-15 for typical corridor cover; DR 30+ for major bulkheads. Most personal weapons are stopped or significantly reduced by ship interior cover. Cover degrades as fire concentrates on a single spot.

Ship's Resistance. How hard the crew fights back depends on who they are — green crews (haulers, civvies): Tactics 10, surrender when the first section falls; trained crews (military, corporate security): Tactics 12-14, fall back by sections, seal bulkheads; elite crews (Mandalorian, Sith, commandos): Tactics 16+, counter-board, vent compartments, fight for every metre. Roll the boarders' Tactics vs the crew's at each section: losers give ground. Movement Penalties. Tight corridors halve movement (Move/2). Larger spaces (cargo bay, bridge) allow normal movement. Multiple combatants in a corridor: each combatant after the first imposes a −1 to skill rolls due to obstruction (friendly fire risk, line of sight blocking).

Gravity & Atmosphere. SW A ship’s artificial gravity may fail under combat damage. If gravity goes, combatants must Acrobatics or DX each turn to maintain position. Atmospheric loss (hull breach without seal) forces vacuum-resistance: sealed armour negates; unprotected combatants suffer per Doc III Part VIII. Boarding parties typically wear sealed armour for this reason.

Defenders’ Advantage. The defending crew knows the ship’s layout, knows where cover is, may have prepared positions. Defenders typically get +1 to combat rolls when fighting in their own ship. Surprise attacks negate this (defenders confused).

Stage 4 — Resolution

Capture the Ship. Goal: take control of the bridge / engineering / weapons. Boarders fight through to these critical locations. Once captured, the ship’s crew either surrenders or fights to the last. The boarders impose their authority through skill checks (Tactics vs Tactics for control, plus weapon presence).

Sabotage / Destroy. Goal: cripple the ship from within. Plant explosives at reactor, hyperdrive, or shield generator. Mechanics rolls to plant correctly; Stealth rolls to remain undiscovered while planting. Successful sabotage = ship critical damage; boarding party then extracts.

Extraction Mission. Goal: rescue a specific prisoner or recover specific cargo. Boarders fight to the target location, locate the target, then exfiltrate. Time-critical — alarms may have been raised, reinforcements en route.

Stage 5 — The Prize. The whole point of ion cannon, neuralyzers and boarding torches: a captured hull is worth credits. Prize value = catalogue price × (intact systems ÷ total systems) — every ✕ you put into her on the way in comes off the cheque. A fence or Hutt broker pays about a third of that, no questions; a legal prize registry (Republic letter of marque, planetary courts) pays about 60% minus fees, with very many questions. Repairing her to flyable first (see the Repair Ladder, Part VIII) can multiply the payout — a freighter with a jury-rigged reactor flies herself to market. Crews are not cargo: ransom officers, release spacers, and remember the Republic hangs slavers. Crew Surrender. Often the cleanest outcome. Once boarders have secured the bridge or threatened the captain, the crew may surrender to avoid destruction. Officer or Leadership rolls to demand and enforce surrender. Surrendered crews are usually taken as prisoners or marooned.

Defending Against Boarders

Repel Boarders Maneuver. Ship’s marines, security teams, and crew members repel boarders. Each defender rolls their combat skill. Boarders rolling theirs. Mass-combat rolls or individual combats per the GM’s preference.

Vacuum Tactics. Defenders may deliberately vent compartments to space — forcing boarders out and into vacuum. Sealed armour negates; unsealed boarders suffer per environmental damage. Risk: defenders’ own crew in those compartments suffer too. Sometimes used as a desperate measure.

Counter-Boarding. Defending forces may include their own boarding party — counter-boarders who exit the ship to attack the attacker’s boarding craft. Difficult; requires a specially trained team. Best with vacuum-rated combat troops and rapid-deployment vehicles.

Scuttling. Last resort. Defending captain orders the ship destroyed rather than captured. Mechanics roll to overcome safety interlocks; the ship reactor goes critical, all aboard (defenders, boarders) face evacuation. Used historically in this era against high-value Sith Empire prizes.

Mandalorian Boarding Doctrine SW

The Mandalorian Style. The Mandalorian Wars introduced refined boarding tactics: high-speed gunship approach, simultaneous breach at multiple hull points, coordinated assault from multiple directions. Mandalorians excel at three-stage boarding — overwhelm the boarding zone, fight to the bridge, demand surrender or destroy.

Equipment. Mandalorian boarders typically carry: full sealed armour (vacuum-rated), light blaster carbines optimized for close quarters, vibroblades for melee, breach charges, micro-grenades. Mandalorians prefer aggressive close-range fights over standoff exchanges.

Speed Boarding. Mandalorian commandos prize speed. From breach to bridge in under 10 minutes; demand surrender in under 15. Lingering boarding parties get hunted down; quick parties survive to extract.

Part VII

Basilisk
War Droids

Mandalorian war machines · skull-mount interface · combat capabilities · campaign role

The Basilisk war droid is the signature Mandalorian war machine of this era. Part vehicle, part mount, part droid. A Mandalorian warrior interfaces with the droid via direct neural-skull mount — controlling the Basilisk like an extension of their own body. Massive armament, atmospheric and space flight, capable of engaging anything from infantry to light starfighters. The Mandalorian Wars saw thousands deployed; their decline marks the end of an era. In campaigns of this era, Basilisks are status symbols, family heirlooms, and the iconic weapon of the Mandalorian warrior caste.

Attribute Value Notes
ScaleVehicle (large)Operates at vehicle scale; can engage at starfighter scale when in space.
HP250-400Heavy mechanical chassis; Mandalorian-quality construction.
DR40-60Beskar-augmented plating (variant models); reflective coating.
Move30-50 hex (300-500 m/turn) atmospheric; ×3 in vacuumRepulsorlift and ion drive. Faster than most ground vehicles.
Crew1 (pilot only, skull-mount interface)Direct neural connection. No separate gunner.
WeaponsTwin blaster cannons (heavy), 2× missile racks (4 missiles total), melee claws (impaling at swing)Multi-role armament. Selectable weapon mode each turn.
SpecialSealed atmospheric / vacuum operation; integrated comms; identification beacon for Mandalorian clanMandalorian heritage. Recognises Mandalorian rider via biometric/clan signal.

The Skull-Mount Interface

Neural Connection. SW The Basilisk pilot interfaces via a skull-mounted neural connection. The rider straddles the head section of the droid; small fibres connect to neural taps in the helmet. The pilot’s senses extend into the Basilisk — they see through its sensors, feel its movements, experience the world from the droid’s perspective.

Skill Mechanics. Piloting the Basilisk uses Pilot (Basilisk) as a separate specialisation. It defaults to other pilot skills at −4. Gunnery uses Gunnery (Vehicle) as normal. The neural interface gives +1 to Pilot rolls (effectively riding the droid intuitively), but the loss-of-connection rule means pilots must maintain their armour seal.

Pilot Fatigue. The neural connection is tiring. Each minute of active combat costs 1 FP from the pilot. Mandalorians train extensively to extend this; standard endurance limit is 1-2 hours of continuous Basilisk piloting before FP exhaustion. Brief exit-and-reentry sessions reset the timer but interrupt combat.

Severance. Sudden loss of neural connection (Basilisk destroyed, helmet damaged, neural mount fault) causes feedback in the pilot: 1d FP loss, possibly 1d HP shock damage. Mandalorian armour includes neural feedback dampeners; most pilots survive a destroyed Basilisk, though they suffer briefly.

Combat Capabilities

Anti-Infantry Mode. Twin blaster cannons fire in suppression-fire mode against ground troops. The Basilisk’s main role at infantry scale is the ”walker-killer” — disrupting infantry formations, taking out heavy weapons emplacements, providing close air support. Anti-infantry fire is devastating: standard cannons rolling 6d×2 against personnel rated cover.

Anti-Vehicle Mode. Against vehicles, the Basilisk uses missile racks for armoured targets and cannons for lightly-armoured. Missiles do 6d×4 to 6d×6 damage with AD 3+. A single Basilisk can engage and destroy a light vehicle in 1-2 turns. Heavy vehicles (heavy tanks, walkers) require multiple Basilisks coordinating.

Anti-Starfighter Mode. SW The Basilisk’s speed and mid-range firepower make it effective against starfighters at close range. Standard cannons can damage starfighter shielding but not penetrate hull DR easily. Missiles can damage a light fighter significantly. Best tactic: pack of Basilisks ambushes a fighter formation at close range; single Basilisk vs single fighter is risky for the Basilisk.

Melee & Claws. Basilisks have grasping claws that can grapple infantry, vehicles, and even smaller starfighters. Grapple rolls vs target’s Pilot or DX. Once grappled, the Basilisk can use its claws as melee weapons (impaling damage 3d×2). Effective for capturing rather than destroying.

Atmospheric & Vacuum Operation

Atmospheric Flight. Basilisks fly via repulsorlift assisted by ion thrusters. Move 30-50 hex/turn at vehicle scale. Maneuverable enough to engage in close-quarters with ground vehicles; less agile than starfighters in atmosphere.

Vacuum Operation. SW Basilisks are vacuum-rated. In space they switch to full ion thrust; Move triples (90-150 hex/turn, ~9-15 km/turn). Their effective scale rises toward starfighter — a Basilisk in vacuum is a small mobile gun platform. Most space combat with Basilisks involves pack tactics (5-15 droids in coordinated assault).

Atmospheric Re-Entry. SW Basilisks can re-enter atmospheric flight from orbit. Heat-shielding on the underbelly; pilot rolls Pilot to manage entry without damage. Mandalorians often deploy Basilisks from orbital drop pods straight into atmospheric combat — the iconic Mandalorian arrival.

Campaign Role & Historical Context

Mandalorian Wars Era. SW The Mandalorian Wars (centuries before the campaign era) saw mass deployment of Basilisks. Republic forces had no consistent counter; Basilisk swarms broke conventional defensive formations. The Republic’s eventual successful counter-strategy was largely tactical — isolating Basilisks from their support, attacking the neural link, using interdictors to trap them in real-space combat.

Decline. The end of the Mandalorian Wars saw most Basilisks destroyed, captured, or scrapped. Republic forces deliberately destroyed Mandalorian factories to prevent reconstruction. By the present age, working Basilisks are rare; the technical knowledge to build them survives only with certain Mandalorian clans and a few specialist artisans.

In the Campaign Era. A working Basilisk is a status symbol — a family heirloom, a war veteran’s prized possession. A Mandalorian character riding a Basilisk announces their heritage. Stealing or recovering one is a story arc. Battles featuring multiple Basilisks are dramatic, signature engagements.

Variants. Several Basilisk variants exist: the standard Basilisk II (common), the older Basilisk I (less capable but more numerous), specialised heavy-weapon Basilisks (anti-capital), reconnaissance-variant Basilisks (lighter, faster, fewer weapons). The GM may introduce specific variant types as campaign-defining gear.

Fighting Against Basilisks

Anti-Basilisk Tactics. Effective tactics against Basilisks: massed fire from heavy weapons (multiple anti-vehicle launchers concentrate fire); ion attacks targeting the neural interface (the pilot’s most vulnerable system); aerial superiority (starfighters at close range can damage Basilisks effectively); traps (gravitic mines, weapon-emplacements in chokepoints).

Targeting the Pilot. A Basilisk’s pilot is mounted on the head section — physically exposed. A targeted attack to the skull-mount has a chance to disable the pilot rather than the droid. Penalty −5 to hit at typical engagement range; Pilot’s armour DR applies. A successful pilot-kill removes the Basilisk from combat as the droid drifts or descends.

Force-User Engagement. SW Jedi facing Basilisks have specific tactics: Force Push to disrupt flight (Doc IV), lightsaber attacks on the head section (using the slow-cut breach mechanics from Part VI), or Force-leap onto a Basilisk to attack the pilot directly. Skilled Jedi can take down a single Basilisk in 2-3 minutes; pack engagements are much harder.

Part VIII

Vehicle &
Ship Damage

DR thresholds · lightsaber slow-cut · personal-vs-vehicle exceptions · critical damage

Vehicle armour is genuinely heavy — DR 20 for a speeder, 60 for a walker, 200+ for a starfighter, 1000+ for a capital ship. Personal weapons (rolling 3-6d typical) cannot meaningfully damage these targets. The exceptions are specific: anti-vehicle launchers, heavy disruptor weapons, lightsabers (the universal slow-cut exception), and concentrated fire from large numbers of personal weapons (eventually). This part covers when personal weapons matter against vehicles and how vehicle/ship damage manifests at scale.

Weapon vs Target Outcome
Blaster pistol / rifle vs speeder bikeDamage doesn’t penetrate. Useful only for harassment / target-of-opportunity shots at exposed pilots.
Heavy blaster vs speeder bikeCan damage. 4d burn × AD 3 = effective 4d × 3 = 12d penetration vs DR 15 = ~30 damage. Will damage but not destroy.
Anti-vehicle launcher vs speeder bikeOne hit destroys.
Personal weapons vs starfighterUniversally ineffective. Even heavy weapons rarely penetrate fighter hull DR 100+.
Lightsaber vs vehicle hullSW Slow cut. See below.
Vehicle weapon vs personAuto-lethal on hit. Don’t roll exact damage.
Vehicle weapon vs coverCover destroyed; person inside may survive if cover absorbed most blast.
Starfighter weapon vs vehicleVehicle destroyed on hit.
Capital weapon vs starfighterStarfighter destroyed on hit.

Lightsaber Slow-Cut SW

The Slow-Cut Mechanic. A lightsaber can cut through any material given time. The cut proceeds at (wielder’s Lightsaber skill ÷ 2) DR-points per turn at 1-metre thickness. So a wielder with skill 18 cuts 9 DR per turn. A vehicle hull section (DR 40, 0.5m thick) takes 40/9 ÷ 2 ≈ 2-3 turns to breach. A starfighter hull (DR 150, 1m thick) takes 150/9 ≈ 17 turns ≈ 3 minutes. A capital ship hull (DR 1000, 2m thick) takes hours.

Concentrate Maneuver. The cut requires Concentrate maneuver each turn. Interrupted concentration (combat, distraction): the cut stalls. Defensive actions while cutting: cut rate halved. Some Force-users (Doc IV) can spend FP to accelerate the cut significantly.

Cortosis & Resistant Materials. SW Cortosis-weave armour shorts out lightsabers on contact (Doc III). Cortosis-rated vehicle / ship sections may completely resist lightsaber slow-cut. Rare and expensive armour, used on high-value Mandalorian war machines and certain Sith vehicles.

Anti-Vehicle Weapons

Personal Anti-Vehicle Weapons. Specialised infantry weapons designed for vehicle damage:

  • Rocket / Missile Launcher. One-shot disposable or reloadable launcher. Damage 6d×3 to 6d×6 burn or imp — AD applies at personal scale only; against vehicles and ships the damage resolves 1:1 vs net hull DR, like all cross-scale fire. Effective against most personal vehicles.
  • Heavy Disruptor. Armour-bypassing energy weapon. Damage 6d×2 imp with AD 10 and ×4 wound multiplier (Doc III Part I). Devastating but rare and illegal in most jurisdictions.
  • Plasma Lance. Heavy plasma weapon. Damage 6d×4 burn with AD 5+. Heavy and slow-firing.
  • Magnetic Mine. Placed mine that attaches to vehicle hull. Damage 6d×10 burn on detonation. Requires placement (Stealth) and timer or trigger.

Vehicle Mounted Weapons. Vehicles carry their own anti-vehicle armament: cannon-class blasters, missile racks, ion cannons. These do vehicle-scale damage and engage other vehicles at the same scale (standard combat resolution).

Critical Damage

Vehicle Critical Hits. A critical hit to a vehicle: 3d on the vehicle critical table. Common results: engine damage (Move halved or stopped), weapons damage (cannot fire), driver wound (driver takes personal damage), fuel rupture (1d-2 turns to explosion, evacuate), fire (burns for 1d turns adding cumulative damage). The GM picks or rolls based on the situation.

Catastrophic Damage. Vehicles taking damage exceeding their HP face: explosion if fuel/power systems compromised; structural collapse if HP very negative; vehicle destroyed completely. Crew survival is by GM call (ejection seats? open cabin? sealed cockpit?). Standard practice: crew Acrobatics or Pilot rolls to escape; failed roll = ejection fails, crew takes vehicle damage.

Starship Critical Damage. See Part III (starfighter critical hits) and Part IV (capital critical hits). Multiple-scale ships have multiple subsystem critical types — engines, weapons, shields, sensors, hyperdrive, life support, hull integrity. The GM rolls 3d on the appropriate table based on the ship’s scale.

Reactor Breach. SW The most dramatic capital-ship damage. Once the reactor goes critical, the ship will explode in 1-3 minutes. Crew may attempt evacuation (1 minute to reach escape pods or fighter hangar). Bridge officers face the choice: stay and try to save the ship (Mechanics rolls at penalty), flee, or stay and die. Iconic capital-ship combat finale.

Damage to Subsystems

Targeted Subsystem Strikes. Skilled gunners may target specific subsystems rather than the general hull. Treat as a Targeted Attack (Doc I), penalty depending on subsystem size: large (engine pod, hangar bay) at −2; medium (shield generator, bridge tower) at −4; small (turbolaser turret, sensor array) at −6. Successful targeted strike applies normal damage to that subsystem’s local HP (typically the ship HP / 5-10 per major system).

Sensor Lock for Precision. The gunner must have sensor lock on the specific subsystem to target it (vs the broader ship). Sensor Ops roll required first. Without lock, only general hull strikes possible.

Critical Subsystem Effects. Destroying specific subsystems: Bridge = command disruption; ship runs on autopilot or random AI behaviour. Reactor = power lost; weapons and shields offline. Shield generator = shields drop. Hyperdrive = ship stranded. Weapons battery = that battery silenced. Hangar = fighter complement destroyed (rolled to apply 1d hits to fighter wing). Engines = sublight crippled.

Part IX

Repair &
Maintenance

Mechanics specialisations · field repair · workshop · hyperdrive specialty · damage control teams

Ships and vehicles take damage. Ship repair is a domain unto itself — different skills, different timescales, different parts than droid repair (Doc III) or organic healing. A starfighter at half HP can be fixed in a hangar bay; a frigate at half HP needs a shipyard. The mechanic and engineering skills behind this are part of every spacefaring crew’s makeup.

Repair Action Skill / Facility Effect / Timescale
Damage Control (combat)Mechanics in-flightIn combat: jury-rig one ◐ Damaged system back into action (Mechanic roll, one attempt per system per scene). It runs until it takes another hit — the first hit re-breaks it. Cannot touch ✕ Destroyed systems.
Field PatchMechanics + toolsOut of combat: bring one ✕ Destroyed system up to ◐ Damaged — working, with its crippled penalty. 15–60 minutes per system with tools.
Hangar / Workshop RepairMechanics + workshopRestore systems fully to ● Intact. Hours per system for fighters; days per section for capital ships.
Hyperdrive RepairMechanics (Hyperdrive) + workshopSpecialty work. Hours to days depending on damage severity.
Shipyard / Drydock RepairMajor facility + crewsRestore destroyed sections, replace hull plating. Weeks to months.
Component ReplacementMechanics + partsSwap damaged module for new (engines, shields, hyperdrive). 1-12 hours per module.
Patch & Seal (hull breach)Mechanics + sealantRestore atmosphere to compartment. 5-30 minutes. Temporary; full repair needed.
System DiagnosticMechanics or Sensor OpsIdentify hidden damage or system fault. Hours.
Routine MaintenanceMechanics + timePrevent slow degradation. Done in port between missions. 1-3 days.

Ship Hit Locations & Called Shots SW

A starship is a conglomerate of parts, and a gunner who forgoes the easy centre-of-mass shot can aim for a specific system exactly like a GURPS hit location. Each location carries an Aim penalty to target it and its own outward DR — the armour a shot must defeat before it touches that part. The model is simple: a surface location (hull, armour) uses the full Hull DR; an exposed mount (engines, weapons, sensor dishes) is only lightly faired-in and uses about ⅖ of hull DR; an internal system (reactor, hyperdrive, bridge) sits behind the whole hull, so a shot must beat full Hull DR to reach it. While shields are up, shield SP soaks first regardless of which location is targeted — you must drop the screens before called shots bite. A crippled part applies the listed penalty immediately, and these stack as a ship is whittled down: this is the attrition curve that turns a dogfight into a duel of system-kills rather than a single lucky blow.

System (location)AimOutward DRIf crippledIf destroyed
Hull (broad facing)0Hull DRStructure cracked — Hull HP cap −25%; repairs at −4 until reframedCatastrophic breach — ship gutted
Armour plate0Hull DRDR -25% on that facingDR 0 on that facing
Sublight engines−2⅖ hull (exposed)-25% Move; -1 evasionMove 0 — dead in space, drifting
Cargo hold−2Hull DR (internal)-50% capacity; contents at riskHold gone; contents lost
Shield generator−3Shield SP soaks firstCap -50%; regen cutSP 0; shields cannot be raised
Weapon mount−3⅖ hull (exposed)-1 to hit, -25% RoFWeapon destroyed
Sensor array−4⅖ hull (exposed)-2 detect / scanBlind to sensors; -6, no auto-lock
Targeting computer−4Hull DR (internal)-1 to all attacks-4 attacks; no missile lock
Reactor / power plant−4Hull DR (internal)Fire every 2nd turn; Move -25%Reactor offline — weapons & drive dead
Hyperdrive−4Hull DR (internal)Astrogation -4; jump time doubledCannot jump to hyperspace
Bridge / crew−5Hull DR (internal)No rest recovery aboardSection vented; 1d crew lost
Navicomputer−5Hull DR (internal)Astrogation -2Astrogation -8; manual plotting only
Life support−5Hull DR (internal)1 hr air; CO₂ climbing5 min air; vac-suits required
ECM suite−5Hull DR (internal)Lose defensive jamming bonusNo jamming or decoys

Aim penalties mirror personal-scale hit locations (broad hull = 0, like the torso; small internal systems = −4 to −5, like the eyes or vitals). The companion character sheet implements this directly: every catalogue ship is built from these parts, and its Vehicles tab shows a live hit-location chart with each system’s Aim, outward DR and damage state, applying the crippling penalties to the ship’s effective stats automatically.

Craft Repair Skills — at a Glance

Which skill. Craft repair uses the Mechanic skill (defined in the Skills book), specialised by craft class — Repulsorlift, Sublight Drive, Hyperdrive, Walker / Locomotion, Power Systems, Shields, and Weapon Mounts — with specialties defaulting to each other at −4. Engineer designs and modifies; Mechanic repairs and maintains. This section covers only how that work plays out in and after combat.

Damage Control in Combat

In-Flight Repair. During combat, damage control teams aboard capital ships work to mitigate ongoing damage. Each turn, a damage control team rolls Mechanics; on success, +1 HP recovered for one critical system, or one critical fault temporarily contained (fire suppressed, hull breach sealed, conduit isolated). Multiple teams = multiple rolls per turn.

Astromechs in Combat. SW A starfighter pilot’s astromech can attempt 1 Mechanics roll per minute during combat. Success: +1 HP recovery, or one subsystem temporarily restored (e.g., shield regen restarted). Astromechs are the difference between a damaged fighter limping home and a destroyed fighter.

Triage. When ship has multiple critical faults simultaneously, the crew must prioritise. Common triage order: 1. Life support (crew dies without air) → 2. Power (everything else fails without power) → 3. Shields / hull (incoming damage) → 4. Engines (need to move) → 5. Weapons (need to fight back). The Captain or Engineer decides; rolls Tactics or Engineer for optimal prioritisation.

Field Patch vs Workshop Repair

Field Patch (Limited). Mechanics in the field — at a landing site, in a hangar bay during a brief layover. Can repair light damage: up to ~25% of vehicle HP. Mechanics roll at no penalty for routine fixes; at −2 for substantial damage. Time: 15-60 minutes per significant repair. Parts may need to be cannibalised from other vehicles or improvised.

Workshop Repair (Full). Proper workshop or hangar with full toolset. Restore all HP and most subsystems. Time scales with damage: a fighter at half HP takes ~6 hours to restore; a fighter near destruction takes 1-3 days. Capital ship sections take days to weeks. Specialised parts may need to be sourced (delayed delivery).

Shipyard / Drydock. For destroyed sections, replaced hull plates, complete rebuilds. Months-long process. Capital ship drydock takes weeks for major repairs, months for refits. The galaxy has a few major shipyards (Kuat, Corellia, Mon Calamari, etc.) where the largest ships are built and repaired.

Hyperdrive Repair

Specialty Work. Hyperdrives are intricate, fragile, and dangerous to work on. Mechanics (Hyperdrive) specialty required. Minor damage (drive can still jump at slower class): 1-4 hours of work. Significant damage (cannot jump until repaired): 6-24 hours. Catastrophic damage (drive destroyed): replacement required, which is a workshop / shipyard task.

Hyperdrive Components. Common replacement parts: hyperdrive motivator (the heart), navicomputer, drive coil, power coupling. Specialty parts are not stocked in most ports; ordering them takes 1d days or more. Black market sources exist but quality varies.

Hyperdrive Modification. Mechanics (Hyperdrive) at +3 or higher: may attempt to improve hyperdrive class by 0.5 (faster). Requires Engineer skill and significant time (days). Critical failure can permanently damage the drive. Common for smugglers and military ships seeking edge.

Parts & Sourcing

Parts Sourcing. Most workshop repairs include parts within the time estimate. Specialty parts (rare ship models, custom equipment): 1d days to source through normal channels; faster with black-market connections (and at price premium). Some Mandalorian craftspeople produce specialised parts that aren’t easily available elsewhere.

Cannibalisation. A wrecked vehicle / ship may provide parts for another. Mechanics roll to salvage useful components. Field salvage gives lower-quality parts; workshop salvage with proper tooling gives full-quality parts. Specific values in the Equipment Compendium (Doc VII).

Custom & Modified Vehicles. SW Many ships of this era are heavily modified — the famous ”freighter with thirty modifications” style. Repair of custom ships requires Mechanics rolls at penalty (the mechanic doesn’t know the modifications). The original modifier or someone trained on the specific ship gets full skill; outside mechanics get −2 to −4. Han Solo’s Falcon style ship — only the regular crew can keep it running.

Routine Maintenance & Long-Term Care

Maintenance Cycles. Vehicles and ships need routine maintenance to remain operational. Skipped maintenance accumulates as ”wear” — small reductions in performance, increased mishap rates, accumulating minor faults. Most ships need 1-3 days of port-time per month of operation for maintenance. Capital ships need scheduled drydock periods every year or two.

Wear & Failures. A ship that skips maintenance: GM may apply −1 to Pilot rolls, +1 to mishap risks, occasional malfunctions (Mechanics roll to fix on the spot). Pushed too far, the ship enters a ”decrepit” state requiring major workshop attention before operation. Catastrophic failure becomes likely.

Captains & Their Ships. A long-time captain knows their ship’s quirks better than any mechanic. May claim a +1 to Pilot rolls on familiar ship; may diagnose problems quickly; may push the ship beyond manufacturer specs without immediate failure. This is the romance of starship travel — the captain who knows every creak and groan.

Part X

The Vehicles
Playbook

Quick-reference tables · scale conversion · dogfighting · hyperspace · boarding · damage

The comprehensive lookup for Doc VI. Use during play when a specific rule or value is needed: scale interactions, dogfighting maneuvers, hyperspace mishap rolls, boarding sequence, vehicle damage thresholds. All Star Wars-tagged entries are setting-specific.

① Scale Reference

Scale 1 Hex Turn Typical HP Typical DR Combat Style
Personal1 m1 sec8-200-20Per Docs I-III. Standard person-scale.
Vehicle10 m5 sec15-251-30Vehicle-vs-vehicle with Pilot/Gunnery/Cover.
Starfighter100 m10 sec25-404-19Dogfighting with position, sensors, shields, range bands.
Capital1 km1 min280-2,40056-500+Abstracted bridge-crew rolls per station per turn.

② Cross-Scale Damage Quick Reference

Weapon vs Target Outcome
Personal weapon vs personal targetStandard combat (Docs I-III).
Personal weapon vs vehicleUsually no penetration. Heavy weapons may damage; anti-vehicle launchers reliably do.
Lightsaber vs vehicle hull SWSlow cut. (Lightsaber skill ÷ 2) DR per turn at 1m thickness. Cuts through any DR over time.
Vehicle weapon vs personAuto-lethal on hit. Don’t roll exact damage.
Vehicle vs vehicleStandard combat at vehicle scale.
Starfighter weapon vs vehicleOne hit destroys vehicle.
Starfighter vs starfighterStandard combat at starfighter scale. See dogfighting tables below.
Capital weapon vs starfighterOne hit destroys starfighter.
Capital vs capitalAbstracted bridge-crew rolls. Combat resolution over minutes.

③ Dogfighting Maneuvers Quick Reference

Maneuver Roll Effect
Tight TurnPilot −2Rotate 90° in one turn. Success: gain rear arc on pursuer.
Barrel RollPilot −1+2 to Dodge against one incoming attack. One attack defended.
LoopPilot −2End turn 180° reversed. Loses momentum (next turn Move halved).
ImmelmannPilot −3Quarter-loop with half-roll. Reverse + position above. Position advantage.
Split-SPilot −2Half-roll, half-loop. Reverse quickly without losing speed.
ScissorsPilot vs Pilot QCDefender −2. Winner gains position. Repeats until one escapes.
Head-On PassBoth Pilot + GunneryPure damage trade; no position. Either can damage the other.
Yo-YoPilot −2Gunnery +2 next turn at cost of position.

④ Hyperspace Quick Reference

Jump Type Astrogation Mod Notes
Microjump (in-system)+210s-1min. Low risk.
Standard (known route)01-10 min. Standard risk.
Long (multi-sector)−210-60 min. Moderate risk.
Galactic Transit−4Hours of calc, days of transit. High risk if unfamiliar.
Blind Jump (uncharted)−8Hours of calc. Severe risk.
Emergency Jump−6 to −10One turn. Severe risk.
— On critical failure: roll on Hyperspace Mishap table (3d, Part V) —

⑤ Boarding Action Sequence

Stage Action / Skill
1. Catch the TargetPilot vs Pilot QC each turn. Tractor beam at 1-5 km range (Electronics Op vs Pilot).
2. Breach the HullLightsaber (slow cut, see ② above), plasma cutter (~1 min, freighter hull), breach charge (30s, loud), docking ring (cleanest).
3. Combat AboardCorridor combat at close range. Cover DR 8-30. Move halved in tight corridors. Defenders +1 in own ship.
4. ResolutionCapture (Tactics for surrender), sabotage (Mechanics + Stealth), extraction (time-critical), or destroy.
— Vacuum & atmosphere mid-fight: sealed armour negates; unsealed combatants suffer per Doc III Part VIII —

⑥ Vehicle & Ship Critical Damage

3d Vehicle / Starfighter Capital Ship
3–4Engines damaged. Move halved.Bridge hit. Officers wounded/killed.
5–6Weapons damaged. One weapon disabled.Reactor damaged. Half ship systems offline.
7–8Shields damaged. One facing destroyed.Shield generator destroyed. Shields drop.
9–10Hull breach. Extra damage; atmosphere venting.Weapons battery destroyed.
11Sensors damaged. Fly blind.Engines damaged. Sublight crippled.
12–13Comms damaged. Silent.Hyperdrive damaged. Cannot jump.
14–15Life support damaged. Pilot at risk.Hangar destroyed. Fighter complement lost.
16–17Pilot wounded. Personal damage; ejection countdown.Cascading damage. Multiple systems failing.
18Catastrophic. Fuel rupture or hyperdrive breach. 1d-2 turns to explosion.Reactor breach. Ship destroyed in 1-3 minutes.

⑦ Repair Quick Reference

Action Skill / Facility Time
Damage Control (combat)Mechanic, in-flightOne roll per turn: success jury-rigs one ◐ Damaged system back into action (the next hit on it re-breaks it). ✕ Destroyed systems cannot be fixed in combat. One attempt per system per scene.
Field PatchMechanic + tools15–60 min per system: restore one ✕ Destroyed system to ◐ Damaged, or make one jury-rig permanent. Hull breaches: sealed but the hull stays ◐ until a workshop.
Workshop RepairMechanic + workshop~6 hrs per system to full ● Intact on a fighter; ~a day per system on freighters; days per section for capitals.
Hyperdrive RepairMechanics (Hyperdrive) + workshop1-24 hrs depending on damage. Specialty work.
Shipyard / DrydockMajor facility + crewsWeeks to months. Full restoration, refits.
Component ReplacementMechanics + parts1-12 hrs per module.
Astromech Combat Repair SWDroid Mechanic (the astromech rolls)1 roll/min during combat: as Damage Control — jury-rig one ◐ system. The astromech is a second pair of hands; it does not share the crew's one-attempt limit.

⑧ Bridge Crew Stations Reference

Station Primary Skill Each Turn
CaptainTactics (Naval) / LeadershipTactical positioning, +1 to +3 bonus to one station for the turn.
Executive OfficerTactics / LeadershipBacks Captain; takes over if needed.
Pilot / HelmPilot (Capital Ship)Maneuver the ship; determine next-turn position.
Tactical OfficerGunnery (Capital)Roll for each weapon battery firing this turn.
Sensor OfficerElectronics Operation (Sensors)Detection, jamming, target identification.
CommunicationsElectronics Operation (Comms)Comms, interception, broadcasts.
EngineeringEngineer (Capital) or MechanicsPower management, hyperdrive, critical repairs.
Damage ControlMechanicsEach turn damage occurred: roll to mitigate.

⑨ Basilisk War Droid Quick Reference SW

Aspect Value / Notes
ScaleVehicle (large). Operates at vehicle scale; can engage at starfighter scale in vacuum.
HP / DR250-400 HP / DR 40-60 (beskar-augmented variants higher).
Move30-50 hex atmospheric; ×3 in vacuum.
Crew1, neural skull-mount interface. Pilot (Basilisk) skill.
WeaponsTwin heavy blaster cannons; 2× missile racks (4 missiles); melee claws (3d×2 impaling).
Pilot Fatigue1 FP per minute of active combat. Mandalorians train for endurance.
Loss of ConnectionBasilisk destroyed: 1d FP / 1d HP feedback damage to pilot.
Targeting the Pilot−5 to hit (pilot mounted on head section). Pilot’s armour DR applies. Kill = Basilisk drifts.

⑩ Crew Operations Cheatsheet SW

The Non-Combat Half of the Game. Everything a working captain checks between the dogfights — fuel, jumps, monthly nut, price modifiers, legality. One page; references back to the full systems for the math.

Two Tanks. RF = reactor fuel (sublight, power, weapons). HF = hyperfuel (jumps only). 10 cr / RF, 50 cr / HF. Full refuel = RF tank × 10 + HF tank × 50.

RF Burn. Cruise — per day of travel. Combat — per hour of hard burn. A light freighter cruises 1 RF/day, fights at 2 RF/hr. A frigate cruises 50 RF/day, fights at 120 RF/hr.

HF per Jump. Ship’s Avg Jump HF × route × drive. Route: short ×0.5, standard ×1, long haul ×2, galaxy-crossing ×4. Drive: racing 0.5–0.75 ×1.5, standard 1–2 ×1, cargo 3–4 ×0.7.

Monthly Nut. Light freighter, small crew: berth + maintenance + crew salaries + resupply ≈ 6,000–9,000 cr / month before fuel. Skip maintenance and the malfunction roll comes due (Doc VI).

Docking Fees / Day. Backwater 10. Standard 50. Core 200. Private berth: 500–5,000 / month.

Crew Pay / Month. Pilot 2,000. Co-pilot/astrogator 1,800. Mechanic 1,500. Gunner 1,500 per turret.

Variation System (Trade & Economy). Listed prices = standard port, peacetime. Apply: at source ×0.5; Core manufactured ×0.8 / Core raw ×1.3; standard ×1; frontier ×1.25; backwater ×1.5; blockaded ×2–3; black market ×2–4. Stack multiplicatively.

Availability Tiers (off LC). —/Lic → common. Res → limited (major ports). Mil → scarce / locked. Illegal → contraband (Streetwise). Rare → not stocked at all.

Haggling. Merchant contest (with Reaction) shifts the price ±10–25%. Contraband needs a Streetwise roll or a contact first.

Cargo Margins (Gross). Raw ore +50–100%. Manufactured outbound to Rim +50–100%. Food +30–60%. Medicine to blockade +200–400% (+ risk). Luxury +100%. Spice: enormous & enormous risk.

Reputation (Part X — new). Each faction tracks a standing from −3 (hunted) to +3 (champion). The number modifies that faction’s Reaction roll, gates what they sell you, and decides whether they shoot first.

The full systems are in: Fuel, Range & Running Costs · Ship Catalogue · Trade & the Galactic Economy · The Variation System · Reputation & Faction Standing.

Ship Construction

Every vessel below is buildable from named, costed parts — hull, reactor, sublight, hyperdrive, shields, armour, weapons and support systems — each with a tier, a credit cost and a build time. Use it to cost a custom ship, refit a stock hull, or see why a given vessel performs as it does.

How to Build

1. Pick a Hull (ships) or Chassis (droids). This dictates size, base HP, base DR, and the slots budget — the total system capacity the vessel can house.

2. Add a Reactor (ships) or Power Cell (droids). Power output. Every powered system you add draws from this budget. Sum of power draws must not exceed reactor output.

3. Add Sublight (ships) or Mobility (droids). Sets Move.

4. Add Hyperdrive (ships, optional). Sets jump class.

5. Add Cognitive Core (droids). Sets IQ.

6. Layer remaining systems — shields, armour, weapons, sensors, targeting, cargo, crew quarters, ECM, astrogation, programming, manipulators, etc.

7. Tally cost, mass, build time, fit time. Build time = the longest single component build (parts can be made in parallel by separate teams). Fit time = sum of all install hours (one shipwright works sequentially).

An over-slot or over-power build is invalid. The Sheet's Ship/Droid Builder enforces this automatically.

Part Tiers

Tier 1 — Civilian. Mass-market, cheap, anywhere available.

Tier 2 — Military-grade. Republic Navy / Mandalorian / corporate-security standard.

Tier 3 — Premium / Specialist. Verpine engineering, Aratech high-flow, race-tuned. Restricted licenses common.

Tier 4 — Advanced. Sienar, Kuat front-line, capital-scale. Military procurement only.

Tier 5 — Masterwork. Refined beskar, Sith Inquisitor cores, prototype cloaking. Vanishingly rare — earned, inherited, or stolen.

Ship Parts Catalogue

88 parts across 13 categories. All times and costs are baseline civilian/light-vessel scale; multiply by 2× for medium, 4× for heavy, 8× for capital-scale parts where not already specified.
