DR shown as eenergy (blue) / kkinetic (red); single value = both equal. * = flexible (blunt trauma applies, Doc III). +ST = powered strength bonus. DD = damage divisor the armour imposes on the attacker (e.g. DD÷2 saber halves a lightsaber’s armour divisor). “Sealed” includes a helmet & life support; “slots” are upgrade mounts (Part XI). Apply armour divisors (Part I) before subtracting DR. See Armour Materials for what each build is made of.

Armour Materials SW

What a suit is made of decides how it behaves long before its DR number does. The same DR 30 means one thing in energy-weave and another in beskar. These are the materials named in the catalogue above, grouped by family; the properties here are what actually matters at the table.

Synthetics, Cloths & Weaves

Heavy cloth & zeyd-cloth. Woven fabric, sometimes with a thin armourweave lining (zeyd-cloth). Light, silent, concealable, always flexible (blunt trauma applies). Token protection — the Jedi and Sith wear it because it hides under robes, not because it stops much.

Fiberplast & ballistic weave. Cheap moulded synthetic fibre, the workhorse of civilian and security kit. Flexible, decent against slugs and shrapnel, mediocre against energy. The bulk of what an ordinary person can buy.

Echani fibre. A flexible armourweave woven on Eshan to an old discipline. Light for its protection and famously supple — the duelist’s cloth. Suits built from it routinely grant +1 to one active defense because they never bind a movement.

Cinnagar war-weave. The refined, premium cousin of Echani fibre, woven in the old Empress Teta system. High-end noble and officer wear: the protection of light armour with the look of fine clothing.

Verpine fibre & zal alloy. Precision-woven by Verpine hivecrafters to tolerances no human shop can match. The defining trait is weight: Verpine armour delivers plate-grade DR at a fraction of the mass, which is why it costs what it does.

Standard Plate Alloys

Plastoid & duraplast. Moulded composite plate — cheap, light, rigid. Plastoid is the militia and riot-line standard; duraplast is its denser, tougher grade. Balanced protection, no special tricks.

Durasteel. The galaxy’s structural alloy and the backbone of military plate. Balanced DR against both energy and kinetic, reliable, repairable anywhere. Most line armour is durasteel or a durasteel composite.

Quadanium steel. Denser starship-grade alloy used in breach and siege plate. Heavy, very tough, slow. When you see quadanium you are looking at a door-kicker’s or a boarding trooper’s suit.

Energy Specialists

Energy-dispersing weave (fibre / mesh / matrix). Armour that actively channels and bleeds off blaster energy. Superb energy DR, feeble kinetic DR — a wall against bolts, tissue against blades. Each grade can, once per fight while defending, throw up a burst of extra energy-DR (fibre +10, mesh +20, matrix +30) against one ranged energy attack. Useless against kinetic.

Reflec. A mirror-coat that reflects coherent light. Near blaster-immune, but offers almost nothing against slugs or blades — the bolt bounces, the knife does not. Wear it against blaster lines, never against a melee specialist.

Ablative laminate. Layered plate that sacrifices an outer skin to shed an energy hit, then degrades. Good energy protection that quietly wears down over a long fight; a field favourite because it is cheap to re-skin.

Agrinium. A rare alloy that drinks energy and radiation. Its real value is defensive breadth — it softens blasters, rad, and disruptor fire, the last of which almost nothing else resists.

Saber-Resistant Metals

Cortosis weave. Ore woven into armour that shorts out a lightsaber blade for one turn when the blade strikes the weave — the signature anti-Jedi material of the era. Heavy and costly, but the only common way for a trooper to survive contact with a saber. The blade deactivates briefly; it does not break the armour.

Phrik. A rare lightsaber-resistant alloy — lighter than cortosis and it does not short the blade; it simply refuses to be cut (treat as DD÷5 against saber cuts). The connoisseur’s saber defence: light, expensive, quietly superb.

Songsteel. A light, strong, saber-tolerant alloy prized for fine work. Not fully saber-proof, but it shrugs off glancing energy-blade contact while staying feather-light — the choice for armourweave that must move like cloth.

Mandalorian iron (beskar). The apex. Unrefined, it is “a bit better than durasteel.” Refined, by closely-guarded clan metallurgy, it resists lightsabers and disruptors alike (up to DD÷10 on covered locations) — the closest thing to un-defeatable personal armour. A beskar-clad warrior is functionally immune to small-arms fire and lightsabers. Two things still beat it. Crushing force: beskar resists cuts and bolts, not impact — a fall, a Wookiee’s club, a cannon’s concussion (Heavy Impact, Doc III) transmits straight through as blunt trauma and knockback, and crushing damage gets none of beskar’s special resistance. Coverage: beskar plates only the locations it covers, so a called shot to a chink, a seam, or an unarmoured limb (Doc III) bypasses it entirely. Beskar runs around 20,000 cr per gram; a full suit is a clan treasure, earned or inherited, almost never bought.

Exotic & Legendary

Dallorian alloy. A near-mythical metal that slowly repairs itself — armour built from it regains lost DR over hours without a workshop. Vanishingly rare; a suit of it marks its owner as someone with very deep pockets or a very old vault.

Alchemy-forged metals (Sith / Krath). Ordinary alloys reworked by Sith alchemy or Krath ritual to carry a protective Force-ward (Doc IV) and often a fear aura. The metal is mundane; the working is not. See Talismans & Wards for what a ward actually does.

Adaptive nano-composite. Experimental armour whose surface re-tunes itself to the incoming threat — once per turn it can shift a block of DR toward the type being hit, and the best versions self-seal breaches. Prototype-grade and priced accordingly.

Ancient relic alloys. One-of-a-kind metals from forgotten foundries — Star Forge-pattern plate and the like — combining several of the properties above at once. Unique items, never catalogue stock; their stats are whatever the relic is.

How to Crack Heavy Armour SW

The principle. No suit is immune to everything — the answer is the right tool, not a bigger number. A standard blaster bouncing off battle plate is the system working as intended; it means “bring something else,” never “nothing works.” Six routes punch through any armour in the book:

1 · A bigger gun (Armour Divisor). AD divides DR before you subtract it. Carbines & vibro AD 2; heavy blasters & cannons AD 3; ion & plasma AD 5; disruptors AD 10. Against DR 40 plate a rifle (AD 1) is useless, but a heavy blaster treats it as DR 13 and a disruptor as DR 4. Every armour tier has weapons that meaningfully threaten it.

2 · The wrong damage type for that armour. Energy-weave (fibre / mesh / matrix) and reflec wall off blasters but fold to kinetic — a vibroblade or slug walks straight through. Plate is balanced; weave is a trap against melee. Read the split DR and exploit the soft number.

3 · Ion, vs anything powered. Powered armour, cyborgs, and droids take ion (AD 5) as system shock regardless of DR — servos and electronics do not care how thick the plate is.

4 · Chinks in armour (skill). A Targeted Attack at −10 finds a joint, seam, or eye-slit and treats the armour as DR 0–1 (Doc III). This is the universal answer for a skilled shooter or duelist — and the only reliable personal-weapon route through beskar.

5 · A critical hit (luck). A 6 on the critical-hit table ignores DR entirely (Doc III). Even a hold-out blaster can, rarely, find the gap — so no one is ever truly untouchable.

6 · Raw impact (concussion). Cannon-class weapons carry Heavy Impact (Doc III): even when DR stops the bolt, the wearer is knocked back and takes concussion through the plate. You do not have to pierce armour to put its wearer flat on the ground — this is how heavy weapons threaten even beskar.

GM note. When the party faces heavy-armoured foes (or fields a tank of their own), make sure at least one weapon on each side can answer — an AD-3+ gun, a disruptor, a vibroblade, or an ion weapon. Tune the encounter so the fight is “bring the right tool,” never a stalemate.

Reading Armour Through the Scaling Lens SW

Worked Example — Battle Armour. Battle armour is DR 18/18. A 5d blaster rifle (avg 17) at AD 1 → full DR 18 → stopped cold. Battle armour shrugs off a standard rifle bolt. A vibrosword sw+2 (say 3d, avg 10) at AD 2 → effective DR 9 → ~1 through. Plate resists both moderately. Now mesh armour (28/6*): the same blaster at AD 2 → effective DR 14 → bounces; the same vibrosword at AD 2 → effective DR 3 → ~7 through plus blunt trauma. The blade beats the energy-armour the blaster can’t.

Worked Example — Reflec. Reflec armour 40/0*. A 6d heavy blaster (avg 21) at AD 3 → effective DR 13 → ~8 through; reduced but real. A 5d slug rifle (avg 17) at AD 1 → effective DR 0 → 17 straight through plus blunt trauma. Reflec makes you nearly blaster-proof and utterly knife-and-slug-vulnerable. Wear it against blaster lines; never against a melee specialist.

The Once-Per-Fight Reaction. SW Fiber, mesh, and matrix armour can, once per fight while defending, throw up a burst of extra energy-DR (10/20/30) against a single ranged energy attack — representing the weave actively channelling a bolt. Declare before damage. Useless against kinetic.

Powered Armour & Life Support

Servo Strength. Powered armour adds a ST bonus (carrying, melee, HP for limbs) and includes sealed life support — vacuum-rated, gas-proof, climate-controlled. The trade-offs: weight, cost, a power supply to maintain, and ion vulnerability (the servos are electronic — ion weapons treat powered-armour wearers partly as machines).

Sealed vs Unsealed. Sealed armour (most military, all powered, Mandalorian-with-vacuum-upgrade) negates gas, vacuum, and corrosive splash (Doc III). Unsealed leaves gaps. The Environmental upgrade (Part XI) seals any suit at +10% cost.

Oxygen Reserve & Leak-on-Breach. A sealed suit carries a finite air supply fed from a life-support unit at a specific location — for Neo-Crusader and powered armour this sits in the torso pack. Typical reserves: light powered and Neo-Crusader run 30–60 minutes; heavy powered carries up to 120. If an attack penetrates DR at the life-support location, the seal is compromised and the reserve bleeds out — treat it as venting over 1d minutes in vacuum, faster if the breach is large, and the suit loses its sealed benefits against gas and vacuum until patched. The companion character sheet tracks each sealed suit’s reserve and its source location, with a one-tap breach toggle the GM flips when a hit gets through. Unsealed armour (Republic trooper plate, ordinary battle armour) has no reserve to lose.

Part VIII

Adventuring &
Utility Gear

Medical & kolto · sensors & visors · stealth · survival · electronics · tools

Between the weapons and the armour is the gear that wins missions — the kolto that keeps you breathing, the visor that makes you an expert, the stealth field that gets you past the guard, the slicer’s spike that opens the door. This is the working operative’s kit, the difference between a fighter and a professional.

Republic field trauma kitHeals 3d+4 HP/turn for 3 turns; +2 First Aid12,700MilSW Military combat-medic issue.

The Kolto Saturation Rule HOUSE

One Dose-Tier Per Day. SW Kolto and bacta work by saturating the body’s tissues with healing agent — and the body can only absorb so much before the chemicals stop helping and start harming. A character can benefit from kolto/bacta application once per day, on a tier ladder: a single military injector, OR two standard medpacks, OR three emergency patches (roughly equal saturation). Mixing counts proportionally — one standard medpack plus one emergency patch is fine; a military injector locks out everything else that day.

Why the Limit. Without it, a character chain-heals through any fight. The saturation cap makes each application a real decision — burn your military injector now, or save it? It also makes tank immersion (which works around the saturation limit by slow controlled absorption) the only way to heal big between fights.

Over-Saturation. SW Exceeding the daily limit: each application past the cap does nothing beneficial and forces a HT roll; failure means kolto poisoning (−1 HP/hour until the body clears it, ~1d hours). Field medics learn the limit the hard way. A regenerative implant (Part IX) raises the cap by one tier.

Healing-Over-Time. The patch/medpack/injector heal across 3 turns from application — pull a wounded ally behind cover, slap on the injector, and they recover 3d+6 over the next three turns (avg ~31 HP total at the military tier). This rewards extraction and protection over standing in the open. The healing ticks even if the medic moves on.

Storage & Carry Gear SW

Carrying it all. SW A bag is worn gear with a capacity (how much weight it holds) and a load-relief rating. Because a good pack rides the load close to the body — or, at the top end, floats it on a repulsor field — the weight of whatever is inside the bag counts for less when working out Encumbrance: relief runs from a token 5% on a cheap satchel up to 25% on a repulsor cargo sled, the best load relief there is. The full weight is still really there (a thief who cuts the strap gets all of it); relief only eases how the carried load drags on your Move and Dodge. On the companion sheet, equip a bag, then assign Inventory items into it; the sheet does the maths and shows the saving. Capacity is a soft cap — overfilling spills the relief, not the contents.

Bags that break. SW Rigid cases and reinforced harnesses have their own DR, HP, and where relevant a Damage Divisor (÷) just like armour (Doc III) — a durasteel cargo case shrugs off a stray bolt that would have shredded a cloth duffel, and a beskar strongbox is effectively a portable vault. When a container drops to 0 HP it bursts and spills (and, for the repulsor sled, an ion hit simply drops it out of the air). Soft bags have no DR and tear on a determined cut — the GM adjudicates.

ItemHoldsReliefWtCostLCDR/HPNotes
Utility satchel14 kg5%1180——SW Ubiquitous spacer's shoulder bag.
Courier sling bag11 kg5%0.8270——SW Swings round for a one-handed grab.
Bandolier rig7 kg8%0.6360——SW Cells, grenades & small kit across the chest.
Spacer's duffel27 kg8%2330——SW Hauls a whole kit between berths.

Part IX

Cybernetics,
Implants & Surgery

Neural implants · prosthetics · sensory & organ replacements · the surgery rules · the Force cost

In the decades of war between the Republic and the Sith Empire, cybernetics became commonplace — life-sustaining implants, augmented eyes, replacement limbs, neural enhancers. Two broad kinds exist: replacements (restore lost function, little strain) and enhancements (exceed natural function, real strain). They range from a slot-in chip you install yourself to full-body prosthesis requiring a surgical suite and weeks of recovery. All of them carry costs: ion vulnerability, a tax on the Force, and — for the heavily augmented — the slow question of how much of you is still you.

The Surgery System SW

Three Installation Classes. Every cybernetic falls into one of three installation tiers, which determine what’s needed to fit it:

Class 1 — Slot Chips (self-install). Subdermal data-chips and socket modules. Plug into an existing cybernetic socket or a standard data-port. Anyone can swap them in moments — no surgery, no skill roll. The catch: you need the socket first, which is itself a Class 3 install. Once you have a neural socket, chips are trivial.

Class 2 — Minor Surgery. Subdermal implants, simple sensory units, basic prosthetic attachments to existing mounts. Requires a Physician or Surgery roll, a medical kit or better, and 1-4 hours. Recovery 1-3 days. Failure risks infection or rejection (HT roll). A field medic can manage these.

Class 3 — Major Surgery. Neural implants (brain-integrated), full limb prosthetics, organ replacements, neural sockets. Requires Surgery skill at +0 or better, a proper surgical suite (or a surgical droid), and the Cybernetic Surgery technique. 4-24 hours. Recovery 1-4 weeks. Failure can cripple, damage the brain, or kill (Doc III). This is hospital or specialist-clinic work.

Field Surgery Penalties. SW Installing a Class 3 cybernetic outside a proper suite — in a ship’s medbay, a cantina back room, a battlefield — is at −4 to −8 to Surgery, with worse failure consequences. A portable surgical kit (Part VIII) reduces the penalty. Surgical droids (the dedicated droid book) often are the suite — a good medical droid installs cybernetics better than most living surgeons.

Brain Modification & Neural Sockets SW

The Neural Socket. Brain-integrated implants (the ”neural” category) require the brain to be prepared with a neural interface socket — a Class 3 install that wires the cortex for cybernetic input. Once socketed, the character can swap neural chips (Class 1) freely. Without a socket, each neural implant is a separate Class 3 surgery. Heavy cyborgs invest in a socket early so they can hot-swap capabilities.

One Active Neural Implant. Even with a socket, the brain integrates only one active neural implant at a time — the neural pathways can’t cleanly run two competing augmentations. Swapping the active chip takes a few minutes (socket) or surgery (no socket). Non-neural cybernetics (limbs, organs, sensory) don’t count against this limit; you can have many.

Implant Training. SW Without the Implant Training perk, a neural implant interferes with normal brain function: −2 to resist mental effects (Will) and a worsened shock response (one step worse on knockdown/stun rolls, Doc III). Trained implant-users suffer neither. Mandalorians, Sith special forces, and dedicated cyborgs train for it.

Neural Implants (Class 3 install; Class 1 chip-swap if socketed)

ImplantEffectCostLCNotes
Adrenal regulatorActivate +2 ST/HT burst 1/fight; FP after18,000ResSW Controlled adrenaline dump.
Bio-stabiliser implantImmunity to poison5,300LicSynthesises antidotes.

Neural recorderPerfect sensory recall/playback7,500ResSW Record everything seen/heard.

Skill chip (slot)Temporary skill at fixed level3,000+LicSW Class 1. Plug-in expertise.

Republic SpecForce combat suite+1 Dodge, squad-link, +1 Guns (integrated)54,000MilSW Military neural package; service-locked.

Prosthetic Limbs (Class 3)

ProstheticEffectCostLCNotes

Masterwork prostheticIndistinguishable from organic; minimal Force cost75,000+ResSW Arkanian/Czerka top-tier. Reduced Force penalty.
Military prosthetic arm+1 ST (that arm); DR 4; integrated tool24,000MilSW Enhanced; built-in tools.
Military prosthetic leg+1 Move; DR 4; jump boost24,000MilSW Enhanced mobility.
Weaponised prosthetic armConcealed integral weapon (pistol/blade)30,000IllegalSW Hidden armament.
Civilian work prostheticRestores function; DR 1; ST/DX as natural4,500—Cheap clinic-grade limb.
Mandalorian war prosthetic+2 ST (that arm); DR 6; integral weapon mount54,000MilSW Clan-forged combat limb.

The Costs of Augmentation SW

The Force Tax. SW Every cybernetic dims a Force-sensitive’s connection, cumulatively by cybernetic mass (per Doc IV): a single limb or one minor implant, no penalty; 25%+ cybernetic = −1 to Force Sense and all Force-skill rolls; 50%+ = −2; near-total = −3, and the machine begins to crowd out the self. Masterwork implants count half toward the threshold; full-body prosthesis and heavy muscle-weave count double. This is why Jedi prefer Force-healing or masterwork limbs, and why a fallen Jedi rebuilt as a cyborg is a tragedy — each part is a step further from the Force.

Ion Vulnerability. SW A cyborg is partly machine, so ion and disruptor weapons (and ion grenades, Part V) bite. An ion hit to a cybernetic limb forces a HT roll or that limb seizes / goes limp (per Doc III ion-vs-droid, scaled to the part). A 50%+ cyborg or full-body prosthesis is treated substantially as a droid by ion — the ion carbine built to kill war droids drops them too.

Rejection, Dependency & Maintenance. SW Without a biofeedback regulator (the foundation implant), heavy augmentation risks rejection: recurring HT rolls, failure meaning pain, infection, or a failing implant. The body also adapts — strip, ion-fry, or capture a cyborg’s augments and they suffer withdrawal (temporary loss of the bonus plus a penalty until re-fitted). Cybernetics are machinery: a damaged part is repaired with Mechanic or Cybernetics & Bioelectronics, not medicine, and neglected implants degrade.

Slicing & the Quieter Cost. SW Any networked neural implant or skill-chip can be sliced — an enemy with Computer Hacking and access may feed false sensory data or lock a limb (restraining-bolt logic in a control chip is every cyborg’s nightmare). Air-gapped implants are safe but cannot share or update data. And the galaxy’s consistent warning holds: the further you go, the less of you remains — emotional flattening, the reported “cold,” and at the extreme the machine crowding out the person. A roleplaying cost, sometimes mechanical at the GM’s call.

Building & Fitting. SW A cybernetic is a complex device on the Maker’s Chain (Skills, Part I·C and the Working-Hours Reference). Design it with Engineer (Cybernetics); build it with Cybernetics & Bioelectronics — circuitry by Electronics Repair (Medical), frame and servos by Machinist, exotic materials (phrik bone-lattice, masterwork limbs) refined by Metallurgy. Install it by surgery per the three classes above: Class 3 work is hours of work-time and real recovery (Part VII), and a critical failure injures the patient. Arkanian and Czerka clinics do the cleanest work (low Force tax, reliable integration); back-alley cyberdocs do it cheap, fast, and at higher rejection and malfunction risk.

Where to Get Work Done. SW Class 1 chips: anywhere with a socket. Class 2: any clinic, field medic, or medbay. Class 3: a proper surgical suite — Republic military hospitals, Sith medical facilities, Czerka cybernetics clinics, Arkanian Microtechnologies (the galaxy’s premier cyberneticists), or a top-grade surgical droid. Black-market cyberdocs install anything for the right price, at higher risk. Arkania and Czerka facilities produce the best replacements; back-alley work is cheap, ugly, and prone to failure.

Part X

Materials &
Item-vs-Item Rules

Beskar (two tiers) · cortosis · phrik · songsteel · energy shields · sonic-vs-saber · mixed damage

This is where the legendary materials get their exact mechanics. How does beskar actually stop a lightsaber that divides DR by 10? What’s the difference between raw Mandalorian iron and refined beskar? How does an energy shield interact with a sonic bolt? Every material and interaction rule lives here, expressed in the armour-divisor and split-DR language of Part I.

Beskar — The Two Tiers SW

Why Two Tiers. Beskar lore is precise: the raw ore is ”useless without the metallurgical knowledge only the Mandalorians possessed,” yet refined and properly forged it ”can repel lightsaber strikes.” The campaign splits these into two distinct materials with very different rules and rarity — exactly as the source describes.

Tier 1 — Unrefined Mandalorian Iron. SW Raw beskar ore, worked like any metal. Relatively common; the Mandalorians use it widely for everyday gear, tools, and standard armour plate. Mechanically it’s simply a sturdy metal — a bit better than durasteel: roughly +2 DR over the equivalent steel item, no special properties. A lightsaber cuts it like any normal armour (full AD 10). This is what most ”Mandalorian iron” gear actually is. It must be properly worked to be worth anything — raw unforged ore is just rock.

Tier 2 — Refined Beskar. SW Full-density beskar (the ”two percent ciridium, no fancy lamination” top-grade), forged with closely-guarded Mandalorian metallurgy. This is the legendary stuff — and it is among the most valuable substances in the galaxy, a clan treasure, never mass-produced. Refined beskar plate is DR 55-60 and carries the saber-resistance rule below. Beskar is essentially priceless — clan-held, rarely sold at any price, and never to outsiders. When it changes hands at all it does so for a fortune or a blood-debt, so even a single small plate (a face-guard, a vambrace) runs about a million credits and protects only the one location it covers; a full suit is worth many millions. The realistic way most characters encounter beskar is a piece at a time: one looted face-plate, one inherited vambrace. Acquiring a full suit is an entire campaign arc, not a purchase.

How Beskar Stops a Lightsaber SW

The Mechanic — Damage Divisor ÷10. Refined beskar carries a Damage Divisor of ÷10 against all damage types. Resolve a hit normally — apply the attacker’s armour divisor (a lightsaber is 8d+8 with AD 10) to beskar’s DR, subtract the DR, and then divide whatever penetrates by 10. Against refined beskar’s DR 60 a saber’s AD 10 gives effective DR 6, so an average 8d+8 (~36) leaks ~30 past the plate — but the ÷10 then drops that to ~3 damage, and a poor roll is reduced to nothing. The blade skitters off the beskar. This is the simple, readable rule the companion sheet uses, and it models the canonical ”repels lightsaber strikes” without any special-case AD juggling.

It Cuts Everything Down. SW Because the divisor applies to all damage types, beskar is fearsome against more than sabers. A blaster bolt, a slug, a frag grenade — whatever gets past the DR is still divided by ten. A grenade dealing 30 past the plate becomes 3. This is why a full beskar suit is a clan treasure worth millions: against personal weapons it is very nearly impervious.

The Slow-Cut Exception. SW Per Doc VI, a lightsaber can slowly cut through anything given uninterrupted time. Against beskar this is dramatically slower — the Freedon Nadd’s-tomb scenario, ”struck repeatedly over several hours in the exact same location.” Treat beskar slow-cut as (Lightsaber skill ÷ 2) ÷ 10 DR per turn — effectively hours for a breach. Not a combat option; a heist or escape mechanic.

Beskar vs Everything Else. SW Refined beskar’s DR 55-60 (both energy and kinetic) also shrugs off blaster fire (the lore ”withstand a direct blaster shot”), resists most explosives, and even tolerates some acids and extreme heat. Against personal weapons it is, practically, impervious — the ”just that tanky” case from Part I. Only disruptors (AD 10, and rare/illegal), vehicle weapons, sustained slow-cutting, or a committed saber thrust threaten it. This is the price of the material’s extreme rarity: the few who wear true beskar are nearly untouchable, and there is very little of it.

Beskar Weapons. SW A beskar blade resists other lightsabers and can parry a saber without being cut — making beskar-forged weapons (rare, and outlawed among some Mandalorian sects) able to stand against Jedi and Sith blade-to-blade. The crushgaunt (Part II) uses micronized beskar for this reason.

Cortosis SW

Resists & Shorts. Cortosis weave makes a lightsaber treat the item at AD 1 (full DR applies) — (cortosis genuinely shorts the blade) — unlike beskar, which instead divides penetrating damage by 10; cortosis items have ordinary DR otherwise (no beskar-level toughness). Raw cortosis ore goes further: contact with a lightsaber blade shorts it out, deactivating the saber for 1d turns (Doc III). A cortosis-armed warrior can disable a Jedi’s weapon entirely — which is exactly why every Force-user dreads it.

The Rarest Material in the Galaxy. SW Make no mistake: cortosis is the single rarest and most difficult material to mine in the entire galaxy — scarcer even than beskar. Even in this lightsaber-haunted era, when defending against Force-users is a constant concern, cortosis-treated gear is a genuine prize — a cortosis-weave vibroblade is the mark of a wealthy, well-connected, or very lucky warrior, not standard issue. The Echani are noted for inlaying cortosis weave into their finest blades; such weapons are heirlooms. Treat any cortosis item as Very Rare — rarer than beskar, found or commissioned, never stocked. +cost is steep and the GM should gate availability tightly.

Phrik & Songsteel SW

Phrik — Obscure & Nearly Indestructible. SW Phrik is a lightweight alloy that resists lightsabers (AD 1) without the short-out, and is so durable it is considered nearly indestructible — it survives blows that would shatter beskar’s brittleness, and famously endures even severe energy discharge. But phrik is widely unknown: its existence and properties are obscure even to most weaponsmiths, and the knowledge of working it is closely held. Used in the finest electrostaffs and shockstaffs and in a handful of legendary hilts. Treat as Rare and require special sourcing; a character who even knows phrik exists is unusual.

Songsteel. Rare, light, strong, saber-resistant (AD 1, no short-out). Prized for melee weapon hilts and polearm shafts (force pikes) so they aren’t sheared through. Lightweight where beskar is heavy — the duelist’s saber-resistant material, and itself uncommon enough to mark a fine weapon.

Sith Alchemical. SW Blades forged through Sith alchemy resist lightsabers (AD 1) AND channel the dark side — a dark-side wielder can use one to Block/Deflect bolts as if it were a lightsaber, and may spend Force points to add Dark Side Score to damage. The ancient Sith answer to the Jedi blade (Part XI template). The process is lost to all but the deepest Sith lore; such weapons are relics.

The Material vs Lightsaber Quick Table

Materialvs Saber Slashvs Saber ThrustOther PropertiesRarity & Value
Normal armour/steelAD 10 (cut through)AD 10 (cut through)—Common; cheap
Unrefined Mandalorian ironAD 10 (cut through)AD 10+2 DR over steel; sturdyCommon among clans; modest
SongsteelAD 1 (resists)AD 1Light; weapon shafts; no short-outRare; marks a fine weapon
Sith alchemicalAD 1 (resists)AD 1Dark-side; can Deflect boltsRare; lost art, relics only
PhrikAD 1 (resists)AD 1Nearly indestructible; light; no short-outVery rare & obscure; few even know it exists
Cortosis weaveAD 1 (resists)AD 1 (resists)Raw ore shorts saber 1d turnsThe single rarest in the galaxy — rarer even than beskar; a near-mythical prize
Refined beskarDD ÷10 (repels)DD ÷10 (still divides)DR 55-60; near-impervious to allVery rare; essentially priceless — clan-held, not sold to outsiders; a suit, millions

Personal Energy Shields SW

A Heavy, Whole-Body Screen. A personal shield projects a screen around the entire body and reduces incoming damage by its rating — its Damage Reduction (DR). Because a shield’s DR faces no armour divisor (a blaster’s AD does not chew through it the way it does worn plate), shields are rated much higher than armour: a minimum of 50 DR, climbing past 150 on the finest models. A shield’s DR stacks on top of any worn armour for the hit.

Energy, Kinetic, or Both. Every shield is tuned to a damage family, and it only stops that family: Energy shields stop burn, blaster bolts, ion, sonic and the burn portion of a lightsaber; Kinetic deflectors stop slugs, needles, shrapnel, vibroblades, fists and falls; a few premium dual-phase units stop both, though they cost a fortune and (at the cheaper end) spread their DR thinner across the two phases. A bolt that hits an energy screen is stopped cold; a slug passes straight through it — and vice-versa. Always know which family a shield covers before you trust it.

Charges & Overload. A shield holds a handful of charges (typically ~5). Raised as a quick action, it provides its DR against its damage family for the rest of the encounter. A single hit big enough to blow past its rating, or running the charges dry, overloads it — the emitter shuts down for the remainder of that fight (overload lockout). It then recharges before it can be raised again: a military unit cycles on a fresh power cell between engagements; a cheap civilian screen needs hours, up to a day. This is the great limiter — a fearsome wall of DR, but one you can lose at the worst moment and not get back mid-fight. The companion character sheet tracks each shield’s charges and its raised/overloaded state with a one-tap toggle.

Shields vs Lightsabers. SW A saber is imp+burn. The shield reduces the burn portion by its DR (energy shields only); the imp portion and the AD 10 mean the blade still cuts. Shields are designed against blaster bolts, not blades — a shielded non-Mandalorian is still cut down by a Jedi.

Named Shields. SW The era’s notable shields are tabulated separately below (after the sonic and mixed-damage rules) — from the basic generator up through the broad-spectrum Verpine prototype and the unique Yusanis’ dueling shield.

Sonic vs Lightsaber SW

Bends Around the Blade. Sonic weapons cannot be stopped by a lightsaber’s Deflect (Doc II) — the sound wraps past the blade. The trade-off is the (0.5) divisor: sonic faces doubled physical DR, so heavy armour resists it. Against a lightly-armoured Force-user, sonic is the great equaliser. The sonic disruptor additionally atomises and lowers the Major Wound threshold.

Mixed Damage Types SW

The Rule. Weapons dealing two types at once (lightsaber imp+burn, bowcaster burn+pi+, shockstaff cr+burn) let any defense keyed to either type apply to the full damage, and the target suffers whichever is worse. A negate-energy Force power negates a lightsaber’s full damage; energy-weave armour’s reaction works against a bowcaster (it’s partly energy). Resolve type-by-type: the energy shield reduces the burn portion of a bowcaster bolt but not the pi+ portion.

Named Energy Shields of the Era SW

ShieldDRDefends vsCostLCNotes
— Civilian & Personal —

Arakyd field shield (civilian)50Energy3,600LicMass-market personal energy screen. The common entry point.
Arakyd kinetic deflector (civilian)50Kinetic4,500LicRepels solid shot and blades. Rarer than energy screens.

— Military Issue —

Republic officer’s deflector (command)90Energy36,000MilHeavier officer screen; squad telemetry uplink.

Sith assault shield (line)100Energy27,000MilSith military issue. Heavy energy coverage.

— Elite, Exotic & Unique —

Mandalorian war shield (masterwork)90Energy + Kinetic48,000MilThe clans’ masterwork. Full energy and kinetic protection.
Sith sorcerer’s ward-shield (alchemical)95Energy + Force-DR42,000RareAlchemy-bound; also reduces Force-power damage (Doc IV).
Krath sorcerous barrier (ritual)100Energy + Force-DR66,000RareAncient Krath working; ward against blasters and the Force alike.
Arkanian dual prototype (experimental)100Energy + Kinetic96,000RareArkanian total-coverage screen; runs hot, short charge life.
Verpine slug-screen (masterwork)110Kinetic33,000ResVerpine engineering — the finest kinetic barrier made.
Verpine aegis prototype (experimental)120Energy + Kinetic84,000ResExperimental Verpine total-coverage screen. Vanishingly rare.
Yusanis’ war-shield (unique)150Energy + Kinetic—RareA named Echani relic. Found, never bought. Total protection.

Grouped by tier. Each stops only its listed damage family (energy / kinetic / both); ~5 charges, once-per-encounter with overload lockout, then recharge (see Charges & Overload). The kinetic and dual-phase units are the rare, costly exceptions — most screens are energy-only, which is why slugs and blades never went away. A shield’s DR stacks on top of worn armour for the same hit, and faces no armour divisor.

The Master Interaction Matrix

Attack ↓ / Defense →Energy-WeavePlateReflecUnrefined Mando IronRefined BeskarEnergy Shield
Blaster (burn, AD 2)Bounces (DR 18-28)Fair (½ DR)Bounces (DR 40)FairNothing−SR
Slug/needle (pi, AD 1)Cuts deep (DR 4-6)FairCuts deep (DR 0)FairNothingIgnores
Vibroblade (cut, AD 2)Cuts deepFairCuts deepFairNothingIgnores
Sonic (son, AD ½)FairPoor (×2 DR)FairPoorNothing−SR; bypasses Deflect
Ion (AD 5)n/a fleshn/a fleshn/an/an/a; wrecks cyborgs−SR
Lightsaber swing (AD 5)Cuts throughCuts throughCuts throughCuts throughRepelled (AD ½)burn −SR; blade cuts
Lightsaber thrust (AD 10)Cuts throughCuts throughCuts throughCuts throughRepelled — chink onlyburn −SR; impales
Disruptor (cor, AD 10)Cuts throughCuts throughCuts throughCuts throughPoor (rare!)−SR

Why blasters bounce off energy-weave: energy-dispersing armour (fibre / mesh / matrix / reflec) halves the armour divisor of energy attacks (Tool 1c), so a heavy blaster’s AD 2–3 collapses toward AD 1 and meets the weave’s full energy DR — which is why even divisor-carrying blasters are walled by the right weave, while kinetic slugs and blades (unaffected) walk straight through. −SR = reduced by a raised energy shield’s rating (Part X).

Part XI

Modifications &
Gear Templates

Weapon & armour accessories · manufacture templates · material treatments · stacking

No two operatives carry identical gear. Accessories bolt on for off-the-shelf improvement; material treatments add cortosis or beskar; manufacture templates capture the unique craft of the galaxy’s cultures. Templated items are Rare by nature — found, earned, or commissioned, never stocked. For the full slot-based component catalogue — kyber crystals, hilt configurations, optics, barrels, packs, armour plating and the rest — see Weapon & Armour Components; the simple accessories below remain valid as loose, slotless add-ons.

AccessoryEffectCostApplies To
Armour reinforcement+2 DR vs one chosen type9,000Armour
Bayonet / vibro-edge mountAdds melee attack (thr imp)900Rifles
Beam splitterBetter power-blast trade3,600Energy ranged
Climate controlExtreme-temp immunity4,500Sealed armour
Cortosis edge (rare)Weapon resists/parries sabers; raw ore shorts saber 1d turns (Part X)Rare/foundMelee weapons
Custom grip+1 to skill (that user)1,200Any weapon
Durasteel bondingWeapon +5 DR; +1 die on crit6,000Slashing melee
Energy-shield integrationMount shield generator in armour+shield costPowered/Mando armour
Environmental seal upgradeSeals suit; vacuum/gas-rated (Doc III)+10%Armour
Extended barrel+1 Acc, +50% range; +1 Bulk2,400Blasters/slugthrowers
Hair triggerReroll Rapid-Fire damage 1/fight13,000Trigger weapons
Improved power cell×2 shots per pack12,000Energy weapons
Integrated weapon mountWrist blaster/flamer/dartsweapon +1,000Powered/Mando armour
Jetpack mountIntegrate jetpack into armour12,000Powered/Mando armour
Mesh underlay+5 vs stun/fatigue threshold7,500Armour
Overcharger+1 die damage; burns packs ×2; overheat risk11,000Blasters
Servo-assist upgrade+1 ST24,000Powered armour
SuppressorQuiet (slug); −Hearing to detect1,800Slugthrowers
Targeting scope+1 to +2 Acc (aimed)500-2,000Ranged weapons

Material Treatments SW

Cortosis Weave / Phrik (Rare). Lightsaber-resistant (Part X). Stacks with one other template. Not a buyable upgrade — cortosis is the rarest material in the galaxy and phrik is obscure; treat any such treatment as a found or commissioned rarity, gated tightly by the GM, never an off-the-shelf option.

Beskar Plating (rare, ≫cost). Refined-beskar treatment — saber-repelling, near-impervious (Part X). Not a purchase; a commission or heirloom. Unrefined Mandalorian iron treatment is cheap (+2 DR) and common.

Songsteel Shaft (+30%). Light saber-resistant treatment for polearms and staff weapons.

Manufacture Templates — What Each One Does SW

A manufacture template represents the distinctive craft of a culture or corporation, applied to an otherwise standard item. Every templated item is Rare — found, earned, or commissioned, never bought off a shelf — and (with three exceptions) an item carries only one template. The tables below spell out exactly what each does and what it costs you.

General TemplateBenefitDrawbackApplies to
ArkanianCold-optimised. Weapons add a fire-damage rider; armour doubles its protection against cold/environmental hazards.Weapons overheat and can’t fire next turn if rapid-fired; armour gives no protection vs heat.Armour or energy ranged weapons
CinnagaranArmour/shields gain DR 5 vs sonic; weapons add a bludgeoning rider.Armour’s Max Dexterity is reduced by 1 (clumsier).Armour or energy ranged weapons
EchaniArmour weighs half as much and lets you move at full speed once per fight; weapons can extend their critical range once per fight.Echani gear is fragile — half the normal hit points; it breaks if abused.Armour or slashing/piercing weapons (no lightsabers)
GenoHaradanArmour grants +2 Stealth to sneak; weapons improve poison delivery (melee) or stun attacks (ranged).Armour loses 1 point of Fortitude protection; weapons jam on a critical miss.Armour or weapons
IridonianArmour/shields give DR 2 vs crushing; weapons add +2 damage on all-out/power attacks.All Iridonian gear is illegal — openly carrying it penalises social rolls with authorities.Armour or weapons
KrathArmour/shields give DR 2 vs energy; weapons inflict follow-up damage next turn on a strong hit.Dark-side taint — the wielder counts as one step more corrupt for effects that target the dark side.Armour or slashing/piercing weapons (no lightsabers)
MandalorianBuilt for customisation: +5 to Mechanics rolls to modify or repair the item, and it accepts upgrades readily.If the item is ever disabled, all its modifications are destroyed and must be re-bought, not merely repaired.Armour or weapons
MassassiArmour can turn a critical hit into a normal hit once per fight; weapons triple the ST damage bonus on a critical.Requires high ST to use without penalty (the gear is built for brutes); below it, penalties apply.Armour or simple melee weapons
VerpineArmour/shields give DR 10 vs ion; weapons can switch to ion-damage mode as a quick action.Built for Verpine physiology — needs high DX (armour) or IQ (weapons) or the user takes penalties.Armour or energy ranged weapons
PrototypeCutting-edge: accepts two tech modifications instead of one.Unstable — weapons misfire and disable on a low roll; prototype armour takes an extra die from critical hits.Armour or weapons (stacks)
Weapon TemplateBenefitDrawback
BaragwinMaster arms-dealer craft: +1 die of damage on a critical hit.Illegal everywhere.
BothanEspionage craft: +1 die of damage when set to stun or dealing ion.−1 die of damage when used lethally (not stun/ion). Only for stun/ion weapons.
DashadePain-craft: targets it wounds struggle to recover (can spend fewer recovery actions).Illegal; −2 to damage rolls (it’s about agony, not killing).
GandImmobiliser craft: stun hits halve the target’s Move; a deep stun immobilises entirely.Treated as an exotic weapon for proficiency — non-Gand users need special training. Stun weapons only.
Quick-DrawDraw or holster as a free action (with the Fast-Draw technique).None significant. Stacks with one other template.
RakatanAncient Infinite-Empire tech: increases the weapon’s damage die one step AND grants +1 to hit.Treated as an exotic weapon for proficiency; extremely costly and alien.
Sith AlchemicalSaber-resistant (AD 1); a dark-sider can Block/Deflect bolts with it like a lightsaber, and spend Force points to add Dark Side Score to damage.A lost art — relics only. Using its dark power deepens corruption. Melee weapons only.
Armour TemplateBenefitDrawback
Bonadan-AlloyDR 2 vs slashing.−1 Fortitude protection.
Bronzium CastOnce per fight, redirect a hit’s damage into the armour itself (the armour has its own HP/DR).If the armour is destroyed it stops protecting; heavy.
Durasteel CastDR 1 against all damage — cheap, reliable hardening.−1 Fortitude protection.
Environmental BastionSealed life support — vacuum- and hostile-atmosphere-rated, hours of air.Adds cost & weight; the seal can be breached by penetrating damage.
EriadunDR 5 vs stun — anti-capture armour favoured by those who fear interrogation.−1 Fortitude protection.

Stacking Rule. One template per item, with three exceptions that may stack with one other template: Prototype, Quick-Draw, and the cortosis/phrik material treatment. A ”Massassi Eriadun” suit is incoherent and not allowed. When an item legitimately carries two, apply costs most-expensive-first. All templated gear is Rare regardless.

The Codex

Detailed Item
Descriptions

Every weapon, every armour, every device — what it is, how it works, how it feels to use

The tables tell you the numbers; the Codex tells you the story. Every item in this compendium is described here in full — what it is, how it functions, who carries it, and exactly how its rules play at the table. Organised in the same order as the catalogue parts. Where an item’s full mechanics were summarised on a stat line, here they are spelled out.

Codex II

Melee Weapons
— in Detail

Knives · vibroblades · staves · swords · exotics · lightsabers

Knives & Light Blades

Vibroknife. SW A short blade whose edge vibrates thousands of times per second, letting it shear through material that would blunt an ordinary knife — hence the AD 2. The Sith agent’s signature backup: small enough to conceal in a sleeve or boot, deadly in a grapple. As a kinetic weapon it ignores energy shields entirely, making it a favoured ”get-inside-the-shield” tool. Runs on a tiny power cell good for many hours; the edge dulls to ordinary-knife performance (lose the AD) if the cell dies. Thrown at ST×1.5 metres by a trained user.

Electroknife. SW A vibroknife with a shock-emitter in the spine, adding 1d burning on a hit and offering a stun setting for non-lethal takedowns. The shock can short minor electronics and makes it especially nasty against lightly-armoured foes. Slightly heavier than a plain vibroknife due to the capacitor.

Combat Knife. A plain steel fighting knife — no power cell, no vibro-edge, just a reliable blade that never runs out of charge. AD 1. Ubiquitous across the galaxy as a tool and last-ditch weapon. Cheap, silent, untraceable.

Shiv / Dirk. An improvised or concealed stabbing blade — a sharpened scrap, a hidden dirk. Minimal damage, but it fits where no real weapon would, and a blade to the vitals is a blade to the vitals. The prisoner’s and the assassin’s fallback.

Vibroblades & Vibroswords

Vibroblade. SW The standard one-handed vibro-weapon of the era — a sword-length blade with an ultrasonic generator in the hilt. The vibrating edge cuts armour that would stop a normal sword (AD 2), and being kinetic it passes straight through energy shields and bites deep into energy-weave armour’s feeble kinetic DR. This is the answer to a blaster-proofed, shield-wearing enemy: close the distance and cut. Needs a power cell (cheap, ~20 hours); without it, performance drops to a plain sword.

Vibrosword. SW A heavier, higher-quality vibroblade — the signature melee weapon of warriors and Force-users who don’t carry a lightsaber. More damage than the basic vibroblade and better balanced. In current service it’s the weapon of choice for those trained in the vibrosword forms; many are cortosis-edged (Part XI) so they can parry a lightsaber without being sheared.

Techblade. SW A powered one-handed melee weapon with integrated tools and a sturdy vibro-edge — favoured by techs, scouts, and companions who need a blade that doubles as field equipment. Slightly lighter than a vibrosword, with built-in utility (cutting torch, sensor spike) in better models.

Dire Vibroblade. SW A two-handed great-vibroblade. Wielded in both hands it gets the doubled-ST damage bonus, making it brutal in strong hands. Heavy and unsubtle — a frontline breacher’s weapon, not a duelist’s.

Double Vibroblade. SW Two curved vibro-blades on a single twisted hilt — a double weapon allowing attacks from both ends (at the two-weapon penalty, Doc I). Difficult to master; devastating once mastered. Needs two power cells. The flashy choice of the show-off and the genuine expert alike.

Double-Bladed Vibrosword. SW A saberstaff-pattern vibro-weapon — a blade at each end of a long hilt. Warriors of this era who can’t wield the Force use these to ape the double-bladed lightsaber’s reach and flurry. Double weapon, two-handed.

War Blade (Sith). SW A heavy Sith-pattern blade, often Sith-alchemically treated (Part XI) so it resists lightsabers and channels the dark side. The weapon of the Sith warrior who scorns a saber, or who wants a brutal off-hand companion to one. Military-controlled.

Staves & Polearms

Techstaff. SW A powered quarterstaff — a double weapon striking with both ends, with a defensive +2 to Parry thanks to its length and balance. Deals crushing damage (good against the unarmoured, resisted by plate). The disciplined martial-artist’s tool; non-lethal-capable in trained hands.

Electrostaff. SW A phrik-shafted combat staff wreathed in disruptive energy — the phrik means a lightsaber does not shear through it (Part X), so an electrostaff can stand against a Jedi or Sith blade-to-blade. Strikes for crushing plus 1d burn from both ends, with a stun setting. The signature anti-Force-user melee weapon for elite guards. Two power cells.

Shockstaff. SW The Old Republic precursor to the electrostaff — phrik-laced, saber-resistant, shock-tipped. Functionally similar: a double weapon that survives lightsaber contact and shocks on a hit. Restricted, expensive, prized by those who expect to fight Force-users.

Force Pike. SW A vibro-tipped polearm with reach 2-3 — strike from outside a swordsman’s range, with a stun option for taking prisoners. Songsteel-shafted variants resist lightsabers. The ceremonial-guard and prison-warden weapon, also a serious battlefield reach-weapon.

Vibro-Pike (thrown). SW A mid-arm-sized, balanced vibro-pike built to be hurled as well as wielded. Thrown, it deals the wielder’s thrust +6 impaling at AD 4 — a heavy armour-defeating javelin — out to ST×1.5 metres. In melee it serves as a reach 1-2 vibro-spear. The Mandalorian skirmisher’s opener: throw it into the charge, then draw a blade. A trained thrower (Spear Thrower, or the Throwing skill) lands it reliably; a master adds +2/die.

Stun Baton. SW A security tool — a club that delivers a stunning shock (HT−3 affliction) on contact alongside light crushing. Non-lethal by design, the standard sidearm of guards, police, and anyone who needs subjects alive and intact.

Conventional & Archaic Swords

Short Sword. A simple one-handed blade, good for paired-weapon fighting and parrying. No vibro-edge (AD 1), but reliable and cheap. The militia’s and the traditionalist’s weapon.

War Sword. A military sidearm blade, slightly longer and harder-hitting than a short sword. The backup steel of soldiers across the galaxy who can’t afford or don’t trust a vibroblade.

Dire Sword. A refined two-handed longsword, crafted with tradition in mind — the duelist’s and classicist’s weapon. Modern metallurgy makes it superb steel, but it carries no vibro-edge.

Double-Bladed Sword. A two-bladed staff-sword (no vibro, no power) — a double weapon for those who fight in the two-ended style without the cost or charge-dependence of a vibro version.

Training Saber. SW A low-power practice lightsaber that bruises rather than cuts — crushing, stun-only damage. Jedi and Sith initiates learn the forms with these before they’re trusted with a live blade. Harmless enough for sparring, real enough to teach respect.

Exotic & Cultural

Shyarn. SW A Cerean blade with a magnetically-charged edge that ”wants” to find the enemy’s weapon and armour — mechanically, no penalty for the Rapid Strike technique (Doc I), letting a trained wielder strike twice with unusual ease. Rare off Cerea.

Fira. SW A Selkath curved sword forged from cortosis-bearing alloy salvaged from kolto harvesters and tempered in undersea volcanic vents. The cortosis content resists lightsabers (AD 1 against the saber). On a strong hit (beating both the target’s defense and HT), the grievous wound bleeds for half-damage again next turn. Sacred to the Order of Shasa.

Arg’garok. SW A massive Gamorrean war-axe, balanced so only the strongest can wield it — ST 15+ or suffer −5 to hit. In the right hands it delivers tremendous crushing-edged force. Gamorreans treat it as a normal weapon; everyone else fights it as an exotic.

Zhaboka. SW A Trandoshan double-polearm — a vibro-edged staff striking from both ends. Favoured by Trandoshan hunters, who prize the reach and the flurry it allows.

Gaderffii (Gaffi Stick). SW The Tusken Raider war-club — a crude but effective bludgeon-and-spike, frequently coated in poison or filth so wounds fester. Cheap, ugly, and surprisingly lethal in a swarm.

Bardiche / Vibro-Axe. SW A heavy two-handed vibro-bladed axe — a Mandalorian favourite for breaching and brutal melee. The vibro-edge (AD 2) plus the mass behind a two-handed swing makes it an armour-opener.

Crushgaunt. SW Gauntlets woven with micronized beskar — they let the wearer crush armour and bone in a grip, and resist lightsabers like solid beskar (Part X). So effective and so feared that they are outlawed in most of the galaxy. A grappling weapon: deal crushing on a successful grapple-and-crush.

Lightsabers & Variants

Standard Lightsaber. SW A blade of pure plasma contained in a force-field, drawing on a focusing crystal. Deals 8d+8 impaling-and-burning at armour divisor 10 — it cuts through nearly any armour (Part X), cauterising as it goes (no bleeding, but cripple and sever are permanent, Doc III). It can be used to Deflect blaster bolts (Doc II). Requires Trained by a Master to wield at all; the untrained are a danger mostly to themselves. Built, not bought.

Shoto / Short Saber. SW A shorter-bladed lightsaber, often paired with a standard saber in the Jar’Kai two-blade style. Lighter, faster, better in confined spaces; less reach and slightly less damage.

Double-Bladed Lightsaber. SW The saberstaff — a blade at each end of a long hilt, requiring two crystals. A double weapon (attacks from both ends at the two-weapon penalty). Iconic of Sith Assassins and Jedi Shadows; lethal to a master, suicidal to a fumbler.

Lightfoil. SW An elegant, slender dueling lightsaber crafted by the Mecrosa Order for the nobles of the Tapani sector. Light and superbly balanced; may be treated as a Small weapon when advantageous. One-handed only. The weapon of the duelist who values finesse over force.

Lightwhip. SW A flexible plasma lash — reach 1-4, hard to parry and hard to use (−2 to Parry with it). A rare and exotic Sith weapon for those who want to strike around guards and shields. Mastery is unforgiving.

Saberstaff (Curved Hilt). SW A single-blade lightsaber with a curved hilt optimised for the Makashi dueling form — +1 to Parry. The precise duelist’s saber, built for blade-to-blade contests rather than open battle.

Great Lightsaber. SW An oversized two-handed lightsaber with a longer, heavier blade — 9d+9. The heavy-assault choice for a strong Force-user who fights like a juggernaut. Rare and unsubtle.

Reference

Weapon Materials
& Power Sources

What blades are forged from · what beam weapons burn · the metals that defy a lightsaber

A weapon’s material decides what it can do as surely as its damage line. The same vibro-edge means little until you know whether the blade beneath it is plain steel or cortosis; a blaster is only as good as the gas it burns and the cell that feeds it. These are the substances named throughout the weapon tables above — what they are and why they matter.

Blade & Haft Materials SW

Forged steel & durasteel. The default for knives, swords, and plain blades. Cheap, reliable, sharpenable anywhere. It cuts flesh well and cheap armour adequately, but a durasteel edge shatters or is sheared the instant it meets a lightsaber.

Vibro-edge (ultrasonic). Not a metal but a mechanism: a powered edge that vibrates thousands of times a second, sawing through armour rather than chopping it. This is what gives every vibroweapon its armour divisor (2) against kinetic DR. Needs a power cell; a dead cell drops it to an ordinary blade.

Cortosis. The duelist’s answer to a Jedi. A cortosis-edged blade (the Selkath fira, cortosis war-swords) can parry a lightsaber without being cut, and contact briefly shorts the blade out. Brittle and heavy, so it makes better edges and parrying-bars than primary weapons, but against a saber it is priceless.

Phrik. A rare, light alloy that simply will not be cut by an energy blade. The shafts of military electrostaves and shockstaves are phrik — which is why a guard can cross one with a Sith and keep both halves. Lighter than cortosis and it does not short the blade; it just holds.

Songsteel. Light, strong, and saber-tolerant. Favoured for fine vibroswords and force-pike shafts where weight and balance matter as much as durability; a songsteel weapon resists a glancing saber strike without the heft of cortosis.

Mandalorian iron (beskar). The apex blade-metal as it is the apex armour-metal: a beskar edge cuts almost anything and shrugs off a lightsaber. Beskar weapons — and the outlawed micronized-beskar crushgaunts — are clan heirlooms, effectively never sold.

Alchemy-forged (Sith / Krath). Ordinary blades reworked by Sith alchemy carry a dark charge — a Sith war-blade may resist sabers, drink Force energy, or wound beyond its size. The metal is mundane; the ritual is everything (Doc IV).

Beam Energy & Power Sources SW

Tibanna gas. The energised gas a blaster excites and hurls as a bolt. Quality varies: spin-sealed Bespin tibanna fires hotter and cleaner, the basis of premium and military-grade blasters. The gas is what is actually consumed; the cell only powers the ignition.

Power cells & power packs. The rechargeable cell behind every blaster’s shot count and every vibroweapon’s edge. A weapon’s Shots is really its cell capacity; a drained cell turns a blaster into a club and a vibroblade into a sharpened bar.

Diatium power cell. The high-density cell at the heart of a lightsaber, feeding the blade indefinitely from a tiny charge. A superior diatium cell is one mark of a well-built saber.

Focusing crystals. The soul of a lightsaber — the crystal shapes and colours the blade and sets its quality. Natural Adegan and Ilum crystals serve the Jedi; synthetic crystals, grown in heat and rage, give the Sith their red blades and can be pushed to deadlier output. A saber is built around its crystal, never simply bought (components ~2,000–6,000 cr plus the crystal).

Ionised charges & stun fields. What electroweapons, stun batons, and ion guns carry instead of a kinetic edge — a charge that overloads droids and shocks organics to the floor (fatigue/affliction, Doc III) rather than cutting. Effective where a blade is not, useless where shielding is.

Quadanium & heavy alloys. The frames and barrels of repeating blasters, heavy weapons, and disruptors. Not exotic, just strong — what lets a weapon vent the heat and recoil of sustained heavy fire without warping.

Material effects are summarised here; the saber-resistance rules these metals trigger live in Part X. Costs throughout the weapon tables are retail; military, faction, and black-market pricing varies.

Codex III

Slugthrowers &
Kinetics — in Detail

Needles · flechettes · slugs · scatterguns · anti-materiel

Needle & Dart Weapons

Needler Pistol. SW Fires bursts of tiny ultra-sharp darts. The damage is small (1d+1 pi-), but it is kinetic — it punches straight through energy shields the SR can’t stop, and through energy-weave armour’s negligible kinetic DR. Against a shielded, weave-armoured target who shrugs off blasters, the needler reaches the flesh. Each dart can be laced with contact poison (tox, Doc III), the assassin’s true purpose for it. Reloads by clip (Ready); no recharge.

Needle Rifle. SW A long-arm needler — more range and a touch more damage, still kinetic and shield-defeating, still poison-capable. The sniper’s choice when the target is shielded.

Dart Pistol. SW A low-velocity dart sidearm built around poison delivery rather than impact. The dart barely wounds; the toxin does the work. Quiet, concealable, deniable.

Wrist Dart Launcher. SW A concealed forearm-mounted dart thrower — the Mandalorian and assassin’s hidden ace. Few shots, but it fires hands-free and surprises. Poison-capable.

Toxin Dartgun. SW A single-shot, near-silent poison delivery weapon. The dart itself is almost harmless (1 pi-); the toxin is the weapon. Illegal nearly everywhere; the GenoHaradan’s tool of choice.

Flechette & Shrapnel

Ripper Pistol. SW Fires irregular shrapnel that tears through energy shields (kinetic) and lodges in the wound. On a hit that breaks the target’s defenses, embedded shrapnel does follow-up damage. A nasty, brutal close-range shield-defeater.

Flechette Launcher. SW Throws a cone of razor flechettes (RoF ×9 at 0.5 divisor) — devastating against the unarmoured at close range, resisted by rigid plate. Shield-ignoring. A breaching and crowd weapon.

Scattergun. SW The Scoundrel’s close-quarters shotgun — a spreading cone of pellets, lethal up close and falling off fast. Kinetic, so it bypasses shields. The boarding-action weapon (Doc VI): one blast clears a corridor.

Heavy Scattergun. SW A bigger scattergun with more pellets and more bite. Same close-range cone profile, heavier punch, heavier kick.

Conventional Slugthrowers

Slugthrower Hold-Out. A tiny concealable projectile pistol — low damage, but it fits in a palm or boot and it ignores energy shields. The deniable backup when blasters are expected and searched for.

Slugthrower Pistol. A conventional projectile sidearm. Cheap, reliable, shield-defeating, and traceable by its brass and slugs. The poor world’s and the careful professional’s gun.

Heavy Slug Pistol. A large-calibre slug pistol — more stopping power (pi+), more recoil. Favoured where shields are common and a single solid hit must count.

Slugthrower Carbine. An automatic-capable projectile carbine (RoF 8) — lays down shield-defeating fire in volume. The kinetic answer to a shielded squad.

Slugthrower Rifle. A long-range projectile rifle, 5d piercing. Hunters, marksmen, and traditionalists who distrust energy weapons. Long max range; shield-defeating.

Slug Sniper Rifle. A precision bolt-action projectile rifle — high accuracy, heavy pi+ damage, integral scope. Kinetic, so it defeats both shields and reflec at range. The professional assassin’s rifle.

Heavy Repeating Slug. A belt-fed kinetic machine gun — sustained shield-defeating fire. Crew-served or braced; slow to reload. Area suppression where energy weapons would be shrugged off.

Slug Autopistol. A rapid-fire machine pistol throwing slugs at RoF 10 — close-range kinetic spray. Beloved of those who value volume over precision.

Projectile Shotgun. A conventional shotgun firing a spread of shot — close-range kinetic, shield-defeating, cheap. The frontier and the freighter-crew standby.

Anti-Materiel Slug Rifle. A massive long-range rifle firing armour-piercing rounds (8d at AD 2, pi++) — built to crack light vehicles, droids, and the heaviest personal armour at extreme range. Braced, heavy, and unmistakable. One of the few personal weapons that genuinely threatens battle plate from afar.

Codex IV

Blasters
— in Detail

Pistols · carbines · rifles · precision · heavy

A Note on Cores. SW Every blaster here runs on a recharging core (Part I): hold your fire for a turn and the weapon claws back +4 charges, or slam in a fresh core as a Ready maneuver. A blaster is rarely truly empty — only momentarily tapped. All are reduced by energy shields (energy damage) and resisted by energy-weave armour; their strength is against the unarmoured and the lightly-plated.

Pistols & Sidearms

Hold-Out Blaster. SW A palm-sized energy pistol, low damage, easily concealed. The gambler’s and spy’s hidden gun. Short range, but a blaster bolt to the face is a blaster bolt to the face.

Sporting Blaster Pistol. SW A civilian-grade sidearm — modest damage, easy to own, common as dirt. The shopkeeper’s drawer gun.

Blaster Pistol. SW The galactic standard sidearm — 3d burning at AD 2, reliable, rechargeable, effective against soft targets. What most people mean when they say ”blaster.” Carried by smugglers, troopers off-duty, and citizens galaxy-wide.

Heavy Blaster Pistol. SW A bigger, harder-hitting sidearm — 4d at AD 3, enough to trouble battle armour. The bounty hunter’s and heavy’s pistol. More recoil, fewer shots per core.

Charric Pistol. SW The Chiss sidearm — a phased-plasma pistol firing tight blue-white bolts. 3d burning at AD 3, out-penetrating the common blaster pistol, and built to keep firing in the extreme cold and sensor-fouling interference of the Unknown Regions where ordinary blasters misfire. Almost never sold outside Chiss space; a charric in other hands is a story in itself.

Blaster Machine Pistol. SW A compact full-auto blaster (RoF 10) — close-range suppression in a one-handed package. Drains a core fast; the trickle-recharge never catches up under sustained fire.

Bryar Pistol. SW A rugged single-shot-style heavy pistol with a punchy bolt — a classic frontier and Republic sidearm, prized for reliability over rate of fire.

Scatter Blaster. SW A blaster shotgun — splits its bolt into a short-range cone (RoF ×5). Energy damage, so shields and energy-weave resist it, but against the unarmoured at close range it’s brutal.

Carbines & Rifles

Blaster Carbine. SW A compact full-auto long-arm — the mainstay of troopers and operatives who need rifle power in a handier package. RoF 10, solid damage.

Blaster Rifle. SW The standard military long-arm — 5d burning, good range, reliable. The Republic and Sith line infantry weapon. Against battle armour (DR 18) at AD 2 it does meaningful but survivable damage; against energy-weave it struggles.

Heavy Blaster Rifle. SW A heavier-bolt rifle at AD 3 — built to punch through battle armour where the standard rifle merely scorches it. The heavy infantry and squad-support choice.

Charric Rifle. SW The Ascendancy’s standard long-arm — 5d burning at AD 3, out-punching the common blaster rifle and sharing the charric’s flawless reliability in cold and interference. Disciplined Chiss line troops favour aimed single shots over spray. Export is all but forbidden; black-market examples cost a small fortune.

Charric Carbine. SW A compact phased-plasma long-arm — 4d burning at AD 3, full-auto capable, the Chiss patrol and boarding weapon. Handier than the rifle, with the same flawless function in cold and interference. Export-restricted like all charric arms.

Heavy Charric Repeater. SW The Chiss squad-support weapon — 6d burning at AD 3 at a high rate of fire, fed from a backpack cell. Phased-plasma bolts shred armour that shrugs off common repeaters. Heavy, tightly controlled, and almost never seen outside Ascendancy hands.

Assault Blaster Rifle. SW The precision automatic rifle — accurate, full-auto, with a stun setting. Originated with the Mandalorians, perfected by Czerka. The elite professional’s rifle, equally at home dropping a target lethally or stunning one for capture.

Repeating Blaster Carbine. SW A dedicated full-auto bullet-hose (RoF 15) — devastating close, inaccurate far. Empties a core quickly. The breacher’s and the desperate-last-stand weapon. Stun-capable.

Disruptor-Resistant Carbine. SW A hardened military carbine built to keep functioning where disruptor residue and energy interference would foul a normal weapon. AD 3, robust, slightly more expensive.

Precision & Heavy

Blaster Sniper Rifle. SW A long-range precision blaster with a high-magnification scope (Acc 6+2). Single-shot, patient, deadly at range. The marksman’s energy weapon — though against a shielded or reflec target the sniper switches to a slug rifle.

Heavy Blaster Sniper. SW A braced anti-personnel/anti-light-vehicle long rifle (5d×2 at AD 3) — reaches out to 5 km and hits like a hammer. The dedicated overwatch weapon.

Assault Cannon. SW A braced heavy repeating blaster (6d×2 at AD 3, RoF 10) — the Commando’s squad weapon, laying down devastating sustained fire. Heavy, hungry for charge, crew-or-brace served.

Repeating Blaster (Emplaced). SW A tripod- or vehicle-mounted heavy blaster for area denial and defense. Huge magazine, devastating fire, immobile. The fixed-position answer to a charge.

Blaster Cannon (Light). SW The smallest vehicle-scale weapon (6d×3 at AD 5) — bridges into Doc VI. Mounted on speeders and emplacements; against personal armour it simply deletes the target. Belt-fed.

Codex V

Heavy & Exotic
Ranged — in Detail

Sonic · ion · disruptor · carbonite · flame · bowcaster · launchers

Sonic Weapons

Sonic Pistol. SW Fires a compressed bolt of pure sound that bends around a lightsaber blade — a Jedi or Sith cannot Deflect it (Part X). That is its entire reason for existing: the civilian and military answer to lightsaber-wielders. The trade-off is the (0.5) divisor — heavy armour resists it well, so it shines against the lightly-armoured Force-user, not the armoured trooper.

Heavy Sonic Pistol. SW A more powerful sonic sidearm with shorter effective range (the compressed bolt dissipates faster). More punch where it lands.

Sonic Rifle. SW A long-arm sonic weapon — rifle range, undeflectable bolts. The standoff anti-Jedi weapon.

Aurial Blaster. SW A Systech sonic sidearm with auxiliary emitters that deafen on a strong hit (−5 Perception). Awkward to wield without training, but the disorientation it inflicts is worth it to specialists.

Sonic Disruptor. SW The most violent sonic weapon — it atomises what it kills and lowers the target’s Major Wound threshold by 5, so wounds that wouldn’t normally stagger become catastrophic. Undeflectable. Fires only once every two turns (huge energy draw). Outlawed by the Republic even against the Sith. The assassin’s weapon when no body must be found.

Sonic Stunner. SW A non-lethal sonic weapon — an undeflectable stun (HT−3). The way to take a Force-user alive: they can’t Deflect it, and it doesn’t kill.

Ion Weapons

Ion Pistol. SW An anti-droid sidearm — AD 5 against machines, near-useless against flesh. The answer to an assassin droid at close range. Also affects cyborgs and powered armour (their electronics).

Ion Carbine. SW An autofire ion weapon built to mow down war droids — RoF 10, AD 5 vs mechanical. Essential kit in an era of HK-series killers and Mandalorian war machines.

Ion Rifle. SW A heavy ion long-arm — disables droids and light vehicles at range. The anti-armour-of-the-mechanical-kind specialist.

Ion Cannon (Portable). SW A man-portable ion cannon that disables vehicles and the heaviest war droids — bridges toward Doc VI. One shot can drop a combat speeder’s systems.

Disruptors

Disruptor Pistol. SW Breaks matter at the molecular level — AD 10 defeats nearly all armour, ×4 wound makes any hit catastrophic. Horrific and illegal. The answer to the un-defeatable target, at the cost of carrying contraband that marks you as a monster.

Disruptor Rifle. SW The long-arm disruptor — 6d×2 at AD 10, ×4 wound. It atomises armour and flesh alike; even beskar’s rarity is its only real defense. Universally outlawed. Wounds don’t heal — they erase.

Disruptor Carbine. SW A rapid-fire disruptor (RoF 3) — black-market only, feared and hunted. For the rare operative who needs to delete heavily-armoured targets in volume.

Specialist Energy & Projectile

Carbonite Rifle. SW Fires a glob of liquid carbonite that solidifies on impact — light damage, but the target rolls HT or is immobilised 1d turns (can act, can’t move from the hex). The capture weapon: freeze the runner, then collect. Empress Teta design. Uses carbonite cartridges.

Cryoban Projector. SW Sprays supercooled chemicals — light cold damage plus a Move-halving slow on a strong hit. Crowd control and pursuit-stopping.

Flame Projector. SW A flamethrower — a cone of burning fuel that ignores cover (it flows around it) and sets persistent fires. The Mandalorian wrist-flamer’s bigger cousin. Terror and area-denial.

Wrist Flamethrower. SW The integrated Mandalorian armour flamer — a short-range cone of fire, hands-free. Iconic close-quarters Mandalorian terror weapon.

Bowcaster. SW The Wookiee weapon — an energy-wrapped quarrel dealing burn AND pi+ simultaneously (mixed type, Part X). It can be Deflected as energy and dispersed as energy, yet drives a physical bolt through. Powerful, heavy, and culturally specific.

Pulse-Wave Pistol. SW Archaic pre-blaster energy tech — devastating at point-blank (+4 damage), useless at range. The relic weapon of backwater worlds and collectors, still deadly up close.

Pulse-Wave Rifle. SW The rifle version of the pulse-wave — +5 at point-blank, falling off fast. A breaching relic.

Sith Lanvarok. SW An ancient wrist-mounted disc-launcher worn rather than held — frees the hands for melee, usable as an off-hand weapon for two-weapon fighting. Fires crushing discs. A relic of the old Sith.

Wrist Rocket (Mandalorian). SW An armour-mounted mini-missile — explosive (4d×2, AoE falloff Part VI), few shots, hands-free. The Mandalorian’s surprise heavy hitter.

Launchers & Anti-Vehicle

Grenade Launcher. SW Lobs grenades far beyond throwing range — load any grenade type (Part VI), reach out to 600 m. The squad’s indirect-fire and area weapon.

Rocket Launcher. SW An anti-vehicle rocket (6d×3 explosive) — the infantry’s answer to a speeder or light walker. Disposable single-shot variants exist. Bridges to Doc VI.

Missile Tube (Guided). SW A sensor-locked guided anti-vehicle missile (6d×4) — reaches out 4 km, hard to evade once locked. Doc VI vehicle-scale weapon in personal hands.

Plasma Lance. SW A heavy anti-armour energy lance (6d×2 at AD 5) — burns through armoured targets where a normal blaster bounces. The dedicated armour-hunter’s heavy weapon.

Codex VI

Grenades &
Explosives — in Detail

Each throwable & charge, its blast type, and its radius

⟐ Full Arsenal by Faction — Quick Reference

Reading this. House The complete grenade, charge and field-gear list as it appears on the character sheet, grouped by who makes or issues it — pick your faction and ignore the rest (a Republic trooper need never browse the Sith Empire shelf). All bonuses shown are the current, rescaled values. Timing: end-of-turn = detonates at the close of the turn cycle, like a Concentrate maneuver; fixed delays cannot be shortened to 1 second; instant = may go off the moment it is thrown.

Grenades, Charges & Mines

BlasTech. Plasma/concussion grenade — 6d(2) burn ex (3 m, end-of-turn); Stun grenade — HT-4 aff (stun 1d turns) (3 m, end-of-turn); Smoke grenade — Concealment cloud (no damage) (4 m, end-of-turn).

Corporate Arms. Ion grenade — 6d(5) ion ex (droids/cyborgs) (3 m, fixed 2-sec); EMP grenade — HT-6 vs electronics; drops shields (3 m, end-of-turn).

Czerka Corporation. Cryoban grenade — 4d(1) cr + slow (3 m, fixed 2-sec); Seismic charge — 6d×3(2) cr ex (huge AOE) (6 m, fixed 3-sec arming).

Hutt Cartel. Carbonite grenade — 2d cr + encase (2 m, fixed 2-sec); Spice-gas grenade (Hutt) — Knockout gas (HT-3 or sleep) (4 m, fixed 2-sec); Nar Shaddaa scrap-frag — 5d(1) cr ex + [2d] frag (3 m, end-of-turn).

Locris Securities. Proximity mine — 6d×2(2) cr ex (3 m, triggered); Magnetic limpet mine — 6d×3(3) burn ex (3 m, fixed 2-sec minimum); Plasma mine (anti-droid) — 6d(5) ion ex (3 m, triggered); Adhesive grenade (glop) — Adhesive — root (no damage) (3 m, end-of-turn).

Mandalorian. Thermal detonator — 6d×3(2) burn ex (4 m, instant); Mandalorian shrapnel charge — 6d(1) cr ex + [3d] frag (3 m, end-of-turn); Conqueror breaching charge — 6d×3(3) cr ex (4 m, fixed 3-sec arming).

Chiss Ascendancy. Demolition satchel — 6d×2(3) cr ex (3 m, fixed 5-sec arming); Vape charge — 6d×3(5) burn ex (focused; vaporises a fixed point) (2 m, fixed 5-sec arming); Charric grenade — 6d(2) burn ex (3 m, end-of-turn).

Merr-Sonn. Fragmentation grenade — 6d(1) cr ex + [3d] frag (3 m, end-of-turn); Flash-bang (flashbang) — HT-4 aff (blind+deaf 1d turns) (4 m, end-of-turn); Incendiary grenade — 4d burn ex + fire field (3 m, fixed 2-sec); Concussion grenade — 5d cr ex (knockback) (3 m, end-of-turn); Plasma grenade (Merr-Sonn) — 6d(3) burn ex (3 m, end-of-turn).

Republic. Plasma/thermal charge (placed) — 6d×4(3) burn ex (5 m, fixed 3-sec minimum arming); Pulse grenade (anti-shield) — 3d(5) ion + shield-collapse (3 m, end-of-turn); Republic frag grenade — 6d(1) cr ex + [2d] frag (3 m, end-of-turn).

Sith Empire. Bioplague grenade (rakghoul) — 3d tox/turn cloud + disease (4 m, fixed 2–3-sec); Sith poison canister — 3d tox/turn cloud + fear (4 m, fixed 2–3-sec); Interdiction grenade (Sith) — 2d burn + Force-suppression (3 m, fixed 2-sec).

SoroSuub. Sonic grenade — 5d(0.5) son ex + HT-3 stun (4 m, fixed 2-sec); Sonic imploder (SoroSuub) — 5d(0.5) son ex + HT-4 stun (4 m, fixed 2-sec); Sonic disruptor mine (SoroSuub) — 5d(0.5) son ex + stun (3 m, triggered).

Underworld. Gas grenade (acid/corrosive) — 3d cor/turn cloud (4 m, fixed 2–3-sec); Incinerator firebomb (Exchange) — 4d burn ex + fire field (3 m, fixed 2-sec); Knockout-gas grenade (Exchange) — Stun gas (HT-3 or stun) (4 m, fixed 2-sec).

Crafting Explosives & Devices

HouseAnatomy of a Device. Any thrown or placed explosive is four parts: a shell (the casing — sets how much fill it holds, whether it is thrown or placed, and whether it sheds fragments), a trigger (how it goes off — see Triggers & Fuzing below), a fill (explosive compound, or a gas/agent payload), and any number of optional modifiers (a frag liner, a shaped-charge liner, a tamper switch, a sensor). Build it with Explosives (the Demolition specialty); wire the exotic triggers with Explosives or Electronics. A bare grenade is ~10 minutes' work; a satchel charge an hour; a doomsday device, days.

HouseCompound → Yield. Yield comes from the fill, not the roll. Standard explosive compound delivers one 6d "block" of explosive damage per ¼ kg packed — so damage ≈ 6d × (4 × kg of compound). Baradium, the fusion compound at the heart of a thermal detonator, is roughly four times denser in yield, which is how half a kilo of it guts a room. The shell caps how much you can cram in (below); pour in more than the shell rates and you have a satchel charge, not a grenade.

Shell / casingFill capThrowable yield ceilingRole
Holdout shell~¼ kg6dConcealable; a coat-pocket charge.
Hand-grenade shell~½ kg6d×2The standard thrown casing.
Detonator-grade shell (reinforced, baradium-rated)~1 kg6d×3 — the thrown ceilingThermal-detonator class. Past this it must be placed, not thrown.
Satchel / breaching charge2–10 kg6d×8 to 6d×40 (placed)Doors, walls, vehicles. Emplaced only.
Demolition drum10–50 kgstructure / vehicle-scale (placed)Bulkheads, bunkers, ship sections.
Strategic casing (containment-rated)50 kg+off-scale (see Doomsday)Ship-killers and worse. GM-gated.

The thrown ceiling is 6d×3 on purpose — a thermal detonator is as much raw blast as any throwable should ever carry. Bigger yields are placed charges, with arming times and a spot to hit, not something you lob across a room.

HouseThe Crafting Rolls. Assemble: one Explosives roll per device (or per batch of identical grenades). The roll does not raise yield — fill does that — it sets reliability. Success: a sound device. Critical success: a free modifier, or +1 hex of radius. Failure: a dud — components wasted, it will not fire. Critical failure: at best it is unstable (every later handling check at −5); at worst it goes off in your hands. A field-engineer hardsuit or a demolitions bench gives the usual +2; rushing at half-time costs −5.

Placing for Effect. Where a charge sits matters as much as its size. Emplacing is an Explosives roll, and margin of success buys placement bonuses: a shaped seating (×2 damage into the surface it is pressed against, for half its radius) or a contained seating in an enclosed space (falloff one step gentler; the whole compartment takes at least the hex-2 value, per Doc III).
