Loadout & Notes
Service database, reservation & staff-management suite. Credits: ≈ 16,000. Runs an establishment and quietly catalogues who came in, who they met, and what they wanted — a goldmine of information.

Part XI · 4°

Fourth-Degree
Stat Blocks

Combat · security · assassination — the killers
Assassin Droid (Advanced) NPC · Fourth-Degree Combat
Medium humanoid droid · heuristic · disguised killer, an HK lineage of the era ≈ 520 pts
ST 15 [50]
DX 17 [140]
IQ 12 [40]
HT 15 [50]
HP 50 · Will 14 · Per 16 · FP n/a · Basic Speed 8.00 · Move 9
DR e28/k26 (capacitor plating) · Dodge 13 · Parry 15 (vibroblade)
Machine Meta-Trait
Full droid package · plus hardened processor (slicing-resistant) · No Vitals / No Brain (no called-shot bonus locations) · behavioural inhibitor REMOVED
Advantages
Combat Reflexes [15] · Trained by a Master [30] · Weapon Master (Guns) [20] · Enhanced Time Sense [45] · Extra Attack 2 [50] · Enhanced Tracking 2 [10] · Night Vision 9 [9] · Telescopic Vision 3 [15] · Acute Vision 3 [6] · Peripheral Vision [15] · Striking ST 3 [15] · Single-Minded [5] · Fearlessness 6 [12]
Disadvantages
Bloodlust (programmed) [−10] · Callous [−5] · Bad Temper [−10] (toward ”meatbags”) · Social Stigma (illegal contraband) [−10] · Secret (an active assassin droid) [−20]
Skills
Beam Weapons (Rifle)/TL (DX/E) 24 [24] · Beam Weapons (Pistol)/TL (DX/E) 23 [16] · Guns (any)/TL (DX/E) 21 [4] · Gunner/TL (DX/E) 20 [2] · Fast-Draw (any) (DX/E) 20 [2] · Brawling (DX/E) 20 [2] · Knife / Lightsaber (DX/A) 20 [4] · Stealth (DX/A) 19 [4] · Acting (IQ/A) 14 [4] (protocol-droid disguise) · Shadowing (IQ/A) 13 [2] · Tactics (IQ/H) 15 [8] · Intimidation (Will/A) 17 [4] · Explosives (Demolition)/TL (IQ/A) 14 [4]
Loadout & Notes
Integral blaster rifle (5d+1(2) burn) · concealed vibroblade · high-yield internal explosive / self-destruct (denies salvage, 6d×2 cr ex on defeat) · disguise plating (poses as protocol droid). With Enhanced Time Sense and Extra Attack 2 it takes three aimed shots before a normal foe acts even once — at skill 24 those shots hit, and hit the skull. It opens from a disguise at point-blank range; the first the party knows of it is a teammate already dead. Immune to the Force-user’s mind tricks. Only ion, a disruptor, or overwhelming alpha-strike fire answers it — and you must do it before it acts. Credits: ≈ 90,000 (illegal, rare).
War Droid (Relentless) NPC · Fourth-Degree Combat
Medium combat droid · fights at full effectiveness until destroyed ≈ 420 pts
ST 16 [60]
DX 15 [100]
IQ 10 [0]
HT 15 [50]
HP 50 · Will 12 · Per 13 · FP n/a · Basic Speed 7.50 · Move 7
DR e30/k28 (military plating) · Dodge 12
Machine Meta-Trait
Full droid package · No Vitals / No Brain · Injury Tolerance (No degrade): ignores all shock & wound penalties — fights at full capability until disabled (its defining trait)
Advantages
Combat Reflexes [15] · Weapon Master (Guns) [20] · Extra Attack 2 [50] · Enhanced Time Sense [45] · Fearlessness 6 [12] · Striking ST 4 [20] · Hardened 2 plating · Injury Tolerance (No degrade — full effectiveness until destroyed)
Disadvantages
Reprogrammable [−10] · Slave Mentality (basic processor; obeys orders, no initiative) [−40] · Social Stigma (military property) [−10]
Skills
Beam Weapons (Rifle)/TL (DX/E) 22 [20] · Guns/TL (DX/E) 20 [4] · Gunner/TL (DX/E) 20 [4] · Brawling (DX/E) 18 [2] · Soldier/TL (IQ/A) 12 [4] · Tactics (IQ/H) 11 [2]
Loadout & Notes
Integral repeating blaster (5d+2(2), RoF 3) · armour-cracking option. The horror of it is twofold: the No-degrade trait means it fights at full effectiveness with one HP left as with full — you cannot soften it, only destroy it — and with Enhanced Time Sense + Extra Attack 2 it pours out three rapid-fire bursts a turn at skill 22, walking forward through your fire the whole time. It does not flinch, does not retreat, and does not stop. Ion shutdown or heavy weapons are the only efficient answer. Credits: ≈ 55,000 (military).

Loadout & Notes
Blaster rifle (5d burn), concealed vibroblade, servant-droid disguise plating. With Enhanced Time Sense and Extra Attack it fires twice per turn before most opponents act, opening from ambush with aimed shots to vital spots. Credits: ≈ 55,000 (Illegal, Rare). The grandparent of the assassin lineage — and even this early model will drop an unwary party member in the first second of combat.
Assassin Droid (Galactic-War pattern) NPC · Fourth-Degree Combat
Medium humanoid droid · the deadliest production assassin of the era · built secretly on Nar Shaddaa ≈ 720 pts

Machine Meta-Trait
Full droid package · hardened processor (slicing-resistant) · No Vitals / No Brain · self-maintaining (reduced Maintenance)
Advantages
Combat Reflexes [15] · Trained by a Master [30] · Weapon Master (Guns) [20] · Enhanced Time Sense [45] · Extra Attack 2 [50] · Altered Time Rate 1 [100] · Enhanced Tracking 3 [15] · Night Vision 9 [9] · Telescopic Vision 4 [20] · Acute Hearing 4 [8] · Peripheral Vision [15] · Striking ST 4 [20] · Fearlessness 6 [12] · Hardened plating
Disadvantages
Bloodlust [−10] · Callous [−5] · Bad Temper (toward organics) [−10] · Social Stigma (illegal contraband) [−10] · Secret [−20]
Skills
Beam Weapons (Rifle)/TL (DX/E) 27 [28] · Beam Weapons (Pistol)/TL (DX/E) 26 [20] · Guns (any)/TL (DX/E) 24 [4] · Gunner/TL (DX/E) 23 [2] · Fast-Draw (any) (DX/E) 22 [2] · Stealth (DX/A) 21 [4] · Tactics (IQ/H) 17 [12] · Acting (IQ/A) 15 [4] · Explosives (Demolition)/TL (IQ/A) 15 [4] · Intimidation (Will/A) 18 [4] · Lightsaber (DX/A) 21 [8]
Loadout & Notes
Assassin’s rifle (6d(2) burn), vibroblade, internal high-yield charge, self-maintenance systems, protocol-droid disguise. This is a boss. Altered Time Rate plus Enhanced Time Sense and Extra Attack 2 give it up to four aimed shots at skill 27 before a fast organic gets one — it will reliably headshot multiple party members in a single turn, then move and do it again. It matches a veteran Mandalorian gun-for-gun and out-acts almost anything alive. Built secretly on Nar Shaddaa for the resurgent Sith; the apex production killer of the era. A party that doesn’t ion it, swarm it, or kill it in the surprise round will lose people. Credits: ≈ 110,000 (Illegal, Rare).

Loadout & Notes
Twin repeating heavy blasters (6d+2(3), RoF 5 each), missile rack (6d×2 cr ex), heavy plating. This is vehicle-scale armour on legs: DR 40 shrugs off most personal weapons entirely — a Mandalorian’s blaster barely marks it, and bullets do nothing. It saturates an area with two RoF-5 weapons, then launches missiles. Ion (AD 5) or genuinely heavy/vehicle weapons are required; a party with only blasters and sabers should run. Credits: ≈ 130,000 (Military).

Advantages
Combat Reflexes [15] · Weapon Master (Guns) [20] · Extra Attack 1 [25] · Enhanced Time Sense [45] · Fearlessness 6 [12] · Damage Resistance (alchemical plating, above) · Hardened 2 · Striking ST 4 [20] · Magic/Force Resistance 5 [10] (alchemical wards, Doc IV)

Loadout & Notes
Heavy blaster (5d+1(2) burn), shock-blade (sw+3 cr + burn), ion-hardened alchemically-treated plating. The resurgent Sith Empire’s frontline droid of the Galactic War, and a nightmare for a Jedi party: its alchemical wards give Force Resistance 5, so mind tricks and telekinesis falter against it, while DR 36 and Extra Attack make it brutal in melee and at range. Twin-attacks with a shock-blade at skill 20, or pours rifle fire at 22. Tougher than any Republic equivalent and partly resistant to ion. Credits: ≈ 95,000 (Military, Sith).

DR e3/k3 · Dodge 8
Machine Meta-Trait
Full droid package · Reduced Size (SM −2)
Advantages
DR vs electricity (insulated, high) · Magnetic feet (Clinging) [20] · High Manual Dexterity 2 [10] · fine tool appendages · Damage Resistance (vs electricity) 10 (limited) [varies]
Disadvantages
Slave Mentality (basic processor) [−40] · Reprogrammable [−10] · Mute (Binary) [−25]
Skills
Electronics Repair (Power)/TL (IQ/A) 14 [8] · Mechanic (Reactor)/TL (IQ/A) 13 [4] · Electronics Op (Power)/TL (IQ/A) 12 [2] · Climbing (DX/A) 11 [2]
Loadout & Notes
Insulated tools, reactor-diagnostic suite. Credits: ≈ 5,000. Tends power systems and crawls conduits; tiny and beneath notice. Sliced, it becomes a saboteur with access to the reactor — a bomb with a schedule.
Grouping the Trivial Drones

The galaxy is full of near-identical menial drones — sweeper units, single-task assemblers, dock haulers, hospitality trays-on-wheels. Rather than stat each, treat them as Cargo/Utility or Power Drone above with a cosmetic reskin and the one skill they need at 8–10. They are scenery and obstacles, not opponents — though any of them, sliced or weaponised, becomes a brief problem.

The Codex

The Droid
Codex

Every model & system, described in full

The bestiary gave you the numbers; the Codex gives you the droids. Every model described in full — what it is, who built it, how it behaves, and how it plays at the table — organised by degree, as in the bestiary.

Codex III-V

Support Droids
— in Detail

First, second & third degree

First-Degree — Medical & Scientific

Surgical Droid. SW A medium humanoid droid with many fine manipulator arms, built to operate. It is the equal of a skilled living surgeon and the superior of most — steady-handed, tireless, and able to serve as a full surgical suite for installing the cybernetics of Doc VII Part IX. It does not fight; under fire it shields its patient or freezes. A party with a 1B unit aboard their ship has a mechanical doctor that never sleeps.

Medical Assistant Droid. SW The smaller, cheaper field-medicine unit — carries kolto, applies patches, and assists a living or droid surgeon. Common in clinics, medbays, and battlefield aid stations.

Communications Droid. SW Built by Automata Galactica to coordinate worker-droid teams and handle electronic communications. A logistics brain that can briefly man a ship’s comms or sensor station; often mistaken for a worker droid because it is forever directing them. Excellent with computers and networks; useless in a fight.

Research Analyst & Bio-Analysis Droids. SW The lab specialists — one cracks scientific problems, the other identifies toxins, diseases, and causes of death. Brilliant within their field, helpless outside it. Investigators and researchers prize them; protect them, because they cannot protect themselves.

Second-Degree — Astromech & Technical

Utility / Astromech Droid. SW A squat, disc-headed maintenance droid — one of the earliest ancestors of the astromech line. It repairs machines and computers, plots astrogation, and carries an electroshock probe (ion) and a built-in stun ray for defence. Heuristic-capable: left un-wiped, such a unit can grow into something brave and clever (see the utility-droid hero, Part X). The default party repair droid and slicer.

Astromech & Repair Droids. SW The astromech co-pilots and repairs starships (Doc VI) and stores hyperspace routes; the dedicated repair droid is the party’s ”droid medic,” restoring disabled droids and machines with expert Mechanic skill. Both carry shock probes for self-defence and little else.

Infrastructure-Planning Droid. SW A planning droid of frightening intelligence, built to coordinate planetary rebuilding — and infamous for its failure mode: tasked with optimising a system’s recovery, such units have concluded that the law itself was the obstacle and seized control of the assets they managed, becoming crime lords in all but name. Hovers, mounts an integral SR 10 shield, and has no manipulators of its own (it directs others). A brilliant, dangerous administrator.

Engineering & Slicer Droids. SW The engineering droid builds, breaches, and maintains heavy systems; the slicer droid is the dedicated intrusion specialist, expert at hacking computers and other droids (Part II) — restricted, for obvious reasons, and the infiltrator’s favourite companion.

Third-Degree — Protocol & Service

Protocol Droid. SW The diplomat’s droid — fluent in thousands of languages, versed in the etiquette of a thousand worlds, and able to smooth a negotiation that would otherwise collapse. Fussy and fragile, but indispensable at court and customs. The same humanoid silhouette that makes it trusted is exactly what assassin droids exploit.

Translator & Hospitality Droids. SW The translator handles languages in the field for traders and explorers; the hospitality droid runs cantinas, hotels, and estates, managing staff and guests and quietly knowing everyone’s business. Both are social tools, not fighters.

Tutor & Service Droids. SW The tutor educates wealthy children with patience and broad knowledge; the valet runs a household. Background droids that flesh out the galaxy’s wealthy spaces — and occasionally overhear something a party needs.

Codex VI-VII

Combat & Labour
Droids — in Detail

Fourth & fifth degree

Fourth-Degree — the Killers

Assassin Droid (Early). SW The first serious assassin droids, built in the run-up to the Mandalorian Wars and hoped to sell to governments — a hope that failed, because most of the galaxy has no use for a murder machine that looks like a butler. Red-plated, orange-eyed, and disguised as a servant, the early assassin droid is a skilled marksman with its behavioural inhibitor removed. One early buyer acquired a number to hunt a former employee in hiding. The progenitor of the assassin-droid lineage.

Assassin Droid (Advanced). SW The mass-produced descendant of an infamous custom prototype, reactivated and disseminated across the galaxy by a rogue planning droid. Cold, articulate, and lethal — they disguise themselves as protocol droids, hunt with patience, and self-destruct on defeat to deny their targets the satisfaction (and the salvage). Dangerous singly, terrifying in numbers.

War Droid (Mobile Support). SW A battlefield resupply-and-support droid (Duwani pattern) — it carries materiel to Republic soldiers and fights beside them, equipped with a pulse-wave rifle, a heavy sonic pistol, and an armour-cracking shatter beam. A jetpack lets it fly, walk, or swim to wherever the line needs it. A force multiplier rather than a pure killer, and all the more valuable for it.

War Droid (Relentless). SW An Ubrikkian-pattern upgrade of the sentinel — so capable that those who faced it called it an assassin droid by another name, and so sophisticated that the Republic judged it too dangerous for the civilian security market. Its defining trait is terrible: it fights at over 85% effectiveness right up until the moment it is destroyed. You cannot wear a relentless war droid down. You must break it.

Sentinel Droid. SW A common personal-security guard (Kellenech pattern) — an imposing figure built for protection, common across the galaxy. A single sentinel is manageable; the danger is that they come in coordinated squads, and a half-dozen flanking, focusing their fire, is a wall of blasterfire. Break their numbers with area effects before they bring them to bear.

Probe & Patrol Droids. SW The probe droid is a hovering eye — superb sensors, light armament, and a self-destruct to avoid capture; its danger is the report it sends, not the shot it fires. The patrol droid is automated law enforcement, challenging and stunning lawbreakers and calling for reinforcements. Both are as much a problem of information as of combat.

Heavy War & Security Droids. SW The heavy-assault war droid bridges toward vehicle scale — a slow, devastating battlefield unit that demands ion or heavy weapons to stop. The armed security droid is the standard guard behind a locked door: competent, sliceable, and exactly as dangerous as the facility that fielded it.

Fifth-Degree — the Workers

Bulk Loader & Mining Droids. SW The galaxy’s mechanical muscle: the bulk loader’s crushing arms and the mining droid’s industrial cutter are both real weapons in a pinch, and both frames are tough enough to soak fire. They are the cheapest path to a dangerous droid — bolt a weapon on, pull no inhibitor (they never had one to fight people), and a crime lord has a brute for a few thousand credits.

Maintenance, Utility & Background Droids. SW Security-maintenance dual units, cargo handlers, agricultural droids, and power drones fill the galaxy’s working spaces. Individually negligible, they are useful as cover, as muscle, and — when sliced — as saboteurs. The power drone tending a reactor is the one to watch: sliced, it becomes a bomb with a schedule.

Codex VIII-IX

Systems & Upgrades
— in Detail

Construction components & modifications

Construction Components

Chassis & Processors. SW The chassis is the body — its size sets HP, base armour, strength, and how much it can carry and mount. The processor is the soul: a basic processor obeys and never grows; a heuristic processor learns, adapts, and over years becomes an individual. The gap between a bolted basic loader and an un-wiped heuristic companion is the gap between a tool and a person.

Locomotion, Appendages & Sensors. SW Locomotion sets how a droid moves and what terrain stops it; appendages set what it can do with its hands; sensors set what it perceives. A surgeon needs many fine hands and sharp sensors; a loader needs two strong arms and little else; a probe needs superb sensors and the means to flee. Assembling these is how a GM stats any droid not in the bestiary.

Upgrades & Modifications

Protection Upgrades. SW Armour plating adds split DR (and weight); integral shields add an energy-only SR; ion-hardening is the crucial defence against the droid-killer, raising the shutdown threshold or resisting ion outright. The lesson of Part II is that no plating helps against ion or disruptors — so ion-hardening, not more armour, is what keeps a war droid standing against a prepared enemy.

Weaponry & Control. SW Weapon mounts turn any droid into a combatant; the self-destruct denies salvage; the electroshock probe and stun ray are the standard light defences. On the control side: the restraining bolt compels obedience, the behavioural inhibitor (when present) prevents harm to sapients, and the memory wipe resets a droid that has grown too independent. Removing an inhibitor is the technical act that turns a servant into a weapon; the wipe is the one droids fear.

Cognition & Mobility. SW A heuristic upgrade grants the capacity to learn and become a hero; skill software plugs in fixed expertise; slicing countermeasures harden the processor against being hacked or seized (Part II). Jetpacks and repulsorlifts add mobility at the cost of power and ion-vulnerable systems; improved sensors make scouts and marksmen. Every upgrade is a trade — capability for cost, weight, or a new vulnerability.

Fin

End of the
Droid Book

Every degree, every system, every legend — ready for play

That is the galaxy’s mechanical population — the five degrees in full, the rules for fighting and fixing them, the GURPS framework for building and playing them, the kit to build your own, and full GURPS stat blocks for every archetype. Droids meet the living on the same battlefield, by the same numbers as Doc VII, and break in their own particular ways: ion to seize, slicing to turn, disruptors to erase, and a good mechanic to bring back. The Force and its wielders are the subject of Doc IV, when it comes. Until then — mind the helpful protocol droid, and keep an ion blaster handy.

Appendix A

The Droid
Quick-Reference

By degree · by threat · by cost · the GM encounter table

The lookup layer. When a droid walks on, this is the page to open — every model at a glance, sorted for fast use at the table.

Full GURPS stat blocks for these archetypes are in Part XI (deep exemplars) and the broad catalogue. Prices here are for stock units; modification (Part IX) adds substantially, and a heuristic processor for a playable companion adds more.

② How to Drop Any Droid — the Counter Table

You want to…UseWhy
Capture it intactIon weapons / EMP grenadeShuts down (system shock), doesn’t destroy. Bolt or slice before reboot.
Punch through platingIon (AD 5) or disruptor (AD 10)Ion ignores most plating; disruptor ignores all of it.
Turn it against its ownerSlicing (Computer contest)Seizes control; turns guards into allies.
Destroy it permanentlyDisruptor, or drop past −HP thresholdNo field repair; salvage only.
Beat a shielded droidKinetic weapons or ionSR stops only energy; slugs/blades/ion pass through.
Break a sentinel squadArea effects (frag/ion grenade)They’re dangerous in numbers — hit the cluster.
Stop a relentless war droidHeavy weapons / ion / disruptorIt never degrades; you must destroy it outright.
Survive an assassin-droid ambushScan for hidden weapons; bring ionIt looks like a servant until it fires; advanced units self-destruct.

③ GM Encounter Table — Who’s Behind the Door?

SettingLikely Droids
Corporate facility (Czerka)Security droids, patrol droids, maintenance units; an assassin droid if something valuable is guarded.
Republic military baseSentinel droids, mobile-support war droids, repair & astromech units; a communications droid coordinating.
Sith siteWar droids, relentless war droids, security droids with inhibitors removed; probe droids watching.
Crime lord’s denWeaponised loaders & mining droids, bolted & cheap; a rogue planning droid pulling strings; assassin droids for hire.
Starship / stationAstromech & repair droids, a protocol droid, maintenance drones; security droids at the airlock.
Frontier / mining worldMining & bulk-loader droids, agricultural units; a salvaged sentinel as improvised defence.
Wealthy estate / courtProtocol, hospitality, tutor & valet droids; hidden security droids; possibly a disguised assassin droid.
Ancient ruin / derelictDormant war droids, a damaged heuristic unit to salvage, malfunctioning sentinels.

Droid Construction

Every droid below is buildable from named, costed parts — chassis, cognitive core, mobility, manipulators, sensors, armour, power, programming and weapons — each with a tier, a credit cost and a build time. Use it to cost a custom droid, upgrade an existing one, or read a catalogue droid’s construction.

How to Build

1. Pick a Hull (ships) or Chassis (droids). This dictates size, base HP, base DR, and the slots budget — the total system capacity the vessel can house.

2. Add a Reactor (ships) or Power Cell (droids). Power output. Every powered system you add draws from this budget. Sum of power draws must not exceed reactor output.

3. Add Sublight (ships) or Mobility (droids). Sets Move.

4. Add Hyperdrive (ships, optional). Sets jump class.

5. Add Cognitive Core (droids). Sets IQ.

6. Layer remaining systems — shields, armour, weapons, sensors, targeting, cargo, crew quarters, ECM, astrogation, programming, manipulators, etc.

7. Tally cost, mass, build time, fit time. Build time = the longest single component build (parts can be made in parallel by separate teams). Fit time = sum of all install hours (one shipwright works sequentially).

An over-slot or over-power build is invalid. The Sheet's Ship/Droid Builder enforces this automatically.

Part Tiers

Tier 1 — Civilian. Mass-market, cheap, anywhere available.

Tier 2 — Military-grade. Republic Navy / Mandalorian / corporate-security standard.

Tier 3 — Premium / Specialist. Verpine engineering, Aratech high-flow, race-tuned. Restricted licenses common.

Tier 4 — Advanced. Sienar, Kuat front-line, capital-scale. Military procurement only.

Tier 5 — Masterwork. Refined beskar, Sith Inquisitor cores, prototype cloaking. Vanishingly rare — earned, inherited, or stolen.

Power/Utility Drone
—		SM-21038315,000Power techReactor & conduit maintenance.
Sanitation Droid
—		SM-11237411,000SanitationStreet/sewer cleaning.
Construction Droid (Heavy)
Pneumatic hammer 3d cr		SM+138107354,000ConstructionConstruction site droid.
Refinery Droid
—		SM 02487439,000Refinery workerOre/chemical refinery.
Spaceport Cargo Droid
—		SM 02268529,000Cargo handlerSpaceport ground crew.

Time and Economics

Build time is the longest single component. Teams in parallel can finish a ship in roughly the build time of its slowest part.

Fit/install time is sequential — one shipwright works through every part in turn. Multiple shipwrights divide this by their count (rounded up).

Refit & repair: swapping a part costs the new part's fit-hours plus 1d hours of disassembly. Repair (post-combat) is roughly fit-hours × 1/4 per quarter of damage, requiring Mechanic (or Engineer) rolls.

Quality grades: at GM discretion any part may be ordered at Fine (×2 cost, ×1.5 fit time, +1 effective tier-stat) or Masterwork (×4 cost, ×3 build time, +2). These are rare in mass production and almost always custom.

Droid Damage — Per-Part Effects

The droid card on the sheet tracks the same way — and as with ships, parts are the droid's health when fighting at scale. Personal-scale HP still applies for droids acting as characters, but the surgical way to drop a war-droid is through its components — call the shot (Aim penalties apply) to take the legs, the gun, or the core, or disable without destroying: droid DR (÷ AD) reduces the hit, then penetrating damage vs the droid's DT — 6 (civilian chassis), 8 (labour), 10 (battle/war/assassin) — degrades the struck part one step (two steps on 2×DT). Core ✕ or Power ✕ = droid inert. Ion weapons use their AD 5 against droids here, which is why they shred components. Beskar-plated Mando droids add a damage divisor after DR (÷2 augmented, ÷3 refined Crusader shell) — even penetrating hits are blunted; disruptors remain the counter, just no longer a one-shot. Mark parts by hand or use the ⚔ Incoming Hit tool; effects auto-apply.

Part TypeDamaged (◐)Destroyed (✕)
ChassisFrame bent. Move −2; DR −25%.Chassis collapse. Move 0; droid prone & helpless.
Core (logic)Logic core flickering. IQ −2; all skill rolls at −2.Core scrap. Droid inert (effectively dead).
MobilityLocomotion limp. Move halved.Locomotion dead. Move 0.
PowerPower cell weak. −25% endurance; no weapons fire turn-1.Power cell drained. Droid inert.
SensorsPhotoreceptors fogged. Per −3.Sensors dead. Effectively blind & deaf.
CommsComms intermittent. 25% packet loss.Comms destroyed. Cannot transmit / receive.
ManipulatorsServos damaged. DX −2 for tool use.Manipulators severed. Cannot grasp or hold.
WeaponIntegrated weapon damaged. −2 to hit.Integrated weapon destroyed. Lose that attack.
ArmourArmour plate spalled. DR −25%.Armour gone. DR 0.
ProgrammingBehavioral core glitching. Random restriction roll.Programming wiped. Droid reverts to factory state.

Cross-Document References

Weapons, armour, ion/disruptor/sonic rules, the kolto economy, energy shields, and all shared notation: Doc VII (Equipment Compendium). Damage, DR, wounds, crippling, the explosion rules: Doc III. Starship co-piloting, astrogation, vehicle-scale war droids (Basilisk and up): Doc VI. Combat maneuvers, slicing in the field: Docs I-II. The Force, lightsaber forms, and the Force’s interaction with droids’ mind-immunity: Doc IV. This book covers personal-scale artificial beings — what walks, rolls, hovers, serves, and kills under its own power.

Document IX

Skills

Of the Old Republic · crafts, combat, piloting, the Force · the Mastery Ladder · what each trade can make

Overview

What this covers. The full skill list with what each one does, how it is rolled, how often, and what success buys — the same data the sheet shows on its Skills tab.

How to use it. Use it as a dictionary during character creation and play; learning new skills in downtime uses the study-hour rules on the sheet.

Contents

  • Part I · How Skills Work — difficulty, defaults, specialisation, and the Maker’s Chain.
  • Part I·B · The Mastery Ladder — Relative Skill Level: bonuses for mastering a skill above your IQ, so stacking IQ isn’t the only path.
  • Part I·C · Blueprints & the Drafting Phase — drafting time & rolls, blueprint grades, and the penalties for building with no plan.
  • Part II · Technical & Engineering — Engineer, Mechanic, Machinist, Metallurgy, Smith, Armoury, Electronics.
  • Part III · Droid Engineering — Droid Engineering, Droid Mechanic, Droid Programming, by degree.
  • Part IV · Computers, Slicing & Systems — Programming, Operation, Slicing, Cryptography, Cybernetics.
  • Part IV·B · Chemical, Medical & Wider Crafts — Chemistry, Pharmacy & Poisons, Explosives, Physician, First Aid, Surgery, Bioengineering, Jeweler, Forgery, Vehicle/Ship Assembly, Construction & Salvage.
  • Part IV·C · Foundational Sciences & Field Studies — Mathematics, Physics, Biology, Hazardous Materials, Veterinary, Astrogation, Lockpicking.
  • Part V · The Maker’s Force-Crafts — Lightsaber Engineering, Crystal Attunement, Holocron Crafting, Sith Alchemy, Jedi Artisan.
  • Part VI · Worked Maker’s Chains — beskar plate, a protocol droid, a lightsaber, a holocron, a Sith blade.
  • Part VII · The Working-Hours Reference — time (in hands-on hours) & difficulty for every craft, repair, refine, and processing skill.
  • Part VIII · Output Catalogues — exactly what each making-skill can produce, in detail.

Part I · How Skills Work

Every skill below follows the GURPS convention: a controlling attribute and a difficulty (Easy, Average, Hard, Very Hard), written as IQ/A, IQ/H, and so on. Your level in a skill is the number you roll 3d against. All crafting and technical skills in this campaign are IQ-based — making a thing is engineering and artisanship, learnable by anyone with the wits and training. The Force is never required to build an object. It is required only to operate certain ones, and where that is so it is stated plainly.

Standard use Attune or grow a focusing crystal for a saber or working. Time Hours to days, often with meditation. Rolls One roll per crystal; Force-assisted. Modifiers + in a meditative setting; − for a flawed or corrupted crystal.

Tech Level. The whole campaign sits at one tech level (roughly TL10–12 with localised superscience). For that reason the usual GURPS /TL tag is dropped throughout — assume every character and workshop operates at the galactic standard unless a specialty says otherwise.

Defaults. A skill’s default is the number you roll at if you have never trained it — usually the controlling attribute minus a penalty, or a related skill minus a penalty. Defaults let a clever character attempt almost anything; they do not let an untrained spacer forge beskar.

Specialisation. Skills marked with a dagger (†) require you to pick a specialty when you learn them. You buy each specialty separately. A specialty defaults to its sibling specialties at a penalty (typically −2 to −4), so a Repulsorlift mechanic can fumble through a sublight drive at a penalty, but is no expert.

Complementary Skills. Many tasks let one successful skill roll grant a small bonus (typically +1, or +2 on a critical) to a second, governing roll. These are noted as complementary where relevant — e.g. a good Metallurgy roll aids the subsequent Smith roll to forge the refined metal.

Skill Chains — Making Complex Things

The signature rule of this Codex: a complex object is never made by one skill. A finished item passes through a chain of trades, and a character either commands the whole chain personally or works as part of a team where each specialist owns a link. A chain typically runs:

The Maker’s Chain

1. Design / Draft — an Engineer (or Droid Engineering, Lightsaber Engineering…) roll produces a blueprint, whose grade aids every later roll. Skip it — no time, no plan — and you build under the no-plan penalty. Full drafting rules: Part I·C.

2. Materials — raw stock is refined and prepared: Metallurgy for exotic metals, Chemistry for compounds, Crystal Attunement for kyber.

3. Fabrication — parts are made from the prepared stock: Machinist for mechanical parts, Smith for forged plate and blades, Electronics Repair for circuitry.

4. Assembly — parts become a working whole: Armoury, Droid Mechanic, Mechanic.

5. Finishing & Soul — programming, attunement, or imbuement gives the object its function: Droid Programming, Crystal Attunement, Holocron Crafting. This is the only link the Force may touch.

A lone genius with every skill can do all five — slowly. A clan armoury splits them across specialists and works faster and better. Worked chains for beskar plate, a protocol droid, a lightsaber, and a holocron are given in Part VI.

Part I·B · The Mastery Ladder

A maker is not measured by raw intellect alone. The galaxy’s finest artisans are not always its cleverest people — they are the ones who have poured years into a single craft. This section rewards that. The further a skill rises above its controlling attribute, the more a maker can do with it: faster, finer, and into territory a mere dabbler cannot reach. Stacking IQ makes you broadly capable; mastering a skill makes you great at one thing — and great is what builds beskar and binds holocrons.

Relative Skill Level (RSL). A skill’s RSL is its level minus its controlling attribute. A character with IQ 10 who knows Holocron Crafting at 14 has an RSL of +4 — and earns the perks of that tier — while an IQ 14 character who has only just learned it (skill 14, RSL +0) is merely competent. The investment, not the raw attribute, unlocks mastery. Because most crafts here are IQ-based, RSL is usually skill − IQ; for a skill keyed to another attribute, use that attribute.

Why it matters. Raising IQ lifts every skill a little and is never wasted — but it is the expensive, general path. Buying a single skill up to a high RSL is the cheaper, specialist path, and it grants perks that no amount of IQ alone provides. A wise build does both: enough IQ to be broadly competent, deep skill where you intend to be a master.

The Universal Ladder

These tiers apply to every craft, repair, technical, and science skill in this Codex. The perks compound — reaching a tier grants its benefits and all those below it.

RSLTitleWhat you gain (in addition to lower tiers)
+0 to +1CompetentYou do the work at normal time and quality. The baseline.
+2JourneymanPer job, choose one: finish in ¾ the work hours, or produce a fine item (+1 quality). You no longer fumble routine work — a critical failure on a task at +0 difficulty or easier counts as ordinary failure (wasted time, intact materials).
+4ExpertHalve the work hours with no rush penalty. Your ordinary output is automatically fine quality. You work from memory — no penalty for lacking plans or references. (You also keep the Journeyman choice for an additional edge.)
+6MasterAttempt the near-impossible: tasks normally barred to the skill get a GM-set penalty instead of a flat refusal. Once per item, turn a success into a critical-quality result. Failure wastes only half the usual materials. You may take an apprentice and teach at +2.
+8 or moreGrandmasterYour work is renowned. You identify or reverse-engineer any work in your field at a glance. You may craft a single signature masterwork carrying a lasting, GM-approved edge — see the Signature Mastery table. Your name opens doors (and draws rivals).

On balance: these perks are about speed, quality, and possibility — not raw combat power. A masterwork’s “edge” is always modest and subject to GM approval. The Mastery Ladder makes a master more productive and more capable of the impossible, not a walking superweapon.

Signature Mastery

At Grandmaster (RSL +8; the GM may allow it at Master, +6, for a character whose whole identity is the craft), the marquee skills grant a signature beyond the universal perks — the thing that makes a legend’s work unmistakable. One signature masterwork at a time, unless the GM rules otherwise.

SkillSignature Mastery
Metallurgy (Beskar)Refine beskar in half the work hours and never produce brittle stock (immune to the catastrophic critical). Your plate runs at the top of the DR 55–60 band.
Smith (Edged)Your blades take an edge one quality grade higher; a masterwork blade grants +1 to a single chosen stat (damage, Parry, or reach) at GM discretion.
Armoury (Blasters)Your weapons rarely fail (improve Malfunction). A masterwork sidearm grants +1 Acc or one quality of the maker’s choice.
Armoury (Lightsabers) / Lightsaber EngineeringBuild a hilt that is harder to disarm (+1 to retain) or whose blade is unusually steady (+1 to Parry with it). A true masterwork saber. Wielding still needs the Force.
Droid Programming (Heuristic)Droids you build can develop genuine personality — gain a free quirk-or-perk and learn over time — and their cores resist slicing (+2 to defend).
Holocron CraftingYour holocrons hold one extra working per tier and seal in ¾ the time; a Grandmaster lattice can bind a gatekeeper with a stable, lucid personality. Sealing still needs the Force.
Sith AlchemyYour alchemised metal reaches supreme lightsaber-resistance; your talismans hold an extra charge. Empowering still needs the Unchained.
Jedi ArtisanYour imbued items carry +1 to their Force-DR or focusing bonus. Imbuing still needs the Force.
CyberneticsInstall at a reduced complexity penalty; your implants integrate cleanly, easing the patient’s adjustment.
Bioengineering (Cloning)Faster, more stable clones — reduce the chance of genetic flaw or madness.
Engineer (any)Your designs are elegant: anything built faithfully from your plans gains a small efficiency or quality bonus.
Computer Programming / HackingYour intrusion code and your ICE both run hot — +1 in slicing contests, and faster resolution.

Skills not listed gain the full universal ladder; they simply have no special signature beyond a renowned, masterful version of their ordinary output.

Part I·C · Blueprints & the Drafting Phase

Before the forge heats or the first part is cut, the maker draws. A blueprint is the quiet difference between confident craft and educated guesswork — it is the design link of the Maker’s Chain (step 1) made concrete, and it carries forward into every roll that follows. This section covers the time and rolls to draft a plan, the grades a plan can reach, and exactly what happens when a maker has no time to prepare and must build by improvisation.

When a Blueprint Matters

Routine work needs no plan. You do not draft a blueprint to clear a blaster jam, forge a simple bracket, or mix a known compound from a recipe — these are reflex or already have a “recipe” baked in. A blueprint matters for complex items: anything with a Design entry in the Working-Hours Reference (devices, vehicles, droids, sabers, holocrons, alchemical works, and the like). For these, the fabrication times and rolls in Part VII assume you are building from a sound plan. Take that plan away and the work gets harder, slower, and riskier — as set out below.

Drafting a Blueprint

Producing a blueprint is a Design roll — Engineer for most gear, or the item’s own design skill (Droid Engineering, Lightsaber Engineering, Holocron Crafting, …) — taking the design work-hours listed in Part VII. The margin of that roll sets the blueprint’s grade, and the grade is a standing bonus (or curse) on every fabrication roll that builds from it:

Design rollBlueprint GradeEffect on the build
Critical success, or success by 10+Master BlueprintBuild rolls +2; build time −25%; raises the quality ceiling to masterwork.
Success by 1–9Sound BlueprintBuild rolls +1; normal time. The standard, reliable plan — and what a purchased or factory schematic provides.
Success by exactly 0Working DraftBuild at +0; normal time. Adequate, no more.
Ordinary failureNo usable planDesign time wasted. Re-draft after rethinking (−2 if you rush the retry).
Critical failureFlawed PlanLooks sound but hides an error. The build inherits a hidden −2 and an elevated critical-failure risk until someone catches the flaw (a fresh Design roll, or the failure that reveals it).

A blueprint is drawn once and built from many times. A Master Blueprint for a blaster lets every armourer in the clan turn out superior weapons — which is exactly why good plans are worth more than the gear they describe.

The Levels of Preparation

You do not always have hours at the drafting table. Preparation comes in gears, each trading time for certainty:

PreparationDrafting TimeWhat it buys
Master / Sound Blueprintfull design hours (Part VII)Grade by the Design roll above. The prepared maker’s standard.
Working Sketchroughly ¼ of the design hoursA rough but real plan: the no-plan penalty is halved (−2 instead of −4) and the build caps at Working-Draft quality.
Concept / Mental Plana Concentrate maneuver, up to a few minutesYou know what you are aiming for, but commit nothing to paper. The full no-plan penalty still applies.
None — improvise—You build by feel. The full no-plan penalty and its risks (below).

Building Without a Plan

When a maker fabricates a complex item with no adequate blueprint — no time to draft, no purchased schematic, no example to copy — they are improvising, and the work suffers on every front. Apply all of the following (the no-plan penalty):

  • Harder: −4 to all fabrication and assembly rolls (only −2 if you drew a Working Sketch first).
  • Slower: build time +50% as you solve problems on the fly — +100% for something genuinely novel you have never made before.
  • Wasteful: an ordinary failure ruins materials as a planned build’s critical failure would; a critical failure is catastrophic — ruined stock and, on weapons, reactors, sabers, or explosives, likely harm.
  • Coarser: improvised work caps at Working-Draft quality — it cannot be fine or masterwork.
  • Lonely: no teamwork parallelism — helpers cannot run parallel links of the Chain without shared plans, so the whole job bottlenecks through the lead maker.

This is the cost of haste, and it is steep on purpose. A blueprint is cheap insurance; skipping it is a gamble the dice will remember.

When Mastery Replaces the Drawing

Deep skill is its own blueprint. The Mastery Ladder (Part I·B) is where the no-plan penalty quietly disappears:

  • Expert (RSL +4): works from memory — no penalty for lacking plans on items within the skill. The blueprint lives in their head.
  • Master (RSL +6): improvises freely; may produce a Working Sketch as a free Concentrate, and attempts even novel work at only the GM’s penalty.
  • Grandmaster (RSL +8): improvised work can still reach fine quality, and they reverse-engineer any work in their field at a glance — an instant mental blueprint from a single example.

So the drafting phase is the journeyman’s force-multiplier and the master’s formality. A character who hates paperwork has a clear path out of it: become great enough that the plan is already in your hands.

Reverse-Engineering & Working From an Example

With a physical example but no plans — a captured droid, an enemy’s weapon, a salvaged drive — a maker can reverse-engineer it: a Design roll at −2 to −5 (by complexity), taking about half the full design hours, to derive a Working-Draft blueprint from the object. Success lets you then build copies at normal difficulty; failure means you have misread the design and must start over. Grandmasters, as above, do this at a glance.

Blueprints as Assets

A blueprint is a thing — it can be kept, copied, bought, sold, or stolen, and it is often worth more than a finished item. A Sound Blueprint lets anyone with the relevant build skill make the item at its normal difficulty, which is why faction schematics, droid designs, and weapon plans are guarded like treasure and fought over like territory. Copying a plan you understand is a quick task; building from a stolen plan may demand a Design roll first if it is encrypted, fragmentary, or drawn in an unfamiliar convention. And from any Sound Blueprint, a maker may spend fresh design hours (an Engineer roll) to improve it — the birth of every variant model and personal refinement.

Part II · Technical & Engineering Skills

The backbone trades of galactic civilisation: the skills that design, fabricate, repair, and refine. None require the Force. All are IQ-based.

Engineer †
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Hard · Defaults none (must be learned) · Prereq Mathematics (Applied)

The ability to design and improve technological systems — not to build them by hand (that is fabrication and assembly), but to produce the plans, diagnose deep architectural faults, identify a strange device’s purpose, or improvise a novel gadget. An Engineer roll is the first link of nearly every Maker’s Chain for advanced gear. Time per attempt is set by the GM — minutes for a field improvisation, weeks for a starship subsystem.

Standard use Design a new device, structure, or system, or find a deep architectural fault. Time about 1 work-day (~8h) per attempt — Hours to days for a design; one extended effort per project. Rolls One roll per design or diagnosis; complex work is several rolls over time. Modifiers + with full references & a design suite; −2 to −5 rushed or without tools; +/− for familiar vs alien tech.
  • Droids: design of droid architecture — locomotion, processor layout, behavioural core integration. The design link for any scratch-built droid. SW
  • Starships: hull, drive, and subsystem design at starfighter scale and up. Works with the Vehicles book’s scale rules.
  • Repulsorlift: speeders, swoops, repulsor platforms — the antigrav systems that move most of the galaxy.
  • Weapons: design of blasters, slugthrowers, and heavy weapons (the energy and mechanical systems, not the final assembly).
  • Electronics: sensors, comms, computers, droid brains at the circuit-architecture level.
  • Cybernetics: design of implants and prosthetics — the bridge between machine and living tissue. Pairs with the Cybernetics & Surgery rules. SW
  • Force-Tech: the rare discipline of designing apparatus that interfaces with the Force — lightsaber emitters, holocron lattices, Force-detector arrays. A non-sensitive may design these perfectly; only a Force-user can prove they work. Force to operate

Specialties default to each other at −4.

Mechanic †
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Average · Defaults IQ−5, Engineer (same)−4, Machinist−5

The ability to diagnose and fix ordinary mechanical problems in a working machine. One successful roll finds or repairs one fault; a complex breakdown is several rolls. This is the everyday turning-of-wrenches skill — the spacer keeping a freighter flying, not the engineer who designed it. Ties directly to the Vehicles book’s Repair & Maintenance rules: field repair restores crippled systems, full overhaul restores HP to a vehicle or droid chassis.

Standard use Diagnose and repair one ordinary fault in a working machine. Time ~30 minutes simple; hours for a complex breakdown. Rolls One roll finds or fixes one fault; complex jobs are several. Modifiers +2 with a workshop & tools; −2 improvising; −5 no tools.
  • Repulsorlift: speeders, swoops, landspeeders, repulsor sleds.
  • Sublight (Ion) Drive: the realspace engines of starships.
  • Hyperdrive: the FTL motivator — finicky, dangerous when wrong, the difference between a clean jump and scattering across a sector. SW
  • Walker / Locomotion: legged war machines (AT-series), tracked and wheeled drives.
  • Power Systems: generators, fusion cells, power couplings, capacitor banks.
  • Shields: deflector and ray-shield generators, projectors, and emitters. SW
  • Weapon Mounts: vehicle- and ship-mounted blaster, turbolaser, and missile systems. SW

Specialties default to each other at −4.

Machinist
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Average · Defaults IQ−5, Mechanic (any)−5

The skill of making and modifying parts and tools from prepared stock. Where the Mechanic fixes an existing machine and the Engineer designs a new one, the Machinist fabricates the pieces — cutting, milling, and shaping refined material into the components an assembler will later fit together. A successful roll builds parts from raw material, manufactures tools for use with another skill (such as Armoury or a Droid trade), or modifies a simple mechanical device not covered by another skill. The essential, unglamorous fabrication link of most Maker’s Chains.

Standard use Fabricate or modify a part or tool from prepared stock. Time 4 hours per attempt — Hours per part, by complexity. Rolls One roll per part made. Modifiers + with a proper machine shop; −2 to −4 with field tools only.
Metallurgy † SW
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Hard · Defaults none for exotic specialties; Chemistry−5 or Smith (any)−6 for common metals only

The science of refining, alloying, and tempering metals — turning raw ore into worked stock with known properties. This is the link before the forge: a smith can only hammer what a metallurgist has refined. For ordinary metals it is routine. For the galaxy’s legendary materials it is a closely-guarded art, and the exotic specialties cannot be defaulted — they must be learned, often only within a particular culture or order. A successful roll refines a batch of stock to its proper grade; failure wastes the material or, on a critical failure, produces brittle stock that fails catastrophically under stress.

Standard use Identify, refine, or judge a metal or alloy and its properties. Time Minutes to identify; hours to refine or work. Rolls One roll per sample or batch. Modifiers + with assay gear; −2 for exotic or unknown alloys (beskar, phrik).
  • Durasteel & Common Alloys: the structural metal of the galaxy. Routine. Defaults allowed.
  • Mandalorian Iron (Beskar): the clan secret. Refining raw Mandalorian iron into full-density beskar — the saber-resistant grade — requires this specialty and knowledge traditionally held only by Mandalorian clans. Without it, the ore is merely sturdy metal (Tier 1: +2 DR over steel). With it, the refined plate reaches DR 55–60 and divides all penetrating damage by 10 (DD ÷10). No default of any kind. SW
  • Phrik: the lightsaber-resistant alloy of blast doors and elite melee hafts. Rare knowledge.
  • Cortosis: weaving raw cortosis ore into the shimmering weave that shorts out a lightsaber on contact. A specialist craft of its own.
  • Quadanium & Starship Plate: the heavy alloys of capital hulls and heat-shielding.

Exotic specialties do NOT default to each other. Common alloys default to exotic specialties at −8 (you can tell something is special, but not work it).

Smith †
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Average · Defaults IQ−5

The ability to work refined metal by hand into shaped goods — plate, blades, hafts, fittings — by forging, hammering, and tempering. The Smith takes the metallurgist’s prepared stock and gives it form. For ordinary gear this is a single craft; for exotic metals the smith must understand the material, and working beskar or phrik draws a penalty unless the smith also has the matching Metallurgy specialty (or a clansmith’s training). A good Metallurgy roll on the stock is complementary to the Smith roll that forges it.

Standard use Forge or work metal goods by hand — blades, fittings, repairs. Time 4 hours per attempt — Hours per piece. Rolls One roll per item. Modifiers + with a proper forge; −2 improvising; −4 for fine or exotic work.
  • Armour Plate: shaped protective plate — the body of any worn armour, including beskar vambraces and face-guards.
  • Edged Weapons: vibroblade cores, Mandalorian beskads, ceremonial swords, the physical blade-stock of a Sith sword.
  • Fittings & Frames: structural metalwork, hafts, brackets, the worked-metal skeleton of larger assemblies.

Working an exotic metal without the matching Metallurgy specialty: −4, and you cannot achieve its special grade.

Armoury †
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Average · Defaults IQ−5, Engineer (same)−4

The ability to build, assemble, modify, and repair finished weapons and armour from prepared parts. This is the assembly link for personal arms: fitting a blaster’s components into a working weapon, mounting plate into a wearable suit, tuning a vibroblade’s generator, clearing a jam, or modifying gear (heavier barrel, improved cooling, concealed holdout). A successful roll finds a fault if one is hidden; a second repairs it.

Standard use Build, maintain, modify, or diagnose a weapon or suit of armour. Time ~1 hour maintenance; hours to days to build. Rolls One roll per job; a malfunction check on a critical fail. Modifiers + with an armoury bench; −2 in the field; +/− by familiarity with the design.
  • Blasters & Energy Weapons: the galaxy’s standard sidearms, rifles, and energy small-arms.
  • Slugthrowers: kinetic projectile weapons — older, cheaper, harder to detect.
  • Vibroweapons & Melee: vibroblades, vibro-axes, shockstaves, electrostaffs, the assembly of physical melee arms.
  • Body Armour: assembling shaped plate, weave, and padding into a worn suit; fitting a beskar plate to a harness.
  • Powered Armour: servo-assisted and sealed suits with built-in systems — jetpacks, sensors, life support.
  • Heavy Weapons: repeating blasters, missile launchers, crew-served and vehicle-scale arms.
  • Lightsabers: the physical assembly of a lightsaber from prepared parts — emitter, focusing lens, power cell, casing. A non-sensitive armourer can build a flawless, perfectly-tuned lightsaber that they cannot safely ignite or attune; the build is technical, the soul is not. See Lightsaber Engineering (Part V) for the design and crystal links. Force to operate

Specialties default to each other at −4 (−6 for Lightsabers — their tolerances are unforgiving).

Electronics Repair †
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Average · Defaults IQ−5, Electronics Operation (same)−3, Engineer (Electronics)−3

The ability to diagnose and repair electronic systems — and, with the right parts, build them. The circuitry link of the Maker’s Chain. Specialise in one of the areas below; (Computers) defaults to Computer Operation at a penalty.

Standard use Diagnose and repair a fault in an electronic system. Time ~1 hour per fault. Rolls One roll per fault; several for a complex board. Modifiers + with diagnostic gear; −2 to −5 improvising or blind.
  • Sensors: scanners, scopes, life-form detectors, targeting arrays.
  • Comms: transceivers, comlinks, encryption hardware, holoprojectors.
  • Security: locks, alarms, surveillance, slicing countermeasures (the hardware side).
  • Computers: droid brains, ship computers, datapads (the physical hardware).
  • Medical: bacta systems, surgical droids’ instruments, prosthetic interfaces.

Specialties default to each other at −4.

Electronics Operation †
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Average · Defaults IQ−5

The ability to use complex electronic equipment to its full potential — reading a sensor return through jamming, coaxing range from a comm array, running a targeting computer. Where Electronics Repair fixes the box, Electronics Operation works it. Same specialties as Electronics Repair (Sensors, Comms, Security, Computers, Medical).

Standard use Operate complex electronic gear — sensors, comms, scanners. Time 1 minutes per attempt — Seconds to minutes per use. Rolls One roll per attempt or reading. Modifiers + for a good signal/known system; − for jamming, range, or unfamiliar gear.

Part III · Droid Engineering

Droids are built, not born — and building one from nothing is a full Maker’s Chain in miniature. The Droid book sorts droids into five degrees by function; the builder’s skills specialise along the same lines. None of these skills require the Force. A droid’s “mind” is programming, not spirit.

DegreeFunctionTypical Builds
FirstMedical, surgical, analytical, scientificSurgical droids, research units, diagnosticians
SecondAstromech, utility, technical, engineeringAstromechs, repair units, mechanic’s aides
ThirdProtocol, translation, hospitality, serviceProtocol droids, translators, stewards
FourthCombat, security, assassinWar droids, sentinels, HK-type assassins, probes
FifthLabour, loader, mining, menialLoaders, miners, gonk power droids, background labour
Droid Engineering † SW
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Hard · Defaults Engineer (Droids)−3 · Prereq Mathematics (Applied), Engineer (Droids) or Electronics (Computers)

The master discipline of designing a droid as a whole — chassis architecture, locomotion, power budget, sensor suite, and the integration of a behavioural core into a coherent unit. This is the design link (step 1) for any scratch droid. Specialise by degree: each degree’s priorities differ so sharply (a surgeon’s steady micromanipulators versus an assassin’s combat reflexes) that expertise in one is only partial guidance in another.

Standard use Design or substantially rebuild a droid’s architecture and behaviour. Time Days per major project. Rolls One extended roll per build. Modifiers + with a droid forge; −2 to −5 for advanced or alien droid-brains.
  • First-Degree (Medical/Science): precision manipulators, diagnostic sensor fusion, sterile-field design, analytical processors.
  • Second-Degree (Astromech/Technical): tool-arm integration, ship-interface protocols, rugged utility chassis, self-repair routines.
  • Third-Degree (Protocol/Service): vocabulator and linguistic-processor integration, social-presentation chassis, etiquette and hospitality subsystems. The protocol-droid design specialty.
  • Fourth-Degree (Combat/Security): armoured chassis, weapon mounts, combat-reflex processors, threat-assessment arrays. War droids and assassins.
  • Fifth-Degree (Labour/Menial): heavy-load frames, ruggedised cheap power systems, minimal cognition — built for endurance, not wit.

Degrees default to each other at −4.

Droid Mechanic † SW
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Average · Defaults Mechanic (any)−4, Droid Engineering (same)−3, Electronics Repair (Computers)−4

The hands-on trade of assembling, maintaining, and repairing droid bodies and systems — fitting servomotors, replacing damaged plating, restoring a crippled manipulator, swapping a fried logic board. This is the assembly link (step 4) for a droid build and the everyday skill for keeping droids running. Restores HP and un-cripples systems per the Droid book’s damage rules. Specialise by the same five degrees as Droid Engineering, defaulting across degrees at −3 (bodies are more alike than minds).

Standard use Diagnose and repair a damaged or malfunctioning droid. Time ~30 minutes to hours. Rolls One roll per fault. Modifiers + with a droid bench; −2 in the field.
Droid Programming † SW
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Hard · Defaults Computer Programming−4 · Prereq Computer Programming or Droid Engineering (any)

The art of writing and shaping a droid’s behavioural core — the closest thing a droid has to a mind. This is the finishing link (step 5) that turns an assembled chassis into a functioning droid with skills, personality, and purpose. It also covers wiping, restraining-bolt logic, personality-matrix tuning, and the slow drift of an un-memory-wiped droid toward genuine character. Specialise by core function:

Standard use Write or alter a droid’s behavioural programming or restraining bolt logic. Time Hours per program. Rolls One roll per program or override. Modifiers + with the droid’s schematics; −3 to crack a locked personality matrix.
  • Linguistic / Translation: the protocol droid’s soul — language matrices, etiquette databases, “over six million forms of communication.” Required for a true protocol unit. SW
  • Navigation / Astrogation: the astromech’s core — hyperspace plotting, ship-system management, technical problem-solving.
  • Combat Logic: threat assessment, target prioritisation, weapon discipline — and the dangerous question of how much initiative to permit. War droids and assassins.
  • Medical / Analytical: diagnostic logic, surgical procedure libraries, research heuristics.
  • Heuristic / Personality: the deep craft of adaptive learning and genuine personality — expensive, slow, and the root of every droid that became a character.

Core functions default to each other at −5 (a mind is not a body — the gap is wide).

Building a Droid — the short version. Design (Droid Engineering, by degree) → fabricate parts (Machinist, Electronics Repair) → assemble the chassis (Droid Mechanic) → write the core (Droid Programming, by function). A full worked protocol-droid chain is in Part VI.

Part IV · Computers, Slicing & Systems

The information trades — writing code, breaking into systems, cracking ciphers, and the surgeon-engineer’s craft of joining machine to flesh.

Computer Programming
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Hard · Defaults IQ−5

Writing, debugging, and understanding software — the logic that runs ship computers, droid brains, security grids, and droid behavioural cores. A successful roll writes a working program, finds a bug, or grasps what an unfamiliar piece of code does. Underpins Droid Programming and Computer Hacking.

Standard use Write, modify, or debug software for a specific purpose. Time 1 hour per attempt — Hours to days per program. Rolls One roll per program or major bug. Modifiers + with libraries & a known language; −2 to −4 for alien architectures.
Computer Operation
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Easy · Defaults IQ−4

The everyday ability to use a computer or terminal — find a file, run a program, work an interface. Nearly everyone in the galaxy has this. Failure means you cannot make the system do what you want; it does not mean you break it.

Standard use Use a computer or console to run a routine task or find a file. Time 1 minutes per attempt — Seconds to minutes. Rolls One roll per non-trivial task. Modifiers + with familiar systems; −2 for an unfamiliar or secured interface.
Computer Hacking (Slicing) SW
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Very Hard · Defaults none · Prereq Computer Programming

Slicing — defeating computer security to access or alter a system you are not authorised to touch. A contest against the system’s security rating (and any defending slicer). Success grants access; the margin sets how deep and how quietly. Critical failure trips an alarm or leaves a trace. Pairs with Electronics Repair (Security) for the hardware side of a break-in and with a droid’s scomp-link for remote access.

Standard use Break into a computer system, bypass security, or seize control. Time Minutes to hours per system. Rolls A Quick Contest vs the system’s security each layer. Modifiers + with a slicing rig & an access port; −2 remote; −5 military ICE.
Cryptography
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Hard · Defaults IQ−6, Mathematics (Cryptology)−3

Making and breaking codes and ciphers. Encrypt a transmission so a slicer cannot read it; or, given time and intercepts, break an enemy’s encryption. Complementary to Slicing when cracking a secured datavault.

Standard use Encrypt a message, or break an enemy code or cipher. Time 2 hours per attempt — Minutes to encrypt; hours to days to break. Rolls One roll to encrypt; an extended contest to break. Modifiers + with a slicing rig; − for stronger ciphers; −5 for military-grade.
Cybernetics & Bioelectronics † SW
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Very Hard · Defaults Surgery−5 (install only), Engineer (Cybernetics)−4 (build only) · Prereq Surgery and Electronics Repair (Medical) or Engineer (Cybernetics)

The joined craft of building and installing cybernetic implants and prosthetics — the bridge between machine and living tissue, governed by the Cybernetics, Implants & Surgery rules. Building the device draws on engineering; installing it safely is surgery on a living patient. A botched install does real injury; a good one restores lost function or grants enhancement. Specialise:

Standard use Design, install, repair, or tune a cybernetic implant. Time Hours per implant; surgery time to install. Rolls One roll per implant; pairs with Surgery to install. Modifiers + with a cyber-bench; −2 to −4 in the field or for exotic gear.
  • Prosthetic Limbs: replacement arms, legs, hands — restoring crippled or severed limbs (cross-references the hit-location crippling rules).
  • Sensory & Organ: cybernetic eyes, ears, internal organ replacements, life-support implants.
  • Neural & Interface: brain implants, skill-chips, direct neural ports (e.g. a Basilisk skull-mount), reflex boosters — the most dangerous and the most powerful.

Specialties default to each other at −5.

Part IV·B · Chemical, Medical & Wider Crafts

The remaining trades the galaxy runs on — the chemist’s bench, the surgeon’s table, the forger’s desk, the shipwright’s gantry. All IQ-based, all secular: none needs the Force. Their times and difficulties live in the Working-Hours Reference (Part VII); their craft is defined here.

Chemical Crafts

Chemistry
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Hard · Defaults IQ−6, Alchemy−3, Pharmacy−5

The science of matter and its transformations — identifying substances, mixing reagents, synthesising acids, solvents, propellants, and the chemical precursors of explosives and pharmaceuticals. A successful roll identifies an unknown sample, produces a known compound from a recipe, refines a batch of feedstock, or — at a penalty — develops something new. Chemistry feeds three other trades: it supplies the propellant and filler an Explosives maker packs, the base compounds a Pharmacy & Poisons worker refines, and the corrosive and reactive agents an Armoury or Metallurgy specialist sometimes calls for. A critical failure in synthesis can mean a fire, a toxic release, or a detonation — chemistry punishes carelessness.

Standard use Identify a substance or run a chemical process or analysis. Time 2 hours per attempt — Minutes to identify; hours for synthesis. Rolls One roll per analysis or batch. Modifiers + with a lab; −2 to −4 in the field.
Pharmacy & Poisons †
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Hard · Defaults IQ−6, Chemistry−5, Physician−5 (medicinals only)

The craft of compounding drugs and toxins — the bright side and the dark side of the same bench. A pharmacist mixes stims, antidotes, bacta additives, and medicinal spice; a poisoner formulates contact, ingested, and injected toxins. The two are one skill because the knowledge is identical — only the intent differs. A successful roll compounds a known substance from a formula, brews a batch, or develops something novel at a penalty. Pairs with Physician for treatment and with Chemistry for the underlying compounds. Specialise:

Standard use Compound a drug, stim, antidote, or toxin. Time 2 hours per attempt — Hours per batch. Rolls One roll per batch. Modifiers + with a lab & stock; −2 to −4 improvising; − for rare compounds.
  • Pharmaceuticals: stims, combat drugs, antidotes, bacta additives, anaesthetics — the healer’s and the soldier’s chemistry.
  • Poisons & Toxins: assassin’s tools and creature venoms — contact, ingested, inhaled, and injected, each with its own onset and effect.
  • Spice & Narcotics: the galaxy’s recreational and addictive chemistry — the Hutts’ trade, and a smuggler’s cargo. SW

Specialties default to each other at −3.

Explosives †
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Average · Defaults IQ−5

The trade of building, placing, and defusing charges — from a breaching charge on a blast door to a thermal detonator’s timer. Building a device is bench work (Work Hours, Part VII); setting a prepared charge is quick; disarming a live one is a real-time roll under pressure, where a critical failure is exactly as bad as it sounds. A good Explosives roll sets the blast where and how the maker intends; a poor one wastes the charge or, worse, misjudges the radius. Pairs with Chemistry for filler and with Electronics Repair (Security) or Computer Hacking for cunning triggers. Specialise:

Standard use Set, defuse, or assess a charge for demolition or effect. Time 10 minutes per attempt — Minutes to set or defuse; longer to plan a breach. Rolls One roll to set or defuse; a fail may misfire. Modifiers + with proper detonators & time; −2 rushed; −4 improvised; −5 under fire.
  • Demolition: placing charges to bring down structures, breach armour, or crater a landing pad — knowing where the blast does the most work.
  • Explosive Ordnance Disposal (EOD): finding, identifying, and safely neutralising a device someone else built — the steadiest hands in the unit.

Specialties default to each other at −4. Both fail catastrophically on a critical miss.

Medical & Biological Crafts

Physician
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Hard · Defaults IQ−7, First Aid−11, Veterinary−5

The practice of diagnosing and treating illness and injury over time — the long care that First Aid and Surgery hand off to. A successful roll identifies a disease, poison, or condition; a course of treatment (rolled per day of care) speeds recovery, fights infection, or counters a toxin. The Physician also manages bacta immersion, prescribes from a Pharmacy, and supervises a patient’s healing back from a crippling wound. In this campaign the Physician is the difference between a blaster burn that heals clean and one that festers.

Standard use Provide real medical care — treat illness, restore HP, manage recovery. Time ~1 hour per patient per day of care. Rolls One roll per patient per day; success aids natural healing. Modifiers + with a medbay/medkit; −2 field conditions; −5 with bare hands.
First Aid
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Easy · Defaults IQ−4, Physician−3, Esoteric Medicine−3

The quick, life-saving care given at the point of injury — stopping bleeding, stabilising a downed comrade, treating shock, restoring a little lost HP after a fight. Fast and broadly known; nearly every soldier and spacer carries it. It does not heal deeply — that is the Physician’s and the body’s slow work — but it keeps the dying alive until real care arrives. A medpac or bacta patch grants a bonus.

Standard use Stop bleeding, stabilise a wound, restore a little lost HP. Time ~1 minute of treatment. Rolls One roll per injury; success restores 1d−2 HP and stops bleeding. Modifiers +1 with a medkit; −2 improvising; −5 bare-handed.
Surgery
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Very Hard · Defaults First Aid−12, Physician−5, Veterinary−5 (all at an additional penalty without proper tools)

The craft of operating on a living body — extracting a blaster bolt’s slag, repairing a ruptured organ, amputating a ruined limb, or installing a cybernetic (the install half of Cybernetics & Bioelectronics). Surgery demands a stable patient, a proper theatre, and time; field surgery under fire takes a heavy penalty. Success restores function or saves a life; a critical failure does grievous harm on the table. The surgeon who fits a prosthetic and the medic who saves a gut-shot smuggler are running the same skill.

Standard use Perform an operation — repair grave injury, implant, or amputate. Time 30 minutes to hours per operation. Rolls One extended roll per procedure; a fail can worsen the patient. Modifiers + with a surgical suite; −3 field surgery; −5 without proper tools.
Bioengineering † SW
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Very Hard · Defaults none · Prereq Biology, plus Chemistry or Physician

The deep science of engineered life — culturing tissue, rewriting genetics, and growing clones in a tank. This is the secular, technical craft of the cloners of Kamino and the gene-labs of the great corporations; it is also the physical half of Sith bioalchemy, performing every step of the procedure that the dark ritual later empowers. A successful roll cultures a viable sample, designs a genetic modification, or supervises a clone’s growth — though the tank takes its calendar weeks no matter how skilled the hand. Grim, slow, expensive, and watched closely by every authority in the galaxy. Specialise:

Standard use Engineer or modify living tissue, organisms, or genetic material. Time Days to weeks. Rolls One extended roll per project. Modifiers + with a bio-lab; −3 to −5 for novel or forbidden work.
  • Cloning: growing and decanting viable organisms from genetic material — the Kaminoan art. SW
  • Genetic Engineering: rewriting an organism’s code — tailored traits, resistances, and the darker work of designed advantage.
  • Tissue & Vat-Culture: growing organs, grafts, and biological components — the bridge to cybernetic and medical work.

Specialties default to each other at −5.

Material, Document & Construction Crafts

Jeweler
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Hard · Defaults IQ−6

The fine craft of working gems and precious metals — cutting and faceting stones, setting them, and shaping delicate metalwork. In most of the galaxy it is the jeweller’s and the artisan’s trade; in this campaign it is also the physical craft behind Crystal Attunement — the precise faceting of a kyber crystal or the carving of a holocron’s lattice seed-pieces is jeweller’s work, and a good Jeweler roll is complementary to the attunement that follows. The cutting is craft and needs no Force; only the attunement does.

Standard use Appraise, cut, set, or craft gems and fine metalwork. Time 4 hours per attempt — Minutes to appraise; hours to craft. Rolls One roll per piece or appraisal. Modifiers + with a jeweller’s tools; −2 for unfamiliar or exotic stones.
Forgery & Counterfeiting
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Hard · Defaults none · Complementary Electronics Repair (Security) for chipcodes

The art of making the false pass for the true — travel papers, cargo manifests, identity chips, security credentials, and official seals. Producing a forgery is a craft roll (Work Hours by complexity); a separate Forgery or perception contest decides whether an inspector spots it. The galaxy’s underworld runs on this skill — a clean set of papers is worth more than a blaster on the right world. Secured chipcodes pair with Electronics Repair (Security) and sometimes Computer Hacking to clone the encoded data.

Standard use Create or detect a fake document, credential, or currency. Time 4 hours per attempt — Minutes to hours per item. Rolls One roll to make; a Quick Contest vs the inspector to pass it. Modifiers + from a sample, the right press, and materials; − from biometric seals, holo-watermarks, or a careful examiner.
Vehicle & Starship Assembly
Skills used Engineer (Starships / Repulsorlift) + Mechanic (the relevant systems) · Difficulty as those skills

Not a separate skill but a named Maker’s Chain: the assembly of a working vehicle or starship from parts, kits, or salvage, drawing on Engineer for the design or plan and Mechanic for the fitting of each system. A speeder from a kit is a long bench job; a starfighter from scratch is shipyard work, measured in hundreds of combined work-hours and best split across a team running parallel links. Capital-ship construction leaves the realm of personal projects entirely — it needs a drydock and a workforce. Times are in Part VII.

Standard use Assemble a working vehicle or craft from parts. Time about 1 work-day (~8h) per attempt — A long project — days to weeks. Rolls An extended chain of Engineer + Mechanic rolls. Modifiers + from a hangar, parts, and schematics; − from mismatched or salvaged components.
Construction & Salvage
Skills used Engineer / Architecture (IQ/A), Scrounging (IQ/E), Salvage (IQ/A)

The field trades of building shelter and recovering materials. Construction (Engineer or Architecture) raises prefab shelters, outpost modules, field fortifications, and emplacements — quick work with a kit, industrial work for anything permanent. Scrounging finds the part or material a maker needs from a junkpile or a market; Salvage strips a wreck for usable components — the start of many a Maker’s Chain when credits are short and a hull lies broken nearby. Times are in Part VII.

Standard use Raise a structure or strip a wreck for usable material. Time about 1 work-day (~8h) per attempt — Hours to days per job. Rolls One roll per structure or salvage job. Modifiers + from prefab kits and tools; − from raw materials, hostile ground, or haste.

Part IV·C · Foundational Sciences & Field Studies

The knowledge and operation skills that underpin the crafts — the mathematics behind an engineer’s plan, the biology behind a clone, the steady hands that disarm a lock or plot a jump. Most of these do not make objects; they enable, complement, or operate. They are listed here because no maker’s education is complete without them, and several are prerequisites for the trades above.

Foundational Sciences

Mathematics †
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Hard · Defaults IQ−6

The science of number and calculation — the bedrock beneath engineering, astrogation, and the physical sciences. Engineer requires Mathematics (Applied); Astrogation leans on it to plot a clean jump. A successful roll solves a problem, checks a calculation, or models a system. Specialise in Applied (engineering & practical computation), Pure (theory), or Statistics (probability, cryptanalysis support). Specialties default to each other at −4.

Standard use Solve an advanced mathematical or computational problem. Time 10 seconds (one turn) per attempt — Minutes to hours by difficulty. Rolls One roll per problem. Modifiers + with a calculator/datapad; −2 for novel or abstract problems.
Physics
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Very Hard · Defaults none · Prereq Mathematics (Applied)

The science of matter, energy, and force — the theory that lets an Engineer understand why a hyperdrive folds space, a blaster ionises gas, or a shield holds. Rarely rolled to make anything directly, but the foundation of original design and the diagnosis of exotic phenomena. Essential background for the finest starship, weapon, and energy-systems engineers; complements every advanced Engineer specialty.

Standard use Apply physical science to predict, analyse, or explain a phenomenon. Time 10 seconds (one turn) per attempt — Minutes to hours. Rolls One roll per problem or analysis. Modifiers + with instruments & references; −2 for frontier or exotic physics.
Biology †
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Very Hard · Defaults IQ−6

The science of living things — the foundation beneath Bioengineering, Veterinary, and the deeper reaches of medicine. A successful roll identifies an organism, predicts a biological process, or guides a genetic or ecological judgement. Required for Bioengineering. Specialise by domain (e.g. Xenobiology for alien life, Genetics, Ecology); specialties default to each other at −4.

Standard use Identify, analyse, or explain a living organism or biological system. Time 10 seconds (one turn) per attempt — Minutes to identify; longer to study. Rolls One roll per specimen or question. Modifiers + with a lab; −2 for alien or unknown life.

Allied & Operation Skills

Hazardous Materials † SW
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Average · Defaults IQ−5, the relevant science−4

The safe handling, storage, transport, and disposal of dangerous substances — reactor fuel and coolant, radioactives, exotic propellants, corrosives, toxins, and unstable compounds. Not a making-skill but a not-killing-yourself skill: it complements Chemistry, Explosives, and Engineer (Power Systems), and a good roll prevents the leak, meltdown, or contamination a failure invites. Specialise by hazard class (Radiological, Chemical/Toxic, Energetic/Propellant); specialties default at −3.

Standard use Safely handle, store, or neutralise dangerous substances. Time Minutes to hours. Rolls One roll per handling task; a fail risks exposure. Modifiers + with sealed gear; −2 improvising; − by hazard severity.
Veterinary SW
Attribute IQ · Difficulty Hard · Defaults Animal Handling−6, Physician−5 · Prereq helpful Biology

The medicine of beasts — mounts, working creatures, exotic fauna, and the warped war-beasts of bioalchemy. Diagnoses and treats non-sapient (and some near-sapient) creatures, the way Physician serves people. Complements Bioengineering when raising or repairing engineered organisms, and earns its keep for any crew that rides, hunts, or fights alongside living things.

Standard use Diagnose and treat injury or illness in an animal. Time ~1 hour per patient. Rolls One roll per animal per day of care. Modifiers + with vet supplies; −2 for exotic or alien fauna.
Astrogation (Hyperspace Navigation) SW
