Standard use Find hidden objects, compartments, or people. Time 10 seconds (one turn) per attempt — Minutes per area. Rolls A Quick Contest vs Holdout/Camouflage. Modifiers + time & a scanner; − haste or clever concealment.
Survival
Attribute Per · Difficulty Average · Defaults Per−5

Staying alive in the wild — finding food, water, and shelter, and avoiding the hazards of a hostile environment. Specialise by terrain (desert, arctic, jungle, swamp, mountain, and so on).

Standard use Find food, water, and shelter in the wild. Time 4 hours per attempt — Per day. Rolls One roll per day. Modifiers + familiar terrain/gear; − a hostile or alien biome. Specialise.
Tracking
Attribute Per · Difficulty Average · Defaults Per−5, Naturalist−5

Following trails and signs of passage — reading prints, broken ground, and spoor to pursue a being or beast across the wild. The hunter’s and bounty-tracker’s craft.

Standard use Follow a trail or sign of passage. Time 10 seconds (one turn) per attempt — Per leg of pursuit. Rolls One roll per leg. Modifiers + fresh sign/soft ground; − rain, hard ground, an old trail.
Urban Survival
Attribute Per · Difficulty Average · Defaults Per−5

Surviving and finding what you need in a city — food, shelter, safe passage, and the right people, in the alleys and underlevels. The street-dweller’s counterpart to wilderness Survival.

Standard use Survive and find resources in a city. Time 4 hours per attempt — Per day. Rolls One roll per day. Modifiers + a known city; − a hostile or strange one.
Intimidation
Attribute Will · Difficulty Average · Defaults Will−5, Acting−3

Cowing others through threat, menace, and force of presence — making a being back down, talk, or flee. The enforcer’s and dark-sider’s blunt instrument, opposed by the target’s Will.

Standard use Cow another through threat and presence. Time 5 minutes per attempt — Seconds to minutes. Rolls A Quick Contest vs the target’s Will. Modifiers + a real threat/reputation; − a brave or cornered foe.
Hobby Skill
Attribute DX/IQ · Difficulty Easy · Defaults none

A trivial pastime or minor knack pursued for pleasure — a game, a craft, a body of trivia. Rarely decisive, occasionally exactly what a scene needs. Specialise by hobby; uses DX or IQ as the hobby dictates.

Standard use Enjoy or apply a trivial pastime or knack. Time 1 hour per attempt — Varies. Rolls One roll if it matters. Modifiers Specialise by hobby; rarely decisive.

Skills Quick Reference

For player convenience, every skill row on the sheet now displays a one-line mechanical summary and a standard usage time. The complete table below covers ~270 skills; both columns are sourced from this document for canon consistency. The sheet's SKILL_INFO map can be overridden in Homebrew → Skills.

SkillMechanical useStandard time
AcrobaticsTumbles, vaults, falls; +2 to defenses or +3 to safe falling.1 second / action
Astrogation (Hyperspace)Plot a hyperspace jump; critical failure = bad jump.10 min — 1 hr / jump
ArmouryMaintain, modify, repair weapons & armour.10 min — hours
Blasters / Guns (any)Aim and fire that class of ranged weapon.1 second / shot
Brawling / Boxing / Karate / Judo / WrestlingUnarmed combat; each branch has its own defenses and damage rule.1 second / attack
ClimbingScale walls / cliffs / rigging. -DX penalties for armour.10–30 seconds / 3 m
Computer HackingSubvert security; access locked systems. Often opposed by IT.10 minutes — hours
CryptographyBreak or make ciphers and codes.Hours — weeks
Detect Lies / Body Language / EmpathyRead intent or deception in conversation.Per exchange
Diplomacy / Fast-Talk / IntimidationPersuade by reason, lies, or threats. Choose your tool.1 minute — hours
Electronics Repair / OperationDiagnose, fix, or use electronic gear.10 minutes — hours
Engineer (any)Design machines/structures of a specialty.Hours — weeks
Explosives (Demolition / EOD)Set or disarm charges.1 min — 1 hr
First AidStop bleeding, stabilise wounded; +1 HT recovery roll.10 minutes / patient
Force HealChannel Light side to restore HP. Specialty by tradition.1 second — minutes
Force Push / Pull / Lightning / ChokeTelekinesis or Dark-side attacks; FP cost per use.1 second / push (sustained for choke)
ForgeryFake documents, ID, art, currency.Hours — days
HoldoutConceal items on the body. Opposed by Observation.1 minute to stash
Knife / Sword / Lightsaber (any)Wield the named melee weapon. Each has its own parry and damage rule.1 second / attack
LockpickingDefeat mechanical or electronic locks.30 seconds — 5 minutes
Mechanic (any)Repair vehicles/machinery in the named specialty.10 minutes — hours
MetallurgyIdentify, work, and alloy metals.1–4 hours per piece
NavigationPlot route on land, sea, or in space.1 minute — 1 hour
Observation / SearchSpot ambushes, surveillance, hidden objects.1 second — 1 minute (Search: 1 min / 10 sq ft)
PharmacyCompound or identify drugs.1 hour
Physician / Diagnosis / SurgeryPractice medicine; Surgery is required for invasive work.30 minutes — hours
Piloting (any)Operate the named class of aircraft or starship.Continuous while at the controls
StealthMove quietly and out of sight. Opposed by Observation / Hearing.Continuous
StreetwiseFind contacts, fences, illicit information in urban areas.Hours
Survival (any)Live off the land in the named terrain.Per day
TacticsSmall-unit combat planning; +1 to initiative.Per scene
TrackingFollow tracks & spoor.1 minute / 100 metres

The sheet's full SKILL_INFO map covers 267 distinct skills; the table above is the GM's quick lookup for the ones rolled most often. For anything not listed, the sheet's summary column is canonical.

Sheet integration. Everything in this addendum is wired into KOTOR_Sheet_v15.html: damage-state dropdowns on every ship/droid card, an attachment-preset dropdown on every weapon's Modifications panel, and a Use / Time column on every skill row.

Learning a Skill from the Skills Side

A player flipping to Document IX (Skills) looking for "how do I level up my Mechanic?" will not find it there. The hour-economy that governs skill progression sits with Document V (Force Training), Part 3 — because the campaign treats Force-progression and skill-progression as one unified system. This Part is the cross-reference for the player who naturally starts at Skills. It re-states what Doc IX says, but framed as the practical question: "My skill is at level N. How long to reach N+1?"

The one number you have to remember. 200 study-hours = 1 character point. Every other rule on this page is a multiplier on those 200 hours.

Step 1 — How many points to reach the next level?

GURPS skill cost depends on difficulty. The cumulative point cost for the first few levels:

DifficultyPts at 1st lvl2nd lvl3rd lvl4th lvl +Hrs (cumulative)
Easy124+4 each200 / 400 / 800 / +800 per
Average248+4 each400 / 800 / 1600 / +800 per
Hard4812+4 each800 / 1600 / 2400 / +800 per
Very Hard81216+4 each1600 / 2400 / 3200 / +800 per

So if your Mechanic (Average) sits at 4 pts (level = attribute), pushing to 8 pts (attribute+1) costs 4 more points = 800 hours. Once any skill is above attribute+0, every +1 level costs a flat 4 pts = 800 hrs regardless of difficulty — mastery plateaus the same for everyone.

Step 2 — Modifiers that shave (or pad) the hours

Apply each that fits as a multiplier on the 800-hour baseline. They stack.

ModifierEffect on hoursWhen it applies
Governing attribute 13–15×0.9The skill's listed attribute is in this band
Governing attribute 16–18×0.8Two-band gift
Governing attribute 19+×0.7The 30% gift
Talent covering the skill×0.95 per level (cap ×0.8)e.g. Gunslinger, Smith, Mathematical Ability
Teacher (has skill higher than your target)×0.75Living master, formal instructor, or holocron of the right tier
Self-teaching (no teacher, no manual)×1.5; & cannot exceed attribute+0Reinventing the wheel by trial

Step 3 — Session caps: how many hours can you actually log in a day?

Hours don't stack infinitely; the mind saturates. Per Doc IX 3.2b:

Skill difficultyHrs/day cap (IQ 10)Hrs/day cap (IQ 14+)
Easy68
Average46
Hard35
Very Hard24

So at IQ 12, a real-world week of dedicated training in Mechanic (Average) gives roughly 5 hrs/day × 6 days = 30 hrs/week. The 800-hour next-level push for Mechanic therefore takes ~27 weeks of dedicated study — or about 13 weeks if you also have a teacher (×0.75 = 600 hrs / 30 hrs-per-week).

Worked example — pushing Mechanic 12 to 13

A character with IQ 12, no teacher, no Talent, currently 4 pts in Mechanic (Average, IQ-based, currently level 12, paid up to attribute+0).

  1. Next level cost: 4 more points (Average from attribute → +1).
  2. Baseline hours: 4 × 200 = 800 hrs.
  3. Attribute scaling: IQ 12 — no bonus (band starts at 13).
  4. Final: 800 hrs.
  5. At 30 hrs/week: ~27 weeks of dedicated study.

If the same character has a teacher (Mechanic 14+): ×0.75 = 600 hrs ≈ 20 weeks. If they then also push IQ to 13: ×0.9 = 540 hrs ≈ 18 weeks.

Sheet integration — the Study & Training Sessions module

The companion sheet (Skills tab) carries a Study & Training Sessions module that does this arithmetic for you. Link a project to a character skill, tick the teacher / self-teaching boxes, and the sheet computes the required hours from the formula above and locks the roll-against target to your current skill level. Each in-fiction session, click ⚄ Roll — a 3d6 success shaves margin × Hrs/Marg off the requirement. When the project completes, click + Grant pt and the sheet credits the earned points to the linked skill, recomputes the next target, and zeroes the progress bar. Crit successes auto-grant. The same module also handles Force-power study (Doc IX), monumental works (sustained campaign-level training), and free-form ad-hoc study with no linked skill.

Why this is repeated. The canon source is Document V, Part 3. This section restates it from the skill-side because the player asking "how do I level up Mechanic?" will not flip to "Force Training" by instinct. The two sections agree exactly; if they ever drift, Doc IX is the source of truth.

A Closing Note for the Maker

This Codex fills the trades the rest of the Compendium assumes but never lists. Its through-line is simple and worth restating: anyone can learn to make almost anything — the Force is for using, not building. A clever engineer and a single Force-user partner can, between them, produce every artefact in the galaxy. The crystal, the holocron, the alchemised blade — the craft of them is wits and training and a steady hand. Only their soul belongs to the Force.

— End of the Skills —

Document X

The Galaxy

An age of wars · regions, worlds & the powers that shape them

Overview

What this covers. The age you stand in — the wars that made the present, the regions and hyperlanes, and a gazetteer of the worlds you will actually visit.

How to use it. Read the Chronicle for the backdrop, then treat the Atlas as a per-world reference. Who holds what is detailed in the Factions document that follows.

Part I

The Age We Stand In

From the Old Sith Wars to the Csilla Accords · the wars that made the present · the powers that now stand

This compendium covers a single turbulent stretch of galactic history, not one fixed year. Within a lifetime the galaxy fought the Mandalorian Wars and the Jedi Civil War, endured the Purge and the hollow years that followed, and now braces for the return of an old enemy — the Sith, long thought broken, are rumoured to be massing again in the dark. In this telling those upheavals run close together, a span of decades rather than the long centuries some star-charts give them, so the Sith’s return is the looming near future and not a distant one. The exact state of any power depends on where along that arc you set your table; what follows describes each in terms broad enough to hold up wherever you place it, and the Factions document profiles them all in turn.

A Short Chronicle of the Wars SW

c. 3996 BBY — The Great Sith War

Before living memory, the fallen Jedi Exar Kun and Ulic Qel-Droma raised the banner of the resurrected Sith and plunged the Republic into the Great Sith War. They drew the dark cultists of the Krath (who seized the Empress Teta system) and the war-clans of the Mandalorians into a tide of darkness that burned worlds from the Deep Core to the Outer Rim. The Jedi broke them at terrible cost. From that victory the galaxy inherited a conviction it still holds — that the Sith are extinct, a closed chapter. The dead were entombed and the tombs were sealed. Some graves never quieted: the spirit of the ancient Sith king Freedon Nadd still broods beneath the jungles of Dxun.

c. 3976–3960 BBY — The Mandalorian Wars

Generations later the Mandalorians returned — no longer a war-clan but a crusade. Under Mandalore the Ultimate, the red-clad Neo-Crusaders swept the Outer Rim, world after world falling while the cautious Jedi Council counselled patience. Whole peoples were broken in the advance — the Cathar driven near extinction in the worst of the atrocities. Two young Jedi refused the Council’s patience: the strategist Revan and his closest friend Malak. Against orders they rallied a generation of knights and the broken Republic fleets, and they turned the war.

Victory was bought with horror. At Malachor V, the superweapon called the Mass Shadow Generator was triggered, annihilating both fleets at once and crushing the world into a graveyard of gravity and bone. The Jedi general who gave that order — remembered afterward only as the Exile, Meetra Surik — did the unthinkable to survive: she severed herself from the Force entirely. The Mandalorians were shattered as a power, their mask-helm taken; Revan and Malak vanished into the Unknown Regions, chasing something they would not name.

c. 3959–3956 BBY — The Jedi Civil War

Revan and Malak did not die in the Rim — they fell. They returned as Dark Lords of the Sith, having found an ancient engine of war: the Star Forge, a factory-leviathan of the long-dead Rakata that fed on the dark side and a nearby star to birth endless fleets. With it they brought the exhausted Republic to the edge of ruin. Then Malak betrayed his master, firing on Revan’s flagship to claim the title of Dark Lord. Revan survived, mind shattered.

The Jedi Council took the broken conqueror, rebuilt a false identity, and — not knowing what they did — set Revan loose to hunt the Forge as their unwitting agent. Across Taris (which Malak bombarded to glass in his hunt for Bastila Shan), Dantooine, Tatooine, Kashyyyk, Manaan, and the Sith tombs of Korriban, Revan gathered the fragments of the Star Maps that led to the Forge. The truth surfaced aboard Malak’s flagship the Leviathan; memory returned; and at the Forge itself, above the Rakatan homeworld of Lehon, Revan struck Malak down and ended the war. Afterward Revan walked away again — alone, into the Unknown Regions, driven by a prophecy of a great and gathering threat beyond the galaxy’s edge that no one else could see. Revan has not returned.

c. 3951 BBY — The First Jedi Purge & the Dark Wars

The peace did not hold. In the war’s shadow a new darkness hunted the survivors — not an army, but three predators who called themselves the Sith Triumvirate. Darth Nihilus was a hunger given form, a void that devoured the life of whole worlds and the Force itself; Darth Sion, the Lord of Pain, was a corpse held together by hatred; behind them moved their teacher, Darth Traya — once the Jedi Kreia. One by one the scattered Jedi were murdered, until the Order was all but erased.

Into the emptiness returned the Exile, Meetra Surik. From the mining wreck of Peragus II to the restoration world of Telos IV, she rebuilt her connection to the Force — and, unlike the Order of old, she did not walk alone. A true band journeyed with her: the rogue Atton Rand, the Miraluka seer Visas Marr, the Zabrak tech-savant Bao-Dur, the bounty huntress Mira, the scholar Mical, the Echani Brianna, and Kreia herself, whose counsel hid a Dark Lord. Together they crossed Nar Shaddaa, Dxun and Onderon (where Queen Talia faced General Vaklu’s coup), Dantooine, and at last Malachor V itself, where the Triumvirate fell.

c. 3951–3942 BBY — The Restoration

The years since the Triumvirate have been an uneasy mending — and the seeds of everything now in motion. A weary Galactic Republic, all but bankrupt and deep in debt to the InterGalactic Banking Clan, poured its remaining strength into the Restoration Zones of Telos and Dantooine. Meetra Surik refounded the Jedi Order — smaller, harder, and far less dogmatic than the Council of old — drawing her surviving companions and a trickle of new sensitives first to the ice world of Ilum.

Beyond the Republic’s tired heart, new powers gathered. The reunified Mandalorian clans passed from Canderous Ordo toward a new generation. The cold, isolationist Chiss Ascendancy of the Unknown Regions, long a rumour, began at last to make formal contact. And in the streets and senates a louder voice rose — the Silent Hand, an anti-Force populist movement preaching that the galaxy would never know peace until both Jedi and Sith were spent like coin. Czerka, Adascorp, the banking clans, and the great syndicates grew fat in the gaps the wars had left.

c. 3941 BBY — The Csilla Accords & the Present Age

The present age is defined by a single event: the great inter-faction Summit on Csilla, hosted by the Chiss in their frozen Unknown-Regions capital, where the powers of the galaxy gathered to redraw the order of things. It was nearly drowned in blood — a hidden conspiracy struck at the summit with an army of perfect impostors — but it survived, and from it came the accords that shape the galaxy as it now stands:

  • The Jedi Order is formally restored under Republic charter and granted the world of Tython, its ancient cradle.
  • The Mandalorians regain Mandalore itself and the right to militarize under Republic oversight, bound as galactic first-responders.
  • The Republic withdraws from the deep Outer Rim, which splits into the Trail Regions and the Mining Coalition under Senator Mon Gazza.
  • The Chiss take wardenship of the Unknown Regions but decline all alliance and trade until they have studied the wider galaxy.
  • The Iktotchi of Iktotch are installed as neutral arbiters of disputes between the great powers.

The Powers, Across the Ages SW

Same players, different game. The powers that carve up the galaxy barely change across this stretch of history — what changes is their strength and their mood. The full who’s-who, traced across the whole span, is the Factions document that follows; this is only the thumbnail. The arc runs in three beats, close enough together that one character can live through all of them.

A generation past — the Wars. The Republic bleeds from the Mandalorian Wars even as Revan and Malak’s Sith Empire falls on it. The Jedi are strong but splitting; the Mandalorians are a beaten army scattering into mercenary work; the Hutts and the Exchange feast on the chaos.

The present — a hollow peace. The wars are over and the galaxy is spent. The Republic is a husk, the Jedi all but hunted to extinction, the lanes unsafe, and crime the only growth industry on the Rim. A fragile peace holds — in some tellings brokered out on the edge of known space — but no one trusts it to last, and the old tombs on Korriban are said to be stirring.

Soon — the enemy returns. The Sith thought broken come back in force: a reconstituted Empire marches out of the Unknown Regions behind a hidden Emperor and a council of dark lords. The Republic must become a superpower again and the Jedi rebuild to meet it; the Mandalorians sell their guns to the highest bidder; and a long war of fleets, spies, and proxies grips a galaxy that never quite learned to stop fighting.

Choose where on that arc your campaign sits — the Factions roster and the Atlas that follow hold up at any point along it.

Part II

Regions & Lanes

How the galaxy is divided — and the worlds in each band at a glance

Regions of the Galaxy SW

Concentric Bands. The galaxy is read in rings outward from the centre. Deep Core & Core Worlds — ancient, wealthy, Republic-thick. Colonies — settled, prosperous, the Republic’s working ring. Inner Rim — still solidly Republic. Expansion Region and Mid Rim — thinner, contested, more dangerous further out.

The Frontier. Outer Rim — the great frontier, where Hutts, corporations and clans run things in practice. Hutt Space — a sphere of Outer Rim worlds the Cartels own outright. Mandalore sector — the clans’ territory, kept apart. Then Wild Space — barely mapped — and beyond it the Unknown Regions, where lanes run out and old empires sleep.

The Lanes. SW The galaxy is connected by the great hyperlanes — the Perlemian Trade Route running Coruscant-to-Outer-Rim, the Corellian Run south through Corellia, the Hydian Way the long route around, the Rimma Trade Route down through the South. Most named worlds sit on or near one. Off the lanes, travel is slow, expensive, and the realm of explorers, pirates and the desperate.

Worlds of the Era — Quick Reference SW

A Working Atlas. The whole roster on one page. Each entry names the region a world sits in, the power that effectively runs it, what it makes or moves, and the one thing that most defines it in this era. The detailed entries that follow expand on the worlds a campaign is most likely to land on.

WorldRegionControlled ByIndustry / ExportsDefining Feature
— Core & Colonies —
CoruscantCoreGalactic RepublicFinance, governance, manufacturedGalactic capital; the Senate, the Jedi Temple, every credit clears through here.
CorelliaCoreRepublic / CECStarships, smugglingCorellian Engineering & the shipwrights; a famously free-trading, gun-running culture.
AlderaanCoreRepublicArt, culture, luxuryPeaceful, ancient, monarchic; the diplomats’ world.
KuatCoreKuat Drive YardsCapital warshipsThe Republic’s great military shipyards orbit the planet in a ring.
Brentaal IVCoreTrade guildsTrade, finance, portsA junction world where the Hydian Way meets the Perlemian.
— Inner & Mid Rim —
Telos IVMid RimRepublic / CzerkaRestoration projectBombed by the Sith; Citadel Station orbits while Czerka strip-mines the surface.
TarisOuter ColoniesSith (ruin) / Republic— (ruined)Bombarded to glass by Darth Malak; the Lower City lives on as scavenger warrens.
Onderon & DxunInner RimOnderon throne / RepublicBeasts, jungle hardwoodCivil war between Queen Talia and General Vaklu; Mandalorian remnants on Dxun.
Ord MantellMid RimRepublic / piratesSalvage, contrabandNotorious frontier port; a smuggler’s favourite hideout.
ManaanMid RimSelkath (neutral)KoltoOcean world; sole source of kolto. Cuts off any side that breaks the peace.
IridoniaMid RimZabrak clansWarriors, miningHarsh, mountainous; produces fearsome warrior-clans-for-hire.
— Outer Rim & Frontier —
TatooineOuter RimHutts (de facto)Moisture farming, slaves, salvageTwin-sun desert; krayt dragons; the Hutts’ favoured Rim playground.
KorribanOuter RimSith / abandonedSith tombs, artifactsThe Sith ancestral world. Tombs full of dark relics; the Academy waxes and wanes here.
DantooineOuter RimRepublic / settlersFarmingOnce held a Jedi enclave; bombarded by Malak but the world endures.
KashyyykOuter RimWookiees / CzerkaTimber, slaves (illicit)Forest world of the Wookiees; Czerka runs a slaving operation against treaty.
RylothOuter RimTwi’lek clansRyll spice, slavesTidally-locked; the Twi’leks export ryll spice and, infamously, their own.
KesselOuter RimHutts / penalGlitterstim spiceMined by convict labour in the dark beneath the surface; the galaxy’s spice well.
Yavin IVOuter RimAbandonedMassassi ruinsJungle moon; ancient Sith temples and Massassi remnants.
— Hutt Space —
Nar ShaddaaHutt SpaceThe HuttsSmuggling, vice, slicingThe Smuggler’s Moon. The galaxy’s biggest black market in one tower-city.
Nal HuttaHutt SpaceThe HuttsHutt courts, contrabandHutt homeworld; a poisoned ruin run by the Cartels in palatial seclusion.
— Mandalore Sector —
Mandalore (Concord Dawn)Mandalore sectorMandalorian clansWar materiel, beskar (hoarded)Clan homeworld; beskar mined here never reaches the open market.
Dxun (Onderon’s moon)Mandalore-occupiedMandalorian remnantsBeasts, military campsWild jungle moon; defeated remnants gather here.
— Wild Space & Unknown Regions —
Malachor VWild SpaceDestroyed— (a graveyard)The final battle of the Mandalorian Wars; a shattered, Force-wounded world.
Lehon (Rakata Prime)Unknown RegionsLostStar Forge (destroyed)The Rakata homeworld and Revan’s Star Forge; the great secret of the Jedi Civil War.
ZiostWild SpaceSith ancestralSith ruinsThe other Sith ancestral world; ice and old palaces.
— Specialty Worlds —
IlumUnknown RegionsJedi (informal)Saber crystalsA frozen world of crystal caves; Jedi pilgrimage, not commerce.
Bespin-type gas giantsVariousMining concernsTibanna gasThe gas-giant mines that fuel blasters and cool hyperdrives.
Peragus IIOuter RimMining concernFuel miningHollowed-out asteroid mining station; volatile.

Reads with the Trade chapter (Trade & Economy doc): the Region column maps directly to the price modifiers (Core ×0.8 manufactured, Outer Rim ×1.25, etc.). A world’s controller column tells you who to bribe, fear, or impress to do business.

Part III

Worlds in Detail

Setting, faction, situation & play-hooks — the worlds a campaign will actually land on

What follows is the gazetteer proper. Each world gets the campaign-essential read: what it is, who runs it, what is happening on it right now, and what a crew can do there. Detailed entries are reserved for the worlds a game is most likely to visit; the quick-reference above carries the rest.

Core Worlds & Colonies SW

The galaxy’s heart: Republic territory, wealth concentrated, the lanes thickest, the law most present. Nothing is truly cheap here, but everything is available. Worlds further out look to the Core for finished goods, finance, and (when it suits the Senate) protection.

Coruscant — The Galactic Capital

A planet-spanning city — Coruscant is the seat of the Galactic Republic, home to the Senate, the Jedi High Council and the Temple, and the clearinghouse for every credit and contract in civilised space. The lower levels descend kilometres into darkness, levels few sentient eyes ever see; the upper towers gleam with the wealth of a thousand worlds’ tribute.

Politically it is the Republic itself — navy, courts, banks. The Jedi Order sits here in this era, watching the Sith threat from the centre of the galaxy and arguing about how to answer it. The undercity is run by a hundred small criminal networks who pay the Hutts and the Exchange a tax.

In play: high-stakes politics, Senate intrigue, Jedi Council business, courier work that pays in favours rather than credits. Anything that needs the eyes of a galactic government — or to escape them — goes through Coruscant.

Corellia — Shipwright & Smuggler

A wealthy Core world with a famously independent streak. Corellian Engineering Corporation builds a vast share of the galaxy’s freighters and many of its warships; Corellian pilots and smugglers are notorious for the “Corellian spread” — the conviction that any rule has at least three good reasons to break it.

Corellia honours its treaties with the Republic but seldom enforces them inwardly; it is one of the easiest worlds in the Core to vanish in, and one of the best places to buy a ship without too many questions.

In play: ship procurement — legitimate or otherwise — pilot recruitment, smuggler contacts, and brawls in the right kind of cantina.

Alderaan — Peace & the Court

Mountains, plains, ancient cities of white stone. Alderaan is a constitutional monarchy that has refused to keep a standing army for centuries; its diplomats are everywhere the Republic needs words instead of guns. In this era it sometimes hosts Senate functions and Jedi councils alike.

Genteel, cultured, deceptively political — the great Houses scheme behind smiles, and an Alderaanian House’s patronage opens doors no credit can.

In play: courtly intrigue, diplomacy, art and cultural objects of staggering value, the social side of the war that never reaches the front lines.

Inner & Mid Rim SW

The middle ring: contested space where Republic writ thins and the local powers, corporations and clans take up the slack. The lanes still run; the protection just costs more, in one currency or another.

Telos IV — Restoration in Progress

A formerly verdant Mid Rim world bombed during the Jedi Civil War — left a poisoned shell, with its population housed in the orbital Citadel Station while the Restoration Project (largely subcontracted to Czerka Corporation) terraforms below.

Czerka’s grip on the contracts is total, and rumour says the Restoration is half a cover for stripping the planet of what survived. The Republic looks the other way because Czerka pays the bills.

In play: corporate corruption, dispossessed Telosian refugees, salvage runs on the surface, station intrigue. A natural starting place for a campaign set in the present.

Taris — The Bombarded City

Once the great urban world of the outer Colonies — a tiered city-planet with a soaring Upper City of nobles and a sunless Lower City of swoop gangs, rakghouls, and the desperate. Then Darth Malak’s fleet glassed it from orbit.

What remains is a ruin: the Upper City reduced to spires and slag, the Lower City warrens half-collapsed but still inhabited by survivors, scavengers, and worse things drawn to the rubble. The rakghoul plague never quite died.

In play: ruin-running, the search for survivors, salvage of pre-bombardment tech, and the simmering grudge that Taris carries against the Sith.

Onderon & Dxun — A Civil War & a Jungle Moon

Onderon — jungle world, walled city of Iziz, ancient beast-riders. In this era it teeters on civil war: Queen Talia holds the throne for the Republic; General Vaklu conspires with the Sith to topple her.

Its sister moon Dxun is a wild jungle world that was a key Mandalorian staging-ground in the Wars; defeated Mandalorian remnants still camp there, watching for a new Mandalore to reunify the clans.

In play: the civil war is one of the era’s great set-piece arcs; players can side with either faction, navigate beasts and assassinations on Onderon, or treat with the Mandalorians on Dxun.

Manaan — The Kolto Monopoly

Ocean world of the Selkath: every landmass is a city built on the sea. Beneath the surface, in carefully-managed reefs, the alazhi plant and arkanian extract are processed into kolto — the era’s premier healing fluid.

The Selkath are aggressively neutral. They sell kolto to everyone, including both sides of any war, and the moment a power tries to coerce or steal from them they revoke that side’s access. The Republic and the Sith both have embassies in Ahto City and both walk very softly.

In play: legal trade and illegal kolto smuggling, espionage between the two embassies, court trials before Selkath judges (notoriously incorruptible), and undersea expeditions.

Ord Mantell — The Smuggler’s Welcome

A Mid-Rim port world the Republic technically controls and largely ignores. The pirates and smugglers who haunt it pay the right local officials and keep the rest of the planet’s mining economy running enough that nobody asks too closely.

In play: anonymous re-supply, contact with the Exchange, contract jobs that walk the line. The kind of port a crew goes to disappear for a while.

Outer Rim & Hutt Space SW

The galaxy’s frontier: where the Republic ends in practice and the Hutts, corporations, and clans rule by money, fear, and inertia. Goods are dearer, news is older, and what passes for law is whoever you paid most recently. This is where a free crew makes its fortune — and most often loses it.

Tatooine — Twin Suns & the Hutts

Outer Rim desert, twin suns, sandstorms, and krayt dragons. Tatooine is firmly Hutt-influenced even where they don’t formally rule; moisture farmers eke out water from the air, Tusken Raiders harry the sands, and Mos Eisley’s spaceport never sleeps.

It produces almost nothing the wider galaxy wants — which is why every kind of person who needs to not be found ends up there. Krayt dragon pearls are the lone fortune-grade export, and you must kill the dragon to get one.

In play: Hutt patronage, bounty work, podracing, dragon hunts, and the kind of cantina business the galaxy is famous for.

Korriban — Tombs of the Sith

A red, dead world in the Outer Rim — the ancestral homeworld of the Sith species and the burial-place of countless dark lords. Its Valley of Dark Lords is lined with the tombs of Marka Ragnos, Naga Sadow, Tulak Hord, Ajunta Pall and others; the dark side bleeds out of the soil.

A Sith Academy operates here whenever the Sith are ascendant — trainees are sent into the tombs as a final test and most of them do not come out. Force-sensitives feel the planet at orbital range.

In play: dark-side temptation, deadly tombs, artefact recovery, Sith infiltration, and the literal pilgrimage to confront the dark.

Dantooine — The Empty Plains

A pastoral, lightly-settled Outer Rim world of grassy plains and stone outcrops. For centuries it hosted a major Jedi Enclave — one of the Order’s great training centres.

Malak ordered the Enclave bombarded from orbit during the Jedi Civil War; the ruins still stand. Settlers live on, mostly farmers, and the Enclave site draws Jedi survivors, dark-side scavengers, and the occasional pilgrim.

In play: the ruins, surviving Jedi in hiding, the Mandalorian remnants who sometimes raid here, and the heavy silence the Force still holds over the place.

Kashyyyk — The Wookiees & the Slavers

A forest world of trees kilometres tall, home to the Wookiees. Wookiees are formidable warriors and dependable allies; their bowcasters and bare hands are equally deadly.

Czerka Corporation runs a slaving operation on Kashyyyk in violation of Republic treaty — protected by bribes, distance, and the fact that few in the Core care. Wookiee resistance is constant, brutal, and slow.

In play: Wookiee allies, anti-Czerka operations, treetop battles, and the very long memory the Wookiees keep of debts owed.

Nar Shaddaa — The Smuggler’s Moon

Nal Hutta’s moon — one continuous vertical city, kilometre after kilometre of neon and refinery smoke and docking towers. If a thing can be bought, sold, smuggled, or hired anywhere in the galaxy, it can be done here, openly, in daylight.

The Hutts own it. The Exchange thrives in it. The Republic has an embassy and no real authority. Every faction maintains agents; every conspiracy you have ever heard of has a safe-house here.

In play: the black market in its purest form, fences and brokers for any contraband, Hutt favour-economy, and the constant low-grade danger of a place where every grievance settles in alleys.

Kessel — The Spice Mines

A bleak, pocked Outer Rim world. Beneath the surface, in chambers no machine has ever fully mapped, convict and slave labour mines the raw substance refined into glitterstim — the premium spice.

Hutt cartels and a permissive penal regime run the operation together; spice from Kessel pays for half the Rim’s vice. The mines are infested with energy-spider analogues that have killed countless miners.

In play: smuggling runs from Kessel are the Rim’s great paying contracts — high margin, hard route, half the patrols in the Hutt sphere paid to look elsewhere.

Ryloth — Twi’leks, Ryll, and Slavers

Tidally-locked: scorched dayside, frozen nightside, the Twi’leks living in the long twilight band. They export the ryll spice (used both medically and illicitly), fine goods, and — under cartel pressure — their own people into slavery.

The clans are proud, fractious, and pragmatic; Hutt and Sith agents bid for influence; the Republic protests slavery from a comfortable distance.

In play: liberation arcs, ryll smuggling, Twi’lek allies, and the politics of clans that have learnt to survive every empire so far.

Mandalore Sector SW

A small, dangerous sector apart — the Mandalorian clans hold their territory by ferocity and by the metal in their armour. Their Wars with the Republic have just ended; the clans are scattered and watchful, and the next Mandalore has yet to take up the mask.

Mandalore (and Concord Dawn) — The Clans’ Home

The clan homeworld and the heart of the sector. Beskar — the saber-resistant Mandalorian iron the whole galaxy covets — is mined here, and almost none of it leaves. Clan-forged armour stays within the clans; outsiders buy unrefined Mando iron at best.

The Mandalorian Wars were lost recently and badly. The clans are scattered: some on Dxun, some hiring out as mercenaries, many waiting for a new Mandalore to unify them. The world is poorer than it was, and proud beyond it.

In play: mercenary contracts, beskar arms-deals (very illegal, very expensive), the politics of the clans, and the long shadow of the Wars in everyone’s memory.

Wild Space & the Unknown Regions SW

Beyond the charted lanes — sparsely-mapped Wild Space, and the unmapped Unknown Regions further still. The Republic doesn’t reach here, the lanes thin to nothing, and old Sith ruins, Rakatan relics and worse things sleep undisturbed.

Malachor V — The Wounded World

The site of the Mandalorian Wars’ final battle. Revan’s superweapon (the Mass Shadow Generator) was unleashed here; the world is shattered, its surface wrong, its place in the Force a screaming silence felt across the galaxy by every sensitive nearby.

It is officially forbidden ground. A Sith Academy of a different and stranger kind is hidden in its core in the present age.

In play: the endpoint of one of the era’s great arcs; for sensitive characters, it is the dark mirror of any pilgrimage they ever made.

Lehon (Rakata Prime) — The Star Forge

Deep in the Unknown Regions: the Rakata homeworld, where Revan and Malak found the Star Forge — the great infinite-empire-era factory orbiting Lehon’s sun, draining the dark side to manufacture warships at impossible speed.

At the close of the Jedi Civil War the Forge was destroyed; the route there is lost again. Survivor Rakata factions still live in the planet’s jungles, and ancient lore sleeps in their temples.

In play: an expedition target if the route can be found; ruined-tech salvage, lost languages, and the warning that no power should build at such scale again.

Specialty Worlds SW

Some worlds matter less for who lives there than for the one thing they produce. A pilgrimage, a fuel-stop, a single resource the galaxy cannot do without.

Ilum — The Crystal Caves

A small, ice-locked world in the Unknown Regions, claimed informally by the Jedi for centuries. Beneath its surface lie the great crystal caves, where Jedi Initiates come to face the trials of the cave and emerge bonded to a personal saber-crystal.

It is not commercial. No price is listed for an Ilum crystal because none is sold; they are taken, by Initiates, under trial, and any other route is theft.

Reputation & Faction Standing SW HOUSE

Per-Faction Standing. The crew carries a separate standing with each faction that matters to them — not one universal reputation. Standing runs −3 to +3, starts at 0 with most factions, and shifts through play (see below). The number gives a Reaction modifier with that faction (Doc I Reaction roll) and gates the gear they will sell and the welcome they offer.

Why It Matters. Standing translates straight into the existing systems: a Reaction bonus or penalty on every social roll with that faction; an availability shift (the Variation System, Trade & Economy) on what they will sell you and at what price; and on hostile worlds, whether the guards arrest, ignore, or salute. A +2 with the Mandalorians lets you buy clan-forged armour at all; a −2 with Czerka means an audit-team materialises every time you dock at a Czerka port.

Rivals & Cross-Effects. Standings are loosely paired. Earning Republic standing makes the Hutts cooler to you. Mandalorian respect costs a step with the Silent Hand. Tracking the GM may impose a one-step shift in a paired faction whenever you take a major step in another; full-table standings reflect that politics is plural.

The Standing Track

StandingEffectHow You Get There
+3 Champion+3 Reaction with the faction; access to its Rare gear; sanctuary on its worlds.Earned through a faction-defining act — saved Tython, broke the blockade, named a Mandalore.
+2 Honoured+2 Reaction; access to its Mil and Res gear at fair prices.Multiple major services rendered; the faction calls you by name.
+1 Respected+1 Reaction; trade favours; you are someone they would help.One major service, or many small ones.
 0 NeutralNo modifier. They do not know you, or they have no opinion.The default; most crews start here with most factions.
−1 Suspect−1 Reaction; watched. Contraband sales refused; questions asked.One offense, or a public association with their enemies.
−2 Enemy−3 Reaction; arrested or attacked on sight on faction-controlled worlds.Deliberate harm; betrayal; killing a respected member.
−3 Hunted−5 Reaction; bounty on the crew; pursued across the galaxy by their agents.A faction-defining offense — sided with their existential enemy, killed a leader.

Earning, Losing, Keeping

Three Scales of Act. Standing moves at three speeds. Small acts are flavour and accumulate but rarely shift standing alone. Major acts are the workhorse — a faction-flagged mission delivered or betrayed moves the track one step. Defining acts — the kind that become campaign moments — can jump straight to Champion or Hunted in one stroke.

TierExampleNotes
— Small (±1 standing point) —
Minor favourDeliver a package, escort a courier, fence a thingFaction-flagged work; never enough alone to shift standing
Public associationBe seen working for or against them onceAdds up — a pattern is noticed even if no single job is
Bribe or apologyPay or perform a gesture to soften an offenseCancels a small offense; never erases a major one
— Major (±1 step on the track) —
Faction mission completedA flagged job that mattered to themThe standard rep-driver; one step up per major mission
Faction mission betrayedA flagged job sabotaged, or its target served insteadOne step down; usually one step toward their rival’s favour
Saved a key figure / hubPulled a leader from death, broke a siege, kept a worldOften jumps two steps if witnessed
— Defining (jumps to +3 or −3) —
Faction-shaping actCrowned the next Mandalore, named the new Jedi Council seatChampion-tier; the act becomes part of the faction’s story
Existential betrayalSold a faction’s heart out to its great enemyHunted-tier; cannot be un-done by any single act

Standing With Each Faction

What Each Faction Wants. The headline values that earn or cost standing with each major power. Read with Part I (The Powers in This Age) and the Trade chapter (Doc VI Trade & Economy): if a faction controls a thing on a world, your standing with them is your access to it.

FactionHow You Earn ItWhat They Care About
— The Powers (Part I) —
Galactic RepublicLawful work, courier service, anti-Sith and anti-Hutt operationsCares about: Senate, treaty, the lanes. Hates: working with the Sith, slavers, large-scale piracy.
Restored Jedi OrderForce-positive acts, mercy, restraint, studyCares about: the Code, the Crossings, knowledge of the past. Hates: dark-side dealings, lying.
Mandalorian ClansHonour-duels accepted, hardship endured, war well-foughtCares about: the Resol’nare, blood-debts, a worthy opponent. Hates: cowardice, dishonour, harm to clan kin.
Chiss AscendancyDiscretion, intelligence shared on the wider galaxy, no demandsCares about: information, decorum, time. Hates: presumption, alliance-talk, mess.
Iktotchi ArbitersTruth-telling, abiding rulings, neutralityCares about: arbitration, honesty. Hates: lying, evasion, vendetta.
Silent Hand / Open PalmAnti-Force action, exposing Jedi or Sith excessCares about: rolling back the Force’s grip on politics. Hates: Force-users above the law (both sides).
— The Crime & Trade Powers —
Hutt CartelsProfitable smuggling, contract debts paid, no rivals favouredCares about: the cut. Hates: stiffing them, working for the Exchange exclusively.
The ExchangeQuiet work, broker fees paid, contracts honouredCares about: the network. Hates: turning informant, breaking a contract.
Czerka CorporationLucrative jobs, no questions, hush-money acceptedCares about: profit, reputation in public, deniability. Hates: anti-slavery activism, leaks.
Mining CoalitionProfitable runs into the Trail Regions, no Republic snitchingCares about: independence from the Core, ore moved. Hates: Republic spies, ore-pirates.
InterGalactic Banking ClanLoans repaid, contracts honoured, debts not defaultedCares about: the ledger. Hates: defaulters; quietly funds bounty hunters who hunt defaulters.
— Regional & Specialty —
Selkath of ManaanStrict neutrality on Manaan, respect for Selkath lawCares about: neutrality. Will revoke kolto access for any side that breaks the peace.
Onderon throneService to Queen Talia’s line; loyalty in the civil-war arcCares about: the throne. The Vaklu remnant cares about its overthrow.
Houses of AlderaanDiscretion, art, social grace, useful intelligenceCares about: their honour and their long memory. Hates: scandal.

This list is the working set; minor factions (a particular Hutt’s court, a Mandalorian clan, a House of Alderaan, an Exchange broker, an individual Jedi Master) can use the same track at the GM’s discretion — the same numbers, narrower scope. A standing with Vogga the Hutt is not the same as standing with the Hutt Cartels at large, though one can earn the other.

The Galactic Lexicon SW

Read When You Hit a Word You Don’t Know. Species, factions, places, tech, doctrine — the in-world vocabulary the rest of the book uses without stopping to explain. Stat-block notation and math live separately in the Jargon Index in Doc VI; this glossary is the setting side of the same job.

TermWhat It Means
— Major Species (the ones you meet) —
HumanThe galaxy’s ubiquitous species — majority on most Core and Rim worlds. The Republic is humans-and-their-allies in practice if not principle.
Twi’lekTall, lekku-headed humanoids of Ryloth. Famed for grace and cunning; many enslaved by Hutts and Sith. Names like Aayla, Mission Vao.
ZabrakHorned, tattooed humanoids of Iridonia and Dathomir. Tough, proud warriors; Bao-Dur and Bastila’s late master were Zabrak.
WookieeTall furred giants of Kashyyyk. Loyal, fierce, long-lived; bowcasters and bare hands equally lethal. Zaalbar is the famous example.
MandalorianNot a species but a culture — mostly Human and Taung, bound by the Resol’nare (the Six Actions). The mask makes the Mando.
SelkathAmphibian natives of Manaan. Aggressively neutral; control the kolto monopoly; their judges are famously incorruptible.
CatharFeline humanoids; nearly driven extinct by Mandalore the Ultimate’s crusade. Survivors are bitter and few.
EchaniPale, silver-haired warriors of the Echani Command. Read combat as a language; the finest unarmed and blade duelists in the galaxy.
VerpineInsectoid masters of fine engineering. Their hive-worlds produce the galaxy’s best slug-rifles and shield prototypes.
ArkanianPale, near-Human bio-engineers of the Colonies. Built kolto’s arkanian extract; some clones, some genetically gifted.
IktotchiTelepathic, twin-horned humanoids of Iktotch. Installed under the Csilla Accords as neutral arbiters between the great powers.
ChissBlue-skinned, red-eyed humanoids of the Unknown Regions. Cold, isolationist, ancient. The Ascendancy is a question, not an ally.
RodianGreen, snouted hunters of Rodia. The classic bounty hunters; trade-tongue speakers everywhere there is work.
BothanFurred near-humanoids; the galaxy’s great spies and information brokers. Bothan dies bring you Bothan secrets.
TrandoshanLizard-folk of Trandosha; vicious slavers and trophy hunters. Wookiees are their preferred prey, and vice versa.
DurosBlue space-faring traders from Duro; among the galaxy’s oldest spacer cultures. Quiet, competent, present everywhere.
BithBig-headed, big-eyed humanoids of Clak’dor; superb musicians, scientists, and analysts. The cantina-band image.
RakataThe fallen Builders — an ancient Force-touched empire that built the Star Forge. Survivors live primitively on Lehon.
MassassiSith-bred warrior species; near-extinct, remnant populations on Yavin IV in service to long-dead Sith lords.
HuttMassive sluglike crime lords of Nal Hutta and Hutt Space. Long-lived, ruthless, the galaxy’s most successful organised criminals.
Sith (species)The original red-skinned, Force-attuned people of Korriban — subjugated, then merged with, the early Dark Jedi exiles. Their name was taken by their conquerors.
— Force Traditions & Doctrine —
The ForceThe energy field generated by all living things — an it the Jedi and Sith both serve. See Doc IV (Living Force).
Light SideThe Force used with patience, restraint, and service. The Jedi path.
Dark SideThe Force used with passion, dominance, and hunger. The Sith path. Always shortens the road, and never gives it back.
The GreyPaths that walk between — the Concordance of Feathers, the Bonded Blade, and others. Stable but always precarious.
Battle MeditationA rare Force discipline (Bastila Shan’s gift) that lifts an army’s morale and coordination — whole fleets win or fold on it.
Force BondA deep psychic link between two Force-users — voluntary or imposed. The Exile is famously a node of bonds.
Wound in the ForceA place or person whose connection to the Force is so violently severed that they show up to it as a silence. Malachor V; the Exile.
— In-World Tech & Materials —
HyperspaceThe faster-than-light dimension that the galaxy’s ships traverse. Mapped along the great hyperlanes (Doc VI).
HyperdriveThe engine that enters hyperspace. Class 0.5 (racing) to Class 4 (cargo); lower is faster — and thirstier (Fuel chapter).
HypermatterThe exotic fuel of a hyperdrive. Sold as hyperfuel (HF) units; 50 cr each.
Tibanna gasGas-giant gas refined for blaster bolts and hyperdrive coolant. Mined at Bespin-type worlds.
BeskarMandalorian iron. Saber-resistant (DD÷10). Mined on Mandalore; almost never sold to outsiders.
PhrikRare saber-resistant alloy (DD÷5). Militaries hoard it; far below beskar in legend, far above durasteel in practice.
CortosisThe rarest ore in the galaxy — scarcer even than beskar — that shorts out a lightsaber blade for one turn on contact. Spikes further in price when a Force war looms.
KoltoThe era’s premier healing fluid. Manaan-only; the Selkath sell to all sides, revoke for any breach.
BactaThe newer rival to kolto, slowly spreading. More portable, less profitable to a monopoly.
ReflecHighly reflective polymer that halves the armour divisor of energy attacks (Tool 1c). Light, brittle, brilliant.
Energy weaveFamily of fibre/mesh/matrix armours that disperse blaster energy — ½eAD, the modern blaster-counter.
LightsaberThe signature weapon of the Jedi and Sith — a plasma blade contained by a magnetic field around a focusing crystal.
Star ForgeRakatan superweapon-factory that fed on the dark side and a star to manufacture warships at impossible rate. Destroyed by Revan.
Mass Shadow GeneratorBao-Dur’s gravitic superweapon used at Malachor V. Annihilated both fleets and wounded the world in the Force.
AstromechThe compact astrogation droid — calculates jump-routes, handles ship repairs, often a crew member in all but contract.
HolocronA crystalline data-vault made by a Force-user, accessible only through the Force. Holds knowledge, training, sometimes a personality.
Holocom / holoprojector3D communications projector. Standard on ships and in offices; a long-haul holocall is dear.
ComlinkPersonal radio. Civilian models on open channels; encrypted (Res) and command-grade (Mil) variants exist.
EcumenopolisA planet-spanning city. Coruscant is the canonical one; Taris was one until Malak glassed it.
RepulsorliftThe grav-tech under speeders, skiffs and atmospheric craft. Cheap power; rechargeable energy cells (EC).
— Factions & Polities —
Galactic RepublicThe galaxy’s elected government. Headquartered on Coruscant; mints the credit; weakened and indebted in this era.
Jedi OrderThe Force-using guardians of the Republic. Refounded by Meetra Surik after the Triumvirate purge; granted Tython under the Csilla Accords.
SithBoth the species (above) and the dark-side Force tradition. Officially extinct in this era; in practice, scattered.
Sith TriumvirateNihilus, Sion, Traya/Kreia — the three predators who carried out the First Jedi Purge.
Mandalorian clansA confederation of warrior clans. Defeated in the Mandalorian Wars; reunified and restored under the Accords.
Chiss AscendancyThe Unknown-Regions empire that hosted the Csilla Accords. Wardens of the Unknown, refusing alliance until they have studied us.
Iktotchi arbitersNeutral judges of disputes between the great powers, by Accord. Telepathic, austere, incorruptible.
Silent HandAnti-Force populist movement. Holds that the galaxy will know no peace until Jedi and Sith are both spent.
Open PalmThe Silent Hand’s quieter cousin, seeking the same goals through reform rather than knives.
Mining CoalitionThe frontier polity the Republic withdrew from in the Accords. Senator Mon Gazza’s ore-and-spice domain.
Hutt CartelsThe organised crime syndicate that runs Hutt Space — smuggling, spice, slavery, gambling. The Outer Rim’s real authority.
The ExchangeA galaxy-spanning criminal network — fences, brokers, bounty contracts. Wherever you are, the Exchange already is.
Czerka CorporationThe era’s most rapacious megacorp — arms, mining, colonisation, slaving. A private army and a sovereign’s arrogance.
AdascorpBiotech and droid research giant — rival to Czerka in respectable circles.
InterGalactic Banking ClanThe IBC — financiers of the Republic’s rebuild and most working starships. Hold the strings on more than the public sees.
Restored Jedi OrderThe Order as refounded on Ilum, then chartered to Tython. Smaller, harder, less dogmatic than its predecessor.
— Places & Lanes —
Core WorldsThe galaxy’s wealthy heart — ancient, Republic-thick, expensive.
Outer RimThe frontier — the Hutts, Czerka, and the clans run things in practice.
Hutt SpaceA sphere of Outer Rim worlds the Cartels own outright.
Wild SpaceCharted but barely settled. Lanes thin. Beyond it lies the Unknown.
Unknown RegionsUnmapped space. Old Sith ruins, the Chiss, and worse things sleep here.
Perlemian Trade RouteThe great hyperlane from Coruscant outward through Core and Colonies.
Corellian RunMajor lane through Corellia southward.
Hydian WayThe long route — the galaxy’s circuit lane.
Rimma Trade RouteThe southern lane down through the Rimma worlds.
— Era Shorthand —
BBY“Before the Battle of Yavin” — the standard galactic year reckoning. The present sits around 3941 BBY — the wars a generation behind, the Sith’s return looming ahead.
The WarsThe Jedi Civil War (c. 3956 BBY) against Revan and Malak, then the First Jedi Purge (c. 3951 BBY) under the Triumvirate — the back-to-back catastrophes a generation behind the present.
The ReturnThe reconstituted Sith Empire’s march out of the Unknown Regions and the long war it brings — gathering now, and in this telling breaking within a lifetime rather than centuries on.
The WarsShorthand for the linked catastrophes: Mandalorian Wars — Jedi Civil War — the Purge. Anyone over thirty has lived through them.
The RestorationThe decade since the Triumvirate fell — the rebuild, the Order’s return, the Accords.
The AccordsThe Csilla Accords of 3941 BBY — the present order: Jedi to Tython, Mandalorians restored, Republic withdrawn from the deep Rim, Chiss as wardens, Iktotchi as arbiters.

— End of The Galaxy —

Document X · B

Factions, Powers & Belief

Who runs the galaxy · who to court, fear, or fight · what people believe

Overview

What this covers. Every power worth courting or fearing — the great governments, crime and commerce, the shadow movements, the era’s faiths, and how a crew earns standing.

How to use it. Keep it open at the table as a who’s-who; the live reputation track lives in the Galaxy document it sits beside.

The Players

Who’s Who
in the Galaxy

The great powers · crime & commerce · the shadows · faith · standing

No party survives these wars without making friends and enemies of the powers that carve it up. This chapter is the consolidated roster: each faction in one place, what it wants, what it cannot forgive, and how a crew earns its favour. The narrative of how the present age came to be is in Document X (The Galaxy); the live standing-track mechanics are there too. This is the reference you keep open at the table.

Flags & Symbols — Quick Reference SW

At a glance. Every great power, house, and corporation flies a mark. The symbols below are the field-recognition key for the galaxy’s named factions and manufacturers; generic local powers (planetary governments, swoop gangs, militias and the like) share the plain field-pennant.

The Great Powers

The Galactic Republic. The galaxy’s one government of galactic scope — the Senate on Coruscant, a Supreme Chancellor at its head, the Republic Military (Army and Navy) and the Judicial Forces enforcing its writ across thousands of member worlds and sector fleets. Slow, corruptible, and perpetually overstretched on the Rim, but the only mint everyone trusts and the only banner a lawful crew can rally to. In every age it is rebuilding from the last war — gutted by the Mandalorian and Civil Wars, a hollow reeling husk after the Purge, and then, when the Sith Empire returns, a true superpower once more, locked against it across the Rim. Allied to the Jedi by ancient habit; courts lawful crews, couriers, and anti-Sith or anti-slaver work; cannot abide collusion with the Sith or large-scale piracy. Kit: Republic-issue blasters and trooper armour, and the Navy’s cruisers and gunships — Doc VII (Faction Loadouts) and the ship catalogue in Doc VI.

The Sith. The Republic’s nemesis and the dark mirror of the Jedi — though what “the Sith” are shifts with the decade, and a campaign should pick which. Under Revan and Malak they were a conquering Sith Empire built from fallen Jedi and the Star Forge’s endless fleets. Under the Triumvirate — Nihilus the hunger, Sion the pain, Traya the betrayal — they unmade the Jedi from the shadows, then seemingly burned out, leaving the name “extinct” and the galaxy at an uneasy peace. That peace is a lie: a reconstituted Empire has been gathering in the Unknown Regions, and when it returns it comes as a galactic superpower — a hidden Emperor, a Dark Council of twelve Sith Lords ruling spheres of war, mystery, and production, an Imperial military and Imperial Guard beneath them, and Korriban’s academy breeding more. Constant throughout: rule by the strong, the apprentice who plots the master’s death, and the creed that passion is power. Allies are tools and betrayal is doctrine. Kit: lightsabers and Sith alchemy (Doc IV); Imperial armour and warships once the Empire marches (Doc VI–VII).

The Jedi Order. The Republic’s ancient guardians — a monastic order of Force-users sworn to the light, governed by a High Council of Masters, ranked Padawan, Knight, and Master, and seated at great temples and enclaves (Coruscant, Dantooine, Tython, Ilum’s crystal caves). Keepers of peace rather than soldiers — though every age drags them into war anyway, and every war thins them: proud and strong before the Mandalorian Wars, all but exterminated in the Purge, then rebuilt and re-chartered just in time to meet the Empire’s return. Bound to the Republic; prizes restraint, mercy, and study; distrusts dark-side dealings, attachment, and deceit. Doctrine and training are Doc IV and Doc V. Kit: lightsabers, and robes worn over light or no armour (Doc IV, Doc VII).

The Mandalorian Clans. Not a government but a warrior culture — scores of clans bound by the Resol’nare (the Six Actions) and, when one leader can unite them, sworn to a single Mand’alor. They live for the worthy fight, and their beskar’gam (armour) and their honour are inseparable. The clans surge and scatter with their leaders: Mandalore the Ultimate’s Neo-Crusaders nearly broke the Republic in the Mandalorian Wars; beaten and leaderless they raid and hire out their guns; Canderous Ordo’s line reunites them; and now, with the Sith Empire returned, Canderous's chosen heir — Torren Wren, Mandalore the Dawn — has won back Mandalore itself and turned the clans from conquest toward honour, craft, and the role of the galaxy's first-responders, bound by the Csilla Accords and watched by the Republic. Honour them with accepted duels, hardship endured, and war well fought — never with cowardice, dishonour, or harm to clan kin. Beskar rarely leaves Mandalorian hands. Kit: clan-forged beskar armour, heavy blasters, flamethrowers, and jetpacks — Doc VII (Mandalorian Armour, Faction Loadouts) and the ship catalogue in Doc VI.

The Clans of Mandalore. The ascendant lines of the reunified people, and where each stands with Mandalore the Dawn (Torren Wren), Canderous's heir who has won back Mandalore itself and bound the clans, by the Csilla Accords, to the galaxy’s first-responder role.

ClanWho they areWith Mandalore the Dawn?
Clan WrenThe ruling clan. Torren Wren wears the mask as Mandalore the Dawn, and the artificer Alra forges the people's gear — together steering Mandalore from conquest toward honour and craft.Rules — holds the mask of Mandalore
Clan OrdoCanderous's elder, prestigious line — he took the mask at the close of the Dark Wars and quietly regathered the scattered clans before passing it on.Joined — the Preserver's founding line
Clan NightfallReborn from the ashes of Clan Varad and now led by Dax Vein — young, hungry, and rising fast.Joined — ascendant; sworn to the Dawn
The Kashyyyk ClansA frontier hold kept by accord with the Wookiees, where chieftain Drax and the hunter Kultak run the war-parties.Joined — frontier hold, by Wookiee accord
Clan CaderaHonour-bound traditionalists. Jicoln Cadera once raised the schism against the Sith-pact Mandalore; his son restored the clan's name. Discipline above glory.Joined — honour-faction — the Dawn's cause is theirs
Clan IteraSteadfast kin who rallied to Cadera's side against the Sith alliance and were welcomed back with their honour intact.Joined — Cadera's sworn allies
Clan BeroyaThe ancient hunter line (beroya, “bounty hunter”) — trackers without equal, now the clans' premier first-responders and manhunters.Joined — works the galaxy's contracts under the Accords
Clan AwaudA hard frontier clan, kin to Beroya, forged on the marches and lately steered through a contested succession.Joined — frontier muscle
Clan RookOf old House Kast, marked by the serpent that eats its own tail — quiet operators who became the clans' eyes and spynet.Joined — the Dawn's shadow service
Clan JendriRelic-keepers who scoured the Rim for the lost Mask after Malachor; loremasters of the Mandalore lineage.Joined — keepers of the mask's lineage
Clan KelbornThe soldier's clan — veterans of Mandalore the Preserver's old guard, drillmasters in the red sergeant's plate.Joined — the Preserver's old guard
Clan LokThe proud line of Artus Lok, the Mandalore who once led the clans into the Sith Empire's pay and the great arenas; they remember the glory days.Divided — knelt to the Dawn, but hardliners chafe at the Republic leash
Clan VizslaA storied, dangerous line scattered in the long fall; whereabouts unknown and loyalty unproven.Apart — lost — and actively sought
Clan VaradThe clan that Clan Nightfall broke and supplanted; its remnants were folded into the reborn Clan Nightfall.Broken — absorbed into Clan Nightfall

The Chiss Ascendancy. A cold, ancient, disciplined civilisation walled off in the Unknown Regions — ruled not by one throne but by the great Houses and their ruling families, served by a famously professional military and a culture that prizes foresight, secrecy, and the long game. They watch the wider galaxy more than they touch it and intervene only on their own terms; pre-emption is policy, and exile is the price of failure. Largely off the board through the wars, and a guarded power once the Sith Empire returns — when some Houses throw in with it. Earn them with discretion and shared intelligence; lose them with presumption or mess. Kit: charric pistols and rifles — phased-plasma weapons that out-penetrate common blasters (Doc VII) — and sleek, sensor-heavy warships (Doc VI).

The hollow peace, and the price of it. The lull after the Purge did not hold itself together by accident. With the Core too spent to police the galaxy, a settlement was brokered far out on the edge of known space — the Csilla Accords, hosted by the Chiss — and its influence ran deeper than the few quiet years it bought. It raised the Iktotchi Arbiters, telepathic and famously incorruptible, as neutral judges trusted to rule between the great powers; it let independent ore-worlds organise as a frontier Mining Coalition beyond the Republic’s reach; and it taught a war-weary galaxy to imagine peace as something other than the mere absence of fighting. None of it survives contact with the Sith’s return — and the scramble to defend, exploit, or bury the Accords is a campaign in its own right.

Crime & Commerce

The Hutt Cartels. The Outer Rim’s real authority — a sprawl of rival kajidics (clan-businesses) run by the Hutts from Nal Hutta and the vice-moon Nar Shaddaa, dealing in spice, smuggling, slavery, gambling, and protection. They answer to no government and care for exactly one thing: the cut. Honour a debt and they deal with you forever; stiff them and the bounty is posted before you make orbit. Across the ages they only grow fatter — and when the Sith Empire returns, the Cartel plays both sides and comes out a power in its own right. Allegiance is rented by the hour. Kit: whatever credits buy — mercenary muscle, modified freighters, and the Exchange’s catalogue (Doc VI–VII).

The Exchange. The galaxy’s largest criminal network — not a gang but a market: fences, slicers, brokers, and contract killers wired through every spaceport, run by crime lords who rarely meet and trust no one. Wherever you are, the Exchange is already there. It brokers everything the law forbids and a good deal it doesn’t. Quiet, professional work and contracts honoured raise you with it; turning informant ends you. Kit: the black market, entire (Doc VII).

The Bounty Brokers’ Association. The guild that lends the hunter’s trade a veneer of order — it registers bounty hunters, posts and authenticates contracts, takes its broker’s cut, and enforces a code that stops hunters simply murdering each other over a mark. Neutral by charter, it sells its services to Hutts, corporations, and governments alike, and in the great hunts it crowns the best of the trade. To a registered hunter it is licence and lifeline; cross it and no honest contract finds you again. Kit: hunter loadouts — tracking gear, restraints, armour and heavy weapons (Doc VII).

Pirates, Slavers & Free Companies. Below the organised cartels swarms everyone else — pirate fleets working the thin Rim lanes, slaver rings feeding Hutt and corporate markets, and free mercenary companies who rent their guns to whoever pays this week. In the lawless years after the wars they multiply: a strong faction buys them, a weak one is hunted by them. No loyalty outlives a better offer. Kit: stolen and surplus military hardware, run hard and patched often (Doc VI–VII).

The megacorporations. Beneath the wars and the rackets, the galaxy’s real machinery is built, armed, fuelled, and financed by a handful of vast firms. Most fly Republic colours for convenience, but a megacorp’s only true flag is its ledger — and more than one sells to every side of a war at once.

Czerka Corporation. The most rapacious of the lot — arms, mining, colonisation, droids, and, wherever it can get away with it, slaving, all backed by a private army and the arrogance of a sovereign state. Czerka goes where the Republic’s writ runs thin and takes what it likes. It pays handsomely for no questions and for silence; it loathes abolitionists, auditors, and leaks. It outlives every regime by selling to all of them. Kit: Czerka-brand blasters, mining and security droids, and survey ships (Doc VII–VIII, Doc VI).

Aratech. The galaxy’s great maker of repulsorlift vehicles and war droids — speeder bikes and swoops, combat and labour droids, and military hardware sold to the Republic and to anyone else with the credits. Solid, ubiquitous, unglamorous engineering that turns up under half the riders and half the patrols in known space. Kit: speeder bikes and Aratech droid lines (Doc VI, Doc VIII).

Corellian Engineering Corporation. If it flies and isn’t a warship, Corellia probably built it — CEC is the dominant maker of freighters, transports, and light craft, and its hulls, endlessly modified by their owners, are the working backbone of honest trade and smuggling alike. Corellians prize free trade and their own independence above anyone’s politics. Kit: the freighter and transport catalogue (Doc VI).

Rendili StarDrive. The old shipwright of the Republic Navy — heavy cruisers and capital hulls, the spine of the war fleet. Conservative, government-tied, and slow to change, but when the galaxy needs warships in a hurry, Rendili is who builds them. Kit: capital warships (Doc VI).

The InterGalactic Banking Clan. The galaxy’s financiers — they underwrite the rebuild, hold the paper on most working starships, and lend to governments and crime lords without distinction. They care only about the ledger, stay studiously neutral in every war, and quietly bankroll the hunters who chase defaulters. Owe them and you are never quite free. Kit: credit, leverage, and the collectors who enforce both.

Adascorp. Czerka’s more respectable rival — the Arkanian-run Adasca BioMechanical Corporation, trading in genetic work, cybernetics, and droid intelligence behind a clean reputation and ruthless ambitions. Where Czerka strip-mines, Adascorp experiments — and is not above selling the results to the highest bidder. Kit: cybernetics, medical and bio-tech (Doc III, Doc VII).

The Shadows & the Movements

The Sith, between the wars. Even when no Empire flies the banner, the dark side never truly leaves the board. Tomb-worlds like Korriban and Ziost still whisper to the ambitious; hidden academies wax and wane; fallen Jedi and self-taught dark-siders cut their own bloody paths; and in the years of the hollow peace a reconstituted Empire is already gathering its strength out in the Unknown Regions (see The Great Powers). Treat any Sith presence as the seed of a campaign, not a single encounter.

The Krath & the GenoHaradan. Old poisons that outlive their makers. The Krath are an ancient alchemical Force-cult, heirs to sorcery older than the Sith Wars, trading in twisted creatures and forbidden lore. The GenoHaradan are a secret guild of assassins who believe they prune the galaxy for its own good, killing kings and crime lords alike on contracts no one admits to issuing. Both deal in death and patience, and both have outlasted every government that tried to stamp them out.

The Order of Revan. A secret society that venerates the fallen Jedi-turned-Sith-turned-redeemer Revan as the one mind who grasped both sides of the Force — and the war still to come. The Revanites recruit quietly from Republic, Empire, and underworld alike, hide masked within every faction, and pursue an end only their inner circle truly knows. To meet one is to never be sure who else in the room wears the mask.

The Silent Hand & the Open Palm. The age’s populist tide — the conviction, born of war-weariness, that the galaxy will know no peace until the Jedi and the Sith are both spent. The Silent Hand pursues it with knives, killing Force-users and the lords who wield them; the Open Palm with reform, organising and undermining. Both swell after every great war and distrust any Force-user who stands above the law. Wherever a war has just ended — or is about to begin — they find recruits.

Regional & Planetary Powers

Below the galactic powers. The galaxy is a patchwork of regional blocs and planet-level factions — the powers a crew actually trips over between the great events. The Atlas (Doc X) maps the worlds themselves; this is the who’s-who of who runs them, and the kinds of local power you will deal with anywhere you land.

Hutt Space. The Hutt-ruled heart of the Outer Rim — a sprawl of worlds where the only law is the kajidic’s word and the only currency is leverage. Nal Hutta and Nar Shaddaa anchor it; spice, slaves, and smuggling flow through it; and the Republic’s writ stops at its border. Every other power treats with the Hutts on the Hutts’ terms. (Atlas: Outer Rim & Hutt Space.)

The Mandalore Sector. The clans’ home stretch of space, ruled in war and peace by whoever holds the title of Mand’alor — fiercely independent, militarised, and shaped entirely by the warrior culture. United, it is a power; scattered, it is a contested frontier picked at by its neighbours. (Atlas: Mandalore Sector.)

The Corellian Worlds. A wealthy, free-trading sector built on shipyards and an independent streak a parsec wide — nominally Republic, practically a law unto itself, and the source of half the hulls in known space. Corellians bristle at outside control and quietly profit from every side of every war. (Atlas: Corellia.)

The Tion Hegemony. An ancient, insular cluster on the Outer Rim that predates the Republic’s reach there — proud of a history older than the Core’s, jealous of its autonomy, and run by its own aristocracy and traditions. A regional power that still remembers when it was the only power for a thousand light-years.

The independent Rim. Beyond the blocs lie countless unaligned worlds — free ports, mining concerns, refugee colonies, and frontier holdouts — that bend to whoever brings trade or guns this season. Ord Mantell and its like thrive on exactly that: everyone is welcome and no one is in charge. (Atlas: the Outer Rim worlds.)

Planetary governments & nobility. Most settled worlds answer to a crown, a council, or a governor — from Onderon’s contested throne to Alderaan’s courtly houses to a company-appointed administrator on a corporate world. Their reach rarely runs past the orbital docks, but on the ground they hold the keys. (Atlas: Onderon, Alderaan, Telos.)

Swoop gangs & street syndicates. Every crowded world has its turf wars — swoop-racing gangs, protection rackets, and spice crews fighting over the lower levels. The rival gangs of a place like Taris are the type: armed, territorial, and useful or lethal depending on the week. Kit: swoop bikes and street weapons (Doc VI–VII). (Atlas: Taris.)

Colonial & corporate holdings. Where a megacorp plants a flag, the company is the government — Czerka’s mines, plantations, and “security zones” on worlds like Tatooine, Kashyyyk, and Korriban, run for profit with a private army and no pretence of consent. (Atlas: Tatooine, Kashyyyk, Korriban; and Czerka under Crime & Commerce.)

Indigenous peoples & their factions. Many worlds belong, by right if not by power, to those who were there first — the Wookiees of Kashyyyk, the Sand People of Tatooine, the Selkath who guard Manaan’s kolto, the Beast-Riders of Onderon’s jungle moon. Some treat with outsiders, some resist them, and all keep politics of their own. (Atlas: Kashyyyk, Tatooine, Manaan, Dxun.)

Spaceport underworlds. The galaxy’s vice-ports are factions unto themselves — Nar Shaddaa the Smuggler’s Moon and a dozen lesser dens where fixers, fences, and gangs hold more real power than any government. If it cannot be bought elsewhere, it can be bought here. (Atlas: Nar Shaddaa, Ord Mantell.)

Local militias, mercenaries & planetary defence. Every world that can afford it fields muscle — a planetary guard, a noble’s house troops, a hired mercenary company, or a militia raised against raiders. They rarely matter to the galaxy at large, and they are the first thing a crew meets when it makes trouble dirtside.

Religion & Belief

The galaxy’s great religion is the Force, and its two famous paths are not the whole of it. The mechanics and doctrine of every tradition live in Document IV (The Living Force) and Document V (Force Training) — this is only the map.

PathBelief, in a LineRead
The Light (Jedi)The Force served with patience, restraint, and duty; passion is the danger.Doc IV
The Dark (Sith)The Force seized through passion and dominance; it shortens every road and never gives it back.Doc IV
The GreyPaths that walk between — the Concordance of Feathers, the Bonded Blade — stable but always precarious.Doc IV
The hungering pathsDarker roads — the Hollowing, the Inscribed Voice, the Unchained — that grant power but bill it to the user’s own body and soul.Doc IV
The minor traditionsThe galaxy’s lesser Force-sects, lone mystics, and half-remembered orders — fragments of belief surviving on the edges.Doc IV
The Resol’nareThe Mandalorian creed — the Six Actions that make a Mando. A culture and a faith, not a Force tradition.10b.1
Selkath neutralityNot a faith but a sworn principle: Manaan sells kolto to all sides and revokes it from any that break the peace.Doc X

For the table. Most ordinary people in this era are not Force-users and never meet one — the Force is awe and rumour to them, not creed. Play the faith of the galaxy mostly as attitude toward Jedi and Sith, and save the deep doctrine for the characters who actually walk a path.

Standing With the Factions

A crew’s reputation with each power rises and falls on what it does for — or to — them. The full standing-track and how points move on it is the reputation system in Document X; this is the quick read.

FactionRaise It ByThey Cannot Forgive
RepublicLawful work, courier runs, anti-Sith / anti-Hutt opsWorking with the Sith; slaving; mass piracy
Jedi OrderMercy, restraint, study, Force-positive actsDark-side dealings; lying
The Sith / Sith EmpireStrength shown; ruthless service; a Lord impressedWeakness; betrayal found out; defying a Sith Lord
Mandalorian ClansDuels accepted, hardship endured, war well-foughtCowardice; dishonour; harm to clan kin
Chiss AscendancyDiscretion; intelligence shared; no demandsPresumption; alliance-talk; mess
Hutt CartelsProfitable smuggling; debts paid; no rival favouredStiffing the cut
The ExchangeQuiet work; broker fees paid; contracts honouredTurning informant; breaking contract
Bounty Brokers’ AssociationContracts fulfilled; the code kept; fees paidKilling registered hunters; skipping a mark; working unregistered
CzerkaLucrative jobs; no questions; hush-moneyAnti-slavery activism; leaks
InterGalactic Banking ClanDebts serviced; deals kept; profit steered their wayDefault; fraud; a stiffed loan
Silent Hand / Open PalmForce-tyrants opposed; their cause aided quietlyShielding a Sith or a lawless Jedi; betraying the cause
Selkath of ManaanStrict neutrality on Manaan; respect for Selkath lawBreaking the peace — they revoke kolto

— End of Factions, Powers & Belief —
